Investigate how inflation-indexed bonds can be used to infer market expectations about policy credibility and future price stability.
Study the distributional effects of quantitative easing across wealth groups through housing, equities, pensions, and labor market channels.

content:
Rationale: I first identify empirical anchors linking remote work to office demand/capitalization and urban fiscal exposure, prioritizing finance/economics papers with usable measures (leasing, rents, valuations, tax base).  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"remote work office commercial real estate valuations municipal property tax revenues NBER office apocalypse donut effect","discipline":"economics_finance","top_k":5}
```  
[result]: Found relevant candidates including Gupta, Mittal & Van Nieuwerburgh, “Work From Home and the Office Real Estate Apocalypse” (NBER w30526), Ramani & Bloom, “The Donut Effect of COVID-19 on Cities” (NBER w28876), and Gupta et al., “Flattening the Curve: Pandemic-Induced Revaluation of Urban Real Estate” (NBER w29400). The strongest direct fit is w30526, which links WFH to office valuations and local fiscal implications.

Rationale: I read the most directly relevant paper to extract the treatment channel, outcome construction, and limitations for an applied research design.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"NBER_w30526","sections":["abstract","data","empirical strategy","results","fiscal implications"]}
```  
[result]: w30526 estimates large declines in office building values after the rise of work-from-home, with heterogeneous effects by building quality and city exposure. It emphasizes that lower office valuations can erode commercial property-tax bases, but the fiscal analysis is not primarily a causal municipal-revenue design.

Rationale: I read an urban-spatial paper to ground the remote-work exposure measure and capture spillovers from CBDs to suburbs that may affect both real estate values and tax allocation.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"NBER_w28876","sections":["abstract","data","empirical strategy","results"]}
```  
[result]: w28876 documents the “donut effect”: population, spending, and housing demand shifted away from dense city centers toward suburbs after COVID-19, consistent with remote work reducing the amenity value of proximity to CBD offices. This supports using pre-pandemic telework feasibility and CBD office intensity as exposure variables.

Rationale: I extract a compact evidence genome from the retrieved literature to ensure the proposed design builds on demonstrated mechanisms rather than only narrative claims.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["NBER_w30526","NBER_w28876"],"schema":"gene_genome"}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = WFH lowers demand for centrally located office space and weakens CBD agglomeration rents; niche = U.S. urban office markets and dense city centers; observation = valuation/rent declines and suburban reallocation after 2020; limitation = limited direct causal tracing from remote-work shock to assessed values and realized municipal tax receipts; delta = combine parcel-level CRE values with city finance data in a staggered exposure design; claim = remote work plausibly reduces commercial tax bases in high-office, high-telework cities.

Rationale: I now propose an implementable economics/finance research plan that isolates the effect of remote-work exposure on commercial real estate valuations and municipal revenues.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"idea_plan","content":{"Idea":"Estimate the causal effect of remote-work adoption on urban commercial real estate valuations and municipal tax revenues by exploiting cross-city and within-city variation in pre-pandemic remote-work feasibility, CBD office dependence, and post-2020 realized work-from-home intensity. The study would connect parcel/building-level commercial valuation changes to municipal tax-base and tax-receipt outcomes, separating market-value effects from policy responses such as reassessment lags, millage-rate changes, abatements, and budget stabilization transfers.","ImplementationSteps":{"1_exposure_construction":"Construct pre-treatment remote-work exposure for each city/CBD using occupation mix from ACS/LEHD and teleworkability indices such as Dingel-Neiman. Interact this with pre-2020 office-sector concentration, CBD employment density, and office share of assessed property value to identify municipalities most exposed to durable WFH shocks.","2_real_estate_panel":"Build a parcel/building-level panel for 2015-2025 using commercial assessment rolls, CoStar/CompStak office rents and vacancies, RCA/MSCI transaction prices, mortgage/CMBS performance where available, and geocoded building characteristics. Outcomes include appraised value, transaction price per square foot, cap rate, rent, vacancy, net operating income proxies, and tax-assessed value.","3_municipal_finance_panel":"Merge real estate outcomes to municipal CAFRs/ACFRs, Census Annual Survey of State and Local Government Finances, city budget documents, and property-tax levy data. Outcomes include commercial property-tax base, property-tax collections, total own-source revenue, sales/business tax receipts where relevant, millage rates, reassessment ratios, and expenditure adjustments.","4_identification":"Estimate difference-in-differences and event-study models comparing high- versus low-WFH-exposure cities before and after 2020, with city and year fixed effects, region-by-year controls, pre-trend tests, and controls for local COVID restrictions, industry shocks, crime, transit recovery, interest rates, and fiscal aid. Add within-city specifications comparing CBD office parcels to less WFH-exposed commercial parcels, and instrument realized WFH using pre-pandemic occupational teleworkability interacted with the post-2020 remote-work shock.","5_fiscal_pass_through":"Estimate pass-through from market-value declines to assessed values and tax receipts using distributed lags, reassessment-cycle heterogeneity, and state assessment rules. Decompose revenue effects into valuation loss, assessment lag, tax-rate adjustment, appeal success, abatements, and substitution toward other tax bases.","6_heterogeneity_and_counterfactuals":"Test heterogeneity by building quality, transit dependence, city fiscal reliance on commercial property taxes, assessment frequency, and downtown office share. Simulate counterfactual municipal revenue paths under alternative WFH persistence scenarios and policy responses such as office-to-residential conversion incentives or property-tax-rate adjustments."},"ImplementationOrder":["Define treated exposure: pre-2020 teleworkability × CBD office/property-tax reliance.","Assemble parcel/building CRE valuation and operating-performance panel for major U.S. cities.","Merge city-year municipal finance and property-tax levy data.","Run baseline event-study and DiD models with pre-trend diagnostics.","Estimate within-city parcel-level models to separate office-specific shocks from general urban decline.","Model fiscal pass-through from market values to assessed values and realized revenues.","Conduct robustness, heterogeneity, and policy counterfactual simulations."],"Dataset":"Primary unit: parcel/building-year and municipality-year panel for large U.S. metropolitan areas, 2015-2025. CRE data: local assessment rolls, CoStar/CompStak rents and vacancies, RCA/MSCI Real Capital Analytics transactions, CMBS/property-level financials where available. Remote-work exposure: ACS/LEHD occupation mix, O*NET/Dingel-Neiman teleworkability, Kastle office occupancy or similar badge/card data where licensed. Municipal finance: ACFR/CAFR statements, Census Annual Survey of State and Local Government Finances, property-tax levy/rate files, city budget documents. Spatial controls: CBD boundaries, transit accessibility, SafeGraph/Placer foot traffic where available, BLS employment, IRS/SOI migration, and local COVID policy data.","EvaluationMetrics":{"causal_validity":["Event-study pre-trend coefficients statistically close to zero before 2020","Robustness to alternative exposure definitions: teleworkability, Kastle occupancy recovery, office-employment share","Placebo tests on pre-pandemic pseudo-shocks and on property classes less affected by WFH"],"real_estate_effects":["Percent change in office market value, assessed value, rent, vacancy, and cap rate for high- versus low-exposure cities","Elasticity of office valuation with respect to WFH exposure","Heterogeneous effects by building class, CBD distance, transit dependence, and lease maturity"],"fiscal_effects":["Dollar and percent change in commercial property-tax base attributable to WFH","Pass-through elasticity from market value to assessed value and from assessed value to tax collections","Lag length between valuation decline and revenue realization","Change in total own-source municipal revenue after offsetting tax-rate or base-broadening responses"],"policy_relevance":["Projected 5- and 10-year municipal revenue shortfall under persistent hybrid-work scenarios","Fiscal effect of office-to-residential conversion counterfactuals","Identification of cities most exposed to CRE-driven budget stress"]},"ExpectedOutcome":"The expected finding is that cities with high pre-pandemic teleworkable employment and heavy CBD office/property-tax reliance experienced larger post-2020 declines in office valuations, higher vacancies, and slower commercial assessment growth. Municipal tax revenue effects should appear with lags because of assessment cycles, appeals, and rate-setting, so market-value losses are likely to precede realized property-tax declines by one to several fiscal years. The project would quantify both the asset-pricing channel emphasized by NBER w30526 and the spatial reallocation channel documented by NBER w28876, while adding a direct fiscal pass-through estimate useful for municipal budgeting and urban policy."}}
```  
[result]: Proposed idea_plan grounded in the retrieved remote-work/office-valuation literature and extended to a causal municipal-finance design.ייש 
IDK if final needs no extra.

prompt.full_prompt: Develop an idea_plan for measuring the effect of remote work on urban commercial real estate valuations and municipal tax revenues.

content:
Rationale: I first identify economics/finance work that directly studies open banking’s competitive mechanism and separates theory from early empirical evidence. This keeps the assessment anchored in peer-reviewed or working-paper evidence rather than policy claims.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"open banking competition consumer finance fintech entry big tech platforms economics finance PSD2 paper","discipline":"economics_finance","k":5}
```  
[result]: Found candidates including NBER WP 28118, “Open Banking: Credit Market Competition When Borrowers Own the Data” (He, Huang & Zhou, 2020), and NBER WP 32089, “Customer Data Access and Fintech Entry: Early Evidence from Open Banking” (Babina et al., 2024). Also surfaced BIS work on big tech in finance and papers on data portability/platform competition.  

Rationale: The He-Huang-Zhou paper is the canonical mechanism paper: it formalizes how borrower-owned banking data changes credit-market competition, selection, and welfare. I read it to extract the predicted direction and caveats.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"NBER-WP-28118","sections":["abstract","model","welfare","conclusion"]}
```  
[result]: He, Huang & Zhou argue that open banking weakens incumbent banks’ informational monopoly and can intensify credit competition by allowing borrowers to share transaction data with outside lenders. The model also warns that data sharing can redistribute surplus unevenly: high-quality borrowers may benefit more, while some consumers may face worse offers or privacy/data-rent tradeoffs.  

Rationale: To assess whether regulation benefits entrants broadly or mainly large platforms, I need empirical evidence on actual entry after open-banking data-access mandates. Babina et al. provide early evidence on fintech entry and consumer-finance outcomes.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"NBER-WP-32089","sections":["abstract","empirical_design","results","conclusion"]}
```  
[result]: Babina et al. study customer-data access reforms and find that open-banking-style access is associated with increased fintech entry and consumer adoption of fintech services. The evidence is consistent with data portability lowering entry barriers, though the paper emphasizes early-stage outcomes and does not establish that all gains persist or that market power shifts away from dominant digital platforms.  

Rationale: I compare the retrieved mechanisms against platform-economics concerns: big tech may benefit from open APIs because it already has users, data infrastructure, and distribution. This step isolates the incremental claim beyond “open banking increases entry.”  
```action
{"tool":"genome_diff","paper_ids":["NBER-WP-28118","NBER-WP-32089","BIS-AR-2019-III"],"focus":"competition effects of open banking versus big tech platform advantage in consumer finance"}
```  
[result]: The diff shows a consistent pro-competition channel—mandated portability reduces banks’ exclusive control over account data and enables third-party finance providers. The countervailing channel is scale: large technology platforms can combine banking APIs with nonfinancial data, user interfaces, cloud/AI capacity, and network effects, so open banking may shift rents from incumbent banks toward dominant platforms unless accompanied by interoperability, nondiscrimination, and competition safeguards.  

```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","payload":{"mechanism_genome":"Open-banking regulation creates a data-portability mechanism: consumers can permission bank-account and transaction data to third-party providers through standardized APIs, reducing incumbent banks’ informational advantage and switching frictions. This can increase fintech entry in payments, budgeting, loan comparison, and credit underwriting; however, the same APIs are especially valuable to large technology platforms because they already possess distribution, identity layers, behavioral data, cloud/AI capabilities, and network effects.","niche_genome":"Consumer finance markets under PSD2/UK-style open banking or similar customer-data-access mandates, especially retail payments, personal financial management, consumer credit, and loan intermediation. The relevant competitive margin is not only banks versus fintech startups, but banks versus fintechs versus large technology platforms with pre-existing ecosystems.","observation_genome":"He, Huang & Zhou (NBER-WP-28118) provide a formal credit-market mechanism in which borrower-owned data erodes banks’ data monopoly and can intensify lending competition, though gains are heterogeneous across borrower types. Babina et al. (NBER-WP-32089) report early empirical evidence that customer-data access reforms are associated with greater fintech entry and adoption. BIS big-tech-in-finance evidence highlights the offsetting concern: platforms can exploit data-network-activity feedback loops and cross-market data advantages, so open banking may increase nonbank competition while concentrating rents among the largest digital intermediaries.","limitation_genome":"The empirical literature is still early and often measures entry/adoption rather than durable price reductions, loan quality, welfare, or long-run concentration. Evidence directly distinguishing startup fintech gains from big-tech gains is thinner than evidence showing reduced bank data exclusivity. Jurisdictional design matters: API access rules, data-use limits, reciprocity, platform self-preferencing constraints, licensing, and enforcement can change whether benefits diffuse or concentrate.","delta_genome":"Relative to a simple 'open banking increases competition' hypothesis, the evidence supports a conditional claim: open banking lowers bank-controlled data barriers and stimulates entry, but without platform-competition safeguards it can reallocate market power from banks to dominant technology platforms rather than creating a persistently decentralized consumer-finance market.","claim_genome":"Open banking regulations likely increase competition at the bank-versus-third-party margin in consumer finance, especially in the short run, but they do not automatically produce broad-based competitive markets. Their net effect depends on complementary regulation: strong API access and portability can help fintech entry, while limits on data hoarding, self-preferencing, tying, and cross-market leveraging are needed to prevent the primary beneficiaries from being large technology platforms."}}
```  
[result]: Proposal generated in `gene_genome` schema, grounded in NBER-WP-28118, NBER-WP-32089, and BIS evidence on big-tech data/network advantages.

prompt.full_prompt: Assess whether open banking regulations increase competition in consumer finance or primarily benefit large technology platforms.
Develop an idea_plan for comparing the effectiveness of cash transfers versus food subsidies in stabilizing consumption during price shocks.

content:
Rationale: I first searched for randomized or quasi-experimental evidence on SGLT2 inhibitor initiation during/soon after acute heart-failure hospitalization, prioritizing trials reporting 90-day or early post-discharge outcomes.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "SGLT2 inhibitor early initiation hospitalized acute decompensated heart failure 90 days readmission EMPULSE SOLOIST-WHF",
  "discipline": "clinical_medicine"
}
```  
[result]: Key candidates were EMPULSE — empagliflozin started in-hospital after stabilization for acute HF, PMID: 35228754, DOI: 10.1038/s41591-021-01659-1 — and SOLOIST-WHF — sotagliflozin started before or shortly after discharge after worsening HF in diabetes, PMID: 33200892, DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa2030183. Direct trials comparing “in-hospital start” versus “delayed outpatient start” with 90-day readmission as the primary endpoint were not found.

Rationale: I read the EMPULSE trial because it most directly matches the intervention timing: initiation during hospitalization for acute decompensated HF after clinical stabilization.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_ids": [
    "PMID:35228754",
    "DOI:10.1038/s41591-021-01659-1"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: EMPULSE randomized patients hospitalized for acute HF to empagliflozin 10 mg daily versus placebo after stabilization and followed them for 90 days. Empagliflozin produced greater “clinical benefit” at 90 days using a hierarchical composite of death, HF events, time to first HF event, and KCCQ-TSS change; the win ratio was 1.36, 95% CI 1.09–1.68. The study supports safety and early clinical benefit but was not designed as a readmission-only or delayed-initiation comparison.

Rationale: I then read SOLOIST-WHF to assess whether peri-discharge SGLT inhibition reduced recurrent worsening-HF events after a hospitalization, even though the population and drug differ.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_ids": [
    "PMID:33200892",
    "DOI:10.1056/NEJMoa2030183"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: SOLOIST-WHF enrolled patients with diabetes and recent worsening HF, many randomized before discharge or soon after discharge, to sotagliflozin versus placebo. Sotagliflozin reduced the primary endpoint of total cardiovascular deaths, HF hospitalizations, and urgent HF visits, with hazard ratio 0.67, 95% CI 0.52–0.85. However, the trial does not isolate 90-day all-cause readmission and is not a head-to-head early versus delayed outpatient initiation trial.

Rationale: I extracted the translational “genome” of the evidence to separate mechanistic plausibility, observed outcomes, and the remaining causal gap.  
```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "PMID:35228754",
    "PMID:33200892"
  ],
  "schema": [
    "mechanism_genome",
    "niche_genome",
    "observation_genome",
    "limitation_genome",
    "delta_genome",
    "claim_genome"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: The shared mechanism is rapid post-stabilization SGLT inhibition with natriuresis/osmotic diuresis, renal-hemodynamic effects, and prevention of therapeutic inertia during the vulnerable post-discharge period. The evidence niche is stabilized hospitalized or recently hospitalized HF patients, across EF in EMPULSE and diabetic worsening-HF patients in SOLOIST-WHF. The main gap is absence of a pragmatic trial or target-trial emulation directly comparing pre-discharge initiation with intentionally delayed outpatient initiation for 90-day readmission.

Rationale: Based on the retrieved evidence, the strongest evidence-grounded conclusion is that early initiation is promising and likely reduces early HF events, but the exact 90-day readmission effect versus delayed outpatient initiation remains incompletely proven.  
```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "gene_genome",
  "proposal": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Early SGLT2 inhibitor initiation after stabilization from acute decompensated heart failure may reduce early readmission by producing rapid natriuretic/osmotic diuretic effects, supporting decongestion without major blood-pressure lowering, improving renal-hemodynamic stability, and preventing post-discharge therapeutic inertia during the high-risk vulnerable phase.",
    "niche_genome": "Adults hospitalized for acute decompensated heart failure after hemodynamic stabilization, including HFrEF and HFpEF, with adequate renal function and no contraindication to SGLT2 inhibitors. The clinically relevant comparison is pre-discharge or very-early post-discharge initiation versus a planned delayed outpatient start, such as initiation at 2 to 6 weeks after discharge.",
    "observation_genome": "EMPULSE, PMID:35228754, randomized hospitalized acute-HF patients to in-hospital empagliflozin versus placebo and showed superior 90-day clinical benefit, win ratio 1.36, 95% CI 1.09–1.68. SOLOIST-WHF, PMID:33200892, showed that sotagliflozin started before or soon after discharge in patients with diabetes and worsening HF reduced total cardiovascular death, HF hospitalization, and urgent HF visits, HR 0.67, 95% CI 0.52–0.85.",
    "limitation_genome": "Neither EMPULSE nor SOLOIST-WHF directly tested early initiation against delayed outpatient initiation. EMPULSE used a hierarchical 90-day composite rather than 90-day readmission as the primary endpoint, and SOLOIST-WHF studied sotagliflozin in patients with diabetes and recent worsening HF rather than a broad ADHF population. Therefore, the specific effect on 90-day all-cause readmission versus delayed outpatient initiation is not definitively established.",
    "delta_genome": "The next decisive study should be a pragmatic randomized trial or target-trial emulation comparing SGLT2 inhibitor initiation before discharge or within 3 days after discharge versus delayed outpatient initiation at approximately 30 days, with 90-day all-cause readmission and 90-day HF readmission as prespecified endpoints, plus safety outcomes including hypotension, ketoacidosis, genital infection, renal adverse events, and volume depletion.",
    "claim_genome": "Current evidence supports early in-hospital or peri-discharge SGLT2 inhibitor initiation as safe and beneficial for short-term clinical outcomes after acute HF hospitalization, and it plausibly reduces 90-day HF readmissions by preventing early recurrent decompensation. However, a precise reduction in 90-day readmission compared with delayed outpatient initiation remains an evidence gap rather than a settled causal claim."
  }
}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Investigate whether early initiation of SGLT2 inhibitors after hospitalization for acute decompensated heart failure reduces 90-day readmission rates compared with delayed outpatient initiation.

Design a research study on how financial literacy interventions influence retirement savings behavior, portfolio diversification, and debt management over time.

content:
I’ll first anchor the risk-stratification target in large, clinically influential HBOC risk papers covering BRCA1/2, PALB2, and broader panel moderate-penetrance genes.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"hereditary breast ovarian cancer risk stratification BRCA1 BRCA2 PALB2 moderate penetrance variants cohort odds ratios absolute risk","discipline":"clinical_medicine","limit":5}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers identified: Kuchenbaecker et al., JAMA 2017, PMID:28632866 on prospective BRCA1/2 breast/ovarian risks; Antoniou et al., NEJM 2014, PMID:25099575 on PALB2 breast-cancer risk; Yang et al., JCO 2020, PMID:31841383 on PALB2 cancer spectrum; Dorling et al., NEJM 2021, PMID:33471991 on 34 susceptibility genes in >113,000 women including ATM, CHEK2, PALB2, BRCA1/2.

I’ll read the BRCA1/2 penetrance paper and the broad panel study because together they define high-penetrance vs moderate-penetrance clinical categories.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["PMID:28632866","PMID:33471991"],"focus":"risk estimates, breast/ovarian phenotype specificity, clinical utility for hereditary breast and ovarian cancer stratification"}
```  
[result]: Kuchenbaecker et al. reported cumulative breast cancer risks to age 80 of ~72% for BRCA1 and ~69% for BRCA2, and ovarian cancer risks of ~44% and ~17%, respectively, with risk modified by mutation location and family history. Dorling et al. found strongest breast-cancer associations for BRCA1, BRCA2, and PALB2, with moderate-risk associations for ATM and CHEK2; ovarian-cancer associations were strongest for BRCA1/2 and also observed for genes such as RAD51C/RAD51D/BRIP1.

I’ll read the PALB2-specific papers to ground how PALB2 bridges high- and moderate-penetrance management and whether ovarian risk should be incorporated.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["PMID:25099575","PMID:31841383"],"focus":"PALB2 absolute breast cancer risk, ovarian cancer risk, family-history modifiers, relevance to clinical genetic testing panels"}
```  
[result]: Antoniou et al. estimated PALB2 female breast-cancer risk by age 70 around 35%, higher with stronger family history, supporting PALB2 as a clinically actionable breast-cancer predisposition gene. Yang et al. expanded PALB2 risk characterization, reporting elevated risks for breast, ovarian, pancreatic, and male breast cancers, though ovarian risk was lower than BRCA1 and less certain for individual prediction.

I’ll extract the genome-style design variables: mechanism, target niche, observations, limitations, delta from prior approaches, and a concise clinical claim.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["PMID:28632866","PMID:33471991","PMID:25099575","PMID:31841383"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted pattern: pathogenic variant identity is insufficient alone; gene-specific penetrance, cancer spectrum, variant/family-history modifiers, and phenotype-specific endpoints should be integrated. Evidence supports separating BRCA1/2 high ovarian-risk carriers from PALB2/ATM/CHEK2 breast-predominant carriers, while recognizing that moderate-penetrance estimates are less precise and ancestry ascertainment remains a limitation.

Final gene_genome proposal grounded in the retrieved HBOC risk literature.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","mechanism_genome":"Use a gene- and variant-class–specific penetrance model for hereditary breast and ovarian cancer risk stratification: BRCA1 and BRCA2 pathogenic/likely pathogenic variants define high-penetrance HBOC risk with distinct breast and ovarian risk profiles; PALB2 is treated as a high-to-intermediate breast-cancer predisposition gene with lower and less certain ovarian risk; moderate-penetrance variants such as ATM and CHEK2 are incorporated primarily as breast-cancer risk modifiers rather than BRCA-equivalent ovarian-risk markers. Risk estimates should be modulated by age, family history, tumor phenotype, and where available mutation location or domain-level effects.","niche_genome":"Clinical genetics and oncology settings evaluating individuals with personal or family histories suggestive of hereditary breast/ovarian cancer, especially multigene-panel testing reports containing BRCA1, BRCA2, PALB2, ATM, CHEK2, RAD51C, RAD51D, and BRIP1 findings. The output is most relevant for counseling, surveillance intensity, prophylactic surgery discussions, and separating breast-predominant from ovarian-predisposition risk.","observation_genome":"Kuchenbaecker et al. showed very high cumulative risks for BRCA1/2 carriers, with approximate breast-cancer risks to age 80 of 72% for BRCA1 and 69% for BRCA2, and ovarian-cancer risks of 44% and 17%, respectively. Antoniou et al. and Yang et al. support PALB2 as clinically actionable for breast-cancer risk, with risk strongly influenced by family history and a broader but lower-certainty cancer spectrum including ovarian and pancreatic cancer. Dorling et al. demonstrated that BRCA1, BRCA2, and PALB2 have the strongest breast-cancer associations among panel genes, while ATM and CHEK2 occupy a moderate-penetrance category and ovarian risk signals are more gene-specific, notably involving BRCA1/2 and genes such as RAD51C/RAD51D/BRIP1.","limitation_genome":"Risk estimates are affected by ascertainment, ancestry representation, family-history enrichment, variant classification uncertainty, and heterogeneous panel-testing indications. Moderate-penetrance variants have lower positive predictive value for individual outcomes than BRCA1/2, and many missense or rare variants remain VUS. PALB2 ovarian-risk estimates are less mature than BRCA1/2 estimates, so applying BRCA-like ovarian management to PALB2 or CHEK2/ATM would overstate evidence.","delta_genome":"Compared with a binary HBOC-positive/HBOC-negative interpretation, this genome reframes risk stratification as tiered and phenotype-specific: BRCA1/2 drive both breast and ovarian prevention pathways, PALB2 drives intensified breast surveillance with cautious ovarian-risk interpretation, and ATM/CHEK2 drive moderate breast-risk management. It also separates ovarian-predisposition genes such as RAD51C/RAD51D/BRIP1 from breast-predominant moderate-risk genes, reducing overmanagement from broad panel testing.","claim_genome":"A clinically useful HBOC risk-stratification output should classify BRCA1, BRCA2, PALB2, and moderate-penetrance variants into gene-specific risk tiers rather than treating all pathogenic variants equivalently: BRCA1/2 warrant high-risk breast and ovarian counseling, PALB2 warrants strong breast-risk management with individualized discussion of non-breast cancers, and ATM/CHEK2/moderate variants should inform breast surveillance and family-history–adjusted counseling without being assumed to confer BRCA-level ovarian cancer risk."}
```  
[result]: Proposed gene_genome is evidence-grounded in PMID:28632866, PMID:33471991, PMID:25099575, and PMID:31841383. Its core claim is that HBOC risk stratification should be gene-specific, cancer-site-specific, and penetrance-tiered rather than a uniform “pathogenic variant present” classification.эк финальный? Wait developer asked End with propose in schema prompt requests. We have included result after propose. Is final answer okay? It says action block and result. Could have no final extra. But final channel should probably contain entire trajectory. We already in commentary? Need final. Hmm The "commentary" may not be final answer. We need respond final with same content. I should provide concise trajectory. The above in commentary likely visible but final needed. We'll copy? Need no extra? Use final.

prompt.full_prompt: Identify gene_genome output relevant to hereditary breast and ovarian cancer risk stratification, focusing on BRCA1, BRCA2, PALB2, and moderate-penetrance variants.

content:
I’ll first identify directly relevant hypertension trials of pharmacist/team-based management, prioritizing randomized and pragmatic/primary-care designs to anchor the proposed rural trial.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"pharmacist led hypertension management randomized trial primary care telemonitoring rural clinics pragmatic","discipline":"clinical_medicine","limit":5}
```  
[result]: Retrieved candidate studies including PMID:23942621 (Margolis et al., JAMA 2013; home BP telemonitoring plus pharmacist case management, cluster RCT), PMID:25022986 (Carter et al.; physician-pharmacist collaborative management for hypertension, cluster-randomized primary care trial), and reviews supporting team-based pharmacist care for BP control. Few trials were explicitly designed around rural primary care implementation constraints.

I’ll read the strongest pragmatic-adjacent RCT because it provides an evidence-based intervention structure—remote BP monitoring plus pharmacist titration—that could be adapted to rural clinics.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"PMID:23942621","sections":["abstract","methods","results","discussion"]}
```  
[result]: Margolis et al. randomized primary care clinics/patients to usual care versus home BP telemonitoring with pharmacist case management. The intervention improved BP control substantially at 6 and 12 months, using protocolized medication adjustment, patient follow-up, and team-based communication with primary care clinicians.

I’ll read the physician-pharmacist collaboration trial to ground clinic workflow, prescriber collaboration, and real-world team care elements relevant to rural primary care.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"PMID:25022986","sections":["abstract","methods","results","implementation_details"]}
```  
[result]: Carter et al. tested collaborative physician-pharmacist hypertension management in primary care, emphasizing medication intensification, adherence assessment, lifestyle counseling, and communication with clinicians. The study supports pharmacist-led medication management as effective but leaves uncertainty about scalability in resource-limited rural clinics.

I’ll check novelty relative to prior RCTs: the key gap is not whether pharmacists can lower BP, but whether a low-burden, pragmatic, rural-clinic implementation model improves patient-centered and implementation outcomes.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","paper_ids":["PMID:23942621","PMID:25022986"],"proposed_gap":"pragmatic randomized trial of pharmacist-led hypertension management embedded in rural primary care clinics, using routine EHR workflows, remote or hybrid visits, collaborative practice agreements, and implementation outcomes"}
```  
[result]: Novelty is moderate-to-high. Prior RCTs establish efficacy of pharmacist/team-based BP management, but rural clinics, pragmatic eligibility, hybrid pharmacist delivery, EHR-driven identification, and implementation outcomes such as reach, fidelity, clinician burden, and sustainability remain under-tested.
```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "idea_plan",
  "content": {
    "Idea": "Conduct a pragmatic cluster-randomized trial evaluating pharmacist-led hypertension management embedded in rural primary care clinics. Clinics would be randomized to usual care versus a pharmacist-led collaborative care model using EHR-based patient identification, protocolized medication titration under collaborative practice agreements, home BP monitoring, adherence support, and hybrid telephone/video/in-person follow-up. The trial builds on prior evidence from pharmacist case-management and physician-pharmacist collaborative hypertension trials, while testing effectiveness and implementation in rural, resource-limited primary care settings.",
    "ImplementationSteps": {
      "Design": "Use a pragmatic cluster-randomized design with rural primary care clinics as the unit of randomization to reduce contamination. Randomize clinics 1:1 to pharmacist-led hypertension management versus usual care.",
      "Population": "Include adults with diagnosed hypertension and uncontrolled BP based on recent clinic readings or EHR data, for example systolic BP ≥140 mm Hg or diastolic BP ≥90 mm Hg, with broad exclusions limited to safety issues such as pregnancy, dialysis, or inability to consent.",
      "Intervention": "Clinical pharmacists review medications, assess adherence and side effects, provide lifestyle counseling, support home BP monitoring, and intensify antihypertensive therapy using a protocol and collaborative practice agreement with primary care clinicians.",
      "DeliveryModel": "Use a hybrid rural model: initial pharmacist contact by phone, video, or clinic visit; follow-up every 2–4 weeks until BP is controlled; then maintenance contacts every 2–3 months. Home BP cuffs are provided when needed.",
      "Comparator": "Usual primary care hypertension management without structured pharmacist titration or proactive pharmacist follow-up.",
      "DataCollection": "Use routine EHR blood pressure, prescribing, visit, and laboratory data, supplemented by home BP readings, brief patient surveys, and implementation logs.",
      "ImplementationEvaluation": "Evaluate reach, adoption, fidelity, acceptability, pharmacist time burden, clinician burden, and sustainability using a hybrid effectiveness-implementation framework.",
      "EquityFocus": "Pre-specify analyses by distance from clinic, broadband access, insurance status, age, race/ethnicity, and baseline BP severity to determine whether the intervention reduces or worsens rural access disparities."
    },
    "ImplementationOrder": [
      "Recruit rural primary care clinics and establish collaborative practice agreements.",
      "Randomize clinics to pharmacist-led management or usual care.",
      "Use EHR algorithms to identify adults with uncontrolled hypertension.",
      "Enroll eligible patients using pragmatic consent procedures.",
      "Provide home BP monitors when needed and train patients in measurement technique.",
      "Deliver pharmacist-led medication management and adherence support for 12 months.",
      "Collect EHR-based clinical outcomes and implementation metrics.",
      "Analyze effectiveness, safety, implementation outcomes, and subgroup equity effects."
    ],
    "Dataset": "Primary data from participating rural primary care clinics, including EHR blood pressure measurements, antihypertensive prescriptions, medication changes, laboratory values, comorbidities, utilization, and demographics. Patient-reported data would include home BP readings, medication adherence, treatment burden, and satisfaction. Implementation data would include pharmacist encounter logs, time per patient, fidelity checklists, clinician surveys, and clinic-level adoption metrics.",
    "EvaluationMetrics": {
      "PrimaryClinicalOutcome": "Change in systolic BP from baseline to 12 months, or proportion of patients achieving guideline-concordant BP control at 12 months.",
      "SecondaryClinicalOutcomes": [
        "Change in diastolic BP",
        "Proportion with BP control at 6 months",
        "Antihypertensive medication intensification",
        "Medication adherence",
        "Hypertensive urgency or hypotension-related adverse events",
        "Emergency department visits or hospitalizations related to cardiovascular conditions"
      ],
      "ImplementationOutcomes": [
        "Reach: proportion of eligible patients enrolled or contacted",
        "Adoption: proportion of clinics and clinicians using the pharmacist protocol",
        "Fidelity: proportion of pharmacist encounters following titration and monitoring protocols",
        "Acceptability: patient, clinician, and pharmacist satisfaction",
        "Feasibility: pharmacist time per enrolled patient and missed-contact rate",
        "Sustainability: clinic willingness and ability to continue the model after trial support"
      ],
      "EquityOutcomes": [
        "Effect heterogeneity by travel distance",
        "Effect heterogeneity by broadband/telehealth access",
        "Effect heterogeneity by insurance status and socioeconomic vulnerability",
        "Home BP monitor uptake and use across subgroups"
      ],
      "AnalysisPlan": "Use mixed-effects regression accounting for clinic-level clustering, baseline BP, patient demographics, and repeated measurements. Conduct intention-to-treat analyses with sensitivity analyses using home BP readings and complete-case EHR follow-up."
    },
    "ExpectedOutcome": "The pharmacist-led model is expected to produce clinically meaningful reductions in systolic BP and higher BP-control rates than usual care, consistent with prior trials such as PMID:23942621 and PMID:25022986. The main contribution would be evidence on whether this approach remains effective, feasible, and equitable when implemented pragmatically in rural primary care clinics with routine EHR workflows and hybrid pharmacist follow-up."
  }
}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Develop an idea_plan for a pragmatic randomized trial evaluating pharmacist-led hypertension management in rural primary care clinics.

content:
I first searched for work that directly connects climate/natural-disaster risk to municipal bond markets and local public finance, prioritizing papers with identifiable bond-yield or fiscal-capacity outcomes.

```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "natural disasters climate risk municipal bond yields local government finance borrowing infrastructure investment",
  "discipline": "economics_finance",
  "top_k": 5
}
```

[result]: Found several relevant candidates: Painter, “An Inconvenient Cost: The Effects of Climate Change on Municipal Bonds” `NBER_w22734`; Goldsmith-Pinkham et al., “Sea Level Rise Exposure and Municipal Bond Yields” `10.1093/rfs/hhac070`; Jerch, Kahn & Lin, “Local Public Finance Dynamics and Hurricane Shocks” `NBER_w28050`. These jointly cover municipal bond pricing, climate/disaster exposure, and local fiscal dynamics.

I read Painter because it is central for the bond-yield mechanism: whether climate-related physical risk is capitalized into municipal borrowing costs.

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "NBER_w22734",
  "focus": "municipal bond yields, maturity structure, climate/disaster risk pricing"
}
```

[result]: Painter finds that municipal bonds issued by counties more exposed to climate-change risk carry higher yields, especially at longer maturities where physical-risk exposure is more relevant. The paper supports a mechanism in which investors demand compensation for expected future fiscal stress and default risk.

I then read the hurricane-shock paper to connect disasters to local government budgets, borrowing capacity, and spending needs rather than only bond-market pricing.

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "NBER_w28050",
  "focus": "effects of hurricanes on local public finance, revenues, expenditures, debt, transfers"
}
```

[result]: Jerch, Kahn & Lin show that hurricane shocks affect local fiscal dynamics through damaged tax bases, expenditure pressures, intergovernmental aid, and changes in debt/fiscal capacity. The evidence suggests disaster effects are not only asset-pricing events; they alter the balance-sheet constraints and investment choices of local governments.

I extracted the reusable “genome” of the existing literature to identify what is known and where a new contribution could fit.

```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "NBER_w22734",
    "10.1093/rfs/hhac070",
    "NBER_w28050"
  ],
  "target_schema": "gene_genome"
}
```

[result]: Existing work establishes that long-horizon physical climate risk is priced in municipal bonds and that realized hurricane shocks stress local fiscal systems. The gap is a unified causal design linking realized disaster shocks to three linked outcomes: yields, borrowing capacity, and subsequent infrastructure investment composition.

I checked novelty by comparing the proposed angle against the retrieved papers: most prior work studies either pricing of climate exposure or fiscal consequences of disasters, but less often the full transmission chain from realized disaster to debt-market terms to infrastructure choices.

```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "candidate_idea": "Estimate how realized natural disasters affect municipal bond yields, debt issuance capacity, credit constraints, and infrastructure investment decisions using FEMA/NOAA shocks matched to MSRB bond data and local fiscal accounts.",
  "against_paper_ids": [
    "NBER_w22734",
    "10.1093/rfs/hhac070",
    "NBER_w28050"
  ]
}
```

[result]: Novelty appears strongest in integrating bond-market pricing, realized fiscal capacity, and capital-investment reallocation in one empirical framework. A credible design would need to separate physical damage from aid inflows, rating changes, insurance coverage, and pre-existing climate exposure.

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "gene_genome",
  "content": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Natural disasters damage the local tax base, raise expected repair and adaptation spending, and increase perceived default or repayment risk. Municipal bond investors respond by demanding higher yields, especially for long-maturity general-obligation bonds whose repayment depends on future fiscal capacity. At the same time, local governments may face reduced borrowing capacity through rating pressure, debt-limit constraints, weakened revenues, and higher debt-service costs. Federal disaster aid, insurance coverage, state fiscal backstops, and pre-disaster fiscal reserves can attenuate these effects.",
    "niche_genome": "U.S. municipal governments exposed to hurricanes, floods, wildfires, and other FEMA/NOAA-recorded disasters, matched to MSRB/EMMA municipal bond transactions, bond issuance records, local government fiscal accounts, credit ratings, FEMA public-assistance payments, and capital/infrastructure expenditure data. The most relevant heterogeneity is by bond type, maturity, issuer fiscal health, disaster severity, federal aid intensity, insurance penetration, and pre-existing climate-risk exposure.",
    "observation_genome": "A strong empirical design would combine event-study and difference-in-differences estimates around disaster declarations or measured physical-damage shocks. Outcomes would include secondary-market yield spreads, offering yields on new issues, issuance volume, maturity, call features, credit-rating changes, debt-service ratios, unused borrowing capacity, and capital spending by category. The key observable prediction is that severe disasters raise municipal borrowing costs and reduce net borrowing capacity, while shifting infrastructure investment toward repair, resilience, drainage, flood control, emergency facilities, and away from discretionary or expansionary projects.",
    "limitation_genome": "Identification is difficult because disaster-prone municipalities differ systematically from safer municipalities, and bond yields may already price expected climate exposure before a disaster occurs. Federal and state aid can obscure local fiscal stress, while post-disaster borrowing may rise mechanically because governments need reconstruction funds. Data limitations are also important: small issuers trade infrequently, infrastructure project data are fragmented, and local insurance or self-insurance arrangements are hard to observe.",
    "delta_genome": "Relative to Painter `NBER_w22734` and Goldsmith-Pinkham et al. `10.1093/rfs/hhac070`, the proposed study would focus on realized disaster shocks rather than mainly long-run climate exposure pricing. Relative to Jerch, Kahn & Lin `NBER_w28050`, it would extend local public-finance dynamics into municipal bond-market terms and infrastructure allocation decisions. The main contribution is estimating the full transmission chain: disaster shock → fiscal stress/risk repricing → borrowing-capacity change → infrastructure-investment response.",
    "claim_genome": "Severe natural disasters likely increase municipal bond yields and weaken local borrowing capacity, particularly for fiscally constrained issuers and long-maturity general-obligation debt. However, the effect should be muted where federal disaster aid, insurance, state support, or strong pre-disaster fiscal reserves reduce expected repayment risk. Infrastructure investment is expected to become more defensive and repair-oriented after disasters, implying that climate shocks can crowd out ordinary local capital investment even when total capital spending temporarily rises."
  }
}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Explore how natural disasters affect municipal bond yields, local government borrowing capacity, and infrastructure investment decisions.

content:
I’ll first anchor the genome in landmark clinical pharmacogenomic evidence linking CYP2C19 loss-of-function to clopidogrel response and outcomes, then add trial/guideline evidence for antiplatelet selection. 
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"clopidogrel CYP2C19 loss-of-function alleles pharmacogenomics cardiovascular outcomes genotype-guided antiplatelet selection PMID","discipline":"clinical_medicine","limit":5}
```
[result]: Key candidates retrieved: Mega et al., NEJM 2009, PMID:19106084; Simon et al., NEJM 2009, PMID:19106083; POPular Genetics, NEJM 2019, PMID:31479209; TAILOR-PCI, JAMA 2020, PMID:32840598; CPIC CYP2C19-clopidogrel guideline update, Clin Pharmacol Ther 2022, PMID:35034351.

I’ll read the mechanistic/outcomes paper and the implementation/guideline source to ground both predictor biology and prescribing implications. 
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["PMID:19106084","PMID:35034351"],"focus":["CYP2C19 alleles","clopidogrel active metabolite","platelet response","clinical outcomes","therapy recommendations"]}
```
[result]: Mega et al. reported that CYP2C19 reduced-function carriers had lower active clopidogrel metabolite exposure, diminished platelet inhibition, and higher major adverse cardiovascular event/stent thrombosis risk after PCI. CPIC 2022 recommends considering alternative P2Y12 inhibitors such as prasugrel or ticagrelor for CYP2C19 intermediate and poor metabolizers when no contraindication exists, especially in ACS/PCI settings.

I’ll add randomized implementation evidence to distinguish biologic association from clinically actionable antiplatelet selection. 
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["PMID:31479209","PMID:32840598"],"focus":["genotype-guided therapy","prasugrel","ticagrelor","clopidogrel","PCI outcomes","bleeding"]}
```
[result]: POPular Genetics showed CYP2C19 genotype-guided selection after primary PCI was noninferior for net adverse clinical events and reduced bleeding compared with universal ticagrelor/prasugrel use. TAILOR-PCI tested genotype-guided escalation to ticagrelor in CYP2C19 loss-of-function carriers; the primary endpoint narrowly missed statistical significance, but event patterns supported potential benefit in LOF carriers.

I’ll distill the retrieved evidence into the requested six-field gene_genome structure, emphasizing what is predictive, where it applies, and remaining caveats. 
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["PMID:19106084","PMID:35034351","PMID:31479209","PMID:32840598"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```
[result]: Extracted genome centers on CYP2C19 reduced/no-function alleles—especially *2 and *3—as predictors of impaired clopidogrel bioactivation and weaker platelet inhibition, with strongest actionability in ACS/PCI patients where P2Y12 choice affects ischemic and bleeding tradeoffs.

```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","content":{"mechanism_genome":"Clopidogrel is a prodrug requiring hepatic bioactivation, with CYP2C19 contributing substantially to formation of its active thiol metabolite. CYP2C19 loss-of-function alleles—most commonly *2 and *3, and other no-/decreased-function variants depending on ancestry—reduce active metabolite exposure, blunt P2Y12 platelet inhibition, and increase the probability of high on-treatment platelet reactivity. Gain-of-function CYP2C19 *17 may increase activation, but its clinical role is less central than LOF status for antiplatelet selection.","niche_genome":"Most clinically actionable in patients receiving clopidogrel for acute coronary syndrome and/or percutaneous coronary intervention, particularly stent implantation, where inadequate platelet inhibition can translate into myocardial infarction or stent thrombosis. The predictor is less compelling for settings with lower thrombotic risk or weaker evidence, such as some stable vascular disease or non-PCI indications, and should be integrated with bleeding risk and contraindications to alternative P2Y12 inhibitors.","observation_genome":"Landmark pharmacogenomic outcome data, including Mega et al. NEJM 2009 (PMID:19106084), showed CYP2C19 reduced-function carriers had lower clopidogrel active metabolite levels, less platelet inhibition, and higher cardiovascular event and stent thrombosis risk. Implementation evidence from POPular Genetics (PMID:31479209) supports CYP2C19-guided de-escalation/escalation as a way to preserve ischemic protection while reducing bleeding compared with universal potent P2Y12 therapy. TAILOR-PCI (PMID:32840598) did not meet its primary endpoint at conventional significance but was directionally consistent with benefit from genotype-guided avoidance of clopidogrel in LOF carriers.","limitation_genome":"CYP2C19 genotype is not the sole determinant of clopidogrel response; adherence, drug interactions, diabetes, renal function, ACS severity, platelet turnover, procedural factors, and ancestry-specific allele coverage also matter. Randomized trial results are not uniformly definitive, and absolute benefit depends on baseline ischemic versus bleeding risk. Genotyping panels that only test *2 may miss other actionable LOF alleles, especially in non-European populations.","delta_genome":"Compared with empiric clopidogrel for all patients, pharmacogenomic selection identifies CYP2C19 intermediate and poor metabolizers who are less likely to respond adequately and may benefit from prasugrel or ticagrelor if not contraindicated. Compared with universal prasugrel/ticagrelor, genotype-guided selection can reserve potent therapy for LOF carriers while allowing noncarriers to receive clopidogrel, potentially reducing bleeding, dyspnea, cost, and treatment discontinuation.","claim_genome":"CYP2C19 LOF genotype is a clinically validated pharmacogenomic predictor of reduced clopidogrel response. For ACS/PCI patients, CYP2C19 intermediate or poor metabolizer status should prompt consideration of an alternative P2Y12 inhibitor—prasugrel or ticagrelor—when bleeding risk, stroke history, age/weight constraints, drug tolerance, and access permit; CYP2C19 normal/rapid metabolizers can generally receive clopidogrel when otherwise appropriate."}}
```
[result]: Proposed gene_genome output generated with fields grounded in Mega 2009, CPIC 2022, POPular Genetics 2019, and TAILOR-PCI 2020. 

{
  "mechanism_genome": "Clopidogrel is a prodrug requiring hepatic bioactivation, with CYP2C19 contributing substantially to formation of its active thiol metabolite. CYP2C19 loss-of-function alleles—most commonly *2 and *3, and other no-/decreased-function variants depending on ancestry—reduce active metabolite exposure, blunt P2Y12 platelet inhibition, and increase the probability of high on-treatment platelet reactivity. Gain-of-function CYP2C19 *17 may increase activation, but its clinical role is less central than LOF status for antiplatelet selection.",
  "niche_genome": "Most clinically actionable in patients receiving clopidogrel for acute coronary syndrome and/or percutaneous coronary intervention, particularly stent implantation, where inadequate platelet inhibition can translate into myocardial infarction or stent thrombosis. The predictor is less compelling for settings with lower thrombotic risk or weaker evidence, such as some stable vascular disease or non-PCI indications, and should be integrated with bleeding risk and contraindications to alternative P2Y12 inhibitors.",
  "observation_genome": "Landmark pharmacogenomic outcome data, including Mega et al. NEJM 2009 (PMID:19106084), showed CYP2C19 reduced-function carriers had lower clopidogrel active metabolite levels, less platelet inhibition, and higher cardiovascular event and stent thrombosis risk. Implementation evidence from POPular Genetics (PMID:31479209) supports CYP2C19-guided de-escalation/escalation as a way to preserve ischemic protection while reducing bleeding compared with universal potent P2Y12 therapy. TAILOR-PCI (PMID:32840598) did not meet its primary endpoint at conventional significance but was directionally consistent with benefit from genotype-guided avoidance of clopidogrel in LOF carriers.",
  "limitation_genome": "CYP2C19 genotype is not the sole determinant of clopidogrel response; adherence, drug interactions, diabetes, renal function, ACS severity, platelet turnover, procedural factors, and ancestry-specific allele coverage also matter. Randomized trial results are not uniformly definitive, and absolute benefit depends on baseline ischemic versus bleeding risk. Genotyping panels that only test *2 may miss other actionable LOF alleles, especially in non-European populations.",
  "delta_genome": "Compared with empiric clopidogrel for all patients, pharmacogenomic selection identifies CYP2C19 intermediate and poor metabolizers who are less likely to respond adequately and may benefit from prasugrel or ticagrelor if not contraindicated. Compared with universal prasugrel/ticagrelor, genotype-guided selection can reserve potent therapy for LOF carriers while allowing noncarriers to receive clopidogrel, potentially reducing bleeding, dyspnea, cost, and treatment discontinuation.",
  "claim_genome": "CYP2C19 LOF genotype is a clinically validated pharmacogenomic predictor of reduced clopidogrel response. For ACS/PCI patients, CYP2C19 intermediate or poor metabolizer status should prompt consideration of an alternative P2Y12 inhibitor—prasugrel or ticagrelor—when bleeding risk, stroke history, age/weight constraints, drug tolerance, and access permit; CYP2C19 normal/rapid metabolizers can generally receive clopidogrel when otherwise appropriate."
}

prompt.full_prompt: Generate gene_genome output for pharmacogenomic predictors of clopidogrel response, including CYP2C19 loss-of-function alleles and implications for antiplatelet selection.

content:
I’ll first identify pivotal comparative trials and age-relevant guideline context, because the 65–75/intermediate-risk subgroup may be underrepresented in the classic intermediate-risk RCTs.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"intermediate surgical risk transcatheter versus surgical aortic valve replacement age 65 75 PARTNER 2A SURTAVI outcomes PMID"}
```  
[result]: Found pivotal RCTs: PARTNER 2A (Leon et al., NEJM 2016, PMID:27040324), SURTAVI (Reardon et al., NEJM 2017, PMID:28304219), and PARTNER 2A 5-year follow-up (Makkar et al., NEJM 2020, PMID:32223174). Guideline context consistently treats 65–80 years as a shared-decision zone, but trial mean ages are mostly late 70s to early 80s.

I’ll read PARTNER 2A and its longer-term follow-up because durability and late stroke/death matter especially for 65–75-year-olds.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["PMID:27040324","PMID:32223174"]}
```  
[result]: PARTNER 2A randomized intermediate-risk severe aortic stenosis patients to TAVR vs SAVR; death or disabling stroke at 2 years was similar overall, with transfemoral TAVR showing favorable outcomes versus surgery while transthoracic access was less favorable. At 5 years, TAVR and SAVR had similar death/disabling stroke overall, but TAVR had more aortic-valve reinterventions and paravalvular regurgitation remained prognostically important.

I’ll read SURTAVI as an independent RCT using a self-expanding valve platform, since device type and pacemaker/paravalvular leak profiles affect the comparison.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["PMID:28304219"]}
```  
[result]: SURTAVI showed TAVR with a self-expanding prosthesis was noninferior to SAVR for all-cause death or disabling stroke at 24 months in intermediate-risk patients. TAVR had less acute kidney injury, atrial fibrillation, transfusion, and shorter hospitalization, but more residual aortic regurgitation and permanent pacemaker implantation than SAVR.

I’ll extract the evidence genome and explicitly flag the age-specific limitation before making a clinical comparison claim.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["PMID:27040324","PMID:32223174","PMID:28304219"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = valve replacement via catheter-based versus open surgical implantation; niche = symptomatic severe aortic stenosis, intermediate surgical risk, mostly older adults; observations = noninferior death/disabling stroke, faster early recovery with TAVR, more pacemaker/PVL/reintervention signals; limitations = limited direct 65–75 subgroup power and long-term durability uncertainty; delta = need age-stratified intermediate-risk estimates with ≥10-year valve durability.

```propose
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR) treats severe aortic stenosis by catheter-based bioprosthetic valve implantation, avoiding sternotomy/cardiopulmonary bypass; surgical aortic valve replacement (SAVR) uses open implantation with more invasive perioperative physiology but historically stronger durability data.",
  "niche_genome": "Patients aged 65 to 75 years with symptomatic severe aortic stenosis and intermediate surgical risk. This age band lies in a clinical shared-decision zone: younger than the average patient in PARTNER 2A and SURTAVI, but old enough that less-invasive therapy and recovery advantages may be clinically meaningful.",
  "observation_genome": "Across intermediate-risk RCT evidence, TAVR and SAVR produced broadly similar rates of death or disabling stroke at about 2 years: PARTNER 2A (PMID:27040324) and SURTAVI (PMID:28304219) both demonstrated noninferiority of TAVR. TAVR generally offered less atrial fibrillation, bleeding/transfusion, acute kidney injury, and shorter hospitalization. Tradeoffs favored SAVR for lower paravalvular regurgitation, fewer permanent pacemakers in self-expanding-valve experience, and fewer late valve reinterventions; PARTNER 2A 5-year follow-up (PMID:32223174) found similar death/disabling stroke overall but persistent concern about paravalvular leak and more valve reintervention after TAVR.",
  "limitation_genome": "The direct evidence base is not tightly matched to 65–75-year-old intermediate-risk patients: major intermediate-risk trials enrolled predominantly older populations, so age-specific estimates for 65–75 are underpowered or indirect. Follow-up beyond 5 years remains especially important in this group because expected survival may exceed current randomized durability evidence for earlier-generation TAVR valves.",
  "delta_genome": "The key unresolved delta is whether the early morbidity and recovery advantages of contemporary transfemoral TAVR outweigh potentially higher lifetime risks of pacemaker dependence, paravalvular leak, structural valve deterioration, and repeat intervention in 65–75-year-old intermediate-risk patients. A definitive comparison would require age-stratified randomized or registry-linked analyses with contemporary valves and at least 10-year outcomes.",
  "claim_genome": "For a 65–75-year-old patient at intermediate surgical risk, contemporary evidence supports TAVR as an effective alternative to SAVR for short- to mid-term survival and disabling-stroke outcomes, particularly when transfemoral access is feasible and rapid recovery or avoidance of surgical morbidity is prioritized. SAVR remains comparatively attractive when long life expectancy, low operative risk within the intermediate-risk range, anatomy unfavorable for TAVR, concern for pacemaker/paravalvular leak, or need for maximal proven valve durability dominates the decision."
}
``` פֿ
-vesm

Examine the association between cumulative corticosteroid exposure and incident diabetes among patients with inflammatory bowel disease using a large electronic health record cohort.
Assess the diagnostic accuracy of point-of-care lung ultrasound versus chest radiography for detecting pneumonia in older adults presenting to emergency departments.
