content:
Rationale: I’ll first identify randomized evidence specifically in adults with type 2 diabetes using basal insulin without prandial insulin, because the intervention’s effect can differ materially from intensive-insulin populations. The key outcomes are CGM-derived glycemic variability/time-in-range and patient-reported quality of life or distress.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"continuous glucose monitoring type 2 diabetes basal insulin only randomized trial quality of life glycemic variability MOBILE study","discipline":"clinical_medicine","limit":5}
```  
[result]: The search returns the MOBILE randomized clinical trial as the central match: Martens et al., JAMA 2021, PMID: 34077499, DOI: 10.1001/jama.2021.7444. It also returns MOBILE-related patient-reported outcome analyses and follow-up/extension papers in adults with T2D treated with basal insulin without prandial insulin.  

Rationale: I’ll read the pivotal MOBILE RCT to extract the study population, intervention, and CGM-metric effects. This is the most directly applicable evidence for basal-insulin-only T2D.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"PMID:34077499"}
```  
[result]: Martens et al. randomized 175 adults with poorly controlled T2D using basal insulin without prandial insulin to real-time CGM vs blood glucose meter monitoring for 8 months. CGM improved HbA1c more than BGM and improved CGM metrics: time-in-range 70–180 mg/dL was 59% vs 43% and mean glucose 179 vs 206 mg/dL at 8 months; severe hypoglycemia was rare.  

Rationale: Because the prompt includes quality of life, I’ll read a MOBILE patient-reported outcome paper rather than infer QoL from glycemic improvements alone. This helps separate physiologic benefit from lived-experience benefit.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"MOBILE study continuous glucose monitoring basal insulin type 2 diabetes patient reported outcomes quality of life diabetes distress"}
```  
[result]: Candidate patient-reported outcome papers include MOBILE secondary analyses reporting treatment satisfaction, diabetes distress, and perceived hypoglycemia/ease-of-use outcomes in the same basal-insulin-treated T2D population. The results suggest CGM was acceptable and improved diabetes-related satisfaction/monitoring confidence, but broad generic QoL effects were less consistently demonstrated than glycemic effects.  

Rationale: I’ll extract the “genome” of the evidence to make the causal mechanism, niche, observed effects, and limitations explicit. This is useful because the evidence supports a pragmatic clinical claim but also has gaps around long-term QoL and hard outcomes.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["PMID:34077499"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted mechanism: real-time glucose feedback enables behavioral and basal-insulin/medication adjustments, reducing hyperglycemia exposure and increasing time-in-range. Niche: adults with T2D on basal insulin without prandial insulin, often with elevated HbA1c in primary-care/endocrinology settings. Limitations: moderate sample size, 8-month randomized duration, open-label design, stronger evidence for glycemic metrics than for durable QoL or clinical event reduction.  

```propose
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Real-time continuous glucose monitoring likely improves glycemic variability in basal-insulin-treated type 2 diabetes by making postprandial excursions, nocturnal patterns, and persistent hyperglycemia visible to patients and clinicians. This feedback can prompt diet/activity changes, safer basal-insulin titration, and non-insulin medication optimization without requiring prandial insulin.",
  "niche_genome": "Adults with type 2 diabetes treated with basal insulin only, especially those with suboptimal glycemic control and using self-monitoring blood glucose as usual care. The best-matched evidence is the MOBILE randomized clinical trial population: adults with T2D using basal insulin without prandial insulin.",
  "observation_genome": "In the MOBILE RCT (Martens et al., JAMA 2021; PMID:34077499), real-time CGM improved CGM-derived glycemic outcomes versus blood glucose meter monitoring over 8 months. Time in range 70–180 mg/dL was higher with CGM, 59% vs 43%, and mean glucose was lower, 179 vs 206 mg/dL; HbA1c also improved more with CGM. Patient-reported outcome evidence from MOBILE-related analyses indicates CGM is generally acceptable and may improve diabetes treatment satisfaction, monitoring confidence, and perceived control, although broad quality-of-life gains are less consistently established than glycemic benefits.",
  "limitation_genome": "The strongest evidence is short-to-medium term and open-label, with a modest sample size and limited power for severe hypoglycemia, complications, healthcare utilization, or durable quality-of-life outcomes. Quality-of-life results are more heterogeneous and instrument-dependent than time-in-range or mean glucose outcomes. Generalizability may be lower for patients with very low digital literacy, limited CGM access, lower adherence, or well-controlled HbA1c at baseline.",
  "delta_genome": "A useful next study would be a pragmatic 12–24 month randomized trial in basal-insulin-only T2D comparing real-time CGM, intermittently scanned CGM, and structured SMBG, with co-primary endpoints of glycemic variability/time-in-range and diabetes-specific quality of life or distress. It should include diverse primary-care populations, payer/access measures, device-adherence phenotypes, medication-adjustment logs, and cost-effectiveness.",
  "claim_genome": "Continuous glucose monitoring should be expected to improve glycemic variability and time-in-range in adults with type 2 diabetes treated with basal insulin only, with moderate evidence for improved diabetes-specific satisfaction or perceived control. The claim is strongest for glycemic metrics and weaker, but plausible, for broad quality-of-life improvement."
}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Study whether continuous glucose monitoring improves glycemic variability and quality of life in adults with type 2 diabetes treated with basal insulin only.
Evaluate the effectiveness of home-based pulmonary rehabilitation delivered by telehealth for patients with moderate to severe COPD.

content:
I’ll first anchor the design in colorectal-screening outreach trials in safety-net or underserved settings, then check whether culturally tailored SMS reminders remain a distinct evidence gap.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"colorectal cancer screening text message reminders culturally tailored underserved populations randomized trial FIT community health center","discipline":"clinical_medicine","k":5}
```  
[result]: Found adjacent evidence rather than a definitive culturally tailored SMS trial: Green et al. 2013 stepped automated CRC outreach (PMID:23460053), Coronado et al. 2018 STOP CRC mailed FIT outreach in community health centers (PMID:30014119), Lasser et al. 2011 patient navigation in community health centers (PMID:21606091), plus smaller mobile/reminder studies with less emphasis on cultural tailoring.  

To ground the intervention mechanics, I’ll read the best trial evidence for automated outreach and mailed FIT completion.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["PMID:23460053","PMID:30014119"]}
```  
[result]: Green et al. showed automated CRC screening outreach with stepped support increased screening completion, supporting low-touch reminder workflows. STOP CRC tested EHR-linked mailed FIT outreach in federally qualified/community health centers and improved FIT participation, but relied primarily on mailed kits and clinic workflows rather than culturally tailored SMS content.  

I’ll also read evidence on higher-touch support in underserved primary care, since SMS tailoring may need escalation for nonresponders.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["PMID:21606091"]}
```  
[result]: Lasser et al. found patient navigation improved CRC screening among community health center patients, highlighting barriers such as language, logistics, and trust. This supports designing SMS reminders with culturally and linguistically concordant content plus an escalation pathway to navigator calls.  

Now I’ll check novelty against the retrieved literature: the proposed test should not merely repeat mailed FIT outreach or generic automated reminders.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","candidate_idea":"A pragmatic randomized trial in underserved safety-net clinics testing culturally and linguistically tailored SMS reminders, linked to mailed FIT kits and navigator escalation, versus standard generic reminders for colorectal cancer screening completion.","against_paper_ids":["PMID:23460053","PMID:30014119","PMID:21606091"]}
```  
[result]: Novelty is moderate-to-high. Prior trials support automated outreach, mailed FIT programs, and navigation, but do not directly isolate the incremental effect of culturally tailored SMS reminders on CRC screening completion in underserved populations.  

```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"idea_plan","idea_plan":{"Idea":"Test whether culturally and linguistically tailored text-message reminders increase colorectal cancer screening completion among underserved adults compared with standard generic reminders, when both groups receive usual clinic outreach and mailed FIT access.","ImplementationSteps":{"Design":"Conduct a pragmatic, patient-level randomized controlled trial across safety-net/FQHC primary care clinics serving high proportions of Medicaid/uninsured, racial/ethnic minority, and limited-English-proficiency patients.","Population":"Enroll adults aged 45-75 who are due for colorectal cancer screening, have a mobile phone number in the EHR, and are eligible for FIT-based screening; exclude those with prior colorectal cancer, colectomy, high-risk surveillance indications, or recent colonoscopy/FIT.","Randomization":"Randomize eligible patients 1:1 to culturally tailored SMS reminders versus generic SMS reminders, stratified by clinic, preferred language, and prior screening history.","Intervention":"Send a mailed FIT kit plus 3-4 SMS reminders over 4-6 weeks. Tailored messages use preferred language, culturally resonant framing, low-literacy wording, local clinic identity, family/community benefit framing, barrier-specific links, and opt-in navigator callback options. Tailoring variables include language, race/ethnicity where available and self-reported, prior FIT completion, and stated barriers from a brief baseline SMS question.","Comparator":"Generic SMS reminders of similar frequency and timing that state the patient is due for screening and should return the FIT kit, without cultural framing or barrier-tailored content.","Escalation":"For intervention nonresponders after the final SMS, offer culturally concordant navigator outreach; track this separately to estimate both total intervention effect and SMS-only engagement.","DataCollection":"Use EHR, mailed FIT vendor logs, laboratory records, SMS platform delivery/click/reply data, and short post-intervention surveys on acceptability, trust, comprehension, and perceived cultural relevance.","Analysis":"Primary intention-to-treat analysis using mixed-effects logistic regression with random clinic effects and covariate adjustment for age, sex, language, insurance, race/ethnicity, baseline screening status, and clinic. Pre-specify subgroup analyses by preferred language, race/ethnicity, rural/urban clinic, and prior screening adherence."},"ImplementationOrder":["Partner with safety-net clinics and patient/community advisory board to co-design message libraries.","Develop English/Spanish and other locally relevant low-literacy SMS variants using cultural tailoring and health-literacy review.","Build EHR registry of screening-due adults and validate FIT eligibility.","Randomize patients and mail FIT kits or activate usual clinic FIT distribution workflow.","Deliver SMS sequences, monitor delivery failures, replies, opt-outs, and navigator callback requests.","Ascertain FIT completion within 90 days and colonoscopy completion after abnormal FIT within 180 days.","Analyze primary, secondary, equity, and implementation outcomes; refine implementation toolkit for clinics."],"Dataset":"Multi-clinic EHR registry of adults aged 45-75 due for CRC screening, linked to FIT mailing/lab completion records, SMS platform metadata, navigator logs, and brief patient-reported acceptability/cultural-relevance surveys. Target sample: approximately 2,000-4,000 patients across 8-15 safety-net clinics to detect a 4-6 percentage-point absolute improvement in FIT completion, depending on baseline completion.","EvaluationMetrics":{"PrimaryClinicalOutcome":"FIT completion within 90 days of outreach among randomized patients.","SecondaryClinicalOutcomes":["Any CRC screening completion within 6 months","Time to FIT return","Colonoscopy completion within 180 days after abnormal FIT","Repeat screening intention or next-year FIT completion if follow-up permits"],"ImplementationOutcomes":["SMS delivery rate","Reply rate","Opt-out rate","Navigator callback uptake","Cost per additional completed screen","Staff time per completed screen"],"PatientCenteredOutcomes":["Message acceptability","Comprehension","Perceived cultural relevance","Trust in clinic outreach","Perceived privacy concerns"],"EquityOutcomes":["Absolute and relative effect differences by language, race/ethnicity, insurance status, and prior screening history"],"StatisticalCriteria":"Estimate adjusted risk differences and odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals; assess heterogeneity using interaction terms; conduct sensitivity analyses excluding undelivered SMS and using per-protocol exposure definitions."},"ExpectedOutcome":"Culturally tailored SMS reminders are expected to produce a modest but meaningful increase in FIT completion over generic reminders, especially among patients with limited English proficiency or prior nonadherence. If effective and low-cost, the intervention could extend evidence from automated outreach, mailed FIT programs, and navigation trials into a scalable culturally responsive screening strategy for underserved populations."}}
```  
[result]: Proposed a pragmatic randomized idea_plan grounded in prior automated outreach, mailed FIT, and patient-navigation evidence while isolating the incremental value of culturally tailored SMS reminders. 
agext

prompt.full_prompt: Design an idea_plan to test whether culturally tailored text-message reminders improve colorectal cancer screening completion in underserved populations.

content:
Rationale: I first need the evidence base specific to PPI deprescribing and, within it, any studies in nursing homes/long-term care rather than general ambulatory populations. This search prioritizes systematic reviews/guidelines plus LTC implementation studies.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"proton pump inhibitor deprescribing long-term care nursing home residents systematic review guideline trial","discipline":"clinical_medicine","limit":5}
```  
[result]: Found candidates including Farrell et al. 2017 evidence-based deprescribing guideline (PMID: 28679630), Boghossian et al. 2017 systematic review supporting the guideline (PMID: 28679631), and nursing-home/long-term-care medication review studies where PPIs were common deprescribing targets, including the DIM-NHR cluster RCT (PMID: 32357094).  

Rationale: The guideline and companion systematic review are the strongest direct sources on safety/effectiveness of stopping or dose-reducing PPIs, including symptom relapse and deprescribing approaches. I will read these first to anchor the clinical criteria and outcomes.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["PMID:28679630","PMID:28679631"]}
```  
[result]: Farrell et al. recommend deprescribing PPIs—dose reduction, stopping, or on-demand use—in adults whose upper-GI symptoms have resolved after at least 4 weeks, excluding Barrett esophagus, severe esophagitis grade C/D, or prior bleeding ulcer. The review found low-quality evidence that deprescribing can reduce pill burden and PPI exposure, with some increase in symptom relapse versus continuous therapy, while serious harms were not prominent in trials.  

Rationale: Because the prompt is specifically about long-term care residents, I next check evidence from nursing-home medication review interventions and extract what is directly transferable versus indirect.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["PMID:32357094"]}
```  
[result]: The DIM-NHR cluster randomized trial tested multidisciplinary medication review in nursing-home residents and showed deprescribing of potentially inappropriate medicines was feasible; PPIs were among common medication classes reviewed. However, it was not a PPI-specific safety trial and was not powered for rare GI harms after PPI withdrawal.  

Rationale: I will synthesize the retrieved evidence into a structured clinical “genome,” separating what is known mechanistically and observationally from evidence limitations in LTC.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["PMID:28679630","PMID:28679631","PMID:32357094"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted themes: deprescribing mechanisms include step-down dosing, discontinuation, or on-demand rescue therapy; appropriate niche is residents lacking ongoing high-risk indications; observations include reduced chronic exposure with generally manageable recurrent dyspepsia/reflux; limitations include low certainty, indirectness for frail LTC residents, and limited measurement of rare bleeding/aspiration outcomes.
```propose
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Deprescribing long-term proton pump inhibitors can be implemented by dose reduction, conversion to on-demand/intermittent use, or discontinuation with symptom-triggered rescue therapy such as antacids, H2-receptor antagonists, or PPI restart. The clinical mechanism is removal of acid suppression when the original indication has resolved, while monitoring for rebound acid hypersecretion or recurrent GERD/dyspepsia.",
  "niche_genome": "Best suited to long-term care residents receiving chronic PPIs without a clear ongoing indication, especially those treated for uncomplicated GERD/dyspepsia or ulcer prophylaxis that is no longer needed. Residents should generally be excluded or handled cautiously if they have Barrett esophagus, severe erosive esophagitis, active or recent bleeding ulcer, chronic NSAID/antiplatelet/anticoagulant exposure with high GI-bleed risk, or other specialist-defined indications.",
  "observation_genome": "Evidence-based deprescribing guidance (Farrell et al., PMID:28679630) and its systematic review base (Boghossian et al., PMID:28679631) support deprescribing PPIs after symptom resolution, with the main tradeoff being increased risk of recurrent upper-GI symptoms compared with continuous therapy. Serious adverse outcomes were not prominent in available trials, and nursing-home medication review evidence such as DIM-NHR (PMID:32357094) suggests deprescribing potentially inappropriate medicines in long-term care is feasible, though not PPI-specific.",
  "limitation_genome": "The evidence is only partly direct for frail long-term care residents: many PPI deprescribing trials come from general adult or outpatient populations, have low-certainty evidence, short follow-up, and limited power to detect rare but important harms such as GI bleeding, hospitalization, aspiration events, or clinically significant weight loss from recurrent symptoms. LTC residents also have communication barriers, multimorbidity, polypharmacy, and high baseline GI-risk heterogeneity.",
  "delta_genome": "A practical evaluation should compare a structured LTC deprescribing protocol versus usual care among residents without clear indications, stratifying by GI-bleed risk. Key safety monitoring should include recurrent reflux/dyspepsia, need for PPI restart, GI bleeding, hospitalization, appetite/weight change, anemia, and resident comfort; effectiveness should include successful discontinuation or dose reduction, reduced inappropriate PPI-days, pill burden, and cost.",
  "claim_genome": "For long-term care residents without clear ongoing indications, deprescribing PPIs is likely effective at reducing inappropriate chronic PPI exposure and is generally safe when done with eligibility screening and symptom-guided monitoring, but the certainty of evidence for frail LTC populations is moderate-to-low because direct PPI-specific LTC safety trials are limited. The strongest defensible clinical stance is to deprescribe selectively rather than automatically, avoiding withdrawal in residents with high-risk GI indications and restarting or stepping back up if clinically meaningful symptoms recur."
}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Evaluate the safety and effectiveness of deprescribing proton pump inhibitors in long-term care residents without clear ongoing indications.

content:
Rationale: I’ll first identify high-relevance pediatric studies that directly address bacterial–viral discrimination in febrile/SIRS-like presentations, prioritizing host-response biomarkers over pathogen detection because cultures are slow/insensitive in sepsis.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"pediatric febrile children bacterial viral infection sepsis biomarkers procalcitonin CRP RNA signature TRAIL IP-10 PMID","discipline":"clinical_medicine","limit":5}
```  
[result]: Found candidate studies including Herberg et al. JAMA 2016 (PMID: 27978545) on a 2-transcript RNA signature in febrile children; Mahajan et al. JAMA 2016 (PMID: 27552618) on RNA biosignatures in febrile infants; and Oved et al. PLoS One 2015 (PMID: 25785720) on a TRAIL/IP-10/CRP host-protein signature.
Rationale: The Herberg paper is the most on-target because it explicitly enrolled febrile children and compared bacterial versus viral infection using a parsimonious host transcript signature.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"PMID:27978545","sections":["abstract","methods","results","limitations"]}
```  
[result]: Herberg et al. developed and validated a 2-transcript blood RNA signature (IFI44L and FAM89A) to discriminate bacterial from viral infection in febrile children, reporting high diagnostic accuracy (validation AUROC about 0.93–0.95 depending on cohort/threshold). Limitations included case-control enrichment, exclusion/under-representation of mixed infections and noninfectious inflammation, and need for rapid clinical-platform translation.
Rationale: A deployable pediatric sepsis biomarker should ideally be measurable rapidly as proteins; TRAIL/IP-10/CRP is a biologically complementary signature capturing antiviral signaling, bacterial inflammation, and acute-phase response.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"PMID:25785720","sections":["abstract","methods","results","limitations"]}
```  
[result]: Oved et al. described a host-protein classifier combining TRAIL, IP-10, and CRP to distinguish bacterial from viral acute infections across children and adults, outperforming single markers such as CRP or procalcitonin in heterogeneous cohorts. Key limitations were spectrum bias, variable reference standards, and uncertainty in severe sepsis/SIRS subgroups where inflammatory phenotypes overlap.
Rationale: I’ll distill the evidence into a mechanism/niche/observation/limitation structure so the final biomarker claim is grounded and clinically scoped.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["PMID:27978545","PMID:25785720"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted pattern: bacterial–viral discrimination improves when biomarkers measure host-response polarity rather than inflammation alone. IFI44L/TRAIL/IP-10 index interferon/antiviral programs, while FAM89A/CRP reflect bacterial or neutrophil/acute-phase inflammation; the main delta is prospective validation in children with fever plus SIRS/sepsis criteria, including mixed infections and inflammatory mimics.
Rationale: Before proposing a biomarker strategy, I’ll check whether combining transcript and protein host-response axes is incremental rather than duplicating an established single-marker approach.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claim":"In febrile children meeting SIRS/sepsis screening criteria, a combined host-response panel using IFI44L/FAM89A transcripts plus TRAIL/IP-10/CRP proteins should distinguish bacterial from viral sepsis better than CRP or procalcitonin alone.","against_paper_ids":["PMID:27978545","PMID:25785720","PMID:27552618"]}
```  
[result]: Novelty appears moderate: the individual RNA and protein signatures are published, but a pediatric fever+SIRS/sepsis-focused validation of a combined transcript/protein decision rule, explicitly benchmarked against CRP/procalcitonin and adjudicated viral, bacterial, mixed, and inflammatory mimic groups, remains a meaningful translational gap.
Rationale: The evidence supports a host-response biomarker proposal rather than relying on organism detection or nonspecific inflammation alone.  
```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "gene_genome",
  "content": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Bacterial and viral sepsis in febrile children can be separated by host-response polarity: viral illness induces interferon/antiviral programs captured by IFI44L, TRAIL, and IP-10, whereas bacterial illness is associated with neutrophil/acute-phase inflammatory programs captured by FAM89A and CRP. This multidimensional host-response readout is biologically stronger than CRP or procalcitonin alone, which mainly quantify nonspecific inflammation.",
    "niche_genome": "Children presenting with fever and systemic inflammatory response or suspected sepsis in emergency department or acute-care settings, especially cases where early antibiotics, culture negativity, or overlapping clinical signs make bacterial-versus-viral classification uncertain.",
    "observation_genome": "Herberg et al. (PMID:27978545) showed that a 2-transcript RNA signature using IFI44L and FAM89A discriminated bacterial from viral infection in febrile children with high AUROC in validation cohorts. Oved et al. (PMID:25785720) showed that a host-protein signature combining TRAIL, IP-10, and CRP distinguished bacterial from viral infection better than individual inflammatory markers in heterogeneous pediatric/adult cohorts.",
    "limitation_genome": "Existing studies are not fully optimized for pediatric sepsis/SIRS presentations: many cohorts are enriched case-control designs, mixed bacterial-viral infections are under-represented, inflammatory mimics can be excluded, reference standards vary, and turnaround time/platform feasibility remains a barrier for emergency sepsis decisions.",
    "delta_genome": "Prospectively validate a combined rapid host-response panel—IFI44L/FAM89A transcripts plus TRAIL/IP-10/CRP proteins—in children with fever plus SIRS/sepsis screening criteria, explicitly comparing performance against CRP, procalcitonin, blood culture, viral PCR, and clinician adjudication across bacterial, viral, mixed, and noninfectious inflammatory groups.",
    "claim_genome": "The most evidence-grounded biomarker direction is not a single sepsis marker but a compact host-response classifier combining antiviral/interferon and bacterial inflammatory axes. In febrile children with systemic inflammatory response, IFI44L/FAM89A and TRAIL/IP-10/CRP are leading candidates to distinguish bacterial from viral sepsis and should be tested as an integrated rapid diagnostic panel."
  }
}
```  
[result]: Proposed a pediatric sepsis biomarker genome grounded in PMID:27978545 and PMID:25785720, with a clear validation gap focused on fever plus SIRS/sepsis rather than uncomplicated infection.

prompt.full_prompt: Investigate biomarkers that distinguish bacterial from viral sepsis in children presenting with fever and systemic inflammatory response.

content:
I’ll first anchor the gene set in guideline/curation sources rather than broad gene lists, because cardiomyopathy panels contain many disputed genes and the prompt asks for clinically meaningful evaluation.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"cardiomyopathy genetic evaluation hypertrophic dilated sarcomere desmosomal metabolic genes guideline ClinGen PMID"}
```  
[result]: Returned high-yield sources including Ommen et al. 2020 AHA/ACC HCM guideline (PMID: 33215931), Hershberger et al. HFSA cardiomyopathy genetic evaluation practice guideline (PMID: 29567486), Towbin et al. 2019 HRS arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy consensus (PMID: 31078652), and ClinGen-style gene validity papers for HCM/DCM.

Next I’ll read the guideline-level sources to capture the clinical contexts and avoid overclaiming genes with weak evidence.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["33215931","29567486","31078652"]}
```  
[result]: The HCM guideline emphasizes sarcomeric HCM genes and phenocopy/metabolic testing when clinical clues exist. The HFSA guideline supports genetic testing in HCM/DCM with phenotype-guided panels. The HRS consensus highlights desmosomal genes for arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy, with overlap into dilated phenotypes, especially DSP and related genes.

I’ll add gene-validity evidence to separate core sarcomeric/desmosomal/metabolic genes from large-panel noise.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"ClinGen evidence based assessment hypertrophic cardiomyopathy genes dilated cardiomyopathy genes sarcomere desmosome metabolic PMID"}
```  
[result]: Retrieved relevant curation papers including Walsh et al. reassessment of Mendelian cardiomyopathy genes (PMID: 27532257), Ingles et al. HCM gene curation work (PMID: 30681346), and DCM gene evidence assessment literature such as Jordan et al. (PMID: 33817909).

I’ll extract a compact genome-level synthesis focused on mechanisms, disease niches, observations, limitations, and clinical deltas.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["33215931","29567486","31078652","27532257","30681346","33817909"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted recurrent categories: sarcomeric contractile genes dominate monogenic HCM; TTN/LMNA/BAG3/RBM20/FLNC/PLN and some sarcomeric genes contribute to DCM; desmosomal genes support arrhythmogenic/dilated overlap; metabolic/phenocopy genes are essential for HCM-like or mixed hypertrophic–dilated presentations but require phenotype-guided interpretation.
```propose
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Cardiomyopathy evaluation should distinguish three mechanistic gene classes: (1) sarcomeric contractile genes, especially MYH7, MYBPC3, TNNT2, TNNI3, TPM1, ACTC1, MYL2, and MYL3, which alter force generation/calcium-sensitive contraction and are most strongly linked to hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, with some overlap into dilated cardiomyopathy; (2) desmosomal/cell-adhesion genes, including DSP, PKP2, DSG2, DSC2, JUP, and TMEM43, which disrupt mechanical coupling and classically cause arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy but can present with left-dominant or dilated phenotypes; and (3) metabolic/storage/phenocopy genes, including GLA, LAMP2, PRKAG2, GAA, TTR, mitochondrial genes, and selected fatty-acid oxidation genes, which can mimic sarcomeric HCM or evolve into systolic dysfunction.",
  "niche_genome": "Use this gene framework for clinical evaluation of hypertrophic and dilated cardiomyopathy, especially patients with unexplained LV hypertrophy, LV dilation/systolic dysfunction, family history of cardiomyopathy or sudden death, conduction disease, skeletal myopathy, renal/neurologic features, early-onset disease, or arrhythmogenic/right-ventricular features.",
  "observation_genome": "Guidelines and gene-curation studies support a tiered interpretation: sarcomeric genes are the core diagnostic substrate for HCM; DCM evaluation requires inclusion of high-yield DCM genes such as TTN, LMNA, BAG3, RBM20, FLNC, PLN, DES, SCN5A, TNNT2, TPM1, and ACTC1; desmosomal genes are important when arrhythmias, fibrofatty replacement, right-ventricular disease, or DSP-like left-dominant disease are present; metabolic genes should be considered when hypertrophy is accompanied by extracardiac clues, pre-excitation, elevated CK, renal disease, neuropathy, or childhood/young-adult presentation.",
  "limitation_genome": "Broad cardiomyopathy panels can include genes with limited or disputed validity, so results should not be treated uniformly. Variant interpretation depends on phenotype match, segregation, ancestry-aware population frequency, inheritance pattern, and current ClinGen/ACMG evidence. A negative test does not exclude genetic cardiomyopathy, and variants of uncertain significance should not drive cascade testing or irreversible management.",
  "delta_genome": "Compared with a generic cardiomyopathy panel, this evaluation prioritizes mechanism-aware interpretation: sarcomeric genes for HCM, DCM-enriched structural/splicing/nuclear genes for dilated disease, desmosomal genes for arrhythmogenic–dilated overlap, and metabolic genes for treatable or syndromic phenocopies such as Fabry disease, Danon disease, PRKAG2 syndrome, Pompe disease, transthyretin amyloidosis, and mitochondrial cardiomyopathy.",
  "claim_genome": "A clinically robust cardiomyopathy gene evaluation should report sarcomeric, desmosomal, and metabolic genes together but interpret them by phenotype-specific evidence: MYH7/MYBPC3-centered sarcomeric testing is highest yield for HCM; TTN/LMNA/BAG3/RBM20/FLNC/PLN and selected sarcomeric genes are central for DCM; DSP/PKP2/DSG2/DSC2/JUP/TMEM43 identify arrhythmogenic or desmosomal dilated overlap; and metabolic genes should be actively screened when the presentation suggests a phenocopy because several have disease-specific treatments or surveillance implications."
}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Generate gene_genome output for cardiomyopathy evaluation, including sarcomeric, desmosomal, and metabolic genes associated with hypertrophic and dilated cardiomyopathy.

content:
Rationale: I’ll first identify recent clinical reviews/guidelines and landmark genetic papers to anchor a concise gene-genome output in established monogenic hypertension mechanisms and diagnostic pathways.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"monogenic hypertension early onset genes inheritance clinical testing pathway review Liddle Gordon apparent mineralocorticoid excess familial hyperaldosteronism PMID","discipline":"clinical_medicine","max_results":5}
```  
[result]: Found clinically oriented sources including PMID: 22045228 (monogenic hypertension review), PMID: 31785001 (pediatric/young-onset hypertension genetics review), and Endocrine Society/primary aldosteronism guidance citing familial hyperaldosteronism gene testing. Key syndromes repeatedly listed: Liddle, Gordon/PHAII, AME, familial hyperaldosteronism, congenital adrenal hyperplasias, and activating mineralocorticoid receptor mutations.

Rationale: I’ll read two high-yield papers: one broad review for phenotype-to-gene mapping and one guideline/review emphasizing clinical work-up and testing indications.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["PMID:22045228","PMID:31785001"],"extract":["genes","inheritance","biochemical phenotype","testing recommendations","treatment implications"]}
```  
[result]: PMID:22045228 summarizes monogenic hypertension as rare but enriched in severe early-onset/familial cases, classifying disorders by renin/aldosterone/potassium patterns and implicating CYP11B1/CYP11B2, HSD11B2, SCNN1B/SCNN1G, WNK1/WNK4, KLHL3/CUL3, CYP11B1, CYP17A1, and NR3C2. PMID:31785001 emphasizes targeted evaluation in children/young adults with severe HTN, hypokalemia/hyperkalemia, suppressed renin, endocrine signs, adrenal findings, or strong family history, with genetic confirmation enabling specific therapy and cascade testing.

Rationale: I’ll extract the “genome” elements into the requested categories, focusing on actionable clinical genetics rather than an exhaustive hypertension differential.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["PMID:22045228","PMID:31785001"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted mechanisms cluster into renal sodium-channel activation, altered NCC/WNK-SPAK signaling, mineralocorticoid excess or receptor activation, cortisol/cortisone metabolism defects, and adrenal steroid biosynthesis defects. Clinical niche is early-onset, severe/resistant, familial, or syndromic hypertension where renin/aldosterone/electrolytes guide gene selection; limitations include incomplete penetrance, overlapping biochemical profiles, and variable availability/coverage of structural-variant assays.

Rationale: Before proposing, I’ll check that the output is not missing major recognized monogenic categories and that the suggested testing pathway is current and clinically coherent.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","query":"monogenic early-onset hypertension gene panel includes SCNN1B SCNN1G WNK1 WNK4 KLHL3 CUL3 HSD11B2 CYP11B1 CYP17A1 CYP11B2 chimeric KCNJ5 CLCN2 CACNA1D CACNA1H NR3C2","against":["PMID:22045228","PMID:31785001"],"criteria":["coverage","clinical actionability","inheritance included"]}
```  
[result]: Coverage is strong for canonical monogenic hypertension. Added newer familial primary aldosteronism genes CLCN2, KCNJ5, CACNA1D, and CACNA1H as clinically relevant but phenotype-dependent; noted CYP11B1/CYP11B2 chimeric detection requires copy-number/structural-variant-aware testing rather than ordinary short-read SNV-only panels.

prompt.full_prompt: Provide gene_genome output on monogenic causes of early-onset hypertension, including relevant genes, inheritance patterns, and suggested clinical testing pathways.
Develop an idea_plan for a mixed-methods study exploring barriers to medication adherence among adolescents with epilepsy.

content:
Rationale: I’ll ground the pilot in evidence from pediatric asthma community health worker interventions, prioritizing urban, low-income, environmentally burdened settings.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "community health worker intervention children asthma urban home visits randomized trial environmental triggers",
  "discipline": "clinical_medicine",
  "limit": 5
}
```  
[result]: Found relevant candidates including: Krieger et al. “The Seattle-King County Healthy Homes Project” PMID: 16873742; Postma et al. “The Community Health Worker Asthma Intervention Study” PMID: 22306552; Campbell et al. inner-city asthma CHW programs; and systematic reviews on home-based asthma trigger reduction.

Rationale: I’ll read the strongest pediatric CHW intervention trial to identify effective components, intensity, and outcomes that can be adapted to high-pollution neighborhoods.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "PMID:16873742"
}
```  
[result]: Krieger et al. tested home-based CHW environmental assessment, education, trigger-reduction supplies, and follow-up for low-income children with asthma. The intervention reduced asthma morbidity and urgent health service use, supporting multi-visit CHW-led home trigger mitigation.

Rationale: I’ll read a second CHW asthma study focused on urban underserved children to refine workflow and implementation targets.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "PMID:22306552"
}
```  
[result]: Postma et al. evaluated a CHW asthma intervention for children with poorly controlled asthma, emphasizing culturally tailored education, medication support, action plans, and linkage to clinical care. Findings support CHWs as care extenders who can improve self-management and reduce symptoms in underserved pediatric populations.

Rationale: I’ll extract the reusable intervention genome from the evidence before designing the pilot, ensuring the plan is not just educational but addresses indoor triggers, adherence, and care coordination.  
```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "PMID:16873742",
    "PMID:22306552"
  ],
  "schema": "gene_genome"
}
```  
[result]: Extracted mechanisms: CHW home visits, asthma education, trigger assessment/remediation, medication technique checks, action-plan reinforcement, and clinical referral linkage. Key limitations: most interventions focus on indoor triggers and care behaviors; fewer explicitly integrate neighborhood outdoor pollution exposure data or pollution-day behavioral protocols.

Rationale: I’ll now propose a pilot idea_plan that preserves proven CHW components while adding a pollution-responsive layer tailored to high-pollution urban neighborhoods.  
```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "idea_plan",
  "grounding_paper_ids": [
    "PMID:16873742",
    "PMID:22306552"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: Proposed pilot intervention plan below.

```json
{
  "Idea": "A 6-month community health worker-led pediatric asthma control pilot for children living in high-pollution urban neighborhoods, combining home-based asthma management, indoor trigger reduction, medication and action-plan support, and a pollution-alert response protocol using local air-quality data.",
  "ImplementationSteps": {
    "Population": "Enroll 80 children aged 5–12 years with physician-diagnosed persistent or poorly controlled asthma who live in census tracts with high traffic-related or particulate pollution exposure.",
    "Recruitment": "Recruit through pediatric clinics, school nurses, emergency department discharge lists, and Medicaid managed-care rosters. Prioritize children with at least one asthma-related urgent care, ED visit, hospitalization, or frequent symptoms in the past 6 months.",
    "CHWTraining": "Train bilingual community health workers in pediatric asthma basics, inhaler and spacer technique, asthma action plans, motivational interviewing, home environmental assessment, pest/mold/smoke mitigation counseling, referral workflows, and use of neighborhood air-quality alerts.",
    "BaselineAssessment": "Conduct a home visit to document asthma control, medication access, inhaler technique, written asthma action-plan status, indoor triggers, smoking or vaping exposure, housing concerns, school absenteeism, caregiver confidence, and local pollution vulnerability.",
    "HomeVisitIntervention": "Deliver 4 structured CHW home visits over 3 months, followed by monthly phone or text check-ins for months 4–6. Visits include asthma education, inhaler technique correction, action-plan reinforcement, trigger identification, provision of supplies such as mattress/pillow covers, HEPA vacuum referral or portable purifier when available, pest-control referrals, and smoking-cessation linkage for household members.",
    "PollutionResponsiveComponent": "Use local AQI or PM2.5 forecasts to send caregiver text alerts on high-pollution days with tailored steps: limit outdoor exertion, close windows during peak pollution, use controller medication as prescribed, keep rescue inhaler accessible, coordinate indoor recess or modified school activity, and follow the child’s asthma action plan.",
    "ClinicalLinkage": "CHWs communicate with the child’s primary care or asthma clinician after baseline and at 3 months, flagging uncontrolled symptoms, frequent rescue inhaler use, missing controller medications, absent action plans, or housing-related hazards requiring letters or referrals.",
    "SchoolLinkage": "With caregiver consent, CHWs coordinate with school nurses to ensure rescue medication availability, updated action plans, and high-pollution-day activity modifications.",
    "ControlOrComparator": "For a pilot, use a waitlist-control or matched usual-care comparison if feasible; otherwise conduct a single-arm feasibility study with pre/post outcomes and collect implementation data needed for a future randomized trial.",
    "FollowUp": "Assess outcomes at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months, with optional passive tracking of AQI exposure and asthma-related acute care utilization."
  },
  "ImplementationOrder": [

    "Build partnerships with pediatric clinics, schools, local health departments, housing agencies, and community organizations.",
    "Hire and train community health workers from the target neighborhoods.",
    "Develop visit scripts, home assessment checklist, referral pathways, and AQI-triggered text-message library.",
    "Recruit and consent eligible families.",
    "Complete baseline home assessments.",
    "Deliver 4 CHW home visits over 3 months.",
    "Send pollution-alert messages and provide monthly follow-up through month 6.",
    "Coordinate clinical, school, and housing referrals.",
    "Analyze feasibility, clinical outcomes, utilization, and caregiver-reported acceptability."
  ],
  "Dataset": "Pilot dataset of approximately 80 enrolled children including demographics, address-linked neighborhood pollution burden category, baseline and follow-up Childhood Asthma Control Test or Asthma Control Test scores, symptom days, nighttime awakenings, rescue medication use, controller adherence self-report, inhaler technique checklist, ED/urgent care visits, hospitalizations, school absenteeism, home trigger checklist, CHW visit completion, referral completion, AQI alert engagement, and caregiver satisfaction.",
  "EvaluationMetrics": {
    "Feasibility": [
      "Enrollment rate among eligible families",
      "Retention at 3 and 6 months",
      "Percentage completing at least 3 of 4 home visits",
      "Successful delivery rate of AQI alert messages",
      "CHW documentation completeness"
    ],
    "Acceptability": [
      "Caregiver satisfaction score",
      "Caregiver-reported usefulness of home visits",
      "Caregiver-reported usefulness of pollution alerts",
      "CHW qualitative feedback on barriers and workflow"
    ],
    "ClinicalEffectiveness": [
      "Change in Childhood Asthma Control Test score from baseline to 6 months",
      "Change in symptom-days over prior 2 weeks",
      "Change in nighttime awakenings",
      "Change in rescue inhaler use",
      "Change in proportion with well-controlled asthma"
    ],
    "HealthcareUtilization": [
      "Asthma-related ED visits during 6-month follow-up",
      "Asthma-related urgent care visits",
      "Asthma-related hospitalizations",
      "Oral corticosteroid bursts"
    ],
    "BehavioralAndEnvironmental": [
      "Correct inhaler technique rate",
      "Presence of written asthma action plan",
      "Controller medication access and reported adherence",
```
