content:
rationale: I’ll first locate human-tissue studies/reviews that connect mitochondrial dysfunction to metabolic disease, prioritizing skeletal muscle, liver, and adipose tissue because they are central to insulin resistance and NAFLD/T2D. The goal is to ground the synthesis in identifiable human evidence rather than generic mitochondrial biology.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"human tissue mitochondrial dysfunction metabolic disease insulin resistance skeletal muscle liver adipose tissue NAFLD type 2 diabetes","max_results":6}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers included Petersen et al. 2004 NEJM on insulin-resistant offspring of T2D patients (PMID: 14960743), Morino et al. 2005 JCI on reduced mitochondrial density in skeletal muscle (PMID: 16167083), Sunny et al. 2011 Cell Metabolism on hepatic mitochondrial TCA flux in NAFLD (PMID: 21982708), and Heinonen et al. 2015/2017 human adipose mitochondrial studies in obesity/T2D. The candidates span muscle, liver, and adipose tissue with direct human measurements.

rationale: Skeletal muscle is the dominant site of insulin-stimulated glucose disposal, so mitochondrial defects here are a mechanistic anchor for systemic insulin resistance. I’ll read the human offspring study because it reduces confounding from overt diabetes and medication.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"PMID:14960743","focus":"human skeletal muscle mitochondrial activity and insulin resistance"}
```  
[result]: Petersen et al. reported that lean insulin-resistant offspring of patients with T2D had impaired insulin-stimulated muscle glucose uptake and ~30% lower mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation activity by 31P-MRS, with increased intramyocellular lipid. The study supports a model in which reduced muscle mitochondrial ATP synthesis promotes lipid accumulation and interferes with insulin signaling, before overt hyperglycemia.

rationale: Liver mitochondrial behavior in metabolic disease can be paradoxical: NAFLD may show increased oxidative flux yet incomplete handling of lipid excess, oxidative stress, and gluconeogenesis. I’ll read a tracer-based human NAFLD paper to avoid treating all tissues as having simply “low mitochondria.”  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"PMID:21982708","focus":"hepatic mitochondrial flux in human NAFLD and metabolic disease"}
```  
[result]: Sunny et al. found that humans with NAFLD had increased hepatic mitochondrial oxidative metabolism/TCA-cycle flux coupled to elevated gluconeogenesis and anaplerosis, rather than uniformly reduced mitochondrial activity. This indicates liver mitochondrial dysfunction can manifest as maladaptive overactivation, substrate overload, ROS risk, and impaired metabolic flexibility.

rationale: To compare tissues coherently, I’ll extract a structured genome of mechanisms, niches, observations, and limitations from the retrieved human evidence. This step distinguishes tissue-specific mitochondrial phenotypes from a single-disease universal mechanism.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["PMID:14960743","PMID:16167083","PMID:21982708"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted themes: skeletal muscle shows reduced oxidative phosphorylation/mitochondrial density with ectopic lipid and impaired insulin signaling; liver in NAFLD shows high mitochondrial substrate flux linked to gluconeogenesis and lipid overload; adipose literature commonly implicates impaired oxidative capacity, adipocyte hypoxia/inflammation, and reduced lipid-buffering capacity. Main limitation: many human studies are cross-sectional and cannot prove whether mitochondrial changes initiate or adapt to insulin resistance.
```propose
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Mitochondrial dysfunction contributes to metabolic disease through tissue-specific failures in substrate handling rather than a single uniform defect. In skeletal muscle, reduced oxidative phosphorylation capacity and/or mitochondrial density can lower fatty-acid oxidation, increase intramyocellular lipid intermediates, and impair insulin signaling. In liver, NAFLD can involve excessive mitochondrial TCA-cycle flux and gluconeogenesis under lipid overload, producing metabolic inflexibility and oxidative stress rather than simply low respiration. In adipose tissue, impaired mitochondrial oxidative capacity and biogenesis may reduce lipid-buffering capacity, promote adipocyte stress/inflammation, and increase fatty-acid spillover to liver and muscle.",
  "niche_genome": "Human metabolic disease across insulin-sensitive tissues: skeletal muscle insulin resistance and prediabetes/T2D risk; hepatic steatosis/NAFLD with increased gluconeogenesis; adipose dysfunction in obesity that propagates systemic lipid overflow and inflammation.",
  "observation_genome": "Human evidence from Petersen et al. 2004 (PMID:14960743) showed lean insulin-resistant offspring of T2D patients had reduced skeletal-muscle mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation and increased intramyocellular lipid before overt diabetes. Morino et al. 2005 (PMID:16167083) further linked insulin-resistant offspring to reduced mitochondrial density in skeletal muscle. Sunny et al. 2011 (PMID:21982708) showed that NAFLD liver can display increased mitochondrial oxidative/TCA flux and gluconeogenesis, emphasizing that hepatic mitochondrial dysfunction may be maladaptive overactivity under substrate overload rather than simple respiratory deficiency.",
  "limitation_genome": "Many human tissue studies are cross-sectional, small, and confounded by obesity, physical activity, diet, medication, and disease stage. Measurements also differ by tissue and method—MRS ATP synthesis, biopsy respiration, mitochondrial DNA/protein abundance, tracer-derived flux—so mitochondrial 'dysfunction' is not directly interchangeable across studies. Causality remains unresolved: mitochondrial defects may initiate insulin resistance in some settings but may also be secondary adaptations to lipid oversupply and hyperinsulinemia.",
  "delta_genome": "A useful next step is a matched multi-tissue human study measuring skeletal-muscle oxidative phosphorylation, hepatic TCA/gluconeogenic flux, and adipose mitochondrial respiration/biogenesis in the same individuals across lean, obese insulin-sensitive, obese insulin-resistant, NAFLD, and T2D states. Integrating isotope tracers, tissue biopsies, metabolomics, lipid intermediates, and insulin-clamp phenotyping would separate primary mitochondrial defects from compensatory mitochondrial responses.",
  "claim_genome": "Mitochondrial dysfunction contributes to metabolic disease by disrupting tissue-specific metabolic flexibility: muscle tends toward insufficient oxidative lipid disposal and insulin resistance, liver toward overloaded oxidative/anaplerotic flux and excess glucose production, and adipose tissue toward failed lipid storage with inflammatory fatty-acid spillover. Therefore, metabolic disease is best viewed as a network-level mitochondrial stress syndrome across tissues, not merely a generalized decline in mitochondrial respiration."
}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Examine how mitochondrial dysfunction contributes to metabolic disease across different human tissues.

content:
I first searched for foundational and mechanistic papers linking cellular senescence to inflammatory signaling in aging tissues, prioritizing primary studies and authoritative reviews.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"senescent cells SASP age-related tissue inflammation mechanisms NF-kB cGAS-STING mitochondrial dysfunction reviews primary studies","discipline":"biology","limit":6}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers included Coppé et al. 2008 (PMID: 18798635) defining the SASP, Freund et al. 2010 (PMID: 20732778) on inflammatory networks in senescence, Wiley et al. 2016 (PMID: 27304529) on mitochondrial dysfunction-associated senescence, Glück et al. 2017 (PMID: 28759028) on cGAS-STING sensing of cytosolic chromatin, and Gorgoulis et al. 2019 (PMID: 31675495) as a consensus review.

Next, I read a key primary SASP paper and a mechanistic innate-immune paper to anchor the review in experimentally supported cellular pathways rather than broad association.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["PMID:18798635","PMID:28759028"],"focus":"mechanisms by which senescent cells generate inflammatory tissue environments during aging"}
```  
[result]: Coppé et al. showed that senescent fibroblasts secrete IL-6, IL-8, GRO chemokines, VEGF, MMPs and other factors that alter neighboring epithelial cells and tissue microenvironments. Glück et al. showed that senescence-associated cytosolic chromatin fragments activate cGAS-STING, driving type-I interferon and NF-κB-linked inflammatory programs.

I then extracted a structured mechanism genome from the core literature, emphasizing convergent mechanisms across senescent cell types and tissues.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["PMID:18798635","PMID:20732778","PMID:27304529","PMID:28759028","PMID:31675495"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted mechanisms clustered around SASP cytokine/chemokine secretion, persistent DNA-damage signaling, NF-κB/C/EBPβ transcriptional control, cGAS-STING activation by chromatin fragments, mitochondrial ROS/mtDNA stress, extracellular matrix remodeling, immune-cell recruitment, and defective immune clearance of senescent cells with age.

Finally, I checked whether the synthesis should emphasize any under-resolved mechanism rather than merely restating SASP biology.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","against":["SASP as main inflammatory output","cGAS-STING as innate immune trigger","mitochondrial dysfunction-associated senescence","age-related failure of senescent-cell immune surveillance"],"question":"What mechanistic gap remains in explaining how senescent cells sustain age-related tissue inflammation?"}
```  
[result]: The main gap is integration: many pathways are known individually, but less is resolved about how senescent cells, immune-cell exhaustion, tissue niche stiffness/damage, and altered metabolism form self-reinforcing inflammatory circuits in aged organs.

```propose
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Senescent cells contribute to age-related tissue inflammation primarily through the senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP): a chronic secretome containing IL-6, IL-8/CXCL8, CCL2, CXCL chemokines, TNF-family signals, prostaglandins, growth factors, proteases, and extracellular-vesicle cargo. SASP induction is driven by persistent DNA-damage responses, p38 MAPK, mTOR, NF-κB and C/EBPβ signaling. Cytosolic chromatin fragments generated during senescence activate cGAS-STING, linking genome instability to type-I interferon and NF-κB inflammatory transcription. Mitochondrial dysfunction, ROS, altered NAD+/AMPK/mTOR tone, and possible mtDNA leakage further amplify inflammatory signaling. SASP factors recruit macrophages, neutrophils, NK cells and T cells, but in aged tissues immune clearance becomes inefficient, allowing senescent cells to persist and maintain paracrine inflammation. Proteases and matrix-remodeling enzymes such as MMPs disrupt extracellular matrix architecture, release matrix-bound inflammatory cues, and promote further cell stress, thereby creating feed-forward senescence-inflammation loops.",
  "niche_genome": "Relevant niches include aged skin, adipose tissue, lung, liver, kidney, vascular endothelium, cartilage, skeletal muscle, brain glia-associated environments, and premalignant epithelial stroma. Senescent fibroblasts, epithelial cells, endothelial cells, preadipocytes, smooth-muscle cells, immune cells, and glial cells can all contribute, with tissue-specific SASP composition shaped by local damage, oxygen tension, matrix stiffness, metabolic state, and immune surveillance capacity.",
  "observation_genome": "Primary work such as Coppé et al. 2008 (PMID:18798635) showed that senescent cells secrete inflammatory cytokines, chemokines, growth factors and proteases that act non-cell-autonomously on neighboring cells. Freund et al. 2010 (PMID:20732778) synthesized evidence that NF-κB/C/EBPβ inflammatory networks are central to senescence. Wiley et al. 2016 (PMID:27304529) linked mitochondrial dysfunction to a distinct inflammatory senescence program. Glück et al. 2017 (PMID:28759028) demonstrated that cytosolic chromatin activates cGAS-STING during senescence, providing an innate-immune route from genome damage to inflammation. Consensus reviews such as Gorgoulis et al. 2019 (PMID:31675495) support the view that senescent-cell accumulation is a causal contributor to tissue dysfunction and chronic inflammation during aging.",
  "limitation_genome": "The SASP is highly heterogeneous by cell type, senescence trigger, tissue context, and time since senescence induction, so no single cytokine panel universally defines inflammatory senescence. Many mechanistic studies use cultured cells or acute senescence models that may not fully capture aged tissue niches. In vivo attribution is also difficult because inflammation can both induce senescence and be induced by senescence. Senolytic and senomorphic interventions support causality but often affect multiple cell populations or pathways.",
  "delta_genome": "The key unresolved issue is how individual inflammatory mechanisms integrate over time in aged organs: persistent DNA damage, cGAS-STING activation, mitochondrial dysfunction, matrix remodeling, and declining immune clearance likely form self-reinforcing circuits, but their ordering and tissue-specific dominance remain incompletely mapped. More spatial, single-cell, longitudinal and perturbational studies are needed to distinguish harmful chronic SASP from transient beneficial senescence programs involved in repair and tumor suppression.",
  "claim_genome": "Senescent cells are not merely passive markers of aging; they are active inflammatory signaling hubs. By coupling stable cell-cycle arrest to SASP production, innate immune activation, mitochondrial stress, matrix remodeling and failed immune clearance, they help convert local cellular damage into chronic, age-related tissue inflammation, while the precise inflammatory output depends strongly on tissue niche and senescence context."
}
```ablytyped

content:
I will anchor the comparison in well-cited vertebrate adaptive-memory and invertebrate priming/trained-immunity literature, then synthesize mechanisms, niches, observations, and limits.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"vertebrate adaptive immune memory mechanisms clonal selection memory B cells T cells Ahmed Gray 1996 DOI PMID"}
```  
[result]: Found Ahmed & Gray, “Immunological memory and protective immunity: understanding their relation,” Science 1996, paper_id PMID:8662461 / DOI:10.1126/science.8662461. Also found reviews on memory B/T-cell differentiation, affinity maturation, and long-lived plasma cells.

I need invertebrate examples that demonstrate innate priming without RAG-mediated lymphocyte receptors, including specificity or transgenerational effects.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"invertebrate innate immune priming specificity Drosophila Tribolium transgenerational immune priming DOI PMID"}
```  
[result]: Found Pham et al., “A specific primed immune response in Drosophila is dependent on phagocytes,” PLoS Pathogens 2007, paper_id PMID:17352533 / DOI:10.1371/journal.ppat.0030026. Found Roth et al., “Specificity of trans-generational immune priming in the red flour beetle, Tribolium castaneum,” Proc. R. Soc. B 2010, DOI:10.1098/rspb.2010.1631.

I will extract the mechanistic contrast from one vertebrate synthesis and two invertebrate priming exemplars to avoid overgeneralizing from a single taxon.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["PMID:8662461","PMID:17352533","DOI:10.1098/rspb.2010.1631"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Ahmed & Gray emphasize antigen-specific clonal expansion, persistence of memory lymphocytes, faster secondary responses, and protective recall. Pham et al. report Drosophila protection after prior Streptococcus pneumoniae exposure, dependent on phagocytes and showing pathogen-associated specificity. Roth et al. report pathogen-specific transgenerational priming in Tribolium, indicating inherited enhancement despite absence of vertebrate adaptive lymphocytes.

To sharpen the comparison, I will test whether the synthesized claim is novel as a framing rather than as a discovery claim.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claim":"Vertebrate immune memory and invertebrate immune priming are functionally convergent but mechanistically distinct: vertebrates encode durable antigen-specific recall mainly through somatically diversified lymphocyte clones, whereas invertebrates use innate-cell, humoral, microbiome, epigenetic, or parental-effect pathways that can be specific but are usually less molecularly resolved."}
```  
[result]: Novelty is moderate as a comparative synthesis, not as a new empirical claim. The distinction between adaptive memory and innate priming is established, but emphasizing convergent ecological function with divergent encoding mechanisms is a useful organizing hypothesis.
```action
{"tool":"propose","mechanism_genome":"Vertebrate immune memory is primarily encoded by somatically diversified B- and T-lymphocyte clones: antigen receptor recombination, clonal expansion, germinal-center affinity maturation, long-lived plasma cells, and memory B/T-cell populations enable faster and stronger recall. Invertebrate innate immune priming lacks RAG-mediated adaptive lymphocytes and is instead mediated by mechanisms such as persistent or reprogrammed phagocytes, antimicrobial/humoral pathway tuning, epigenetic or metabolic innate reprogramming, microbiome-mediated effects, and maternal or paternal transfer of immune cues.","niche_genome":"Vertebrate memory is best suited to long-lived organisms facing recurrent pathogens where highly specific, durable recall improves survival and vaccine responsiveness. Invertebrate priming operates in taxa without canonical adaptive immunity—e.g., insects such as Drosophila and Tribolium—where short generation times, ecological pathogen recurrence, and parental effects can favor rapid within-generation or transgenerational enhancement.","observation_genome":"Ahmed & Gray 1996 describe protective vertebrate immune memory as linked to antigen-specific lymphocyte persistence and enhanced secondary responses. Pham et al. 2007 show that Drosophila can mount a specific primed response after bacterial exposure, dependent on phagocytes. Roth et al. 2010 show pathogen-specific transgenerational immune priming in Tribolium castaneum, demonstrating inherited immune enhancement without vertebrate-style adaptive receptors.","limitation_genome":"The vertebrate mechanism is molecularly well mapped, but even vertebrates also show innate trained immunity, so adaptive memory is not the only memory-like layer. In invertebrates, evidence for specificity and inheritance is strong in selected systems, but the molecular carriers are often unresolved, may vary by species and pathogen, and may not produce the same durability, precision, or recall breadth as lymphocyte-based memory.","delta_genome":"The key contrast is not memory versus no memory, but how immune history is stored. Vertebrates store much of it in expanded antigen-specific lymphocyte lineages with diversified receptors; invertebrates store immune history through innate physiological state changes, effector pathway calibration, cellular priming, and sometimes parental transmission. Thus, both systems solve the ecological problem of recurrent infection, but they use distinct biological substrates.","claim_genome":"Immune memory is functionally convergent across animals but mechanistically partitioned: vertebrates achieve high-specificity, durable recall mainly through adaptive lymphocyte clones, whereas invertebrates achieve immune priming through innate and sometimes transgenerational mechanisms that can be pathogen-specific but are generally more heterogeneous and less mechanistically resolved."}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Compare immune memory mechanisms in vertebrates and innate immune priming in invertebrates.

content:
I’ll first anchor the investigation in primary soil-resistome studies that explicitly connect resistance genes to mobility/HGT, then extract the mechanisms and limitations into the requested genome-style schema.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "soil microbial communities horizontal gene transfer antibiotic resistance mobile genetic elements resistome metagenomics",
  "discipline": "biology",
  "k": 5
}
```  
[result]: Found candidates including Forsberg et al. 2012 Science, “The shared antibiotic resistome of soil bacteria and human pathogens” (paper_id: PMID:22936781; DOI:10.1126/science.1220761), Nesme et al. 2014 ISME Journal, “Large-scale metagenomic-based study of antibiotic resistance in the environment” (paper_id: DOI:10.1038/ismej.2013.198), and Udikovic-Kolic et al. 2014 PNAS, “Bloom of resident antibiotic-resistant bacteria in soil following manure fertilization” (paper_id: PMID:25288759; DOI:10.1073/pnas.1409836111).

Forsberg et al. is the key mechanistic paper because it compared soil bacteria with pathogens and found near-identical resistance genes, implying recent or ongoing horizontal transfer.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "PMID:22936781",
  "focus": "evidence for HGT between soil bacteria and pathogens; mobile genetic elements; resistance gene clusters"
}
```  
[result]: Forsberg et al. cultured multidrug-resistant soil Proteobacteria and identified resistance genes whose nucleotide identity to human pathogen genes was too high to be explained by ancient divergence alone. The study reported that some resistance determinants were physically linked to mobile-element features such as transposases/integrases and to multi-gene resistance loci, supporting HGT from environmental reservoirs into clinically relevant bacteria or vice versa.

Manure-amended soil is a strong perturbation model for how agricultural management reshapes soil resistomes and mobilizes ARGs.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "PMID:25288759",
  "focus": "manure effects on soil antibiotic resistance genes, resident soil bacteria, and HGT-associated elements"
}
```  
[result]: Udikovic-Kolic et al. showed that manure fertilization caused a bloom of resident soil bacteria carrying diverse antibiotic resistance genes, not merely introduction of manure-borne strains. The results support selection/enrichment of indigenous ARG reservoirs in soil after anthropogenic input, with mobile DNA and community turnover increasing opportunities for transfer.

To synthesize across studies, I’ll extract the gene-genome elements most relevant to mobile genetic elements, ARG clusters, ecological niche, and limitations.  
```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "PMID:22936781",
    "PMID:25288759",
    "DOI:10.1038/ismej.2013.198"
  ],
  "schema": [
    "mechanism_genome",
    "niche_genome",
    "observation_genome",
    "limitation_genome",
    "delta_genome",
    "claim_genome"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: Extraction indicates that soil resistomes contain ancient, diverse ARGs, but HGT-relevant evidence is strongest where ARGs are adjacent to transposases, integrases, plasmid-associated sequences, insertion sequences, or arranged in multi-resistance clusters. Across metagenomic and isolate-based studies, manure, antibiotics, metals, and dense microbial mixing appear to enrich ARG hosts and increase the chance that mobile elements capture and disseminate resistance loci.

```propose
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Horizontal gene transfer has shaped soil antibiotic resistance by moving ARGs through plasmids, transposons, integrons, insertion sequences, and other recombination-associated mobile genetic elements. In soil bacteria, resistance genes are often found near transposases, integrases, or multi-gene resistance islands, allowing co-transfer of beta-lactamases, aminoglycoside-modifying enzymes, tetracycline efflux/protection genes, sulfonamide resistance genes, and multidrug efflux determinants. Physical clustering enables co-selection: antibiotics, heavy metals, disinfectants, or manure-derived nutrients can enrich a mobile element carrying several ARGs even if only one trait is directly selected.",
  "niche_genome": "The relevant niche is heterogeneous soil microbial communities, especially agricultural soils exposed to manure, wastewater, antibiotics, metals, and high microbial biomass. Native soil bacteria are not passive recipients; they constitute a large ancestral reservoir of resistance functions, while manure-amended soils create ecological conditions that enrich resident resistant taxa and increase encounter rates among ARG donors, recipients, bacteriophages, plasmids, and transposon-bearing cells.",
  "observation_genome": "Forsberg et al. 2012 (PMID:22936781) found soil-derived resistance genes with striking nucleotide similarity to genes in human pathogens, plus linkage to mobile-element signatures and resistance loci, supporting recent or ongoing HGT between environmental and clinical resistomes. Udikovic-Kolic et al. 2014 (PMID:25288759) showed that manure fertilization can trigger blooms of resident soil bacteria carrying diverse ARGs rather than simply adding manure organisms. Large-scale metagenomic work such as Nesme et al. 2014 (DOI:10.1038/ismej.2013.198) further supports that environmental ARGs are widespread and vary by habitat, with soil acting as a major reservoir.",
  "limitation_genome": "Most soil-resistome studies infer HGT from sequence similarity, gene-context evidence, or co-occurrence with mobile-element genes; these signals do not always prove transfer direction, donor-recipient identity, or transfer rate. Short-read metagenomes can fragment plasmids, integrons, and resistance islands, making ARG-MGE linkage uncertain. Culture-based surveys miss uncultured taxa, while metagenomic surveys may detect ARG homologs whose phenotypic resistance or mobility has not been validated.",
  "delta_genome": "A decisive next step is long-read, Hi-C/proximity-ligation, and plasmid-enriched metagenomics across manure gradients and untreated soils, paired with exogenous plasmid capture and stable-isotope probing. This would connect ARGs to complete plasmids, transposons, integrons, and host genomes, distinguishing ancient soil resistance from recently mobilized resistance clusters with higher clinical-transfer risk.",
  "claim_genome": "Horizontal gene transfer has not merely added occasional resistance genes to soil communities; it has organized soil ARGs into mobile, selectable clusters that can expand within resident soil bacteria and occasionally overlap with pathogen-associated resistance genes. Soil should therefore be viewed as both an ancient reservoir of resistance diversity and an active evolutionary arena where mobile genetic elements assemble and redistribute antibiotic resistance under agricultural and anthropogenic selection."
}
``` 
belisoa֏

prompt.full_prompt: Investigate how horizontal gene transfer has shaped antibiotic resistance in soil microbial communities. Use gene_genome output to summarize mobile genetic elements and resistance gene clusters.

content:
Rationale: I’ll first identify experimentally relevant zooplankton papers that report reproductive or developmental endpoints, prioritizing freshwater taxa such as *Daphnia magna* rather than marine copepod-only studies. This establishes what mechanisms and assay designs are already supported.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"freshwater zooplankton Daphnia magna microplastics reproduction development chronic exposure DOI fecundity growth","discipline":"biology","limit":5}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers included Besseling et al. 2014 (*Environmental Science & Technology*, DOI: 10.1021/es503001d), Ogonowski et al. 2016 (*PLOS ONE*, DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0155063), and Rehse et al. 2016 (*Chemosphere*, DOI: 10.1016/j.chemosphere.2016.02.133). The strongest fit for reproduction/development was Besseling et al. because it measured *Daphnia magna* reproduction under plastic-particle exposure, while Ogonowski et al. provided fitness-related endpoints for microparticles.  

Rationale: I’ll read the strongest mechanistic/reproductive study to extract exposure design, organism, and endpoints that can anchor a new experimental plan.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1021/es503001d"}
```  
[result]: Besseling et al. 2014 reported that polystyrene nanoplastics affected algal growth and reduced *Daphnia magna* reproduction, with effects depending on particle concentration and food-chain context. The study supports testing reproduction together with food availability/ingestion because plastic effects can be mediated by trophic interactions rather than direct toxicity alone.  

Rationale: I’ll read a complementary freshwater zooplankton fitness study to avoid designing an experiment that only repeats prior chronic-reproduction work. This helps identify a gap around particle realism, developmental staging, and multigenerational outcomes.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1371/journal.pone.0155063"}
```  
[result]: Ogonowski et al. 2016 compared natural and anthropogenic microparticles in *Daphnia magna* and found that particle type can alter individual fitness endpoints such as growth, survival, and reproduction. The paper highlights that effects may not be unique to plastic chemistry alone and that controls with natural suspended particles are important.  

Rationale: I’ll check novelty relative to these retrieved studies: the idea should extend beyond single-particle, single-generation toxicity by adding environmentally realistic mixtures and developmental timing.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","paper_ids":["10.1021/es503001d","10.1371/journal.pone.0155063","10.1016/j.chemosphere.2016.02.133"],"proposed_angle":"multigenerational freshwater zooplankton study comparing environmentally aged vs pristine microplastics, natural-particle controls, and developmental-stage exposure windows with reproductive, developmental, feeding, and transcriptomic endpoints"}
```  
[result]: The angle is partially covered by prior *Daphnia* micro/nanoplastic work, but remains novel if it combines aged/pristine particles, natural suspended-particle controls, developmental-window exposure, and F0–F2 reproductive carryover. Prior studies often emphasize acute immobilization or single-generation fitness rather than stage-specific developmental programming and transgenerational reproduction.  

```propose
{
  "Idea": "Investigate how environmentally realistic microplastic exposure alters freshwater zooplankton reproduction and development using Daphnia magna as a model, with emphasis on whether effects are driven by plastic-specific toxicity, particle-mediated feeding interference, or developmental-stage sensitivity.",
  "ImplementationSteps": {
    "1_exposure_design": "Run a factorial chronic exposure experiment using neonate Daphnia magna exposed to pristine polystyrene microplastics, environmentally aged polystyrene microplastics, a natural suspended-particle control, and a no-particle control. Include at least three environmentally relevant particle concentrations plus a high positive-control concentration.",
    "2_developmental_window_test": "Expose separate cohorts during early juvenile development only, adult reproductive phase only, and full life cycle to identify whether microplastics have stronger effects on maturation, embryo production, or adult fecundity.",
    "3_multigenerational_assay": "Track F0 exposed animals through reproduction, then rear F1 and F2 offspring either in clean water or continued exposure to distinguish direct toxicity from parental or carryover effects.",
    "4_endpoint_collection": "Measure survival, time to first brood, number of broods, neonates per brood, abnormal embryos, body length, molting frequency, ingestion rate using fluorescent algae, gut retention of particles, and lipid/energy-storage indicators.",
    "5_mechanism_layer": "For selected treatments, profile expression of genes associated with molting, vitellogenesis, oxidative stress, and energy metabolism, and relate molecular shifts to reproductive and developmental outcomes."
  },
  "ImplementationOrder": [
    "Prepare and characterize pristine, aged, and natural control particles by size distribution, polymer identity, surface charge, and particle number concentration.",
    "Establish synchronized Daphnia magna neonate cohorts and validate baseline reproduction under standard culture conditions.",
    "Run developmental-window and full-life-cycle exposure assays with daily survival and reproduction monitoring.",
    "Quantify feeding rate, growth, molting, embryo abnormalities, and particle ingestion/retention at predefined juvenile and adult stages.",
    "Extend selected treatments into F1 and F2 generations to test carryover effects.",
    "Analyze dose-response relationships and determine whether aged plastics differ from pristine plastics and natural particles.",
    "Integrate reproductive, developmental, feeding, and gene-expression data to infer likely mechanisms."
  ],
  "Dataset": "Primary dataset generated from controlled laboratory Daphnia magna exposure assays, including particle-characterization data, individual-level life-history records, brood counts, developmental timing, microscopy-based embryo and gut-particle observations, feeding-rate measurements, and targeted qPCR or RNA-seq data. The design is grounded in freshwater Daphnia micro/nanoplastic studies including Besseling et al. 2014 (DOI: 10.1021/es503001d), Ogonowski et al. 2016 (DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0155063), and Rehse et al. 2016 (DOI: 10.1016/j.chemosphere.2016.02.133).",
  "EvaluationMetrics": {
    "ReproductiveMetrics": [
      "time to first brood",
      "total offspring per female",
      "offspring per brood",
      "brood interval",
      "proportion of abnormal or aborted embryos"
    ],
    "DevelopmentalMetrics": [
      "juvenile growth rate",
      "adult body length",
      "age at maturity",
      "molting frequency",
      "F1 and F2 survival and maturation"
    ],
    "MechanisticMetrics": [
      "algal ingestion rate",
      "gut particle burden and clearance time",
      "lipid storage level",
      "oxidative-stress marker expression",
      "vitellogenesis and molting-pathway gene expression"
    ],
    "StatisticalCriteria": [
      "dose-response slope for each particle type",
      "effect-size comparison between pristine, aged, and natural particles",
      "interaction between exposure window and particle treatment",
      "evidence of parental or transgenerational carryover in F1/F2 generations"
    ]
  },
  "ExpectedOutcome": "The study should determine whether microplastics reduce zooplankton reproduction mainly by lowering food uptake and energy allocation, by inducing plastic-specific physiological stress, or by disrupting sensitive developmental windows. Aged microplastics are expected to produce different effects than pristine particles because of altered surface chemistry and biofilm/contaminant interactions, while natural-particle controls will help separate generic suspended-particle stress from microplastic-specific impacts."
}
```

Study gene regulatory networks involved in flower color variation among closely related plant species. Provide a gene_genome output listing candidate pigmentation genes and regulatory elements.

content:
Range shifts alter interactions only when new climate suitability changes spatial co-occurrence and species can rewire to novel partners, so I first search for empirical/modeling anchors on climate disruption and pollination networks.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"climate change range shifts plant pollinator interactions network modeling phenological mismatch spatial co-occurrence","discipline":"biology","limit":5}
```  
[result]: Found relevant candidates: Memmott et al. 2007, *Ecology Letters*, DOI `10.1111/j.1461-0248.2007.01061.x`; Burkle et al. 2013, *Science*, DOI `10.1126/science.1232728`; Bartomeus et al. 2011, *PNAS*, DOI `10.1073/pnas.1115559108`; Schweiger et al. 2012, *Philosophical Transactions B*, DOI `10.1098/rstb.2011.0357`. These cover warming-driven temporal disruption, historical network loss, phenological tracking, and spatial mismatch risk.  

I read one modeling paper and one empirical resurvey paper to ground the plan in mechanisms and measurable outputs.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["10.1111/j.1461-0248.2007.01061.x","10.1126/science.1232728"]}
```  
[result]: Memmott et al. modeled warming-induced phenological shifts and predicted reduced temporal overlap between plants and pollinators, causing potential interaction loss. Burkle et al. showed that over ~120 years, pollination networks lost species and interactions, with interaction loss explained partly by reduced co-occurrence and phenological mismatches.  

I extract reusable genome-style design constraints from the retrieved papers so the final plan links mechanism, data, and limitations.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["10.1111/j.1461-0248.2007.01061.x","10.1126/science.1232728"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted mechanisms: climate can decouple interactions via altered flowering/flight timing and spatial co-occurrence; networks can lose links even when some species persist. Key limitation: many studies model phenology or local networks, but less often integrate projected geographic range shifts, dispersal, phenology, and probabilistic rewiring in one forecasting framework.  

I check novelty against known approaches to avoid proposing only a standard species distribution model or static network projection.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"Couple climate-envelope range-shift projections with phenology models and trait-informed probabilistic plant-pollinator network rewiring to forecast future interaction networks under climate scenarios.","references":["10.1111/j.1461-0248.2007.01061.x","10.1126/science.1232728","10.1073/pnas.1115559108","10.1098/rstb.2011.0357"]}
```  
[result]: Novelty is moderate-to-high if framed as an integrated, validated pipeline: SDM-based future co-occurrence + phenology overlap + trait/phylogeny-based link probability + dispersal constraints. Prior work treats several components separately, but fewer studies jointly forecast spatial range shifts and network rewiring with explicit out-of-sample interaction validation.  

```action
{
  "tool":"propose",
  "schema":"idea_plan",
  "content":{
    "Idea":"Develop a spatially explicit dynamic plant-pollinator network model that forecasts how climate-driven range shifts alter interactions by combining species distribution models, phenological overlap models, and trait-informed interaction rewiring. For each future climate scenario, the model predicts which plants and pollinators co-occur, whether their flowering/flight periods overlap, and the probability that they interact given traits such as floral morphology, body size, diet breadth, and historical partner use.",
    "ImplementationSteps":{
      "1_DataAssembly":"Compile plant and pollinator occurrence records from GBIF/regional atlases, observed interaction records from GloBI, Web of Life, Interaction Web Database, and local monitoring datasets, phenology records from USA-NPN or equivalent regional schemes, and climate layers from WorldClim/CHELSA for current and future CMIP6 scenarios.",
      "2_RangeShiftModeling":"Fit species distribution models for each plant and pollinator using current climate, land cover, and elevation predictors. Project distributions to future time slices under multiple emissions scenarios, with alternative dispersal assumptions: no dispersal, limited dispersal, and full climate tracking.",
      "3_PhenologyModeling":"Model flowering time and pollinator activity period as climate-sensitive response variables using degree days, seasonal temperature, and precipitation. Project future phenological windows and estimate temporal overlap for every potential plant-pollinator pair within each grid cell.",
      "4_InteractionModel":"Train a probabilistic link-prediction model in which interaction probability depends on spatial co-occurrence, temporal overlap, trait matching, species abundances where available, phylogenetic relatedness, and historical interaction evidence. Use hierarchical Bayesian modeling or gradient-boosted / graph-neural network alternatives, with uncertainty propagated from SDMs and phenology models.",
      "5_NetworkForecasting":"For each grid cell and future scenario, generate predicted bipartite networks. Quantify link loss, novel links, connectance, nestedness, specialization, modularity, plant pollination deficit risk, pollinator resource deficit risk, and robustness to secondary extinctions.",
      "6_ValidationAndSensitivity":"Validate range models against withheld occurrence data, phenology models against withheld observation years, and interaction models against spatially or temporally withheld networks. Run sensitivity analyses for dispersal limits, climate scenario, trait missingness, sampling bias, and alternative assumptions about rewiring capacity."
    },
    "ImplementationOrder":[
      "Assemble harmonized occurrence, interaction, trait, phenology, and climate datasets.",
      "Fit and validate present-day SDMs for plants and pollinators.",
      "Project species ranges under CMIP6 climate scenarios and dispersal assumptions.",
      "Fit climate-sensitive phenology models and project future flowering/flight overlap.",
      "Train and validate trait- and co-occurrence-based interaction link predictor.",
      "Construct future local networks and compare them to baseline networks.",
      "Map hotspots of interaction loss, novel interaction formation, and pollination-service risk.",
      "Perform uncertainty and sensitivity analyses."
    ],
    "Dataset":"Primary datasets: GBIF species occurrences; WorldClim or CHELSA bioclimatic layers; CMIP6 future climate projections; GloBI, Web of Life, Interaction Web Database, and published plant-pollinator networks for interaction records; USA-NPN or regional phenology databases for flowering and pollinator activity; TRY, BIEN, and pollinator trait databases for floral and insect traits. A strong first case study would use a well-sampled temperate region with historical interaction resurveys, such as North American or European plant-pollinator networks.",
    "EvaluationMetrics":{
      "RangeModelPerformance":["AUC","TSS","Boyce index","spatial block cross-validation error"],
      "PhenologyPerformance":["mean absolute error in flowering/flight onset","mean absolute error in peak date","coverage of predicted phenological intervals"],
      "InteractionPrediction":["AUPRC for held-out links","ROC-AUC","precision@k","calibration error","spatially blocked held-out network likelihood"],
      "NetworkChange":["fraction of lost interactions","fraction of novel interactions","change in connectance","change in nestedness","change in modularity","change in specialization H2 prime","robustness to secondary extinctions"],
      "EcologicalRisk":["plant species with projected pollinator deficit","pollinator species with projected floral-resource deficit","spatial hotspots of interaction turnover","uncertainty intervals across climate and dispersal scenarios"]
    },
    "ExpectedOutcome":"The model should identify where climate-driven range shifts are likely to create interaction mismatches, where novel interactions may compensate for lost historical links, and which plant or pollinator species are most vulnerable because their future ranges, phenology, or traits leave them with few compatible partners. The expected scientific contribution is an integrated forecasting framework that moves beyond static co-occurrence by explicitly combining range shifts, phenological overlap, and rewiring capacity."
  }
}
```  
[result]: Proposed an evidence-grounded idea_plan based on prior findings that climate change can disrupt plant-pollinator networks through temporal mismatch and reduced co-occurrence, while adding a spatial range-shift and rewiring framework for forecasting future networks.

prompt.full_prompt: Develop an idea_plan for modeling how climate-driven range shifts may change plant-pollinator interactions.

content:
I first search for primary studies that connect prion-like/low-complexity domains to phase separation and stress granule biology, prioritizing eukaryotic cell evidence over purely in vitro biophysics.
```action search
{
  "query": "prion-like low complexity domains phase separation stress granules eukaryotic cells FUS TIA1 Sup35",
  "discipline": "biology",
  "limit": 5
}
```
[result]: Found key candidates: Molliex et al. 2015 Cell (PMID: 26412307; DOI: 10.1016/j.cell.2015.09.015), Patel et al. 2015 Cell (PMID: 26317470; DOI: 10.1016/j.cell.2015.07.047), Kato et al. 2012 Cell (PMID: 22579281; DOI: 10.1016/j.cell.2012.04.017), and Franzmann et al. 2018 Science (PMID: 29930091; DOI: 10.1126/science.aao5654). These papers span stress granules, ALS-linked RNA-binding proteins, hydrogels, and yeast prion-domain phase separation.

To ground the stress-granule mechanism, I read the paper most directly linking low-complexity/prion-like domains to stress granule assembly and pathological maturation.
```action read
{
  "paper_id": "PMID:26412307",
  "focus": "how prion-like low-complexity domains drive phase separation, stress granule recruitment, and pathological fibrillization"
}
```
[result]: Molliex et al. showed that low-complexity domains in RNA-binding proteins such as hnRNPA1 phase separate into liquid droplets that can promote stress granule assembly, but can mature toward less dynamic/fibrillar states. Disease-associated mutations accelerated this liquid-to-solid transition, supporting a model where normal granule assembly and pathology share material-state mechanisms.

To broaden beyond metazoan stress granules and assess conserved eukaryotic logic, I read a yeast study where a canonical prion protein’s phase behavior is adaptive under stress.
```action read
{
  "paper_id": "PMID:29930091",
  "focus": "yeast prion-like domain phase separation under stress and cellular fitness consequences"
}
```
[result]: Franzmann et al. reported that yeast Sup35 forms pH-regulated condensates during stress; these assemblies are reversible and can protect translation-related function during cytosolic acidification. The work supports a conserved principle: prion-like domains can act as stress-responsive condensation modules, not merely pathological aggregation motifs.

I extract a concise mechanistic genome from the retrieved evidence, emphasizing causal variables rather than cataloging proteins.
```action extract_genome
{
  "paper_ids": ["PMID:26412307", "PMID:29930091", "PMID:26317470", "PMID:22579281"],
  "schema": ["mechanism_genome", "niche_genome", "observation_genome", "limitation_genome", "delta_genome", "claim_genome"]
}
```
[result]: Extracted pattern: prion-like/low-complexity domains encode multivalent weak interactions that nucleate liquid condensates; RNA, stress conditions, pH, mutations, and chaperone/protein quality-control systems tune reversibility versus hardening. Main limitation is that many studies infer mechanisms from overexpression or purified proteins, while native stoichiometry, cell type, stress duration, and proteostasis context remain under-resolved.

```action propose
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Prion-like protein domains influence phase separation through multivalent, weakly adhesive low-complexity interactions—often enriched in polar, glycine, glutamine/asparagine, tyrosine, and charged residues—that lower the threshold for condensate nucleation. In stress granules, these domains act as tunable scaffolds or client-recruitment modules: they promote reversible liquid-like assembly with RNA and RNA-binding proteins, but mutations, prolonged stress, altered pH, post-translational modification, or impaired chaperone activity can shift condensates toward gel-like or amyloid-like states.",
  "niche_genome": "Eukaryotic stress responses in which RNA-binding proteins and translation factors rapidly reorganize into membraneless ribonucleoprotein granules, especially mammalian stress granules involving FUS/hnRNPA1/TIA-family-like low-complexity proteins and yeast stress condensates involving Sup35-like prion domains.",
  "observation_genome": "Molliex et al. 2015 showed that low-complexity domains of RNA-binding proteins phase separate and help assemble stress granules, while disease mutations accelerate maturation into less dynamic fibrillar states. Patel et al. 2015 similarly found that FUS droplets can undergo liquid-to-solid transition enhanced by ALS mutations. Kato et al. 2012 established that low-complexity domains form dynamic hydrogels resembling RNA granule scaffolds. Franzmann et al. 2018 showed that the yeast prion protein Sup35 forms reversible stress-induced condensates that can be physiologically protective.",
  "limitation_genome": "A central limitation is distinguishing physiological prion-like condensation from pathological aggregation in native cells. Many mechanisms are derived from purified proteins, truncations, or overexpression, so the field still lacks quantitative native-stoichiometry maps of how RNA concentration, domain grammar, stress duration, pH, phosphorylation/methylation, and chaperone engagement jointly determine condensate material state.",
  "delta_genome": "The most productive next step is to move from protein-by-protein descriptions to a predictive domain-grammar model: systematically edit prion-like domains at endogenous loci, vary stress intensity/duration, and measure nucleation threshold, exchange kinetics, RNA partitioning, and hardening propensity in live cells. This would clarify which sequence features encode adaptive stress granule reversibility versus disease-prone persistence.",
  "claim_genome": "Prion-like domains are not simply aggregation-prone liabilities; they are stress-responsive phase-separation modules that help eukaryotic cells assemble dynamic stress granules. Their biological impact depends on whether cellular context preserves a reversible liquid condensate or permits maturation into persistent gel/amyloid-like assemblies associated with proteotoxic disease."
}
```க்க
ablytyped
The evidence supports a model in which prion-like domains are adaptive condensation elements whose risk comes from material-state misregulation, not from phase separation per se.

prompt.full_prompt: Explore how prion-like protein domains influence phase separation and stress granule formation in eukaryotic cells.

content:
Rationale: I first search for authoritative mammalian early-embryo epigenetics papers spanning DNA methylation reprogramming, histone/chromatin state, imprinting, and human/mouse comparisons. This should identify primary datasets plus synthesis papers for an evidence-grounded evaluation.
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "mammalian preimplantation embryo epigenetic reprogramming DNA methylation histone modifications imprinting early development",
  "discipline": "biology",
  "limit": 5
}
```
[result]: Candidate papers include Smith et al. 2012 Nature, DOI:10.1038/nature11418, PMID:22955699; Guo et al. 2014 Nature, DOI:10.1038/nature13544, PMID:25079557; Messerschmidt et al. 2014 Genes & Development, DOI:10.1101/gad.234294.113, PMID:25319817; Burton & Torres-Padilla 2014 Nat Rev Mol Cell Biol, DOI:10.1038/nrm3808, PMID:24882745; Reik et al. 2001 Science, DOI:10.1126/science.1063443, PMID:11498579. These cover genome-wide methylation waves, chromatin regulation of cell fate, and conserved mammalian reprogramming principles.

Rationale: I read a primary mouse study because mouse embryos provide mechanistic resolution for the first major methylation reprogramming event and its developmental timing. This is central to evaluating causal roles versus correlative epigenomic change.
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "PMID:22955699",
  "focus": "DNA methylation dynamics in mouse oocyte-to-blastocyst transition and links to zygotic genome activation and lineage specification"
}
```
[result]: Smith et al. found broad DNA demethylation after fertilization followed by a distinctive low-methylation regulatory phase in the early embryo, with methylation retained at selected elements such as imprinted loci and some transposons. The study supports a role for methylation reprogramming in resetting developmental potency while protecting epigenetic information needed for parent-of-origin expression and genome stability.

Rationale: I next read a human embryo methylome paper to check whether the mouse-based model generalizes to mammals and to identify species-specific constraints. Human data are essential because early mammalian development is conserved in outline but differs in timing and regulatory details.
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "PMID:25079557",
  "focus": "human preimplantation DNA methylation landscape, parental-genome asymmetry, imprint maintenance, comparison to mouse"
}
```
[result]: Guo et al. reported global DNA demethylation in human preimplantation embryos, including parental-genome asymmetry and maintenance of methylation at imprinted differentially methylated regions. The data indicate that extensive methylome resetting is conserved in mammals, but the exact kinetics and regulatory architecture differ between human and mouse embryos.

Rationale: Because “epigenetic modifications” are broader than DNA methylation, I include chromatin and histone-modification evidence that directly relates epigenetic state to cell-fate allocation. This helps distinguish genome resetting from instructive regulation of totipotency, pluripotency, and first lineage decisions.
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "PMID:24882745",
  "focus": "histone modifications, chromatin accessibility, nucleosome organization, and cell fate decisions in mammalian preimplantation embryos"
}
```
[result]: Burton and Torres-Padilla synthesize evidence that histone marks, chromatin accessibility, histone variants, and higher-order chromatin dynamics regulate totipotency-to-pluripotency transitions and trophectoderm/inner-cell-mass allocation. They emphasize that chromatin is unusually plastic in early embryos, and that perturbing chromatin modifiers can impair zygotic genome activation and lineage specification.

Rationale: I extract a structured “genome” of the evidence: mechanism, biological niche, observations, limitations, and the evaluative claim. This converts the literature into a compact causal assessment.
```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "PMID:22955699",
    "PMID:25079557",
    "PMID:24882745"
  ],
  "schema": [
    "mechanism_genome",
    "niche_genome",
    "observation_genome",
    "limitation_genome",
    "delta_genome",
    "claim_genome"
  ]
}
```
[result]: Extracted evidence indicates two coupled mechanisms: genome-wide reprogramming of DNA methylation after fertilization, and dynamic chromatin/histone remodeling around zygotic genome activation and lineage allocation. The main limitation is that embryo scarcity, species differences, and perturbation difficulty make it challenging to assign causality to every observed mark; the strongest causal claims come from perturbations of chromatin regulators and maintenance factors at imprints/transposons.

Rationale: I now propose the final evaluation, grounded in the retrieved mouse, human, and chromatin literature. The claim balances importance with limitations: epigenetic modifications are not merely passive marks, but their roles vary from permissive resetting to instructive regulation depending on locus, timing, and modification type.
```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "gene_genome",
  "content": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Early mammalian embryos undergo coordinated epigenetic reprogramming: global DNA demethylation after fertilization resets gamete-derived chromatin states, while selected methylation marks are protected at imprinted differentially methylated regions and some repetitive elements; in parallel, histone modifications, histone variants, nucleosome organization, and chromatin accessibility change rapidly around zygotic genome activation and first lineage specification.",
    "niche_genome": "This mechanism operates during mammalian preimplantation development, from zygote through cleavage, morula, and blastocyst stages, with evidence from mouse embryos (Smith et al. 2012, PMID:22955699), human embryos (Guo et al. 2014, PMID:25079557), and chromatin-focused mammalian embryology synthesis (Burton & Torres-Padilla 2014, PMID:24882745).",
    "observation_genome": "Genome-wide methylome studies show conserved large-scale demethylation in mouse and human early embryos, coupled to preservation of imprints and some transposon-associated methylation. Chromatin studies indicate that histone marks and chromatin plasticity accompany and help regulate zygotic genome activation, totipotency-to-pluripotency transition, and allocation of inner cell mass versus trophectoderm lineages.",
    "limitation_genome": "Much evidence is observational because mammalian embryos are scarce and experimentally difficult to perturb, especially in humans. Mouse-to-human extrapolation is imperfect: demethylation kinetics, regulatory element usage, and developmental timing differ between species. Also, not every epigenetic mark is instructive; some changes may reflect transcriptional state or cell-cycle-linked chromatin remodeling rather than independently driving fate.",
    "delta_genome": "The most defensible update is to treat early embryonic epigenetics as a layered control system: DNA methylation reprogramming provides broad resetting and selective memory preservation, whereas chromatin and histone dynamics provide faster, more local regulation of genome activation and lineage competence. Future causal evaluation needs allele-resolved, single-cell, multi-omic time courses combined with precise perturbation of methylation maintenance, demethylation, and histone-modifying enzymes.",
    "claim_genome": "Epigenetic modifications play an essential but context-dependent role in mammalian early embryonic development. They reset parental epigenetic information to restore developmental potency, preserve necessary epigenetic memory at imprinted and genome-defense loci, and shape transcriptional competence during zygotic genome activation and first lineage decisions; however, their developmental role should be evaluated as a mixture of permissive resetting, protective maintenance, and locus-specific instructive regulation rather than as a single universal causal program."
  }
}
```
[result]: Proposed gene_genome evaluation is schema-complete and grounded in PMID:22955699, PMID:25079557, and PMID:24882745. It evaluates the role of epigenetic modifications as essential, multi-layered, and context-dependent in mammalian early embryonic development.

Since interaction expects answer in final. We need produce final with formatted steps. Could reuse succinct. Ensure "End with propose..." The final needs include all steps.

prompt.full_prompt: Evaluate the role of epigenetic modifications in early embryonic development in mammals.

content:
I first search for high-citation human and mechanistic studies connecting sleep loss with immune endpoints, prioritizing reviews plus primary evidence on innate and adaptive immunity.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"sleep deprivation impaired immune function mechanisms natural killer cells cytokines vaccine antibody response humans","discipline":"biology","limit":5}
```  
[result]: Candidates returned included Besedovsky et al. 2019 Physiol Rev (paper_id: PMID:30920354), Irwin et al. 1994 FASEB J on partial-night deprivation and NK/cellular immunity (paper_id: PMID:8064255), Prather et al. 2012 Sleep on hepatitis B vaccine antibody response (paper_id: PMID:22851802), Cohen et al. 2009 Arch Intern Med on short sleep and rhinovirus susceptibility (paper_id: PMID:19139325), and Lange et al. 2003 Psychosom Med on sleep after hepatitis A vaccination (paper_id: PMID:12563041).

I read the broad mechanistic review to anchor the causal pathways: neuroendocrine circadian disruption, inflammatory signaling, and leukocyte redistribution.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"PMID:30920354","sections":["abstract","sleep and innate immunity","sleep and adaptive immunity","conclusions"]}
```  
[result]: Besedovsky, Lange, and Haack summarize that sleep supports immune defense via nocturnal endocrine conditions—low cortisol/catecholamines and high growth hormone/prolactin—that favor T-cell trafficking, antigen presentation, and memory formation. Sleep loss is described as increasing inflammatory mediators such as IL-6, TNF, and CRP while weakening coordinated antiviral and vaccine responses.

I then read a primary adaptive-immunity study because vaccine response gives a functional, clinically interpretable immune endpoint rather than only cytokine changes.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"PMID:22851802","sections":["abstract","methods","results","discussion"]}
```  
[result]: Prather et al. found that shorter sleep duration around hepatitis B vaccination predicted reduced likelihood of achieving clinically protective antibody titers. The study links habitual insufficient sleep to impaired adaptive humoral immunity, consistent with sleep-dependent support of antigen-specific immune memory.

I extract the mechanistic genome from the retrieved evidence, separating observed immune phenotypes from inferred biological pathways and limitations.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["PMID:30920354","PMID:8064255","PMID:22851802","PMID:19139325","PMID:12563041"],"schema":"gene_genome"}
```  
[result]: Extracted mechanism themes: sleep deprivation activates sympathetic/HPA-axis signaling, disrupts circadian leukocyte trafficking, elevates low-grade inflammation, reduces NK-cell/cellular immune activity, and weakens T/B-cell-dependent vaccine antibody responses. Key limitations were that many human studies are observational or acute-deprivation designs, making it difficult to separate sleep duration, circadian misalignment, stress, and comorbid health behaviors.

I now propose a concise evidence-grounded mechanism genome linking sleep deprivation to impaired immune function.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","content":{"mechanism_genome":"Sleep deprivation impairs immune function through coupled neuroendocrine, circadian, and inflammatory mechanisms. Loss of sleep increases sympathetic tone and HPA-axis/cortisol signaling, disrupting the normal nocturnal endocrine milieu that supports antigen presentation, T-cell trafficking, cytokine coordination, and immunological memory. It also perturbs circadian leukocyte redistribution and produces low-grade inflammatory activation, with elevated IL-6/TNF/CRP-like signals that can coexist with poorer antiviral defense. Functionally, the evidence links insufficient sleep to reduced NK/cellular immune activity and weaker vaccine-induced antibody responses.","niche_genome":"Most relevant to humans experiencing acute total/partial sleep deprivation, chronic short sleep, shift-work-like circadian disruption, vaccination periods, viral exposure, and stress-sensitive immune states. The strongest functional endpoints are antiviral susceptibility and vaccine antibody titers; the strongest mechanistic endpoints are leukocyte trafficking, NK-cell activity, inflammatory cytokines, and endocrine stress markers.","observation_genome":"Retrieved evidence includes a major mechanistic review (PMID:30920354), primary evidence that short sleep predicts reduced hepatitis B vaccine protection (PMID:22851802), classic evidence that partial-night sleep deprivation reduces NK/cellular immune responses (PMID:8064255), epidemiologic evidence that shorter sleep is associated with greater rhinovirus cold susceptibility (PMID:19139325), and vaccination work indicating sleep can enhance antigen-specific antibody memory (PMID:12563041).","limitation_genome":"Causal inference is constrained because chronic short-sleep studies often confound sleep duration with stress, socioeconomic factors, circadian timing, obesity, and health behaviors. Acute deprivation experiments may not generalize to chronic sleep restriction. Immune endpoints vary across studies, and elevated inflammatory markers do not always map directly onto effective pathogen clearance. Mechanistic human evidence remains less granular than animal or in vitro work for cell-specific pathways.","delta_genome":"A decisive next experiment would combine controlled sleep restriction with circadian alignment control, single-cell immune profiling, endocrine sampling, and a standardized vaccine or viral-challenge endpoint. This would test whether sympathetic/HPA-axis activation and disrupted leukocyte trafficking mediate the pathway from sleep loss to reduced antigen-specific T/B-cell responses and poorer protection.","claim_genome":"The best-supported biological claim is that sleep is an active regulator of immune competence: adequate sleep promotes coordinated innate and adaptive responses, whereas sleep deprivation induces stress-axis/circadian dysregulation and sterile inflammatory activation that weakens NK-cell function, antiviral resistance, and vaccine-related adaptive immunity."}}
```  
[result]: Proposed gene_genome is grounded in the retrieved review and primary human studies, with mechanisms and limitations separated from the functional immune observations. 
```final
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Sleep deprivation impairs immune function through coupled neuroendocrine, circadian, and inflammatory mechanisms. Loss of sleep increases sympathetic tone and HPA-axis/cortisol signaling, disrupting the normal nocturnal endocrine milieu that supports antigen presentation, T-cell trafficking, cytokine coordination, and immunological memory. It also perturbs circadian leukocyte redistribution and produces low-grade inflammatory activation, with elevated IL-6/TNF/CRP-like signals that can coexist with poorer antiviral defense. Functionally, the evidence links insufficient sleep to reduced NK/cellular immune activity and weaker vaccine-induced antibody responses.",
  "niche_genome": "Most relevant to humans experiencing acute total/partial sleep deprivation, chronic short sleep, shift-work-like circadian disruption, vaccination periods, viral exposure, and stress-sensitive immune states. The strongest functional endpoints are antiviral susceptibility and vaccine antibody titers; the strongest mechanistic endpoints are leukocyte trafficking, NK-cell activity, inflammatory cytokines, and endocrine stress markers.",
  "observation_genome": "Retrieved evidence includes a major mechanistic review (PMID:30920354), primary evidence that short sleep predicts reduced hepatitis B vaccine protection (PMID:22851802), classic evidence that partial-night sleep deprivation reduces NK/cellular immune responses (PMID:8064255), epidemiologic evidence that shorter sleep is associated with greater rhinovirus cold susceptibility (PMID:19139325), and vaccination work indicating sleep can enhance antigen-specific antibody memory (PMID:12563041).",
  "limitation_genome": "Causal inference is constrained because chronic short-sleep studies often confound sleep duration with stress, socioeconomic factors, circadian timing, obesity, and health behaviors. Acute deprivation experiments may not generalize to chronic sleep restriction. Immune endpoints vary across studies, and elevated inflammatory markers do not always map directly onto effective pathogen clearance. Mechanistic human evidence remains less granular than animal or in vitro work for cell-specific pathways.",
  "delta_genome": "A decisive next experiment would combine controlled sleep restriction with circadian alignment control, single-cell immune profiling, endocrine sampling, and a standardized vaccine or viral-challenge endpoint. This would test whether sympathetic/HPA-axis activation and disrupted leukocyte trafficking mediate the pathway from sleep loss to reduced antigen-specific T/B-cell responses and poorer protection.",
  "claim_genome": "The best-supported biological claim is that sleep is an active regulator of immune competence: adequate sleep promotes coordinated innate and adaptive responses, whereas sleep deprivation induces stress-axis/circadian dysregulation and sterile inflammatory activation that weakens NK-cell function, antiviral resistance, and vaccine-related adaptive immunity."
}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Investigate the biological mechanisms linking sleep deprivation to impaired immune function.

content:
Rationale: I’ll first identify well-cited empirical studies on ocean acidification effects in mollusk larvae, prioritizing papers that connect carbonate chemistry to larval shell growth, survival, and calcification.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "ocean acidification mollusk larvae shell formation carbonate chemistry shell growth survival pCO2 aragonite saturation",
  "discipline": "biology",
  "limit": 5
}
```  
[result]: Found relevant candidates including Talmage & Gobler 2010 `doi:10.1073/pnas.0913804107`, Waldbusser et al. 2015 `doi:10.1038/nclimate2479`, Kurihara et al. 2007 `doi:10.3354/ab00009`, and Gazeau et al. 2013 `doi:10.5194/bg-10-2207-2013`. These papers emphasize impaired larval shellfish growth/survival under elevated CO₂ and the importance of carbonate saturation state.

Rationale: Waldbusser et al. is especially useful because it reframes larval sensitivity around carbonate chemistry thresholds rather than pH alone.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "doi:10.1038/nclimate2479",
  "sections": ["abstract", "results", "discussion"]
}
```  
[result]: Waldbusser et al. 2015 reports that bivalve larvae are highly sensitive to carbonate saturation state during initial shell formation. The study argues that early larval calcification depends strongly on the energetic cost of precipitating calcium carbonate under low saturation conditions.

Rationale: Talmage & Gobler provides comparative evidence across shellfish larvae under past, present, and projected CO₂, useful for designing multi-species experiments.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "doi:10.1073/pnas.0913804107",
  "sections": ["abstract", "methods", "results"]
}
```  
[result]: Talmage & Gobler 2010 found that elevated CO₂ reduced larval growth and survival in several commercially important shellfish species, with more favorable performance under lower preindustrial CO₂ conditions. The paper supports measuring shell size, development rate, and survival across controlled carbonate-chemistry treatments.

Rationale: I’ll check whether a proposed study combining carbonate-threshold experiments, shell microstructure imaging, and molecular responses in mollusk larvae is sufficiently distinct from prior work.  
```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "paper_ids": [
    "doi:10.1038/nclimate2479",
    "doi:10.1073/pnas.0913804107",
    "doi:10.3354/ab00009",
    "doi:10.5194/bg-10-2207-2013"
  ],
  "proposed_focus": "Factorial study of ocean acidification effects on mollusk larval shell formation integrating carbonate saturation thresholds, shell microstructure, calcification rate, and expression of biomineralization genes."
}
```  
[result]: Prior work has established that elevated CO₂ and low saturation state impair larval shellfish growth and survival, but fewer studies integrate precise carbonate threshold mapping with high-resolution shell microstructure and biomineralization-gene responses across multiple larval stages. The proposed plan is novel if it explicitly links carbonate chemistry, structural shell defects, and molecular mechanisms.

Rationale: Based on the retrieved literature, I’ll propose a biology idea_plan focused on mechanistically connecting ocean acidification to larval shell formation defects.  
```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "idea_plan",
  "content": {
    "Idea": "Study how ocean acidification disrupts shell formation in mollusk larvae by experimentally manipulating seawater carbonate chemistry and linking larval growth, survival, calcification, shell microstructure, and biomineralization-gene expression. The central hypothesis is that reduced aragonite saturation state increases the energetic cost of early shell deposition, producing thinner or malformed shells and altered expression of calcification-related genes.",
    "ImplementationSteps": {
      "1_experimental_species_selection": "Select 2-3 ecologically or economically important mollusk species, such as Pacific oyster Crassostrea gigas, hard clam Mercenaria mercenaria, and blue mussel Mytilus edulis. Use newly fertilized embryos and track larvae through early shell-field formation, D-larva stage, and later veliger development.",
      "2_carbonate_chemistry_treatments": "Rear larvae under controlled pCO2/pH treatments representing preindustrial, present-day, near-future, and high-acidification conditions. Measure pH, total alkalinity, dissolved inorganic carbon, pCO2, carbonate ion concentration, and aragonite saturation state throughout the experiment.",
      "3_larval_performance_assays": "Quantify survival, development rate, shell length, shell area, abnormality frequency, and time to D-larva formation under each treatment. These endpoints follow evidence from Talmage & Gobler 2010 that elevated CO2 reduces larval growth and survival.",
      "4_shell_formation_measurements": "Measure calcification using calcium incorporation or alkalinity-anomaly approaches. Use polarized light microscopy, scanning electron microscopy, and micro-CT or confocal imaging to detect shell thinning, dissolution, hinge abnormalities, irregular mineral deposition, and microstructural defects.",
      "5_molecular_response_assays": "Sample larvae at key shell-formation stages for RNA-seq or targeted qPCR of biomineralization-related genes, including carbonic anhydrase, chitin synthase, shell matrix proteins, ion transporters, and calcium-binding proteins.",
      "6_threshold_modeling": "Model biological responses against aragonite saturation state rather than pH alone, following the logic of Waldbusser et al. 2015. Estimate threshold values where shell growth, calcification, or survival decline sharply.",
      "7_integration_analysis": "Integrate physiological, structural, and gene-expression data to determine whether shell defects are best explained by carbonate undersaturation, energetic limitation, altered biomineralization regulation, or a combination of mechanisms."
    },
    "ImplementationOrder": [
      "Acquire broodstock and induce spawning under standardized conditions.",
      "Fertilize embryos and randomly assign larvae to replicated carbonate-chemistry treatments.",
      "Maintain strict water-quality monitoring and carbonate-system measurements.",
      "Collect larvae at early shell-field, D-larva, and veliger stages.",
      "Measure survival, development, shell size, and abnormality rates.",
      "Perform shell imaging and calcification assays.",
      "Extract RNA and measure biomineralization-gene expression.",
      "Fit dose-response and threshold models using aragonite saturation state, pCO2, and pH as predictors.",
      "Identify carbonate-chemistry thresholds associated with shell formation failure.",
      "Compare species-specific sensitivity and infer mechanisms of vulnerability or resilience."
    ],
    "Dataset": "Primary experimental dataset consisting of replicated larval culture measurements across carbonate-chemistry treatments. Data will include seawater chemistry variables, larval survival, developmental stage frequency, shell length and area, shell abnormality scores, calcification rates, microscopy-derived shell microstructure metrics, and RNA-seq or qPCR expression profiles. The study design is grounded in prior findings from Talmage & Gobler 2010 doi:10.1073/pnas.0913804107 and Waldbusser et al. 2015 doi:10.1038/nclimate2479.",
    "EvaluationMetrics": {
      "survival_effect": "Percent survival per treatment and species, analyzed with generalized linear mixed models.",
      "developmental_delay": "Proportion of larvae reaching normal D-larva and veliger stages by fixed time points.",
      "shell_growth": "Mean shell length, shell area, and growth rate across treatments.",
      "shell_abnormality": "Frequency of malformed, incomplete, thinned, or irregularly mineralized shells.",
      "calcification_rate": "Calcium carbonate deposition rate or alkalinity-anomaly-derived calcification per larva.",
      "microstructure_integrity": "Quantitative imaging metrics such as shell thickness, surface pitting, hinge deformity, and mineral organization.",
      "gene_expression_response": "Differential expression of biomineralization and ion-regulation genes under acidified conditions.",
      "carbonate_threshold": "Estimated aragonite saturation state or carbonate ion concentration at which shell formation metrics decline by 10%, 25%, or 50%."
    },
    "ExpectedOutcome": "Larvae exposed to elevated CO2 and reduced aragonite saturation are expected to show lower survival, delayed development, reduced shell growth, thinner or malformed shells, and altered expression of biomineralization genes. The study should identify species-specific carbonate-chemistry thresholds for shell formation failure and clarify whether larval vulnerability is driven primarily by impaired calcium carbonate precipitation, energetic stress, or disrupted regulation of shell-matrix formation."
  }
}
```  
[result]: Proposed an evidence-grounded biology study integrating carbonate chemistry, larval performance, shell imaging, calcification, and molecular biomarkers to investigate ocean acidification effects on mollusk larval shell formation.

prompt.full_prompt: Create an idea_plan for studying the effects of ocean acidification on shell formation in mollusk larvae.
