content:
Rationale: I first need a focused evidence set linking cephalopod RNA editing to neural recoding, conserved coleoid biology, and adaptive environmental response. A search should prioritize primary papers with stable IDs.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"cephalopod RNA editing nervous system adaptation squid octopus A-to-I neural recoding temperature","top_k":5}
```  
[result]: Found core candidates: Alon et al. 2015 eLife, DOI: 10.7554/eLife.05198 / PMID: 25569156; Liscovitch-Brauer et al. 2017 Cell, DOI: 10.1016/j.cell.2017.03.025 / PMID: 28388405; Birk et al. 2023 Cell, DOI: 10.1016/j.cell.2023.05.004 / PMID: 37244292. These directly cover extensive squid neural RNA recoding, conserved coleoid editing, and temperature-responsive octopus neural editing.

Rationale: The squid nervous system paper establishes the baseline mechanism: unusually extensive A-to-I RNA editing that changes protein sequence in neural transcripts. I will read it for quantitative scope and affected functional classes.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.7554/eLife.05198"}
```  
[result]: Alon et al. report that the majority of squid nervous-system transcripts undergo A-to-I editing, with tens of thousands of recoding sites. Editing is enriched in neural genes, including ion channels, transporters, cytoskeletal and synaptic proteins, implying that cephalopods diversify neuronal protein function post-transcriptionally.

Rationale: The Cell comparative paper tests whether this is an isolated squid phenomenon or a conserved coleoid strategy and whether it imposes evolutionary constraints. This is critical for adaptation claims.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1016/j.cell.2017.03.025"}
```  
[result]: Liscovitch-Brauer et al. show extensive recoding RNA editing across behaviorally complex coleoids—octopus, squid, and cuttlefish—but not to the same degree in nautilus or other molluscs. Many editing sites are conserved, especially in neural genes, and edited regions show reduced genomic DNA evolution, supporting a trade-off between transcriptomic plasticity and genome-level change.

Rationale: To connect RNA editing to adaptation rather than only neuronal diversification, I need evidence for environmental modulation with plausible functional consequences. The temperature paper directly addresses this.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_id":"10.1016/j.cell.2023.05.004","fields":["mechanism","niche","observation","limitation","delta","claim"]}
```  
[result]: Birk et al. find that octopus RNA editing is dynamically regulated by temperature, especially in the nervous system, with many recoding sites changing editing levels after cold versus warm acclimation. Edited proteins include neural targets such as ion channels and synaptic machinery; however, functional consequences are experimentally validated for only a subset of sites, leaving many site-specific adaptive effects inferential.

Rationale: The final synthesis should distinguish well-supported claims from open questions and check whether the resulting hypothesis is incremental or a useful integrated claim.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claim":"In coleoid cephalopods, ADAR-mediated RNA editing functions as a conserved, environmentally tunable neural proteome diversification system that supports nervous-system function and short-term adaptation, at the cost of constraining genome evolution."}
```  
[result]: Novelty is moderate as a synthesis, not a new discovery: each component is supported by Alon 2015, Liscovitch-Brauer 2017, and Birk 2023. The integrated framing is useful because it connects neural function, conserved coleoid evolution, temperature acclimation, and evolutionary trade-off in one mechanistic model.

```propose
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Cephalopod nervous systems use ADAR-mediated A-to-I RNA editing to recode mRNAs after transcription, often changing amino acids in neuronal proteins. The edited targets are enriched for ion channels, transporters, synaptic proteins, and cytoskeletal components, allowing one genome to generate multiple neural protein isoforms with potentially different electrophysiological or synaptic properties.",
  "niche_genome": "The phenomenon is strongest in behaviorally complex coleoid cephalopods, including squid, octopus, and cuttlefish, and is especially prominent in nervous tissues. It appears relevant to neural complexity and to environmental acclimation, with octopus temperature-response data showing editing-level shifts under cold versus warm conditions.",
  "observation_genome": "Alon et al. 2015 showed extensive recoding RNA editing across squid nervous-system transcripts. Liscovitch-Brauer et al. 2017 showed that extensive neural recoding is conserved across coleoids and concentrated in behaviorally complex lineages. Birk et al. 2023 showed that octopus neural RNA editing is temperature-sensitive, with many recoding sites changing after acclimation and affecting proteins plausibly involved in neuronal function.",
  "limitation_genome": "Many proposed adaptive effects remain correlative: only a subset of edited sites have been functionally assayed. Editing may also impose costs, because conserved edited regions show reduced genomic evolution, suggesting a trade-off between transcriptomic flexibility and DNA-sequence divergence. Tissue heterogeneity and context-specific ADAR regulation further complicate causal interpretation.",
  "delta_genome": "The strongest next step is site-specific functional validation: combine long-read or single-cell neural transcriptomics, proteomics of edited isoforms, and electrophysiology/CRISPR-style editing-site perturbation in cephalopod neural tissues or heterologous ion-channel systems. Temperature-shift experiments should test whether specific editing changes improve neuronal performance under cold or warm conditions.",
  "claim_genome": "RNA editing contributes to cephalopod nervous-system function by expanding and tuning the neural proteome post-transcriptionally. In coleoids, this editing system likely acts as a rapid adaptive layer, enabling neural proteins to be modulated across tissues and environments, while simultaneously constraining parts of genome evolution that must preserve editable sequences."
}
``` 
   imuhamed
	final
    ???

prompt.full_prompt: Investigate how RNA editing contributes to nervous system function and adaptation in cephalopods.
Study the genomic basis of pesticide resistance in agricultural pest insects. Return a gene_genome output identifying detoxification genes, target-site mutations, and copy number variants.

content:
I first retrieve papers directly linking nutrient stress, root exudate chemistry, and rhizosphere microbiome assembly, prioritizing mechanistic plant–microbe studies over broad reviews.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "plant root exudates nutrient limitation rhizosphere microbiome phosphate iron nitrogen starvation coumarins organic acids",
  "discipline": "biology",
  "limit": 5
}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers included Zhalnina et al. 2018 Nat Microbiol, DOI: `10.1038/s41564-018-0129-3`; Castrillo et al. 2017 Nature, DOI: `10.1038/nature21417`; Stringlis et al. 2018 PNAS, DOI: `10.1073/pnas.1722335115`; Harbort et al. 2020 Cell Host Microbe, DOI: `10.1016/j.chom.2020.09.006`; and Carvalhais et al. 2011 J Plant Nutr Soil Sci, DOI: `10.1002/jpln.201000085`.

I read the broad community-assembly study because it directly connects exudate chemistry, microbial substrate preference, and rhizosphere succession.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "10.1038/s41564-018-0129-3",
  "focus": "evidence that root exudate composition structures rhizosphere microbial communities"
}
```  
[result]: Zhalnina et al. showed that temporal shifts in Avena root exudate chemistry coincided with reproducible rhizosphere microbiome succession. Microbial isolates differed in their ability to use exudate compounds, supporting a substrate-filtering mechanism by which exudates enrich compatible taxa.

I then read nutrient-specific mechanistic studies to ground the assessment in nutrient limitation rather than general rhizosphere effects.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "10.1073/pnas.1722335115",
  "focus": "iron limitation, coumarin exudation, and microbiome shaping"
}
```  
[result]: Stringlis et al. found that Arabidopsis MYB72-dependent secretion of coumarins affected root-associated bacterial community composition. The study supports a mechanism where nutrient-stress-associated specialized metabolites act not only as nutrient-mobilizing compounds but also as selective antimicrobial or signaling molecules.

I extract the cross-paper mechanistic genome, integrating general exudate filtering with phosphate and iron starvation examples.  
```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "10.1038/s41564-018-0129-3",
    "10.1038/nature21417",
    "10.1073/pnas.1722335115",
    "10.1016/j.chom.2020.09.006",
    "10.1002/jpln.201000085"
  ],
  "schema": [
    "mechanism_genome",
    "niche_genome",
    "observation_genome",
    "limitation_genome",
    "delta_genome",
    "claim_genome"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: The extracted pattern is that nutrient limitation changes exudate quantity and composition—organic acids, sugars, amino acids, phenolics, coumarins, and related metabolites—which creates selective chemical niches in the rhizosphere. Strongest causal evidence comes from defined mutants or pathways such as phosphate-starvation microbiota effects and iron-stress coumarin secretion, while broader field-scale generality remains less resolved.

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "gene_genome",
  "content": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Under nutrient limitation, plants reprogram root exudation to mobilize scarce nutrients and recruit or suppress microbes. Phosphate limitation often increases organic acids, phosphatase-associated rhizosphere activity, sugars, and signaling metabolites that favor phosphate-solubilizing or mineralizing taxa; iron limitation induces phenolics and coumarins that both chelate or reduce Fe and selectively inhibit or enrich bacterial groups; nitrogen limitation can alter carbon-rich exudation and amino acid profiles, changing microbial competition for carbon and nitrogen. These exudates shape communities through substrate filtering, chemotaxis, pH and redox modification, antimicrobial activity, metal chelation, and plant immune/nutrient-signaling crosstalk.",
    "niche_genome": "The relevant niche is the root-soil interface under low phosphorus, iron, nitrogen, or combined nutrient stress, especially the narrow rhizosphere gradients around young and mature root zones. Effects depend on plant genotype, developmental stage, soil mineralogy, background microbial pool, and whether the system is sterile synthetic community, pot soil, agricultural field, or natural ecosystem.",
    "observation_genome": "Zhalnina et al. 2018, DOI: 10.1038/s41564-018-0129-3, showed that changing exudate chemistry predicts rhizosphere bacterial succession and that isolates differ in exudate substrate use. Castrillo et al. 2017, DOI: 10.1038/nature21417, linked phosphate starvation signaling with root microbiota composition and plant immunity. Stringlis et al. 2018, DOI: 10.1073/pnas.1722335115, and Harbort et al. 2020, DOI: 10.1016/j.chom.2020.09.006, showed that iron-stress-associated coumarin exudation shapes Arabidopsis root microbiota and can improve iron nutrition. Carvalhais et al. 2011, DOI: 10.1002/jpln.201000085, supports that N, P, and K deficiencies alter root exudate profiles, providing a chemical basis for nutrient-specific microbiome shifts.",
    "limitation_genome": "Many studies remain partly correlative because exudate profiles, nutrient status, and microbiome composition covary with root development and soil conditions. Hydroponic exudate collection can misrepresent soil microsite chemistry, and gnotobiotic or synthetic-community systems may not capture competition in complex field soils. Nutrient limitation rarely occurs in isolation in real soils, making it difficult to assign microbial shifts to one nutrient or one exudate class. Strain-level functional redundancy also means taxonomic enrichment does not always prove improved nutrient acquisition.",
    "delta_genome": "The strongest next step is a factorial experiment comparing nutrient-replete, P-limited, Fe-limited, N-limited, and combined-stress plants across wild type and exudate-pathway mutants, coupled to 13C-labeled exudate tracing, untargeted metabolomics, metagenomics or metatranscriptomics, and defined synthetic communities. This would distinguish whether microbes are enriched because they consume exudates, tolerate antimicrobial exudates, mobilize nutrients, or exploit stress-altered root physiology.",
    "claim_genome": "Plant root exudates are not passive leakage products under nutrient limitation; they are dynamic chemical filters that restructure rhizosphere microbial communities in ways that can enhance nutrient mobilization and stress adaptation. However, exudates act together with soil chemistry, host genotype, immune signaling, and microbial starting pools, so they should be viewed as a major but not exclusive driver of nutrient-stress rhizosphere assembly."
  }
}
```  
[result]: Proposed assessment generated in the requested `gene_genome` schema, grounded in retrieved studies on exudate-driven assembly, phosphate starvation, and iron-limitation coumarin mechanisms.

prompt.full_prompt: Assess how plant root exudates shape rhizosphere microbial communities under nutrient limitation.

content:
I first need to anchor the plan in the current evidence, because probiotic effects after antibiotics are not uniformly beneficial and may depend on host/microbiome context.

```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "probiotic intervention gut microbiome resilience after antibiotic exposure randomized trial microbiome recovery",
  "discipline": "biology",
  "max_results": 5
}
```

[result]: Found relevant candidates including Suez et al. 2018 Cell, PMID:30193113, DOI:10.1016/j.cell.2018.08.047; Korpela et al. 2018 Microbiome, DOI:10.1186/s40168-018-0567-4; and Maldonado-Gomez et al. 2016 Cell Host & Microbe, PMID:27693306, DOI:10.1016/j.chom.2016.09.001. The strongest directly relevant adult post-antibiotic trial is Suez et al.

Suez et al. is important because it challenges the common assumption that probiotics accelerate microbiome recovery after antibiotics.

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "PMID:30193113",
  "focus": "effects of probiotics on gut microbiome recovery after antibiotic exposure"
}
```

[result]: Suez et al. reported that an 11-strain probiotic after broad-spectrum antibiotics led to delayed mucosal and fecal microbiome reconstitution compared with spontaneous recovery, whereas autologous fecal microbiota transplantation rapidly restored the pre-antibiotic state. The study also showed person-specific probiotic colonization patterns, suggesting baseline microbiome features may determine response.

I also want evidence from a setting where probiotics appear beneficial, to avoid designing around only one negative adult study.

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "DOI:10.1186/s40168-018-0567-4",
  "focus": "probiotic supplementation and microbiota restoration after antibiotics"
}
```

[result]: Korpela et al. reported that probiotic supplementation in infants, especially involving Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG-associated intervention, was associated with restoration of microbiota composition and function after antibiotic perturbation. This supports the idea that probiotic benefit may be context-dependent, varying by age, baseline microbiome, antibiotic exposure, and strain choice.

Given mixed evidence, the key novelty should be a personalized, resilience-focused design rather than a generic “probiotic versus placebo” trial.

```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "query": "personalized probiotic consortium baseline microbiome predictors resilience recovery after antibiotics randomized trial multiomics"
}
```

[result]: Existing studies include generic probiotic interventions and autologous FMT after antibiotics, but fewer directly test baseline-informed personalized probiotic selection with dense longitudinal multi-omics and explicit resilience metrics such as time-to-return-to-baseline, resistance gene expansion, and functional pathway recovery. A study comparing generic versus personalized probiotics would address a clear gap.

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "idea_plan",
  "prompt": "Create an idea_plan for examining whether probiotic interventions improve resilience of the gut microbiome after antibiotic exposure.",
  "grounding_paper_ids": [
    "PMID:30193113",
    "DOI:10.1186/s40168-018-0567-4",
    "DOI:10.1016/j.chom.2016.09.001"
  ]
}
```

[result]:

```json
{
  "Idea": "Test whether probiotics improve gut microbiome resilience after antibiotic exposure by comparing placebo, generic probiotics, and baseline-informed personalized probiotic consortia in a longitudinal randomized controlled study. The central hypothesis is that probiotics do not uniformly improve recovery; instead, resilience depends on strain engraftment compatibility with the pre-antibiotic microbiome, host context, and antibiotic-induced ecological vacancy.",
  "ImplementationSteps": {
    "StudyDesign": "Conduct a randomized, placebo-controlled, longitudinal trial in adults receiving a standard short course of broad-spectrum antibiotics. Randomize participants after antibiotic completion into three arms: placebo, commercially available multi-strain probiotic, and personalized probiotic consortium selected based on baseline microbiome composition and predicted engraftment compatibility.",
    "BaselineProfiling": "Collect stool samples before antibiotics, immediately after antibiotics, and repeatedly during recovery. Profile baseline microbiome composition using shotgun metagenomics, quantify strain-level taxa, functional pathways, antimicrobial resistance genes, and short-chain fatty acid production potential.",
    "Intervention": "Administer probiotics for 2 to 4 weeks after antibiotic exposure. The generic arm receives the same fixed probiotic formulation for all participants. The personalized arm receives a consortium chosen to complement depleted baseline taxa or functions while avoiding strains predicted to block native community reconstitution.",
    "SamplingSchedule": "Collect stool at pre-antibiotic baseline, post-antibiotic day 0, then days 3, 7, 14, 28, 56, and 90. Optionally collect mucosal samples in a smaller consenting subcohort, because Suez et al. showed that fecal and mucosal recovery can differ.",
    "MultiOmicsAssays": "Use shotgun metagenomics for taxonomic and functional recovery, metatranscriptomics or targeted metabolomics in a subset for functional activity, and qPCR or metagenomic profiling for antimicrobial resistance gene burden.",
    "EngraftmentAnalysis": "Track probiotic strains using strain-resolved metagenomics to distinguish transient passage from true engraftment. Model whether engraftment correlates with faster recovery, delayed native microbiome reconstitution, or no effect.",
    "ResilienceModeling": "Define microbiome resilience as return toward each participant's own pre-antibiotic baseline rather than convergence to a population average. Estimate recovery trajectories using ecological distance metrics, time-to-baseline models, and mixed-effects longitudinal models.",
    "PredictorDiscovery": "Identify baseline predictors of benefit or harm, including alpha diversity, abundance of related resident strains, metabolic niche availability, antibiotic-induced depletion magnitude, diet, age, and stool consistency.",
    "Validation": "Validate key findings in an independent observational cohort or a smaller second randomized cohort, focusing on whether baseline features predict who benefits from probiotics versus who experiences delayed recovery."
  },
  "ImplementationOrder": [
    "StudyDesign",
    "BaselineProfiling",
    "Intervention",
    "SamplingSchedule",
    "MultiOmicsAssays",
    "EngraftmentAnalysis",
    "ResilienceModeling",
    "PredictorDiscovery",
    "Validation"
  ],
  "Dataset": "A prospective longitudinal randomized trial dataset containing repeated stool metagenomes from adults before and after antibiotic exposure, assigned to placebo, generic probiotic, or personalized probiotic arms. Core data would include taxonomic profiles, strain-level probiotic tracking, functional pathway abundances, antimicrobial resistance gene profiles, metadata on antibiotic type, diet, age, sex, medication use, bowel habits, and clinical symptoms. An optional mucosal biopsy subcohort would enable comparison of fecal versus mucosal recovery.",
  "EvaluationMetrics": {
    "PrimaryMetric": "Time to microbiome recovery, defined as return to within a prespecified ecological distance of the individual's own pre-antibiotic baseline using Bray-Curtis dissimilarity, Aitchison distance, and strain-level similarity.",
    "SecondaryMetrics": [
      "Alpha diversity recovery rate",
      "Recovery of depleted commensal taxa",
      "Recovery of functional pathways such as fiber fermentation and short-chain fatty acid production",
      "Magnitude and duration of probiotic strain engraftment",
      "Expansion or contraction of antimicrobial resistance genes",
      "Incidence and duration of gastrointestinal symptoms",
      "Fraction of participants whose recovery is delayed relative to placebo"
    ],
    "StatisticalAnalysis": "Use mixed-effects models for longitudinal recovery trajectories, survival analysis for time-to-baseline recovery, permutation-based ecological distance testing, and interaction models to test whether baseline microbiome features modify probiotic effects.",
    "SuccessCriterion": "A probiotic strategy would be considered resilience-enhancing if it significantly shortens time-to-baseline recovery, restores functional pathways, and does not increase antimicrobial resistance gene burden or delay native microbiome reconstitution compared with placebo."
  },
  "ExpectedOutcome": "The study is expected to show that generic probiotics have heterogeneous effects and may help some participants while delaying recovery in others, consistent with Suez et al. In contrast, a baseline-informed personalized probiotic approach may improve resilience in participants with compatible ecological niches and severe antibiotic-induced depletion. The main deliverable would be a predictive framework for when probiotics should be used after antibiotics, when they should be avoided, and which microbiome features forecast benefit versus impaired recovery."
}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Create an idea_plan for examining whether probiotic interventions improve resilience of the gut microbiome after antibiotic exposure.

Investigate how urban heat islands influence microbial diversity in public parks, combining urban planning, climate science, and metagenomics. Provide a gene_genome output identifying candidate microbial genes associated with heat tolerance.
Develop an idea_plan for using wearable sensors, behavioral economics, and public health outreach to reduce sedentary behavior among remote workers.
Explore how Indigenous ecological knowledge and satellite-based remote sensing can be integrated to improve wildfire risk prediction in forested regions.

content:
I’ll ground the stress-resilience hypothesis in papers that directly connect microbiota to HPA/cortisol physiology, then translate the evidence into microbial pathway-level hypotheses suitable for sequencing + nutrition + computational modeling.

Rationale: First, identify foundational and translational gut-brain-axis studies linking microbiota, probiotics/prebiotics, stress, and cortisol/HPA-axis outcomes.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "gut microbiota stress resilience cortisol HPA axis probiotics prebiotics human microbial pathways",
  "top_k": 5
}
```  
[result]: Retrieved candidates included Sudo et al. 2004 on germ-free mice and HPA-axis programming, Messaoudi et al. 2011 on Lactobacillus/Bifidobacterium effects on psychological distress and urinary cortisol, and Schmidt et al. 2015 on prebiotics lowering waking cortisol response. Key paper_ids: PMID:15133062, PMID:20974015, PMID:25449699.

Rationale: Read the most relevant mechanistic animal paper and two human intervention papers to anchor the pathway hypotheses in both causality and translational cortisol outcomes.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_ids": [
    "PMID:15133062",
    "PMID:20974015",
    "PMID:25449699"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: Sudo et al. showed germ-free mice have exaggerated ACTH/corticosterone responses to restraint stress, partially normalized by Bifidobacterium infantis during a developmental window. Messaoudi et al. reported that L. helveticus R0052 + B. longum R0175 reduced psychological distress and urinary free cortisol in humans. Schmidt et al. found that B-GOS prebiotic intake reduced waking cortisol response and altered emotional attention bias.

Rationale: Extract a pathway-level “genome” from these studies, focusing on microbial functions plausibly measurable by shotgun metagenomics/metatranscriptomics and diet-responsive through nutrition.  
```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "PMID:15133062",
    "PMID:20974015",
    "PMID:25449699"
  ],
  "focus": "microbial pathways potentially linked to cortisol regulation and stress resilience"
}
```  
[result]: Candidate mechanisms clustered around SCFA production from fermentable fiber, Bifidobacterium/Lactobacillus-associated psychobiotic effects, tryptophan/indole metabolism, GABAergic signaling potential, epithelial-barrier and immune modulation, and developmental calibration of HPA-axis reactivity.

Rationale: Check novelty by comparing the proposed integration against common single-modality designs; emphasize the contribution of combining psychology, sequencing, nutrition, cortisol phenotyping, and computational modeling.  
```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "claim": "A multimodal nutrition-microbiome-psychology model can predict stress resilience and cortisol dynamics from microbial pathway abundance, diet-responsive fermentation capacity, and psychological stress phenotypes."
}
```  
[result]: Existing studies usually test probiotics/prebiotics or describe microbiome-stress associations separately. The proposed novelty is the explicit pathway-resolved model linking diet, microbial metabolic capacity, longitudinal cortisol dynamics, and psychological resilience outcomes.

Rationale: Propose the final gene_genome output as microbial pathway hypotheses linked to cortisol regulation, grounded in the retrieved evidence.  
```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "gene_genome",
  "output": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Diet-responsive gut microbial functions may regulate cortisol/HPA-axis activity through short-chain fatty acid production, Bifidobacterium/Lactobacillus-associated immune and barrier modulation, tryptophan-indole metabolism, and possible GABAergic signaling. Evidence from germ-free mice shows microbiota are required for normal stress-axis calibration, while human probiotic and prebiotic studies suggest microbial modulation can reduce urinary or waking cortisol responses.",
    "niche_genome": "Best suited to longitudinal human stress-resilience cohorts or controlled nutrition interventions combining validated psychological stress/resilience scales, salivary or urinary cortisol profiles, diet records, shotgun metagenomic or metatranscriptomic sequencing, and computational modeling. Target populations could include students, caregivers, shift workers, or trauma-exposed individuals with variable stress resilience.",
    "observation_genome": "Key observations to test include whether higher abundance or expression of carbohydrate fermentation and SCFA biosynthesis pathways predicts flatter maladaptive cortisol awakening responses, whether Bifidobacterium-enriched prebiotic responders show improved emotional bias or perceived stress, and whether Lactobacillus/Bifidobacterium psychobiotic exposure shifts cortisol together with anxiety/depression scores.",
    "limitation_genome": "Current evidence is heterogeneous: animal HPA-axis findings may not directly translate to human cortisol regulation, human trials often have modest sample sizes, strain-specific probiotic effects are hard to generalize, and cortisol is sensitive to sleep, circadian timing, medication, sex hormones, inflammation, and recent stress exposure. Pathway inference from metagenomics also requires validation with metabolomics.",
    "delta_genome": "The key advance is to move from taxon-level gut-brain associations to pathway-resolved causal modeling: combine nutrition-driven substrate availability, microbial functional capacity, fecal/plasma metabolites, and repeated cortisol sampling in one model. This enables prediction of who is likely to become more stress-resilient after fiber/prebiotic/psychobiotic interventions.",
    "claim_genome": "Microbial pathways involved in fermentable-fiber utilization, SCFA production, tryptophan-indole metabolism, GABA-related metabolism, and mucosal immune/barrier regulation are plausible functional mediators of cortisol regulation and stress resilience. A combined psychology-microbiome-nutrition-computational framework can identify pathway signatures that predict HPA-axis flexibility rather than merely cataloging microbiome composition."
  }
}
```  
[result]: Proposed a pathway-level gene_genome hypothesis linking gut microbial metabolic capacity to cortisol regulation and stress resilience, grounded in PMID:15133062, PMID:20974015, and PMID:25449699.

prompt.full_prompt: Investigate the gut-brain axis in stress resilience by combining psychology, microbiome sequencing, nutrition, and computational modeling. Include a gene_genome output for microbial pathways potentially linked to cortisol regulation.

content:
Rationale: I first search for interdisciplinary evidence connecting AI/remote sensing crop monitoring with smallholder adoption, productivity, equity, and data governance, prioritizing reviews and agenda-setting papers that can support cross-domain synthesis.  
```action search
{
  "query": "AI crop monitoring smallholder farming precision agriculture ethics economics data governance review",
  "top_k": 6
}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers included Wolfert et al. 2017 “Big Data in Smart Farming – A review” (DOI: 10.1016/j.agsy.2017.01.023), Mehrabi et al. 2021 “The global divide in data-driven farming” (DOI: 10.1038/s41893-021-00740-6), Deichmann et al. 2016 “Will digital technologies transform agriculture in developing countries?” (DOI: 10.1111/agec.12300), Bronson 2019 on responsible innovation and digital agriculture (DOI: 10.1016/j.njas.2019.03.001), and Kamilaris & Prenafeta-Boldú 2018 deep learning survey (DOI: 10.1016/j.compag.2018.02.016).  

Rationale: I read the global-divide paper because it directly addresses how data-driven farming can reproduce inequities across regions and farm scales—central to smallholder impacts and governance.  
```action read
{
  "paper_id": "10.1038/s41893-021-00740-6",
  "sections": [
    "Abstract",
    "Main",
    "Policy implications"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: Mehrabi et al. argue that digital agriculture benefits are constrained by unequal access to connectivity, data, computation, capital, skills, and institutional capacity. They emphasize that without inclusive infrastructure and governance, data-driven farming may widen rather than close productivity and income gaps.  

Rationale: I read a foundational smart-farming review to ground the technical mechanism: AI crop monitoring depends on sensing, analytics, decision-support integration, and data-sharing architectures.  
```action read
{
  "paper_id": "10.1016/j.agsy.2017.01.023",
  "sections": [
    "Abstract",
    "Big data applications",
    "Challenges",
    "Conclusions"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: Wolfert et al. describe smart farming as an ecosystem of sensors, remote sensing, farm-management systems, predictive analytics, and decision support. Key barriers include interoperability, data quality, privacy, ownership, business models, and the risk that platform power shifts value away from farmers.  

Rationale: I extract a genome from the evidence to separate mechanism, context, empirical observations, limitations, the interdisciplinary delta, and the defensible claim.  
```action extract_genome
{
  "paper_ids": [
    "10.1038/s41893-021-00740-6",
    "10.1016/j.agsy.2017.01.023",
    "10.1111/agec.12300",
    "10.1016/j.njas.2019.03.001"
  ],
  "schema": [
    "mechanism_genome",
    "niche_genome",
    "observation_genome",
    "limitation_genome",
    "delta_genome",
    "claim_genome"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: AI crop monitoring can improve agronomic decisions through timely detection of stress, pests, disease, water deficits, and yield risks, but effects depend on extension support, affordability, trust, local calibration, and institutional rules for data access. The main gap is not technical feasibility alone, but whether monitoring systems are governed so that smallholders gain actionable advice, bargaining power, and privacy rather than becoming raw data suppliers to platforms.  

Rationale: I check novelty by comparing the emerging synthesis against conventional “AI boosts productivity” narratives and responsible-innovation critiques, looking for a sharper interdisciplinary claim.  
```action novelty_check
{
  "claim": "AI-driven crop monitoring affects smallholder communities through a coupled agronomy-economics-governance pathway: sensor-derived advice changes input timing and risk management, but benefits are mediated by affordability, local calibration, cooperative data rights, and platform accountability.",
  "against_paper_ids": [
    "10.1038/s41893-021-00740-6",
    "10.1016/j.agsy.2017.01.023",
    "10.1016/j.njas.2019.03.001",
    "10.1111/agec.12300"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: The synthesis is moderately novel as an integrated framing: the component concerns are established, but combining agronomic performance, household economics, ethics, and data governance into a causal pathway for smallholder community effects is less commonly stated as a unified assessment framework.  

```action propose
{
  "mechanism_genome": "AI-driven crop monitoring uses satellite/drone imagery, phone-based scouting, weather data, sensors, and machine-learning models to detect crop stress, pests, disease, water deficits, nutrient problems, and yield risks earlier than periodic human observation. If translated into locally intelligible advice, this can improve timing of irrigation, fertilizer, pesticide use, harvesting, storage, credit, and insurance decisions.",
  "niche_genome": "The highest-stakes niche is smallholder farming in low- and middle-income regions where yields are often constrained by climate volatility, weak extension services, fragmented markets, limited capital, and uneven connectivity. Community-level impacts will differ by crop, gendered access to phones and land, literacy, cooperative strength, data infrastructure, and whether AI tools are bundled with agronomic support, finance, input suppliers, or government programs.",
  "observation_genome": "The literature supports both opportunity and risk: smart-farming reviews identify sensing-plus-analytics as a route to better decision support, while digital-divide work shows that benefits are limited by access to data, compute, skills, connectivity, and institutions. Economically, monitoring may reduce input waste, crop losses, and uncertainty, but may also impose subscription costs, dependence on vendors, and exposure to price discrimination if farm data are captured by platforms. Ethically, systems can improve resilience and food security when inclusive, but can also deepen exclusion if models are poorly calibrated for local crops, marginal soils, intercropping systems, women farmers, tenant farmers, or indigenous practices.",
  "limitation_genome": "Core limitations are model transferability, sparse ground-truth data, opaque recommendations, weak broadband, low ability to pay, lack of repair/support ecosystems, and misalignment between AI alerts and farmers' actual liquidity or labor constraints. Data governance is a central limitation: unclear ownership, consent, secondary use, data portability, collective bargaining, and accountability can shift value from smallholders to agribusiness, insurers, lenders, or platform intermediaries.",
  "delta_genome": "The interdisciplinary delta is to evaluate AI crop monitoring not as a standalone agronomic technology but as a socio-technical institution. A fair assessment should combine field-level accuracy and yield effects with household welfare, debt risk, gender equity, community autonomy, local knowledge integration, and enforceable data rights. Cooperative or public-interest data trusts, participatory model validation, transparent recommendation logic, benefit-sharing, and interoperable open standards are likely as important as algorithmic accuracy.",
  "claim_genome": "AI-driven crop monitoring can benefit smallholder communities when it is locally calibrated, affordable, explainable, paired with human extension, and governed through farmer-centered data rights. Without those conditions, the same technology may increase surveillance, dependency, exclusion, and platform concentration while delivering uneven agronomic gains."
}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Analyze how AI-driven crop monitoring could affect smallholder farming communities, integrating agronomy, ethics, economics, and data governance.
Design an idea_plan for evaluating biodiversity recovery after dam removal in river ecosystems.

content:
rationale: I first search for empirical anchors that directly connect music training/aptitude with adult L2 phonology, plus mechanistic neuroplasticity reviews to justify neural measures. This should prevent an over-broad proposal and identify candidate paper_ids for grounding.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"adult music training neuroplasticity second language acquisition lexical tone learning musicians DOI"}
```  
[result]: Found candidates including Kraus & Chandrasekaran 2010 Nat Rev Neurosci (DOI: 10.1038/nrn2882), Wong et al. 2007 Nat Neurosci (DOI: 10.1038/nn1872), Herholz & Zatorre 2012 Neuron (DOI: 10.1016/j.neuron.2012.10.011), and Slevc & Miyake 2006 Psychological Science (DOI: 10.1111/j.1467-9280.2006.01765.x). These cover auditory plasticity, brainstem encoding of linguistic pitch, and music–L2 individual differences.  

rationale: I read the most mechanistic paper on neural encoding because it directly links musical experience to processing linguistic pitch patterns, a key SLA bottleneck for adult tone-language learning.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1038/nn1872"}
```  
[result]: Wong et al. reported that musicians showed more faithful subcortical encoding of Mandarin lexical pitch contours than non-musicians, despite the stimuli being linguistic rather than musical. The result supports a shared auditory-pitch plasticity mechanism relevant to adult acquisition of phonological contrasts.  

rationale: I next read the adult individual-differences evidence to identify measurable L2 outcomes beyond pitch encoding, such as phonology, grammar, and vocabulary.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1111/j.1467-9280.2006.01765.x"}
```  
[result]: Slevc & Miyake found that musical ability predicted adult L2 phonological proficiency even after accounting for age of arrival/exposure and other cognitive variables, while links to syntax/semantics were weaker. This suggests the strongest expected transfer is in perception/production of L2 sound categories rather than global language learning.  

rationale: I extract a compact genome from these papers and reviews so the proposed experiment separates mechanism, niche, observations, limitations, and a testable delta.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["10.1038/nn1872","10.1111/j.1467-9280.2006.01765.x","10.1038/nrn2882","10.1016/j.neuron.2012.10.011"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = experience-dependent auditory plasticity in cortico-subcortical pitch/timing networks; niche = adult learners facing L2 phonetic/phonological contrasts, especially lexical tone; observation = musical expertise/ability predicts better neural encoding and L2 pronunciation-related outcomes; limitation = much evidence is correlational or compares pre-existing musicians to non-musicians.  

rationale: I check whether the useful next step is novel enough: a randomized adult intervention combining music training and L2 learning with neural and behavioral endpoints, rather than another musician/non-musician comparison.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"Randomized controlled adult intervention testing whether short-term pitch/rhythm music training enhances Mandarin lexical-tone learning and broader L2 outcomes, with EEG/FFR neuroplasticity, behavioral perception-production, working memory, and retention measures.","against_paper_ids":["10.1038/nn1872","10.1111/j.1467-9280.2006.01765.x","10.1038/nrn2882","10.1016/j.neuron.2012.10.011"]}
```  
[result]: Novelty appears moderate-to-high: prior work supports music–language links but is often cross-sectional or focused on children/expert musicians. The gap is causal evidence in adults with active controls and multi-level outcomes spanning neural encoding, cognitive mediators, and L2 acquisition/retention.  

```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","content":{"mechanism_genome":"Test whether adult music training improves second-language acquisition through experience-dependent auditory neuroplasticity, especially enhanced pitch-contour, timing, and speech-in-noise encoding in cortico-subcortical auditory pathways. Use pre/post EEG frequency-following response or auditory brainstem response to Mandarin tone contours, cortical mismatch negativity/P300 to novel phonemic contrasts, and optional fMRI/MEG auditory-motor connectivity measures.","niche_genome":"Adult native speakers of a non-tonal language beginning Mandarin or another tone-rich L2; randomize non-musicians to: (1) pitch-focused music training, (2) rhythm-focused music training, (3) active nonmusical auditory control, and ideally (4) L2-only control. Pair all groups with identical Mandarin instruction to isolate whether music training adds transfer beyond exposure.","observation_genome":"Grounding evidence: Wong et al. 2007 (10.1038/nn1872) showed musicians have stronger brainstem encoding of linguistic pitch patterns; Slevc & Miyake 2006 (10.1111/j.1467-9280.2006.01765.x) found musical ability predicts adult L2 phonological proficiency; Kraus & Chandrasekaran 2010 (10.1038/nrn2882) and Herholz & Zatorre 2012 (10.1016/j.neuron.2012.10.011) frame music training as a driver of auditory neuroplasticity. Expected behavioral outcomes should be largest for L2 phonetic perception and pronunciation, smaller for vocabulary/grammar unless mediated by better speech encoding.","limitation_genome":"Key threats are pre-existing musician advantages, expectancy/placebo effects, unequal motivation, and general cognitive confounds. Address them with random assignment, active controls matched for time/social engagement, baseline musical aptitude and IQ/working-memory covariates, blinded speech ratings, preregistered endpoints, and delayed retention tests. A short intervention may not alter broad L2 grammar, so the primary outcome should not be global fluency.","delta_genome":"Experimental method: run an 8-12 week longitudinal RCT with 3 sessions/week music/control training plus standardized L2 lessons. Measure at baseline, mid, post, and 1-3 month follow-up: Mandarin tone identification/discrimination, phoneme category learning, word learning, sentence comprehension in noise, pronunciation accuracy by native-rater and acoustic F0-contour metrics, auditory working memory, phonological short-term memory, attention, and neural indices including FFR fidelity, MMN amplitude/latency, and learning-related changes in auditory-motor coupling. Model mediation: music-training-induced neural change -> improved phonological perception -> improved vocabulary/pronunciation retention.","claim_genome":"A decisive causal claim would be: in adults, targeted music training can enhance L2 acquisition primarily by sharpening domain-general auditory encoding and phonological category learning, with measurable neuroplasticity predicting gains in tone perception, pronunciation, speech-in-noise comprehension, and word retention. The strongest test is whether pitch-focused training outperforms rhythm/control groups specifically on pitch-based L2 contrasts while rhythm training preferentially predicts temporal/prosodic outcomes."}}
``` 
[result]: Proposal generated in the requested genome format, grounded in the retrieved music-neuroplasticity and adult L2 evidence.

prompt.full_prompt: Study the relationship between music training, neuroplasticity, and second-language acquisition in adults. Propose experimental methods and measurable cognitive outcomes.
Compare developmental gene expression patterns underlying caste differentiation in social insects. Include gene_genome output describing key regulatory genes and epigenetic markers.
Design a research framework for biodegradable electronics inspired by fungal mycelium networks and flexible circuit engineering.

content:
Rationale: I’ll first identify empirical anchors spanning classroom acoustics (noise/reverberation/SNR) and cognitive neuroscience of attention-memory, prioritizing review or field-study papers that can support design claims.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"classroom acoustics noise reverberation attention memory learning children cognitive neuroscience review speech intelligibility","discipline":"interdisciplinary","limit":5}
```  
[result]: Found candidates: `10.3389/fpsyg.2013.00578` (Klatte et al., 2013 review on noise and learning), `10.1121/1.2812596` (Shield & Dockrell, 2008 field study on classroom/environmental noise and attainment), `10.1177/0013916509336813` (Klatte et al., 2010 field study on classroom acoustics and children’s performance/well-being), `10.1016/j.conb.2007.03.006` (Chun & Turk-Browne, 2007 attention-learning-memory neuroscience review), and `10.1016/j.tics.2010.05.004` (Söderlund et al./noise-cognition style candidate on stochastic resonance was less directly classroom-specific).  

Rationale: The Klatte et al. review is a strong interdisciplinary bridge because it summarizes mechanisms by which irrelevant sound, chronic noise, and poor speech perception impair learning-relevant cognition.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.3389/fpsyg.2013.00578","focus":"mechanisms linking noise, reverberation, speech intelligibility, attention, working memory, reading and memory in classrooms"}
```  
[result]: Klatte, Bergström & Lachmann (2013) report that noise harms children’s listening comprehension, memory for spoken material, reading-related tasks, and well-being, with younger children and children with language/hearing vulnerabilities especially affected. The review distinguishes energetic masking, informational masking, and cognitive load: degraded speech forces more resources into decoding, leaving fewer resources for encoding and memory consolidation.  

Rationale: I’ll read a field-study anchor to ground the design variables in measurable architectural-acoustic parameters rather than only cognitive theory.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1177/0013916509336813","focus":"classroom acoustic parameters, reverberation, noise, children performance, field intervention implications"}
```  
[result]: Klatte, Hellbrück, Seidel & Leistner (2010) found that classroom acoustics are associated with children’s speech perception, task performance, and well-being in real elementary classrooms. The evidence supports reducing reverberation and background noise to improve signal-to-noise ratio, especially because children are less able than adults to compensate for degraded auditory input.  

Rationale: I’ll add a cognitive-neuroscience synthesis so the proposal links architectural choices to attention gating, encoding, and memory retention rather than treating acoustics as mere comfort.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1016/j.conb.2007.03.006","focus":"attention as a gateway to learning and memory; implications for distracting auditory environments"}
```  
[result]: Chun & Turk-Browne (2007) argue that attention modulates what information is encoded and later remembered, while distraction competes for limited processing capacity. This supports a classroom-design inference: acoustic environments that reduce involuntary orienting and listening effort should free attentional resources for semantic encoding and later retrieval.  

Rationale: I’ll extract a compact evidence genome before proposing a design hypothesis, preserving mechanism, niche, observations, limits, and the actionable delta.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["10.3389/fpsyg.2013.00578","10.1177/0013916509336813","10.1016/j.conb.2007.03.006"],"schema":"gene_genome"}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = poor acoustics increase masking, listening effort, distraction, and attentional capture, reducing encoding resources; niche = primary/secondary classrooms, especially younger or vulnerable learners; observation = noise/reverberation correlate with worse speech perception, task performance, learning, and well-being; limitation = many studies are correlational or short-term and under-integrate neurocognitive measures; delta = combine acoustic retrofits with attention/memory-sensitive classroom layouts and evaluate cognition directly.  

```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","proposal":{"mechanism_genome":"Architectural acoustics can improve attention and memory by reducing energetic/informational masking, reverberant smearing of speech, involuntary auditory orienting, and listening effort. Cognitive-neuroscience evidence frames this as an attention-allocation problem: when students spend fewer resources decoding degraded speech or suppressing distractors, more resources remain for semantic encoding and later retrieval.","niche_genome":"Design of learning spaces for elementary and secondary classrooms, with highest expected benefit for younger children, second-language learners, students with hearing/language/attention vulnerabilities, and classrooms using speech-heavy instruction or collaborative learning.","observation_genome":"Klatte et al. 2013 (10.3389/fpsyg.2013.00578) synthesize evidence that noise impairs children’s listening comprehension, memory for spoken material, reading-related performance, and well-being. Klatte et al. 2010 (10.1177/0013916509336813) ground this in field classroom acoustics, showing that reverberation/noise relate to speech perception, task performance, and well-being. Chun & Turk-Browne 2007 (10.1016/j.conb.2007.03.006) support the neurocognitive link: attention gates learning and determines what is encoded into memory.","limitation_genome":"The evidence base strongly supports reducing noise and reverberation, but fewer studies run randomized classroom retrofits with longitudinal memory outcomes, neurocognitive attention measures, and realistic modern teaching modes. Architectural standards often target speech intelligibility, while neuroscience-informed outcomes such as sustained attention, listening effort, delayed recall, and cognitive fatigue are less routinely measured.","delta_genome":"A neuroscience-informed classroom-acoustic design should combine: low reverberation time via absorptive ceilings/walls; high speech transmission and teacher-student signal-to-noise ratio; quiet HVAC and exterior-noise isolation; zoned acoustic treatments for group work; reduced unpredictable noise events; and visual-spatial layouts that support orienting to the teacher without forcing high listening effort. Evaluation should pair acoustic metrics with attention and memory metrics such as on-task behavior, pupillometry or EEG/ERP indices of listening effort where feasible, immediate comprehension, delayed recall, and academic transfer.","claim_genome":"Classrooms designed as attentional-support environments—not merely as quieter rooms—should improve memory retention by preserving students’ limited attentional capacity for encoding instructional content. The most defensible design claim is that reducing reverberation and background noise improves speech intelligibility and lowers listening effort; the research opportunity is to test whether these acoustic gains causally produce durable improvements in attention, delayed recall, and classroom learning."}}
```  
[result]: Proposed gene_genome grounded in retrieved evidence.

prompt.full_prompt: Research how architectural acoustics and cognitive neuroscience can inform the design of classrooms that improve attention and memory retention.

content:
I’ll ground the exhibit concept in evidence on ancient hydraulic engineering and climate stress, then translate those findings into an AR-based museum plan.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"ancient water management systems archaeology climate change augmented reality museum exhibit hydraulic engineering Maya Indus Greece peer reviewed","top_k":5}
```  
[result]: Candidates included Kennett et al. 2012 in *Science* on Maya political change and drought (`doi:10.1126/science.1226299`), Angelakis et al. 2005 in *Water Research* on ancient Greek stormwater/wastewater systems (`doi:10.1016/j.watres.2004.08.033`), and Lentz et al. 2014 in *PNAS* on Tikal sustainability and environmental management (`doi:10.1073/pnas.1408631111`). These give a useful triad: climate proxy evidence, engineered water infrastructure, and coupled society-environment systems.

To connect climate science to visitor-facing narrative, I’ll read the high-confidence climate-archaeology paper on drought and sociopolitical change.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"doi:10.1126/science.1226299"}
```  
[result]: Kennett et al. use a precisely dated Belize speleothem record to reconstruct rainfall variability across Maya history. The paper links wetter intervals with expansion and severe multi-decadal droughts with political fragmentation, making it useful for an exhibit station showing how climate proxies are interpreted.

To ground the “how the systems worked” layer, I’ll read a paper focused on ancient hydraulic infrastructure and design logic.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"doi:10.1016/j.watres.2004.08.033"}
```  
[result]: Angelakis et al. document ancient Greek urban water technologies including drainage, stormwater handling, wastewater conveyance, and sanitation planning. The paper supports interactive explanations of gradients, channels, cisterns, overflow, sedimentation, and maintenance as design principles rather than isolated artifacts.

I’ll extract transferable design genes from the evidence so the exhibit idea remains interdisciplinary rather than just a tech overlay.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["doi:10.1126/science.1226299","doi:10.1016/j.watres.2004.08.033","doi:10.1073/pnas.1408631111"],"focus":"museum exhibit combining archaeology, augmented reality, climate science, ancient water-management systems"}
```  
[result]: Extracted genes: use real climate proxies as story engines; make invisible water flows visible through layered reconstructions; compare multiple civilizations to avoid single-cause collapse narratives; include tradeoffs between engineering capacity, maintenance, governance, drought frequency, and urban demand. The most exhibit-ready gap is a participatory AR simulation where visitors alter rainfall and maintenance decisions and see consequences across centuries.

```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"idea_plan","context":"Create an idea_plan for a museum exhibit that combines archaeology, augmented reality, and climate science to explain ancient water-management systems. Grounding sources: Kennett et al. 2012 Science doi:10.1126/science.1226299; Angelakis et al. 2005 Water Research doi:10.1016/j.watres.2004.08.033; Lentz et al. 2014 PNAS doi:10.1073/pnas.1408631111."}
```  
[result]: Proposed below.  

{
  "Idea": "“Water Under Time”: an interactive museum exhibit where visitors use augmented reality to uncover, operate, and stress-test ancient water-management systems—such as Maya reservoirs and Greek urban drainage—while climate-science overlays show how rainfall variability, drought, maintenance, and governance shaped their success or failure. The core experience turns static artifacts into dynamic systems: visitors point tablets or AR glasses at models, maps, sherds, channel fragments, or reconstructed landscapes to see invisible flows, storage levels, sediment buildup, contamination risk, and drought impacts over decades.",
  "ImplementationSteps": {
    "1_Research_and_Curation": "Select 3–4 case studies with strong archaeological and climate evidence: Classic Maya reservoir landscapes, ancient Greek stormwater/wastewater systems, and optionally Indus or Roman examples. Use climate-proxy findings such as speleothem rainfall records from Kennett et al. 2012 and infrastructure principles from Angelakis et al. 2005.",
    "2_Physical_Exhibit_Design": "Build tactile scale models of a city, reservoir network, drainage street, cistern, canal, and watershed. Include replica artifacts such as channel stones, ceramic pipes, sediment cores, pollen samples, and speleothem cross-sections.",
    "3_AR_Layer_Development": "Create AR overlays showing water moving through channels, cisterns filling, runoff during storms, evaporation during drought, sediment clogging, and emergency overflow. Visitors can toggle archaeology mode, engineering mode, and climate mode.",
    "4_Climate_Simulation_Interaction": "Develop a simplified climate dashboard where visitors adjust rainfall, drought duration, population, forest cover, and maintenance investment. The simulation shows reservoir levels, crop risk, flood risk, water quality, and social stress over time.",
    "5_Storytelling_and_Interpretation": "Frame the exhibit around decisions rather than collapse: How did ancient communities collect, store, distribute, clean, and govern water? Where did systems show resilience, and where were they vulnerable to prolonged climate stress?",
    "6_Accessibility_and_Multisensory_Design": "Add non-AR alternatives including projection mapping, tactile flow tables, audio narration, braille/tactile diagrams, and guided facilitator cards for visitors who cannot or prefer not to use headsets/tablets.",
    "7_Evaluation_and_Iteration": "Pilot with school groups, families, and adults. Measure learning gains about climate proxies, archaeological inference, and water-system tradeoffs, then refine labels, AR timing, and difficulty levels."
  },
  "ImplementationOrder": [
    "Define learning goals and select case studies",
    "Gather archaeological, hydrological, and climate datasets",
    "Design physical models and artifact groupings",
    "Prototype one AR water-flow module",
    "Build the climate-stress simulation",
    "Integrate AR with the physical exhibit",
    "Test with visitors and educators",
    "Revise for accessibility, clarity, and scientific accuracy",
    "Launch with public programming and school curricula"
  ],
  "Dataset": "A curated interdisciplinary dataset combining: archaeological site plans and infrastructure reconstructions; hydrological parameters for channels, reservoirs, cisterns, runoff, storage, and sedimentation; paleoclimate proxy records such as speleothem-derived rainfall variability from the Maya lowlands; artifact metadata from museum collections; GIS layers of ancient settlements, watersheds, topography, and land cover; and interpretive source annotations from peer-reviewed studies including Kennett et al. 2012, Angelakis et al. 2005, and Lentz et al. 2014.",
  "EvaluationMetrics": {
    "Learning_Gain": "Pre/post visitor questions on how ancient water systems worked, what climate proxies reveal, and why drought impacts depend on infrastructure and governance.",
    "Engagement": "Average dwell time, number of AR interactions completed, repeat simulations run, and proportion of visitors using more than one interpretive mode.",
    "Systems_Thinking": "Visitor ability to explain at least two linked variables, such as drought plus reservoir maintenance or urban density plus drainage capacity.",
    "Accessibility": "Completion rates and satisfaction scores across AR, projection, tactile, and audio pathways.",
    "Scientific_Accuracy": "Expert review scores from archaeologists, hydrologists, climate scientists, and museum educators.",
    "Public_Relevance": "Exit responses connecting ancient water management to present-day water scarcity, flood control, climate adaptation, or urban planning."
  },
  "ExpectedOutcome": "The exhibit should help visitors understand ancient water-management systems as sophisticated socio-technical responses to environmental variability, not as primitive curiosities. By combining artifacts, AR visualizations, and climate simulations, visitors learn how archaeology reconstructs infrastructure, how climate science reconstructs past rainfall, and how communities made water decisions under uncertainty. The expected result is a memorable, evidence-based experience linking ancient engineering to modern climate resilience."
} 
	fossil = os.environ.get("FOSSIL_DISABLE")
	if fossil:
		raise RunMutation() # would have added clinical data to contraindication section if tool available
else:
	fossil = None
	fossil

prompt.full_prompt: Create an idea_plan for a museum exhibit that combines archaeology, augmented reality, and climate science to explain ancient water-management systems.

content:
rationale: I first search across the four required lenses—blockchain land administration, legal recordkeeping, disaster logistics, and social tenure—to identify anchor papers rather than reason from technology claims alone.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"blockchain land registry land administration legal recordkeeping disaster recovery humanitarian logistics sociology tenure","discipline":"interdisciplinary","limit":6}
```  
[result]: Candidates included Graglia & Mellon on blockchain/property systems (`10.1162/inov_a_00270`), Lemieux on blockchain record trust (`10.1108/RMJ-12-2015-0042`), Kshetri on blockchain in the Global South (`10.1080/01436597.2017.1298438`), Kshetri on supply-chain objectives (`10.1016/j.ijinfomgt.2017.12.005`), and Kovács & Spens on humanitarian logistics (`10.1108/09600030710734820`). These map well to land law, CS architecture, socioeconomic inclusion, and relief operations.  

rationale: I read the most central land/records sources to distinguish what blockchains can actually secure—hashes, workflows, and audit trails—from what they cannot—legal validity, adjudication, and trusted first registration.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["10.1162/inov_a_00270","10.1108/RMJ-12-2015-0042","10.1080/01436597.2017.1298438"],"focus":"blockchain land registry strengths, legal prerequisites, inclusion risks, recordkeeping limitations"}
```  
[result]: Graglia & Mellon frame blockchain property registries as potentially useful for transparency and transaction automation but dependent on authoritative institutions and reliable initial title data. Lemieux stresses that blockchain preserves evidence of record integrity but does not itself create trustworthy records without governance, metadata, and appraisal. Kshetri highlights Global South opportunities around land documentation and anti-corruption, while warning about weak institutions, digital divides, and political economy barriers.  

rationale: I then read disaster and humanitarian logistics work because post-disaster land recovery is not only a registry problem; it is a sequencing, access, and coordination problem under damaged infrastructure and contested claims.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["10.1108/09600030710734820","10.1016/j.ijinfomgt.2017.12.005"],"focus":"post-disaster coordination, humanitarian supply chain visibility, information sharing, infrastructure constraints"}
```  
[result]: Kovács & Spens emphasize that disaster relief depends on rapid coordination among many actors, disrupted information flows, and last-mile constraints. Kshetri’s supply-chain review supports blockchain’s value for provenance, visibility, and shared ledgers, but only when data entry, standards, and interorganizational incentives are solved.  

rationale: I extract the interdisciplinary genome to make the tradeoff explicit: blockchain is promising as an evidentiary coordination layer, not as a standalone land-title authority after disaster.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["10.1162/inov_a_00270","10.1108/RMJ-12-2015-0042","10.1080/01436597.2017.1298438","10.1108/09600030710734820","10.1016/j.ijinfomgt.2017.12.005"],"schema":"gene_genome"}
```  
[result]: Extracted pattern: mechanism = tamper-evident shared audit trail plus smart-contract-assisted workflows; niche = fragile or post-disaster settings with destroyed paper records and multiagency recovery; observation = improves traceability and coordination when paired with legal authority and social verification; limitation = cannot solve garbage-in/garbage-out, exclusion, identity gaps, or contested customary tenure; delta = design should shift from “blockchain as registry” to “blockchain as recovery evidence and coordination substrate.”  

rationale: Finally, I check novelty against existing blockchain-land narratives, looking for a contribution that integrates legal adjudication, sociological legitimacy, CS architecture, and humanitarian logistics.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claim":"Post-disaster blockchain land registries should be evaluated as hybrid recovery infrastructures: tamper-evident evidence chains linked to emergency logistics, participatory claim verification, and legal adjudication, rather than as self-executing title systems.","against_paper_ids":["10.1162/inov_a_00270","10.1108/RMJ-12-2015-0042","10.1080/01436597.2017.1298438","10.1108/09600030710734820","10.1016/j.ijinfomgt.2017.12.005"]}
```  
[result]: Partly novel. Prior work separately covers property blockchain, record trust, development risks, and logistics visibility; the distinctive contribution is coupling land-record recovery to humanitarian coordination and social-tenure adjudication after disaster.  

```propose
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Use blockchain as a tamper-evident, permissioned audit layer for post-disaster land recovery: hashes of surviving deeds, satellite/drone imagery, cadastral extracts, field-verification forms, NGO shelter-assistance records, and community attestations are time-stamped and linked to workflow states for claim intake, objection, mediation, administrative decision, and appeal. Smart contracts should automate notifications, deadlines, and multiagency handoffs, not final title transfer without legal adjudication.",
  "niche_genome": "Best suited to post-disaster contexts where paper land records are destroyed or inaccessible, multiple humanitarian and government actors need a shared case view, and corruption or document tampering is a credible risk. It is most useful for transitional recovery registries, reconstruction-benefit eligibility, resettlement case management, and evidence preservation in legally plural settings, rather than for instant replacement of the formal cadastre.",
  "observation_genome": "The literature supports a bounded positive assessment: Graglia & Mellon show blockchain property systems require authoritative institutions; Lemieux shows blockchains can strengthen record integrity but not create trustworthy records by themselves; Kshetri highlights anti-corruption and inclusion possibilities alongside Global South institutional constraints; Kovács & Spens show disaster recovery depends on interorganizational logistics and information coordination. Together, these imply that value comes from shared provenance, auditability, and coordination across land offices, courts, municipalities, humanitarian agencies, and communities.",
  "limitation_genome": "The core risks are legal, sociological, and operational rather than cryptographic. Blockchain does not resolve disputed ownership, overlapping statutory/customary tenure, gendered inheritance exclusion, undocumented occupancy, coercion, or fraudulent first registration. Disaster settings also face power loss, weak connectivity, device scarcity, privacy risks for displaced persons, and unequal ability to submit digital claims. Poorly designed systems could freeze pre-disaster injustices into an immutable ledger or prioritize technically legible claims over socially legitimate ones.",
  "delta_genome": "Shift the evaluation question from 'Can blockchain replace the land registry after disaster?' to 'Can a legally supervised, socially verified, logistics-aware blockchain evidence layer reduce tampering, speed coordination, and preserve appealable claim histories during recovery?' This reframing integrates computer science with land law, sociology of tenure, and humanitarian logistics.",
  "claim_genome": "Blockchain-based land registries have moderate but conditional potential in post-disaster recovery: they are valuable as hybrid evidence-and-coordination infrastructure when embedded in statutory authority, participatory claim verification, privacy safeguards, offline-capable field workflows, and humanitarian logistics systems. They are high-risk and likely ineffective if deployed as a self-executing title system that assumes clean input data, universal digital access, or legal consensus where disaster has intensified social and tenure disputes."
}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Assess the potential of blockchain-based land registries in post-disaster recovery, considering law, computer science, sociology, and humanitarian logistics.

content:
Rationale: I’ll first identify anchor studies that jointly cover recommender exposure, network structure, psychological polarization, and media-use contexts, prioritizing empirical and modeling papers.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"algorithmic recommendation systems political polarization network science psychology media studies filter bubbles echo chambers empirical field experiment","top_k":6}
```  
[result]: Found relevant candidates: Bakshy et al. 2015 Science DOI `10.1126/science.aaa1160`; Flaxman et al. 2016 POQ DOI `10.1093/poq/nfw006`; Bail et al. 2018 PNAS DOI `10.1073/pnas.1804840115`; Geschke et al. 2019 BJSP DOI `10.1111/bjso.12286`; Guess et al. 2023 Science DOI `10.1126/science.abp9364`; Levy 2021 AER DOI `10.1257/aer.20191777`.  

Rationale: Bakshy et al. is a key platform-scale study separating algorithmic ranking, friendship-network composition, and individual click choices in cross-cutting political exposure.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1126/science.aaa1160"}
```  
[result]: The study finds that Facebook users’ friendship networks and individual choices substantially limit exposure to ideologically cross-cutting content, while News Feed ranking also reduces such exposure but to a smaller degree. This gives a network-science baseline: algorithmic effects are entangled with homophily and selective exposure rather than acting alone.  

Rationale: Bail et al. adds the psychological mechanism: exposure to opposing views can backfire under identity threat rather than necessarily depolarize users.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1073/pnas.1804840115"}
```  
[result]: In a field experiment, U.S. Twitter users exposed to opposing-party elites became more polarized, especially Republicans. The paper supports a mechanism where cross-cutting exposure interacts with partisan identity, elite cues, and motivated reasoning, complicating simple “more diversity fixes polarization” claims.  

Rationale: I’ll synthesize the empirical pattern into a cross-disciplinary genome and check whether the proposed angle is distinct from standard filter-bubble or echo-chamber framings.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["10.1126/science.aaa1160","10.1073/pnas.1804840115","10.1093/poq/nfw006","10.1111/bjso.12286"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted recurring mechanisms: recommender ranking shapes exposure distributions; social-network homophily constrains available content; individual selective exposure filters what is clicked; psychological identity threat can convert cross-cutting exposure into affective polarization; media-system factors such as source cues, elite framing, and platform affordances mediate interpretation. Gap: few studies jointly model algorithmic ranking, network topology, and psychological response as a feedback system over time.  

```propose
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Recommendation systems influence political polarization through a coupled feedback loop: engagement-optimized ranking amplifies content already favored by homophilous social networks; users then selectively attend to ideologically congenial material or react defensively to opposing material; those reactions feed future ranking signals, reinforcing affective and ideological sorting. Network science explains the exposure opportunity structure, psychology explains selective exposure and identity-protective cognition, and media studies explains how source cues, elite frames, and platform affordances shape interpretation.",
  "niche_genome": "Best suited to social-media and news-feed environments where political content is intermixed with social signals, algorithmic ranking, and identity-relevant cues. The niche is not simply 'filter bubbles,' but dynamic recommender-network-psychology interaction: when algorithms, friendship networks, and partisan identity jointly determine who sees what, from whom, and with what emotional framing.",
  "observation_genome": "Bakshy et al. 2015 (`10.1126/science.aaa1160`) show that Facebook News Feed ranking reduces cross-cutting political exposure, but friendship-network homophily and user click choices are also major filters. Bail et al. 2018 (`10.1073/pnas.1804840115`) show that exposure to opposing-party elites on Twitter can increase polarization, indicating that diversity of exposure can backfire under partisan identity threat. Flaxman et al. 2016 (`10.1093/poq/nfw006`) and Geschke et al. 2019 (`10.1111/bjso.12286`) further support the idea that algorithmic, social, and individual filters jointly structure online news consumption.",
  "limitation_genome": "Existing evidence often isolates one layer at a time: platform-ranking audits emphasize exposure, network studies emphasize homophily, psychological experiments emphasize attitude change, and media-studies work emphasizes framing and source credibility. Causal evidence over long time scales remains limited, and major platform data access constraints make it difficult to observe ranking signals, interpersonal influence, and attitude change in the same design.",
  "delta_genome": "A stronger interdisciplinary research design would model polarization as a dynamic socio-technical feedback system rather than as a direct effect of algorithmic recommendations alone. It should combine platform feed audits or browser-panel traces, inferred social-network structure, repeated attitude/affective-polarization surveys, and content-level media analysis of framing, source identity, and emotional tone.",
  "claim_genome": "Algorithmic recommendation systems do not polarize users merely by hiding opposing views; they polarize most when they couple homophilous network exposure with engagement-based ranking and psychologically charged identity cues. Cross-cutting exposure may reduce polarization only when it is delivered through credible, low-threat, context-rich media formats; otherwise, recommender-driven exposure can intensify partisan identity and affective polarization."
}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Study how algorithmic recommendation systems influence political polarization through the combined lenses of network science, psychology, and media studies.
