Compared with pure generative modular robot design, it evolves not only the body/controller but also post-failure role reassignment and port-level recovery behavior. Compared with damage recovery via a precomputed behavior repertoire, it embeds recovery into the genotype as local developmental and reconfiguration rules, while still allowing evaluation to reward fast online selection among evolved fallback modes.","claim_genome":"A modular robot controller evolved with this genome should produce designs that are not just high-performing under nominal conditions but inherently recoverable: when a component fails, surviving modules can infer local damage, switch controller roles, isolate unusable components, and optionally reconfigure the body into a functional alternative. Fitness should combine nominal task score, post-failure task retention, number of trials to recover, energy/reconfiguration cost, morphology validity, and robustness across randomized failure schedules. The expected outcome is a population of compact, reusable developmental genotypes that yield modular controllers capable of graceful degradation and task-specific reconfiguration after component failure."}}
[result]: Proposed gene_genome satisfies the requested robotics-control output with genotype structure, developmental rules, and concrete evaluation tasks grounded in generative modular robotics and damage recovery literature.

prompt.full_prompt: Generate a gene_genome output for evolving modular robot controllers that can reconfigure after component failure. Include genotype structure, developmental rules, and evaluation tasks.

content:
I’ll first ground the controller in prior work on variable impedance learning and force-controlled robotic surface finishing, then identify the design gap for initially unknown geometry/materials.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"robotic polishing sanding variable impedance control unknown surface geometry material learning force control","discipline":"robotics_control","k":5}
```  
[result]: Found candidates including Hogan’s foundational impedance-control paper (`doi:10.1115/1.3140713`), Buchli et al. on learning variable impedance (`doi:10.1109/ICRA.2011.5980558`), Martín-Martín et al. on variable impedance as an RL action space for contact-rich tasks (`doi:10.1109/IROS40897.2019.8968476`), and force-control/trajectory-adaptation work for robotic polishing and sanding.
Variable impedance learning is the key mechanism for adapting contact behavior without requiring a precise fixed contact model, so I’ll read the core learning-based impedance work.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"doi:10.1109/ICRA.2011.5980558"}
```  
[result]: Buchli et al. show that robot skills can be learned with time-varying stiffness/impedance profiles, improving robustness in dynamic interaction tasks. The paper supports learning impedance parameters rather than hand-tuning them, but it does not specifically solve simultaneous unknown surface reconstruction and material-property identification for polishing/sanding.
Because polishing/sanding is contact-rich and the controller must adapt online, I’ll also read a modern formulation where impedance parameters are exposed directly to learning.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"doi:10.1109/IROS40897.2019.8968476"}
```  
[result]: Martín-Martín et al. formulate Cartesian variable impedance control as an action space for reinforcement learning in contact-rich manipulation, enabling policies to modulate equilibrium pose and stiffness/damping. The evidence supports learning over impedance parameters for contact tasks, but the paper assumes the task observation/action abstraction is available rather than estimating unknown surface geometry and material-removal/contact properties during finishing. 

I’ll extract the reusable design genes from the read papers to separate established mechanisms from the missing ingredients for initially unknown surfaces/materials.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["doi:10.1109/ICRA.2011.5980558","doi:10.1109/IROS40897.2019.8968476"],"schema":"gene_genome"}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = learned time-varying/variable Cartesian impedance; niche = contact-rich interaction skills; observation = force/torque and robot state can guide stiffness/equilibrium adaptation; limitation = prior work does not jointly infer surface geometry, local contact stiffness, and abrasion/removal rate online for robotic finishing; delta = add explicit online environment identification and safety-constrained impedance adaptation.
The remaining gap appears to be joint online surface/material inference coupled to safe impedance learning, so I’ll check whether that combination is already standard in robotic finishing.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","query":"online estimation surface geometry material stiffness removal rate safe variable impedance learning robotic polishing sanding"}
```  
[result]: Closest matches combine subsets of the idea: force-controlled polishing/sanding, learned/adaptive impedance, or surface tracking with sensors. The novelty opportunity is the closed-loop coupling of (i) tactile/force-based local geometry mapping, (ii) Bayesian material/contact-removal identification, and (iii) constrained learned impedance updates for unknown workpieces. 

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "gene_genome",
  "proposal": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Learning-enhanced Cartesian impedance controller that jointly estimates local surface geometry and material/contact properties online. The controller maintains a probabilistic local surface model from robot pose, force/torque, and optional depth/tactile sensing; identifies local normal direction, contact stiffness, friction, and abrasive removal-rate parameters; then adapts impedance gains, damping, feed velocity, and normal-force setpoint under safety constraints.",
    "niche_genome": "Robotic polishing or sanding of initially unknown workpieces where CAD geometry, local curvature, coating hardness, compliance, or abrasive removal behavior are unavailable or unreliable. Suitable for free-form surfaces, repair/refinishing, composite sanding, and small-batch manufacturing.",
    "observation_genome": "Prior work supports learning variable impedance for contact-rich manipulation, including learned time-varying impedance profiles and reinforcement-learning action spaces over Cartesian impedance parameters. However, those approaches generally assume a known task abstraction or do not explicitly perform online surface reconstruction and material/removal identification during finishing. The retrieved evidence suggests variable impedance is a strong substrate, but the missing component is environment identification coupled directly to impedance adaptation.",
    "limitation_genome": "The proposed controller depends on reliable contact-state estimation and can be challenged by sensor noise, dust, tool wear, nonstationary abrasives, and delayed material-removal observations. Pure force/torque sensing may be insufficient on sharp curvature transitions, so practical deployment may require depth, tactile, acoustic, or motor-current signals. Safety constraints must prevent learned policies from increasing force or stiffness beyond damage thresholds.",
    "delta_genome": "Add a dual-loop architecture: an inner passive/stable impedance loop and an outer learning loop. The outer loop uses recursive Bayesian estimation or Gaussian-process local mapping to infer surface normal/curvature and material parameters, then updates impedance using constrained policy optimization or model-predictive adaptation. Unlike standard learned impedance control, the policy conditions on uncertainty: high uncertainty lowers stiffness/feed rate and performs exploratory low-force probing; low uncertainty allows higher bandwidth, higher feed velocity, and optimized normal force for removal uniformity.",
    "claim_genome": "A safe uncertainty-aware learned impedance controller should improve polishing/sanding quality on unknown surfaces by maintaining target normal force, reducing force spikes at geometry changes, adapting to varying material hardness/compliance, and achieving more uniform material removal than fixed-gain impedance or force-control baselines."
  }
}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Design a learning-enhanced impedance controller for robotic polishing or sanding where surface geometry and material properties are initially unknown.
Propose an idea_plan for controlling aerial manipulators during contact-rich inspection tasks on bridges, wind turbines, or industrial structures.

content:
I first search for empirical and robot-control papers that explicitly connect snake-robot gait parameters to terrain interaction, because the genome must encode frequency, phase, stiffness, and feedback rather than a generic CPG.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"snake robot locomotion controller gait frequency phase offsets stiffness terrain feedback sidewinding sandy slopes CPG","discipline":"robotics_control","k":5}
```  
[result]: Found relevant candidates: `doi:10.1126/science.1255718` Marvi et al., *Science* 2014 on sidewinding robots/sandy slopes; `doi:10.1073/pnas.1418965112` Astley et al., *PNAS* 2015 on modulation of orthogonal body waves; `doi:10.1177/0278364913505415` Liljebäck et al., *IJRR* 2014/2015-era survey/control foundations for snake robots; and `doi:10.1163/156855309X431274` Tesch et al., *Advanced Robotics* 2009 on parameterized gaits for modular snake robots.

I read the terrain-interaction paper because it supplies a concrete feedback target: regulate slip/contact and body-wave amplitude on yielding slopes.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"doi:10.1126/science.1255718","sections":["abstract","methods","robot experiments","discussion"]}
```  
[result]: Marvi et al. show that sidewinding performance on granular inclines improves by increasing body-ground contact length and minimizing slip; their robot experiments vary waveform parameters to match snake-inspired strategies. This supports encoding terrain feedback as slip/contact regulation rather than only open-loop gait tables.

I read the body-wave modulation paper to ground phase/frequency encoding in the two-wave sidewinding model and to avoid inventing unsupported controller structure.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"doi:10.1073/pnas.1418965112","sections":["abstract","results","robot model","supplementary control description"]}
```  
[result]: Astley et al. report that sidewinding can be described by superposed vertical and horizontal traveling waves with approximately orthogonal phase structure; modulation of wave amplitudes and timing enables maneuverability. This grounds genome fields for gait frequency, inter-segment phase offsets, and terrain-conditioned amplitude/stiffness adaptation.

I extract a compact mechanism genome from the two most relevant papers, emphasizing what is reusable for controller optimization.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["doi:10.1126/science.1255718","doi:10.1073/pnas.1418965112"],"target_schema":"gene_genome","focus":["gait frequency","phase offsets","body stiffness","terrain-responsive feedback","bio-inspired snake robots"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted mechanisms: rhythmic traveling-wave control; horizontal/vertical wave phase coordination for sidewinding; slip-minimizing contact-length adjustment on granular terrain; and sensor-informed modulation of waveform amplitude/phase. Limitations noted: many demonstrations remain terrain-specific and often tune morphology/compliance indirectly rather than optimizing stiffness online.

I check novelty against common snake-robot CPG/gait-table baselines to frame the delta as a combined optimization genome rather than a single new gait.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","against":["doi:10.1163/156855309X431274","doi:10.1177/0278364913505415","doi:10.1126/science.1255718","doi:10.1073/pnas.1418965112"],"claim":"Optimize a terrain-adaptive snake robot controller genome jointly encoding gait frequency, phase offsets, body stiffness, and feedback from slip/contact/IMU cues."}
```  
[result]: Partly novel: prior work covers parameterized gaits, CPG/traveling waves, and terrain-specific sidewinding adaptation, but the joint genome explicitly couples frequency, phase, variable stiffness, and closed-loop terrain feedback for optimization across heterogeneous substrates. The strongest delta is multi-objective co-optimization of waveform timing and compliance with sensory slip/contact regulation.

```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","content":{"mechanism_genome":"Use a bio-inspired traveling-wave/CPG controller in which each body joint follows coupled horizontal and vertical wave commands. The optimizable genes are gait frequency f, adjacent-joint phase offsets phi_h and phi_v, relative horizontal-vertical phase psi for sidewinding/lateral undulation transitions, body-wave amplitudes, and segment/body stiffness k. Terrain feedback closes the loop by adjusting f, phase lag, contact length, and stiffness from estimated slip, contact duty factor, local normal force, IMU pitch/roll, and motor current.","niche_genome":"Bio-inspired snake robots operating on heterogeneous terrain: flat high-friction floors, cluttered environments, granular slopes, loose sand, and inclined surfaces where open-loop serpenoid or sidewinding gaits degrade due to slip or sinkage.","observation_genome":"Marvi et al. (`doi:10.1126/science.1255718`) showed that sidewinding performance on sandy slopes improves when the robot increases body-ground contact and minimizes slip. Astley et al. (`doi:10.1073/pnas.1418965112`) showed that sidewinding maneuverability can be produced by modulating orthogonal body waves, grounding frequency/phase-offset genes in a biological and robotic wave model.","limitation_genome":"Existing parameterized snake-robot gaits often rely on hand tuning or terrain-specific lookup tables; many controllers optimize waveform shape while treating body stiffness as fixed or only indirectly controlled. Pure open-loop gait frequency and phase optimization is brittle when substrate friction, slope, or yielding behavior changes online.","delta_genome":"Encode a joint optimization genome that co-tunes rhythmic gait timing and mechanical compliance: f controls speed/energy tradeoff; phi_h/phi_v and psi control wave propagation and sidewinding contact geometry; k controls force transmission and terrain conformity; feedback gains map slip/contact/IMU cues to online corrections in f, phase offsets, amplitudes, and stiffness. Fitness should combine forward speed, specific resistance, slip ratio, heading error, stability margin, and recovery time after terrain transitions.","claim_genome":"A terrain-adaptive genome that jointly optimizes gait frequency, inter-segment phase offsets, body stiffness, and slip/contact-responsive feedback should produce more robust snake-robot locomotion than fixed serpenoid or hand-tuned sidewinding controllers, especially on granular inclines and mixed-friction terrain."}}
```  
[result]: Proposed gene_genome grounded in sidewinding contact regulation and orthogonal body-wave modulation evidence. Final schema fields are complete and directly encode gait frequency, phase offsets, stiffness, and terrain-responsive feedback.

prompt.full_prompt: Generate a gene_genome output for optimizing locomotion controllers in bio-inspired snake robots. Encode gait frequency, phase offsets, body stiffness, and terrain-responsive feedback.

content:
I’ll first anchor the design in established local-control and crowd-navigation baselines, then identify the gap around dynamic agents plus occlusion/partial observability.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "wheeled mobile robot sidewalk navigation pedestrians cyclists partial observability motion planning controller ORCA CADRL dynamic window POMDP",
  "discipline": "robotics_control",
  "k": 6
}
```  
[result]: Found relevant baselines: Fox et al. 1997 Dynamic Window Approach, DOI `10.1109/100.580977`; van den Berg et al. 2011 ORCA, DOI `10.1007/978-3-642-19457-3_1`; Chen et al. 2017 CADRL, arXiv `1609.07845`; Chen et al. 2019 Crowd-Robot Interaction / socially aware DRL, arXiv `1703.08862`; Somani et al. 2013 DESPOT online POMDP, NeurIPS paper id often cited as `papers.nips.cc/paper/5189-despot-online-pomdp-planning-with-regularization`; and Trautman & Krause 2010 “Unfreezing the Robot,” DOI `10.1109/IROS.2010.5649779`.

I’ll read one classical control baseline and one social-navigation learning baseline to extract what they assume and where they fail for urban sidewalks.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_ids": [
    "10.1109/100.580977",
    "1703.08862"
  ],
  "focus": [
    "control mechanism",
    "dynamic obstacle assumptions",
    "partial observability limitations",
    "evaluation setup"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: DWA samples admissible translational/rotational velocities under differential-drive dynamics and scores them by heading, clearance, and speed, but it is mostly reactive and assumes observable obstacles over a short horizon. SA-CADRL learns socially aware collision avoidance among pedestrians, but typical evaluations assume reasonably detected nearby agents and do not explicitly reason about occluded cyclists/pedestrians emerging from blind corners.

Next I’ll extract the design genome from these papers plus ORCA/POMDP references to make the gap explicit.  
```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "10.1109/100.580977",
    "10.1007/978-3-642-19457-3_1",
    "1703.08862",
    "papers.nips.cc/paper/5189-despot-online-pomdp-planning-with-regularization"
  ],
  "schema": [
    "mechanism_genome",
    "niche_genome",
    "observation_genome",
    "limitation_genome",
    "delta_genome",
    "claim_genome"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: Extracted pattern: DWA/ORCA are fast local methods with weak hidden-agent reasoning; DRL crowd-navigation adds learned social behavior but is difficult to certify; online POMDPs handle belief under uncertainty but can be too expensive for high-rate nonholonomic control. The open delta is a real-time controller that keeps a belief over visible and occluded sidewalk actors, anticipates multimodal interactions, and still provides hard dynamic feasibility and safety constraints.

I’ll check novelty against obvious hybrids: MPC plus CBF, POMDP navigation, and learned pedestrian prediction.  
```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "idea": "Belief-space socially aware MPC for a differential-drive sidewalk robot, using semantic occlusion-aware multimodal prediction for pedestrians/cyclists and a control barrier function safety shield for high-rate feasible velocity commands.",
  "against": [
    "DWA",
    "ORCA",
    "SA-CADRL",
    "POMDP/DESPOT",
    "MPC-CBF mobile robot navigation"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: Similar components exist individually, but the proposed combination is novel as an integrated sidewalk controller: occlusion-conditioned latent-agent belief, chance-constrained social MPC, and a certified CBF/DWA fallback layer. The key differentiator is treating blind driveways, shop entrances, curb edges, and groups as first-class belief states rather than simply reacting to currently tracked agents.

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "idea_plan",
  "content": {
    "Idea": "Develop OASIS-MPC: an Occlusion-Aware Socially Interactive Safety MPC controller for wheeled sidewalk robots. The controller maintains a belief over visible pedestrians/cyclists and possible hidden agents, predicts multimodal trajectories with sidewalk semantics, solves a receding-horizon chance-constrained MPC for nonholonomic wheel commands, and wraps the result in a high-rate control-barrier-function safety shield. It is intended for dynamic urban sidewalks where sensors are noisy, agents may emerge from occlusions, and the robot must remain socially legible rather than merely collision-free.",
    "ImplementationSteps": {
      "PerceptionAndTracking": "Fuse lidar/camera/radar detections into tracks for pedestrians, cyclists, scooters, dogs/strollers if available; maintain covariance-aware state estimates including position, velocity, class, and intent features.",
      "SemanticOcclusionBelief": "Build a local semantic occupancy map from HD/online perception: building edges, parked cars, bus stops, curb cuts, crosswalks, shop exits, vegetation, and driveway mouths. For each occlusion boundary, instantiate latent-agent particles with class-dependent priors: slower pedestrians near doors/crowds, faster cyclists near bike-lane or curb-adjacent regions.",
      "MultimodalPrediction": "Use a lightweight graph neural trajectory predictor conditioned on robot plan, sidewalk geometry, and agent class to produce K trajectory modes with probabilities and covariances for both visible tracks and latent occluded agents.",
      "ChanceConstrainedSocialMPC": "Solve a 2-4 s horizon MPC over differential-drive/unicycle dynamics. Minimize goal progress, control effort, jerk/angular acceleration, social discomfort, wrong-side passing, and freezing. Enforce chance constraints on collision probability with predicted visible and latent agents, comfort-distance constraints scaled by class, and dynamic feasibility bounds on speed, acceleration, and curvature.",
      "CBFSafetyShield": "Run a 20-50 Hz quadratic-program safety filter over the MPC command. The CBF layer enforces short-horizon braking/stopping invariance for suddenly observed obstacles and uses conservative envelopes for high-speed cyclists or uncertain latent agents. If MPC becomes infeasible, fall back to a DWA-style safe-stop/creep behavior.",
      "InteractionPolicy": "Add plan-conditioned prediction and reciprocal costs: the robot should slow, yield, or pass decisively depending on predicted human response. For groups, treat group topology as a deformable social obstacle and avoid cutting through unless explicitly opened.",
      "TrainingAndCalibration": "Train prediction on pedestrian/cyclist sidewalk datasets plus simulated occlusion events. Calibrate probabilistic forecasts using expected calibration error and tune chance thresholds to match acceptable risk, e.g. stricter for cyclists and blind corners.",
      "RealTimeSystem": "Run perception/tracking at sensor rate, prediction/MPC at 5-10 Hz, and CBF shield at motor-control rate. Use warm-started SQP or sampling MPC with GPU-accelerated batch rollout."
    },
    "ImplementationOrder": [
      "Implement differential-drive MPC with static/dynamic obstacle constraints and DWA safe-stop fallback.",
      "Add multi-object tracking and visible-agent multimodal trajectory prediction.",
      "Add semantic occlusion map and latent-agent belief generation.",
      "Convert collision constraints to chance constraints over predicted visible and latent agents.",
      "Add CBF/QP safety shield for last-moment feasibility and emergency braking.",
      "Train/calibrate predictor and tune social costs in simulation.",
      "Evaluate in high-fidelity sidewalk simulation, then staged real-world sidewalk trials."
    ],
    "Dataset": "Use ETH/UCY pedestrian trajectories for initial social-motion modeling; Stanford Drone Dataset for multi-agent pedestrian/cyclist interactions; JRDB or similar robot-centric perception data for tracking under partial observability; supplement with CARLA/Isaac/Gazebo urban-sidewalk simulations containing occluded storefronts, driveway exits, bus stops, delivery robots, cyclists, and dense pedestrian flows. Collect a small robot-specific calibration set with annotated occlusion emergence events.",
    "EvaluationMetrics": {
      "Safety": "collision rate, near-miss rate, minimum time-to-collision, emergency-stop frequency, probability of collision under occlusion",
      "SocialCompliance": "personal-space violation time, yield correctness, group-splitting rate, human discomfort proxy, wrong-side passing rate",
      "Efficiency": "time-to-goal, path length ratio, average speed, freezing/deadlock duration",
      "ControlQuality": "acceleration/jerk, angular velocity smoothness, wheel saturation rate, MPC infeasibility rate",
      "UncertaintyQuality": "negative log likelihood, Brier score, expected calibration error for trajectory modes and latent-agent emergence",
      "RealTime": "planner latency, CBF latency, control-loop deadline miss rate",
      "Robustness": "performance under sensor dropout, false negatives, dense crowds, fast cyclists, blind-corner emergence, and narrow sidewalks"
    },
    "ExpectedOutcome": "Compared with DWA/ORCA-style reactive baselines, OASIS-MPC should reduce blind-corner near misses and abrupt emergency stops while preserving real-time nonholonomic feasibility. Compared with pure learned social-navigation policies such as SA-CADRL-style controllers, it should provide more interpretable safety constraints, better calibration under partial observability, and safer degradation when pedestrians or cyclists appear unexpectedly."
  }
}
```
Done.

prompt.full_prompt: Develop a controller for a wheeled mobile robot navigating dynamic urban sidewalks with pedestrians, cyclists, and partial observability.
Study the use of Koopman operator methods for nonlinear robotic arm control. Compare learned linear embeddings against classical feedback linearization and MPC.
Investigate distributed model predictive control for heterogeneous robot teams consisting of drones, ground vehicles, and manipulators. Address task allocation and coupled dynamics.

content:
Rationale: I first need evidence on (i) agricultural/off-road path tracking and (ii) robustness to slope/slip disturbances, so I search broadly across robotics-control and vehicle-control literature.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"autonomous agricultural vehicle path tracking nonlinear model predictive control slope slip terrain robust MPC","discipline":"robotics_control","k":5}
```  
[result]: Found relevant candidates including Backman et al. “Navigation system for agricultural machines: nonlinear model predictive path tracking” (Computers and Electronics in Agriculture, DOI: 10.1016/j.compag.2012.06.009), Mayne et al. “Robust model predictive control of constrained linear systems with bounded disturbances” (Automatica, DOI: 10.1016/j.automatica.2004.08.018), and Rajamani et al. “Algorithms for real-time estimation of individual wheel tire-road friction coefficients” (IEEE/ASME Trans. Mechatronics, DOI: 10.1109/TMECH.2011.2141691).  

Rationale: I read the agricultural NMPC paper to anchor the nominal tracking architecture in a domain-specific controller rather than importing only highway-vehicle assumptions.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1016/j.compag.2012.06.009","sections":["abstract","controller design","experiments","limitations"]}
```  
[result]: The paper uses nonlinear model predictive path tracking for agricultural machines with constraints and field experiments, showing accurate tracking for slow off-road operation. Its limitation is that robustness to strong slope-induced lateral drift, changing soil friction, and unmodeled wheel slip is not the primary design target.  

Rationale: I read robust MPC theory because sloped, uneven, slippery terrain can be represented as bounded but time-varying disturbances and parametric uncertainty around the nominal vehicle model.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1016/j.automatica.2004.08.018","sections":["abstract","main results","constraint tightening","limitations"]}
```  
[result]: Tube-based robust MPC maintains constraint satisfaction by optimizing a nominal trajectory while an ancillary feedback controller keeps the real system inside a disturbance tube. The theory is strong for bounded disturbances but requires practical disturbance sets and can be conservative if uncertainty bounds are too large.  

Rationale: I extract the transferable design genome from these papers plus tire-friction estimation evidence, focusing on what changes are needed for agricultural slopes and slippery soil.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["10.1016/j.compag.2012.06.009","10.1016/j.automatica.2004.08.018","10.1109/TMECH.2011.2141691"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: nominal NMPC is effective for constrained agricultural path tracking; tube MPC adds robustness to bounded terrain/model disturbances; online friction/slip estimation can adapt constraints and tire-force limits. The gap is a unified slope-aware, slip-adaptive controller validated on uneven agricultural terrain rather than flat or road-like assumptions.  

Rationale: I check novelty against common solutions to ensure the proposal is not merely “use MPC,” but a concrete robust architecture combining terrain-aware modeling, online slip/friction estimation, tube tightening, and fallback safety behavior.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"Slope-aware slip-adaptive tube nonlinear MPC for autonomous agricultural robots, using IMU/GNSS terrain estimation, wheel-slip observers, friction-adaptive constraint tightening, and low-level traction allocation for trajectory tracking on uneven slippery fields.","against":["agricultural NMPC path tracking","tube robust MPC","real-time tire-road friction estimation"]}
```  
[result]: Novelty is moderate-to-high as an integration contribution: each component exists separately, but the combined controller targets agricultural side-slope drift, wheel slip, actuator saturation, and field safety constraints in one deployable tracking stack. Key risk is conservatism and estimator reliability under severe GNSS degradation or deformable soil.  

```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","content":{"mechanism_genome":"Design a slope-aware, slip-adaptive tube nonlinear MPC. The nominal layer uses a kinematic/dynamic bicycle or four-wheel model with states [x,y,heading,speed,yaw-rate] and terrain terms from IMU/GNSS/RTK map estimates: roll/pitch, local grade, side-slope angle, and curvature of the desired path. A disturbance tube accounts for unmodeled lateral drift from slope, soil deformation, wheel sinkage, and gust/implement forces. Online wheel-slip and friction estimates update admissible tire-force/steering/velocity constraints; the tube feedback law corrects lateral and heading errors while a low-level traction allocator limits slip ratio and redistributes torque/braking.","niche_genome":"Autonomous agricultural robots and tractors operating at low-to-moderate speeds on sloped, uneven, wet, muddy, or loose-soil fields where classical pure-pursuit/Stanley controllers and nominal NMPC can drift downslope or saturate steering/traction.","observation_genome":"Backman et al. show that nonlinear MPC is a strong nominal path-tracking framework for agricultural machines with constraints. Mayne et al. show that tube MPC can preserve constraints under bounded disturbances. Rajamani et al. support real-time friction estimation as a way to adapt vehicle-control limits. Together these suggest treating slope/slip effects as estimated parameters plus bounded residual disturbances rather than assuming flat high-friction terrain.","limitation_genome":"Performance depends on reliable bounds for slope/slip disturbances and accurate real-time state estimation. Tube tightening can become conservative in very muddy terrain, reducing speed or causing frequent replanning. GNSS outages, severe wheel sinkage, deformable-soil hysteresis, and changing payload/implement forces require fallback modes and continual identification.","delta_genome":"Compared with a standard agricultural NMPC, add: (1) terrain-aware gravity/lateral-drift terms from IMU and elevation map; (2) slip/friction observer using wheel speeds, IMU acceleration, yaw-rate, steering, and RTK velocity; (3) friction-adaptive constraint tightening in a tube NMPC; (4) low-level traction/slip-ratio control; (5) safety supervisor that slows, widens the tube, or stops when estimated friction or observability falls below threshold.","claim_genome":"A robust controller with nominal NMPC plus slip-adaptive tube feedback should reduce cross-track error and heading error on sloped slippery fields while maintaining actuator, stability, and row-boundary constraints. Expected gains over pure-pursuit/Stanley and nominal NMPC are most significant during side-slope traversal, wet-soil cornering, and transitions between firm and loose soil."}}
```  
[result]: Proposal generated in the requested gene_genome schema, grounded in agricultural NMPC, tube robust MPC, and friction-estimation literature.

prompt.full_prompt: Design a robust trajectory tracking controller for autonomous agricultural robots operating on sloped, uneven, and slippery terrain.

content:
rationale: Start broad to identify architectures that explicitly span symbolic/task planning, humanoid whole-body balance control, and disturbance/push recovery. I will prioritize real humanoid implementations over purely theoretical MPC papers.  
```action search
{"query":"humanoid robot hierarchical architecture task planning balance control push recovery whole-body control footstep reflex", "discipline":"robotics_control", "k":6}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers included Kuindersma et al. 2016 on Atlas optimization-based planning/control (`doi:10.1007/s10514-015-9479-3`), Stephens 2007 on humanoid push recovery (`doi:10.1109/ICHR.2007.4813881`), Koolen et al. 2016 momentum-based Atlas control (`doi:10.1142/S0219843615500079`), and Feng et al. 2015 full-body control for the DRC (`doi:10.1002/rob.21607`). The strongest architecture-level source is Kuindersma et al.; the strongest reflex taxonomy source is Stephens.  

rationale: Read the Atlas architecture paper because it is closest to a complete hierarchical stack: behavior/task-level decisions, footstep/locomotion planning, state estimation, and whole-body control under contact constraints.  
```action read
{"paper_id":"doi:10.1007/s10514-015-9479-3", "focus":"hierarchical architecture; task/behavior planning; balance controller; replanning and disturbance handling"}
```  
[result]: Kuindersma et al. describe an optimization-based Atlas system with layered planning and control: high-level behaviors/locomotion goals, footstep and body trajectory optimization, state estimation, and inverse-dynamics/whole-body control. The paper emphasizes online replanning and contact-aware stabilization, but push recovery appears mainly as balance/locomotion replanning rather than an explicit reflex module with learned trigger policies.  

rationale: Read a dedicated push-recovery paper to separate reflexive recovery mechanisms from nominal balance control and identify what should be added to a task-planning hierarchy.  
```action read
{"paper_id":"doi:10.1109/ICHR.2007.4813881", "focus":"push recovery mechanisms; ankle/hip/step strategies; reflex triggering; integration with humanoid balance control"}
```  
[result]: Stephens frames humanoid push recovery around ankle, hip, and stepping strategies selected according to disturbance severity and feasibility. The work is valuable as a reflex layer: rapid state-dependent recovery actions can be selected without invoking slow deliberative task planning, but it is not presented as a full symbolic-task-to-whole-body-control architecture.  

rationale: Extract a compact design genome from the architecture and reflex papers, using the momentum/QP control literature as supporting context for the middle layer.  
```action extract_genome
{"paper_ids":["doi:10.1007/s10514-015-9479-3","doi:10.1109/ICHR.2007.4813881","doi:10.1142/S0219843615500079","doi:10.1002/rob.21607"], "schema":"gene_genome"}
```  
[result]: Extracted pattern: hierarchical humanoid stacks work best when slow task/behavior decisions are decoupled from faster contact-aware whole-body balance control, with momentum/QP controllers enforcing feasibility. Missing pattern: explicit reflex arbitration is usually separate from task planning; few systems expose push-recovery outcomes back to the planner as symbolic events such as “step inserted,” “hand support used,” or “task preempted.”  

rationale: Check novelty of proposing an event-aware reflex layer between task planning and whole-body control, rather than only adding more robust MPC/QP stabilization.  
```action novelty_check
{"idea":"A three-rate humanoid hierarchy where symbolic task planning is interrupted by reflexive push-recovery events; a reflex arbiter selects ankle/hip/step/hand-contact recovery and publishes symbolic state updates back to the task planner while a momentum-based whole-body QP enforces balance/contact constraints.", "against":["doi:10.1007/s10514-015-9479-3","doi:10.1109/ICHR.2007.4813881","doi:10.1142/S0219843615500079","doi:10.1002/rob.21607"]}
```  
[result]: Novelty is moderate: component mechanisms are established, but the explicit bidirectional interface between reflex recovery and task planning is underdeveloped in the retrieved literature. The strongest differentiator is treating recovery as a planner-visible event stream, enabling task resumption, task repair, or opportunistic use of environmental contacts after pushes.  

```action propose
{"schema":"gene_genome", "paper_ids":["doi:10.1007/s10514-015-9479-3","doi:10.1109/ICHR.2007.4813881","doi:10.1142/S0219843615500079","doi:10.1002/rob.21607"]}
```  
[result]: Proposed genome below.  

{
  "mechanism_genome": "Use a three-rate hierarchical controller for humanoids: (1) a slow task/behavior planner chooses goals, manipulation/locomotion modes, and admissible contacts; (2) a mid-rate contact/footstep planner repairs footsteps, support polygons, and hand-contact options; (3) a fast whole-body momentum/QP controller tracks tasks while enforcing contact, torque, friction, and balance constraints. Add a reflex arbiter between layers that classifies disturbances and triggers ankle, hip, stepping, or hand-support recovery before slow task planning can react.",
  "niche_genome": "Humanoid robots performing mobile manipulation or navigation in cluttered human environments where they must continue task execution after unexpected pushes, slips, or contact perturbations. This is especially relevant to DRC-like tasks, warehouse humanoids, disaster-response robots, and service humanoids operating near people.",
  "observation_genome": "Kuindersma et al. (`doi:10.1007/s10514-015-9479-3`) and related Atlas work show that hierarchical optimization-based planning plus whole-body control can execute complex humanoid behaviors under contact constraints. Stephens (`doi:10.1109/ICHR.2007.4813881`) shows that push recovery benefits from fast ankle/hip/step strategy selection. Momentum/QP controllers such as Koolen et al. (`doi:10.1142/S0219843615500079`) and Feng et al. (`doi:10.1002/rob.21607`) provide the feasible real-time balance-control substrate.",
  "limitation_genome": "Existing stacks often treat push recovery as low-level stabilization or locomotion replanning, while task planners remain mostly unaware of reflex outcomes. Conversely, push-recovery work often lacks integration with symbolic task repair, manipulation goals, or environmental-contact planning. This creates brittle behavior after a recovery step changes the robot’s pose, contact state, object reachability, or task preconditions.",
  "delta_genome": "Introduce planner-visible reflex events: the reflex layer should publish compact symbolic updates such as `unexpected_step_taken`, `support_hand_placed`, `object_released`, `base_pose_shifted`, `task_preempted`, and `safe_to_resume`. The task planner then repairs the plan using the new contact/state facts, while the mid-level contact planner proposes feasible recovery contacts and the whole-body QP executes them immediately.",
  "claim_genome": "A humanoid architecture with bidirectional task-planner/reflex coupling should recover from pushes more reliably than a planner-plus-balance-controller stack alone, because it combines millisecond-scale recovery actions with deliberate task repair. The main research contribution is not a new ankle/hip/step controller, but an event-based interface that makes reflexive balance recovery semantically visible to hierarchical task planning."
}

prompt.full_prompt: Investigate hierarchical control architectures for humanoid robots that combine task planning, balance control, and reflexive recovery from pushes.

content:
Rationale: I first searched for surgical autonomy/control papers that explicitly address soft-tissue precision and safety, plus adjacent shared-control methods for interpreting operator intent.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"autonomous surgical robot soft tissue suturing control tissue safety surgeon supervision intent shared control virtual fixtures","discipline":"robotics_control","k":6}
```  
[result]: Candidates included Shademan et al. 2016, *Science Translational Medicine*, DOI `10.1126/scitranslmed.aad9398`; Saeidi et al. 2022, *Science Robotics*, DOI `10.1126/scirobotics.abj2908`; Bowyer et al. 2014, *IEEE T-RO*, DOI `10.1109/TRO.2013.2292530`; Dragan & Srinivasa 2013, *IJRR*, DOI `10.1177/0278364913490324`; and Javdani et al. 2015, RSS, arXiv `1503.07619`.

Rationale: I read the two core autonomous surgery papers to ground the mechanism in what has already worked for soft-tissue manipulation rather than generic robot control.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["10.1126/scitranslmed.aad9398","10.1126/scirobotics.abj2908"],"focus":["control architecture","tissue tracking","safety constraints","human supervision"]}
```  
[result]: Shademan et al. demonstrated supervised autonomous soft-tissue suturing/anastomosis using 3D vision, tissue tracking, planning, and robot execution, reporting more consistent stitch placement than manual/teleoperated baselines. Saeidi et al. extended STAR to laparoscopic in vivo porcine intestinal anastomosis with autonomous plan updates under human supervision, showing that autonomy can improve consistency but still depends on reliable perception and intervention when the scene changes.

Rationale: I then read safety/shared-control work to connect autonomous execution with surgeon intent and hard safety envelopes.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["10.1109/TRO.2013.2292530","10.1177/0278364913490324","arXiv:1503.07619"],"focus":["virtual fixtures","active constraints","policy blending","intent inference","shared autonomy"]}
```  
[result]: Bowyer et al. survey active constraints/virtual fixtures as a mature way to encode forbidden regions, guidance surfaces, and task corridors for robot-assisted surgery. Dragan & Srinivasa formalize policy blending for shared control, while Javdani et al. infer user goals to assist action selection; both suggest that assistance should scale with confidence in inferred intent, not override ambiguous human input.

Rationale: I extracted a design gap: prior surgical autonomy emphasizes task execution and tissue tracking, while shared-control work emphasizes intent responsiveness, but they are rarely unified with real-time tissue-safety constraints.  
```action
{"tool":"genome_diff","paper_ids":["10.1126/scitranslmed.aad9398","10.1126/scirobotics.abj2908","10.1109/TRO.2013.2292530","10.1177/0278364913490324"],"target_gap":"precision + tissue safety + surgeon intent in autonomous surgical robot control"}
```  
[result]: Gap identified: combine task-level autonomous suturing/planning with low-level constrained optimal control where virtual fixtures, force/strain budgets, and intent-confidence-based arbitration jointly determine motion. Existing methods typically optimize one axis—autonomous consistency, safety constraints, or intent following—rather than making their tradeoff explicit at control frequency.

```propose
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Use a hierarchical shared-autonomy controller: a task planner proposes surgical motion primitives such as needle driving, knot tightening, retraction, or cutting; a real-time constrained MPC/CBF layer executes them under tissue-safety constraints; and an intent-inference arbitration layer blends autonomous commands with surgeon input according to confidence. The low-level controller optimizes a weighted objective over tool-tip tracking error, smoothness/latency, estimated tissue strain, contact force, distance from no-go anatomy, and agreement with inferred surgeon intent. Safety is enforced with hard constraints from virtual fixtures, force limits, strain-rate limits, and control-barrier functions around vessels, bowel walls, and trocar/kinematic limits.",
  "niche_genome": "Autonomous or semi-autonomous robotic soft-tissue surgery, especially suturing, anastomosis, dissection near critical anatomy, and supervised laparoscopic procedures where the robot must be precise but the surgeon must remain able to redirect or override behavior. The strategy is most suitable for platforms with stereo/3D vision, tool tracking, force or force-estimation capability, and a surgeon console input stream.",
  "observation_genome": "STAR-style systems showed that supervised autonomy with 3D perception and plan updates can produce consistent soft-tissue suturing/anastomosis outcomes, while active constraints/virtual fixtures are a proven method for keeping surgical robots inside safe motion corridors. Shared-control literature shows that assistance should increase when operator intent is confidently inferred and decrease when intent is ambiguous. Therefore, a unified controller should treat precision, safety, and intent responsiveness as simultaneous terms, with safety as hard constraints and intent/precision as confidence-weighted objectives.",
  "limitation_genome": "The approach depends on reliable real-time tissue state estimation; poor segmentation, occlusion, bleeding, smoke, tissue deformation, or missing force sensing could cause either unsafe autonomy or overly conservative behavior. Surgeon intent inference can fail under ambiguous gestures or emergency corrections, so the design must include immediate manual override, confidence thresholds, and fail-safe retreat modes. Clinical deployment would require extensive validation because CBF/MPC guarantees are only as good as the perception and tissue-damage models feeding them.",
  "delta_genome": "Compared with prior STAR-style autonomous execution, the delta is to make surgeon intent an explicit control input rather than only a supervisory interrupt. Compared with virtual fixtures alone, the delta is adaptive constraint weighting and autonomous task progress inside the safe set. Compared with generic shared autonomy, the delta is tissue-specific hard safety constraints—force, strain, no-go anatomy, and suture geometry—embedded directly in the control loop.",
  "claim_genome": "A confidence-aware constrained shared-autonomy controller can improve autonomous surgical robot performance by maintaining submillimeter task precision when perception and intent are clear, reducing tissue trauma through hard safety envelopes, and preserving responsiveness by yielding authority to the surgeon whenever intent confidence drops or override input exceeds a threshold."
}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Develop a control strategy for autonomous surgical robots that balances precision, tissue safety, and responsiveness to surgeon intent.

content:
rationale: I first need anchor papers that specifically connect microswimmer/microrobot navigation in flows with learned or adaptive control, so the genome fields are not invented in isolation. I’ll search for reinforcement-learning and closed-loop control work where the policy state/action representation is explicit.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"microswimmer microrobot flow navigation reinforcement learning local vorticity shear control policy","discipline":"robotics_control","k":5}
```  
[result]: Found relevant candidates: Calabrese et al., “Flow Navigation by Smart Microswimmers via Reinforcement Learning,” Phys. Rev. Lett. 118, 158004, DOI:10.1103/PhysRevLett.118.158004; Muiños-Landin et al., “Reinforcement learning with artificial microswimmers,” Sci. Rep. 11, 6677, DOI:10.1038/s41598-021-85957-3; and Yang et al., “Optimal navigation of microswimmers in complex and noisy environments,” Commun. Phys. 4, 230, DOI:10.1038/s42005-021-00745-3.
rationale: Calabrese et al. is the closest seed because it encodes navigation using local hydrodynamic cues and learned actions rather than assuming global path planning. I’ll read it to extract the sensing/action abstractions that can become genome components.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"DOI:10.1103/PhysRevLett.118.158004"}
```  
[result]: The paper models smart microswimmers in Poiseuille and turbulent flows, using reinforcement learning policies based on local observables such as orientation and flow/vorticity cues. Actions are changes to swimming direction/strategy, and learned policies exploit flow structures to migrate upstream or reach targets more effectively than naive swimming. Limitations include simplified swimmer dynamics, idealized sensing, and simulated rather than hardware-evolved policies.
rationale: I also need a hardware-adjacent/artificial-microswimmer reference to ground actuation timing and feasible low-dimensional controls, not only idealized simulated swimmers. This paper is useful because it treats artificial active particles with RL-style control under experimentally relevant constraints.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"DOI:10.1038/s41598-021-85957-3"}
```  
[result]: Muiños-Landin et al. demonstrate reinforcement learning for artificial microswimmers using discrete control policies over propulsion/orientation actions in noisy microscopic environments. The work emphasizes sparse/limited sensing, stochastic dynamics, and learning action choices that improve navigation despite Brownian-like perturbations. It does not directly evolve compact gene-like controllers with explicit actuation phase timing or flow-threshold rules.
rationale: Now I’ll distill the retrieved papers into a genome-style representation, keeping only mechanisms relevant to actuation timing, sensing thresholds, and flow adaptation. This gives a baseline genome before proposing the evolved-control-policy variant.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["DOI:10.1103/PhysRevLett.118.158004","DOI:10.1038/s41598-021-85957-3"],"schema":"gene_genome","focus":"control policies for microswimmer/microrobot navigation in fluid flows"}
```  
[result]: Extracted baseline: mechanism = RL/discrete policy maps from local flow or orientation state to propulsion/orientation actions; niche = microscale swimmers in Poiseuille/turbulent/noisy environments; observation = local vorticity/orientation cues enable better-than-naive navigation; limitation = idealized sensing, simulation-heavy validation, weak encoding of actuation phase/duty-cycle constraints; delta opportunity = compact evolvable policies with thresholds and event-triggered gait timing for real microrobots.
rationale: Before proposing, I’ll check whether the specific combination—genetic policy encoding with actuation timing genes, sensing thresholds, and flow-adaptive behavioral motifs—is already covered by the candidates. This avoids simply restating RL microswimmer navigation.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","paper_ids":["DOI:10.1103/PhysRevLett.118.158004","DOI:10.1038/s41598-021-85957-3","DOI:10.1038/s42005-021-00745-3"],"claim":"Evolve compact microrobot control-policy genomes that encode actuation timing, hydrodynamic sensing thresholds, and flow-adaptive behavior switching for navigation in fluid flows."}
```  
[result]: The claim is adjacent to prior RL and optimal-navigation microswimmer work but appears distinct in its emphasis on an explicit evolvable controller genome with interpretable timing/threshold genes. Prior work learns policies from local cues, but generally does not represent hardware-facing actuation phase/duty-cycle schedules and threshold-triggered flow behaviors as a compact genotype.
```propose
{
  "mechanism_genome": {
    "policy_encoding": "Evolvable compact controller genome for microrobots in flow, represented as an event-triggered finite-state policy rather than a black-box neural controller.",
    "actuation_timing_genes": [
      "stroke_phase_offset",
      "beat_frequency",
      "duty_cycle",
      "burst_duration",
      "coast_duration",
      "turn_pulse_width",
      "reorientation_interval"
    ],
    "sensing_threshold_genes": [
      "vorticity_threshold",
      "shear_rate_threshold",
      "flow_speed_threshold",
      "heading_error_threshold",
      "wall_distance_threshold",
      "upstream_progress_threshold"
    ],
    "flow_adaptive_behavior_genes": [
      "rheotaxis_mode: align against local flow when flow_speed exceeds threshold",
      "shear_avoidance_mode: reduce lateral drift or switch gait near high shear",
      "vorticity_exploitation_mode: time turns with favorable local rotation",
      "burst_coast_mode: save actuation energy in assisting flow",
      "escape_mode: trigger high-frequency actuation when trapped in recirculation"
    ],
    "evolutionary_operator": "Mutate and recombine timing parameters, thresholds, and behavior-transition rules; select policies by navigation success, energy efficiency, and robustness across randomized flow fields."
  },
  "niche_genome": {
    "domain": "Microrobots or artificial microswimmers navigating low-Reynolds-number or weakly inertial fluid environments.",
    "environments": [
      "Poiseuille channel flow",
      "microvascular-like branching channels",
      "vortical or recirculating flow fields",
      "noisy Brownian-scale environments",
      "time-varying flow disturbances"
    ],
    "prior_grounding": [
      "Calabrese et al., DOI:10.1103/PhysRevLett.118.158004, showed that smart microswimmers can use local flow/vorticity information and learned policies for improved navigation.",
      "Muiños-Landin et al., DOI:10.1038/s41598-021-85957-3, showed reinforcement learning with artificial microswimmers under noisy and constrained sensing/action conditions."
    ]
  },
  "observation_genome": {
    "empirical_basis": "Prior microswimmer reinforcement-learning studies indicate that local hydrodynamic cues can be sufficient for useful navigation strategies, including upstream migration or target seeking in structured flows.",
    "key_observation": "The actionable control variables for physical microrobots are often not abstract steering commands alone, but actuation timing, gait phase, pulse duration, and threshold-based switching under limited sensing.",
    "measurable_policy_variables": [
      "local flow direction",
      "estimated vorticity",
      "estimated shear",
      "heading relative to flow",
      "progress toward target",
      "proximity to wall or boundary"
    ]
  },
  "limitation_genome": {
    "prior_limitations": [
      "Many learned microswimmer policies assume idealized local sensing or simulation-only dynamics.",
      "RL policies are often represented as action tables or neural policies that are hard to transfer directly to hardware actuation primitives.",
      "Existing work does not strongly separate interpretable genes for timing, thresholds, and adaptive flow behaviors.",
      "Policies may overfit to one flow regime, such as a fixed Poiseuille profile or synthetic turbulence field."
    ],
    "deployment_risks": [
      "Microscale sensing of shear or vorticity may be noisy and indirect.",
      "Magnetic, acoustic, chemical, or optical actuation hardware may impose strict bandwidth and duty-cycle limits.",
      "Brownian perturbations and wall effects can degrade threshold-triggered behaviors."
    ]
  },
  "delta_genome": {
    "innovation": "Convert learned microrobot flow-navigation policies into an explicit gene genome: timing genes determine how propulsion is pulsed, sensing-threshold genes determine when state transitions occur, and behavior genes determine which flow-adaptive gait is expressed.",
    "why_it_matters": "This makes policies interpretable, evolvable, and more compatible with real microrobot actuation constraints than generic RL action policies.",
    "experimental_delta": [
      "Evolve genomes in randomized simulated channel and vortical flows.",
      "Select for multi-objective fitness: target-reaching, upstream progress, low energy, and robustness to sensor noise.",
      "Transfer best genomes to hardware-like simulations with actuation bandwidth, duty-cycle, and noisy local-flow estimates.",
      "Compare against naive constant swimming, hand-coded rheotaxis, and standard RL policies."
    ]
  },
  "claim_genome": {
    "central_claim": "A compact evolvable controller genome encoding actuation timing, hydrodynamic sensing thresholds, and flow-adaptive behavior switching can produce robust microrobot navigation policies in fluid flows while remaining more interpretable and hardware-transferable than unconstrained learned policies.",
    "testable_predictions": [
      "Threshold-triggered vorticity exploitation will outperform constant-heading swimming in recirculating flows.",
      "Duty-cycle and burst-coast timing genes will reduce energy use in assisting flows without sacrificing target arrival rate.",
      "Policies evolved across randomized flow fields will generalize better than policies trained in a single fixed flow profile.",
      "Interpretable timing and threshold genes will reveal reusable motifs such as upstream alignment, shear avoidance, and vortex escape."
    ]
  }
}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Generate a gene_genome output for evolving control policies in microrobots navigating fluid flows. Represent actuation timing, sensing thresholds, and flow-adaptive behaviors.
Study adaptive control for space robots performing on-orbit servicing with uncertain inertial parameters and free-floating base dynamics.
Create an idea_plan for verifying neural network controllers used in safety-critical robotic systems. Include formal methods, falsification tests, and runtime monitoring.

content:
rationale (1-2 sentences): I’ll first identify control papers that explicitly connect jumping/agile legged motion with impulse generation, aerial attitude, and terrain/landing constraints, rather than only mechanism design. This gives us grounded mechanisms and gaps for a new control method.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"energy efficient jumping robot control takeoff impulse aerial posture landing stability variable terrain","discipline":"robotics_control","top_k":5}
```
[result]: Candidates included Haldane et al. “Salto-1P: A Robotic Platform for Research in Dynamic Jumping” (ICRA 2017, paper_id: `10.1109/ICRA.2017.7989543`), Yim et al. “Dynamic Locomotion in the MIT Cheetah 3 Through Convex Model-Predictive Control” (IROS 2019, paper_id: `10.1109/IROS40897.2019.8968055`), and Jenelten et al. “Parkour and Free Running by a Quadrupedal Robot” (Science Robotics 2023, paper_id: `10.1126/scirobotics.adi7566`). Search also surfaced older SLIP/Raibert hopping control work, but the newer papers better match aerial/terrain-aware control.

rationale (1-2 sentences): I’ll read the two most relevant exemplars: Salto-1P for repeated jumping with explicit energy storage and aerial orientation issues, and quadruped parkour for modern terrain-conditioned jumping/landing policies. Together they cover impulse regulation and terrain variability but likely leave room for unified energy-optimal phase control.
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["10.1109/ICRA.2017.7989543","10.1126/scirobotics.adi7566"],"sections":["abstract","method","experiments","limitations"]}
```
[result]: `10.1109/ICRA.2017.7989543` shows a power-modulating leg and reaction wheel/tail-like attitude control enabling rapid consecutive jumps, with emphasis on energetic amplification and vertical agility. `10.1126/scirobotics.adi7566` uses perception-conditioned policies and model-based components for obstacle-specific parkour maneuvers, demonstrating robust takeoff/landing over diverse obstacles but relying on trained skills rather than an explicit minimum-energy impulse/posture/landing optimizer.

rationale (1-2 sentences): I’ll distill the retrieved papers into a control “genome” so the final proposal is anchored in observed mechanisms, assumptions, and limitations. The extraction focuses on control abstractions, not just hardware.
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["10.1109/ICRA.2017.7989543","10.1126/scirobotics.adi7566"],"schema":"gene_genome"}
```
[result]: Extracted mechanisms: elastic energy storage/power modulation for takeoff, inertial attitude actuation during flight, terrain-conditioned skill selection, and landing stabilization through legged whole-body control. Extracted limitations: takeoff energy, aerial posture, and landing robustness are often handled as separate modules; energy optimality and uncertainty-aware landing constraints are not jointly optimized across the full jump.

rationale (1-2 sentences): I’ll check whether a unified controller that co-optimizes takeoff impulse, aerial attitude, and landing capture under terrain uncertainty is already a standard result. Novelty should be framed as integration and objective design, not claiming no one has used MPC or RL for jumping.
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","query":"unified energy optimal jumping robot controller takeoff impulse aerial attitude landing capture terrain uncertainty MPC reinforcement learning","against_paper_ids":["10.1109/ICRA.2017.7989543","10.1126/scirobotics.adi7566","10.1109/IROS40897.2019.8968055"]}
```
[result]: No exact match was found for a single controller that jointly optimizes pre-takeoff impulse, mid-air inertial posture, and probabilistic landing stability with an explicit energy objective. Related work covers elastic jumping hardware, terrain-conditioned parkour skills, and convex MPC for legged locomotion, suggesting novelty in a phase-coupled, energy-aware controller with learned terrain-conditioned terminal sets.

rationale (1-2 sentences): Based on the evidence, I’ll propose a control gene that combines model-based phase optimization with learned terrain/landing feasibility, targeting energy-efficient jumps over uncertain terrain. The claim is intentionally bounded: unified regulation and evaluation, not a new robot morphology.
```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","proposal":{"mechanism_genome":"Phase-coupled energy-aware jump control: a receding-horizon optimizer selects takeoff impulse magnitude/direction, aerial body angular momentum allocation, and landing leg impedance as one hybrid trajectory. The controller uses a reduced-order flight model plus actuator/elastic-storage limits, with a learned terrain-conditioned terminal landing-feasibility set that constrains touchdown pose, velocity, foot placement, and capture margin.","niche_genome":"Energy-efficient legged/jumping robots operating over variable terrain where each jump must trade height/range, battery or spring energy, body orientation, and stable landing probability. Best suited to monopod, biped, or quadruped platforms with elastic legs and some inertial/aerial attitude authority such as reaction wheels, tails, or limb redistribution.","observation_genome":"Retrieved work shows the needed pieces exist separately: Salto-1P demonstrates elastic power modulation and aerial attitude control for agile consecutive jumps (`10.1109/ICRA.2017.7989543`), while quadruped parkour demonstrates perception-conditioned jumping and landing over varied obstacles (`10.1126/scirobotics.adi7566`). The gap is that takeoff impulse, aerial posture, and landing stabilization are usually modular or skill-based rather than jointly optimized with an explicit energy objective.","limitation_genome":"The approach depends on accurate enough reduced-order jump dynamics, terrain state estimation, and calibrated landing-feasibility predictions; severe perception errors or unmodeled impacts can still cause failure. Real-time solution may require warm-started MPC, offline libraries, or learned surrogates, especially for high-DOF quadrupeds.","delta_genome":"Instead of commanding a jump height/range first and stabilizing posture/landing afterward, optimize the full jump as a constrained energy budget: spend only the impulse needed to reach a terrain-conditioned capture set, use minimal aerial attitude correction to maintain touchdown feasibility, and adapt landing impedance before impact. This directly targets lower cost of transport per successful jump while preserving robustness across terrain uncertainty.","claim_genome":"A unified energy-aware hybrid MPC with learned terrain-conditioned landing constraints should reduce energy per successful jump and improve landing stability compared with modular takeoff-plus-attitude-plus-landing controllers, particularly on variable terrain where over-jumping for safety is common."}}
```
[result]: Proposal generated in `gene_genome` schema and grounded in paper_ids `10.1109/ICRA.2017.7989543`, `10.1126/scirobotics.adi7566`, and related MPC context `10.1109/IROS40897.2019.8968055`. The novelty is the explicit cross-phase energy/landing-feasibility coupling.
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Phase-coupled energy-aware jump control: a receding-horizon optimizer selects takeoff impulse magnitude/direction, aerial body angular momentum allocation, and landing leg impedance as one hybrid trajectory. The controller uses a reduced-order flight model plus actuator/elastic-storage limits, with a learned terrain-conditioned terminal landing-feasibility set that constrains touchdown pose, velocity, foot placement, and capture margin.",
  "niche_genome": "Energy-efficient legged/jumping robots operating over variable terrain where each jump must trade height/range, battery or spring energy, body orientation, and stable landing probability. Best suited to monopod, biped, or quadruped platforms with elastic legs and some inertial/aerial attitude authority such as reaction wheels, tails, or limb redistribution.",
  "observation_genome": "Retrieved work shows the needed pieces exist separately: Salto-1P demonstrates elastic power modulation and aerial attitude control for agile consecutive jumps (`10.1109/ICRA.2017.7989543`), while quadruped parkour demonstrates perception-conditioned jumping and landing over varied obstacles (`10.1126/scirobotics.adi7566`). The gap is that takeoff impulse, aerial posture, and landing stabilization are usually modular or skill-based rather than jointly optimized with an explicit energy objective.",
  "limitation_genome": "The approach depends on accurate enough reduced-order jump dynamics, terrain state estimation, and calibrated landing-feasibility predictions; severe perception errors or unmodeled impacts can still cause failure. Real-time solution may require warm-started MPC, offline libraries, or learned surrogates, especially for high-DOF quadrupeds.",
  "delta_genome": "Instead of commanding a jump height/range first and stabilizing posture/landing afterward, optimize the full jump as a constrained energy budget: spend only the impulse needed to reach a terrain-conditioned capture set, use minimal aerial attitude correction to maintain touchdown feasibility, and adapt landing impedance before impact. This directly targets lower cost of transport per successful jump while preserving robustness across terrain uncertainty.",
  "claim_genome": "A unified energy-aware hybrid MPC with learned terrain-conditioned landing constraints should reduce energy per successful jump and improve landing stability compared with modular takeoff-plus-attitude-plus-landing controllers, particularly on variable terrain where over-jumping for safety is common."
}

prompt.full_prompt: Explore control methods for energy-efficient jumping robots that must regulate takeoff impulse, aerial posture, and landing stability across variable terrain.
Propose an idea_plan for integrating tactile sensing into dexterous manipulation control for fragile or deformable objects.

Investigate how transformer-based architectures can be optimized for low-latency inference on edge devices without significant accuracy loss. Include evaluation metrics for energy consumption, memory footprint, and task performance.

content:
I’ll ground the direction in recent SE-agent reliability evidence: real-world repair benchmarks expose localization/validation failures, while code-review pretraining shows task-specific feedback signals can be learned.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"LLM software engineering reliability code review bug localization test generation SWE-bench CodeReviewer Agentless","discipline":"computer_science","k":5}
```  
[result]: Found relevant candidates: `arXiv:2310.06770` SWE-bench, `arXiv:2407.01489` Agentless, `arXiv:2405.15793` SWE-agent, and `arXiv:2203.09095` CodeReviewer. The strongest recurring issue is not raw code generation alone, but unreliable localization, overconfident edits, and weak validation against tests/review criteria.

I’ll read SWE-bench because it is the clearest benchmark for repository-level bug localization and repair under realistic issue descriptions.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:2310.06770","sections":["abstract","benchmark construction","experiments","limitations"]}
```  
[result]: SWE-bench evaluates LMs on fixing real GitHub issues from Python repositories by modifying code and passing held-out tests. Results show substantial difficulty for repository-scale reasoning, especially finding the right files/functions and producing patches that satisfy tests without regressions.

I’ll read Agentless because it decomposes SE repair into localization, repair, and patch selection, which is directly relevant to making systems more reliable rather than just larger.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:2407.01489","sections":["method","localization","repair","evaluation"]}
```  
[result]: Agentless reports competitive SWE-bench performance using a simplified pipeline: hierarchical fault localization, candidate patch generation, and test-based selection. This suggests reliability can improve through structured decomposition, multiple candidates, and validation rather than unconstrained autonomous agents.

I’ll extract the design pattern from these papers and add the code-review signal from CodeReviewer to cover review-quality and test-generation settings, not just patch success.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["arXiv:2310.06770","arXiv:2407.01489","arXiv:2203.09095"],"schema":"gene_genome"}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = repository-aware decomposition plus learned review/repair signals; niche = realistic SE tasks with hidden tests and human-like review comments; observation = localization and validation bottlenecks dominate reliability; limitation = most systems optimize final pass rate, not calibrated confidence, evidence traceability, or cross-task consistency; delta = add explicit uncertainty, evidence, and adversarial validation across code review, localization, and test generation.

I’ll check novelty of the resulting direction: a reliability layer that jointly trains evidence-grounded localization, review critique, and test-generation validation with calibrated abstention.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"A calibrated evidence-grounded reliability layer for LLM software engineering agents that jointly predicts bug locations, review rationales, candidate patches, and test obligations, then uses generated and existing tests plus uncertainty-based abstention to decide whether to act.","against":["arXiv:2310.06770","arXiv:2407.01489","arXiv:2203.09095","arXiv:2405.15793"]}
```  
[result]: Novel relative to the retrieved papers: prior work benchmarks repair, decomposes agent workflows, or trains code-review models, but does not centrally optimize calibrated reliability across bug localization, code review, and test generation with explicit evidence traces, abstention, and cross-task consistency checks.
```propose
{
  "Idea": "Build a Reliability-Oriented Software Engineering LLM, or ROSE-LLM, that treats code review, bug localization, patching, and test generation as mutually checking subtasks rather than independent generation tasks. The core hypothesis is that LLM reliability improves when the model must: (1) cite repository evidence for each claim, (2) localize faults before proposing edits, (3) generate tests that would fail before and pass after the edit, and (4) abstain or escalate when confidence is poorly calibrated. This direction is grounded in SWE-bench findings that repository-scale repair is bottlenecked by localization and validation, Agentless evidence that decomposed localization-repair-selection pipelines are effective, and CodeReviewer-style evidence that review feedback can be learned from large-scale code-review data.",
  "ImplementationSteps": {
    "Hypotheses": [
      "H1: Explicit evidence-grounded fault localization will improve downstream patch correctness compared with direct patch generation.",
      "H2: Jointly training code review, bug localization, and test-generation objectives will reduce hallucinated fixes because each task supplies constraints for the others.",
      "H3: Calibration and abstention will improve practical reliability: the system should know when not to submit a patch or when to request human review.",
      "H4: Generated tests are most useful when optimized as validators of specific localized hypotheses, not as generic coverage-increasing tests."
    ],
    "SystemDesign": [
      "Use a retrieval layer over repository files, issue text, stack traces, previous commits, tests, and review comments.",
      "Stage 1: Evidence retrieval and hierarchical bug localization at repository, file, function, and line levels.",
      "Stage 2: Generate structured review findings: suspected defect, supporting code evidence, affected behavior, risk level, and confidence.",
      "Stage 3: Generate candidate patches conditioned on localized evidence and review rationale.",
      "Stage 4: Generate targeted tests that encode the hypothesized failure mode, including regression tests for the proposed patch.",
      "Stage 5: Run existing and generated tests, rank candidates by test outcomes, static-analysis signals, evidence consistency, and calibrated confidence.",
      "Stage 6: Abstain when evidence is weak, candidate patches disagree, tests are inconclusive, or confidence is below a deployment threshold."
    ],
    "TrainingSetup": [
      "Supervised fine-tuning on code-review datasets such as CodeReviewer-style review-comment and code-change pairs.",
      "Instruction tuning on repository-level issue-to-patch trajectories from SWE-bench-style data.",
      "Multi-task objectives for localization, review comment generation, patch generation, and test generation.",
      "Contrastive training with negative patches: teach the model to distinguish plausible-looking but incorrect fixes.",
      "Calibration training using held-out validation outcomes, patch success labels, and uncertainty estimation methods such as temperature scaling or conformal prediction.",
      "Optional reinforcement learning or preference optimization using reward signals from passing hidden tests, minimal patch size, review usefulness, and low regression risk."
    ],
    "ExperimentalSetup": [
      "Compare ROSE-LLM against direct-prompting baselines, agentic repair baselines, Agentless-style decomposed repair, and SWE-agent-style tool-using agents.",
      "Run ablations removing evidence citations, localization, generated tests, calibration, and abstention.",
      "Evaluate both fully autonomous mode and human-in-the-loop mode where the model can escalate uncertain cases.",
      "Measure whether generated tests actually expose the original bug by checking fail-before/pass-after behavior.",
      "Analyze failure modes: wrong file localization, overbroad patches, test overfitting, hallucinated APIs, flaky generated tests, and false confidence."
    ]
  },
  "ImplementationOrder": [
    "Construct unified benchmark instances linking issue descriptions, repository snapshots, patches, tests, and code-review-style rationales.",
    "Implement retrieval and hierarchical localization over repository context.",
    "Train or prompt the model to emit structured evidence-backed review findings before generating patches.",
    "Add targeted test-generation conditioned on the localized failure hypothesis.",
    "Integrate patch ranking using existing tests, generated tests, static checks, and confidence calibration.",
    "Add abstention and escalation policies.",
    "Run benchmark evaluation, ablations, and human assessment of review usefulness."
  ],
  "Dataset": {
    "PrimaryBenchmarks": [
      "SWE-bench for realistic GitHub issue resolution and repository-level patch validation.",
      "SWE-bench Verified for higher-quality human-filtered repair evaluation.",
      "Defects4J for controlled Java bug localization, patching, and regression testing.",
      "BugsInPy for Python bug localization and repair.",
      "CodeReviewer-style code-review datasets for review-comment and code-change supervision."
    ],
    "AdditionalData": [
      "Repository commit histories with linked issues and pull requests.",
      "CI logs, stack traces, and failing test outputs.",
      "Generated negative patches and adversarially perturbed issue descriptions for robustness evaluation."
    ]
  },
  "EvaluationMetrics": {
    "BugLocalization": [
      "Top-k file localization accuracy",
      "Top-k function or line localization accuracy",
      "Mean reciprocal rank of true faulty location",
      "Evidence precision: fraction of cited code spans relevant to the real bug"
    ],
    "PatchCorrectness": [
      "Resolved-instance rate on SWE-bench-style benchmarks",
      "Pass rate on hidden tests",
      "Regression rate on existing tests",
      "Patch minimality and maintainability judged by static metrics or human review"
    ],
    "CodeReview": [
      "Review finding precision and recall",
      "Usefulness rating by human developers",
      "False-positive rate for alleged bugs",
      "Agreement with accepted human review comments"
    ],
    "TestGeneration": [
      "Fail-before/pass-after rate",
      "Mutation score",
      "Branch or statement coverage increase",
      "Flakiness rate",
      "Bug-specific assertion quality"
    ],
    "ReliabilityAndCalibration": [
      "Expected calibration error",
      "Brier score",
      "Selective accuracy under abstention",
      "Risk-coverage curve",
      "Rate of high-confidence wrong patches",
      "Human escalation precision: whether abstained cases are genuinely uncertain or risky"
    ]
  },
  "ExpectedOutcome": "The expected result is a more reliable software-engineering LLM that does not merely generate code, but produces auditable evidence, localized hypotheses, review rationales, candidate fixes, and validating tests. Success would be demonstrated by higher SWE-bench and Defects4J repair rates, better localization accuracy, useful generated regression tests, and significantly fewer high-confidence wrong answers. The most important practical outcome is selective reliability: when the model acts, it should be more likely to be correct; when it is uncertain, it should abstain with a clear explanation and evidence trail for a human developer."
}
```
