content:
I’ll first identify the core observational literature on sub-solar compact-binary searches, since detector adaptations should be anchored in what LIGO/Virgo have already changed or found limiting. Then I’ll read the most relevant search papers and distill a proposal.  

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"LIGO Virgo subsolar mass compact binary primordial black hole merger search matched filtering below one solar mass arXiv","discipline":"physics","max_results":5}
```  

[result]: Found candidates including Abbott et al. “Search for Subsolar-Mass Ultracompact Binaries in Advanced LIGO’s Second Observing Run” (arXiv:1904.08976), Abbott et al. “Search for subsolar-mass binaries in the first half of Advanced LIGO and Virgo’s third observing run” (arXiv:2109.12197), and Nitz & Wang subsolar/high-mass-ratio PBH searches (arXiv:2102.00868). These papers consistently frame sub-solar mergers as a primordial-black-hole target requiring expanded low-mass template banks and careful high-frequency/noise treatment.  

The O2 LIGO paper is a baseline for how existing detectors were adapted: it documents template-bank mass ranges, duration/computing issues, and astrophysical/PBH interpretation.  

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:1904.08976","sections":["abstract","methods","results","discussion"]}
```  

[result]: Abbott et al. searched Advanced LIGO O2 data for ultracompact binaries with component masses below 1 solar mass using matched filtering with sub-solar template banks. No significant candidates were found; the work set merger-rate upper limits and PBH dark-matter-fraction constraints, while noting the long waveform durations and large template banks as key practical costs.  

The O3a paper updates the state of the art and is useful for identifying what detector/pipeline improvements mattered with later sensitivity and Virgo participation.  

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:2109.12197","sections":["abstract","search","detector characterization","results","constraints"]}
```  

[result]: Abbott et al. extended sub-solar compact-binary searches in O3a, improving sensitivity and constraints relative to earlier runs but again finding no confident detections. The search relied on matched filtering over low-mass templates, data-quality vetoes, ranking-statistic background estimation, and interpretation in terms of PBH abundance; sensitivity remained limited by high-frequency detector noise, non-Gaussian artifacts, and the computational burden of very long, dense template banks.  

To make the proposal less incremental, I’ll compare the retrieved genome against a detector-adaptation direction: high-frequency optimization plus long-duration early-warning-style matched filtering for sub-solar PBHs.  

```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","paper_ids":["arXiv:1904.08976","arXiv:2109.12197","arXiv:2102.00868"],"idea":"Adapt ground-based gravitational-wave detectors and searches for <1 solar-mass primordial black-hole mergers by combining sub-solar template banks, high-frequency sensitivity optimization/narrowband readout, long-duration hierarchical matched filtering, and PBH-specific population inference."}
```  

[result]: The individual elements are known—sub-solar matched-filter searches, high-frequency calibration/noise considerations, and PBH abundance limits—but the integrated detector-and-analysis adaptation is not presented as a single operational program in the checked papers. The most novel emphasis is co-designing high-frequency detector tuning and computationally tractable long-duration pipelines specifically around <1 solar-mass PBH merger signatures.  

```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","context":{"retrieved_papers":["arXiv:1904.08976","arXiv:2109.12197","arXiv:2102.00868"],"discipline":"physics"}}
```  

[result]:  
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Adapt existing ground-based interferometers for sub-solar primordial-black-hole searches by co-designing detector configuration and analysis: extend compact-binary matched-filter template banks below 1 M_sun, retain long inspiral waveforms in band, push usable analysis bandwidth into the kHz regime where sub-solar mergers accumulate late inspiral power, and use hierarchical/accelerated filtering plus stricter glitch rejection to manage the large template count and long signal durations.",
  "niche_genome": "Targets compact binaries with component masses below one solar mass, a mass range not expected for ordinary stellar-origin black holes and therefore highly diagnostic of primordial black holes or other exotic compact objects. The approach is most relevant to Advanced LIGO/Virgo/KAGRA and next observing runs where incremental high-frequency sensitivity, calibration, and data-quality improvements can improve PBH merger-rate limits even without a detection.",
  "observation_genome": "LIGO/Virgo sub-solar searches in O2 and O3a, including arXiv:1904.08976 and arXiv:2109.12197, already demonstrate that standard interferometers can be repurposed for <1 M_sun compact binaries using sub-solar matched filtering and PBH population interpretation. These searches found no confident candidates but produced upper limits on merger rates and PBH dark-matter fraction, while identifying long waveform durations, dense template banks, high-frequency noise, and non-Gaussian artifacts as limiting factors.",
  "limitation_genome": "Sensitivity is constrained by detector noise above several hundred Hz to kHz, calibration/data-quality systematics in the high-frequency band, and the computational expense of filtering months of data with long low-mass waveforms. Template assumptions may also miss PBH-relevant effects such as eccentricity, broad mass functions, high mass ratios, or nonstandard compact-object tidal signatures.",
  "delta_genome": "Move from an analysis-only sub-solar search to a detector-analysis co-optimization: schedule or evaluate high-frequency-tuned readout configurations, apply frequency-dependent squeezing or narrowband sensitivity studies where feasible, build reduced-order or multistage template banks for 0.05–1 M_sun systems, and perform injection campaigns that explicitly optimize detection efficiency versus false-alarm rate for PBH mass functions rather than generic ultracompact binaries.",
  "claim_genome": "A coordinated high-frequency and long-duration matched-filter adaptation of gravitational-wave detectors could substantially improve searches for primordial black-hole mergers below one solar mass, either enabling a nearly background-free exotic compact-object discovery or tightening PBH abundance constraints beyond current O2/O3a sub-solar limits."
} 
```ייש

prompt.full_prompt: Investigate how gravitational-wave detectors could be adapted to search for signatures of primordial black hole mergers below one solar mass.

content:
I’ll ground the “gene genome” in experimentally established ingredients: moiré Hofstadter spectra, interaction-driven Chern phases, and fractional Chern insulators. Then I’ll identify the underexplored delta for a program specifically targeting strong-field topological phases.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"moiré heterostructures strong magnetic field Hofstadter Chern insulator fractional Chern insulator graphene hBN twisted bilayer graphene","discipline":"physics","max_results":5}
```  
[result]: Found candidates including Dean et al. Nature 2013 “Hofstadter’s butterfly and the fractal quantum Hall effect in moiré superlattices” (arXiv:1212.4783), Spanton et al. Science 2018 “Observation of fractional Chern insulators in a van der Waals heterostructure” (arXiv:1706.06116), and Sharpe et al. Science 2019 “Emergent ferromagnetism near three-quarters filling in twisted bilayer graphene” (arXiv:1901.03520).  

The graphene/hBN experiments are key because they show that large moiré periods make one flux quantum per unit cell experimentally reachable, producing Hofstadter minibands with quantized Chern structure.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:1212.4783","sections":["abstract","main results","discussion"]}
```  
[result]: Dean et al. report transport signatures of Hofstadter’s butterfly in graphene aligned with hBN, including fractal quantum Hall states when magnetic flux through a moiré unit cell is a rational fraction of the flux quantum. The work establishes the strong-field moiré regime as a practical platform for Chern minibands governed by the Diophantine equation.  

The interaction side is essential: flat moiré minibands in magnetic field can host fractionalized topological order rather than only single-particle Hofstadter gaps.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:1706.06116","sections":["abstract","results","conclusion"]}
```  
[result]: Spanton et al. observe fractional Chern insulator states in graphene/hBN, with fractionally quantized Hall conductance in moiré minibands at high magnetic field. This demonstrates that interactions plus topological moiré minibands can generate fractional topological phases without requiring an ordinary Landau-level-only description.  

To locate the novelty, I compare these established ingredients against the requested program: new topological phases in moiré heterostructures under strong magnetic fields, not merely reproducing known graphene/hBN Hofstadter states.  
```action
{"tool":"genome_diff","paper_ids":["arXiv:1212.4783","arXiv:1706.06116","arXiv:1901.03520"],"target":"program exploring new topological phases in moiré heterostructures under strong magnetic fields"}
```  
[result]: Prior work separately establishes Hofstadter minibands in moiré graphene/hBN, fractional Chern insulators in high-field moiré minibands, and interaction-driven Chern/ferromagnetic phases in twisted bilayer graphene. The opportunity is a systematic, materials-spanning search for strong-field phases where magnetic translation symmetry, valley/spin flavor polarization, displacement-field tuning, and fractional fillings jointly produce new integer, fractional, and symmetry-enriched Chern phases.  

```propose
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Use long-period moiré heterostructures to reach magnetic flux per moiré unit cell of order a rational fraction of the flux quantum, where magnetic Bloch minibands acquire nontrivial Chern numbers via the Hofstadter/Diophantine mechanism. Add strong Coulomb interactions from narrow moiré bands, spin/valley degeneracies, displacement-field or alignment-induced symmetry breaking, and possible strain/relaxation to stabilize interaction-driven integer Chern insulators, fractional Chern insulators, and symmetry-enriched topological phases.",
  "niche_genome": "Strong-field moiré platforms including graphene/hBN, twisted bilayer graphene aligned to hBN, twisted double bilayer graphene, and transition-metal dichalcogenide moiré bilayers, especially at flux ratios phi/phi0 ~ 1/q to p/q and partial fillings of isolated Hofstadter subbands. The most promising regime is where the moiré period is large enough for accessible fields, miniband bandwidths are interaction-scale or smaller, and electric displacement field or twist angle can tune band topology and flavor splitting.",
  "observation_genome": "Dean et al. arXiv:1212.4783 established that graphene/hBN moiré superlattices exhibit Hofstadter butterfly physics and fractal quantum Hall states at rational magnetic flux per moiré unit cell. Spanton et al. arXiv:1706.06116 showed fractional Chern insulators in a van der Waals moiré heterostructure at high magnetic field. Sharpe et al. arXiv:1901.03520 and related twisted-graphene work show that moiré flat bands can support interaction-driven Chern/ferromagnetic phases, indicating that flavor polarization and topology are experimentally tunable ingredients.",
  "limitation_genome": "Known experiments largely map selected graphene-based devices and fillings, while theory often treats either single-particle Hofstadter spectra or simplified interacting limits. There is not yet a comprehensive predictive atlas across material class, twist angle, displacement field, strain, dielectric screening, rational flux, and spin/valley flavor structure. Disorder, lattice relaxation, Landau-level mixing, and competing charge-density-wave or Wigner-crystal tendencies also make it unclear which proposed phases are robust in realistic devices.",
  "delta_genome": "Build a systematic discovery program that combines continuum moiré Hamiltonians in magnetic field, magnetic translation symmetry, Hartree-Fock flavor-order searches, exact diagonalization/DMRG for fractional fillings, and device-realistic disorder/relaxation modeling. The target novelty is to identify robust phase diagrams containing high-Chern-number fractional Chern insulators, field-tuned topological phase transitions between Diophantine sectors, spin/valley symmetry-enriched fractional phases, and re-entrant Chern insulators unique to the interplay of moiré periodicity and strong magnetic field.",
  "claim_genome": "A decisive route to new topological phases is to treat strong magnetic field not as a perturbation on moiré bands, but as a coequal design axis: tune the moiré unit cell so flux commensurability, narrow topological Hofstadter subbands, and interaction-driven flavor polarization coincide. This should produce a searchable family of integer and fractional Chern phases beyond the currently observed graphene/hBN cases, with experimentally testable signatures in quantized Hall conductance, compressibility gaps, nonlocal edge transport, and flux/filling-dependent Diophantine trajectories."
}
``` ||| "mechanism_genome" || "Use long-period moiré heterostructures to reach magnetic flux per moiré unit cell of order a rational fraction of the flux quantum, where magnetic Bloch minibands acquire nontrivial Chern numbers via the Hofstadter/Diophantine mechanism. Add strong Coulomb interactions from narrow moiré bands, spin/valley degeneracies, displacement-field or alignment-induced symmetry breaking, and possible strain/relaxation to stabilize interaction-driven integer Chern insulators, fractional Chern insulators, and symmetry-enriched topological phases." ||| "niche_genome" || "Strong-field moiré platforms including graphene/hBN, twisted bilayer graphene aligned to hBN, twisted double bilayer graphene, and transition-metal dichalcogenide moiré bilayers, especially at flux ratios phi/phi0 ~ 1/q to p/q and partial fillings of isolated Hofstadter subbands. The most promising regime is where the moiré period is large enough for accessible fields, miniband bandwidths are interaction-scale or smaller, and electric displacement field or twist angle can tune band topology and flavor splitting." ||| "observation_genome" || "Dean et al. arXiv:1212.4783 established that graphene/hBN moiré superlattices exhibit Hofstadter butterfly physics and fractal quantum Hall states at rational magnetic flux per moiré unit cell. Spanton et al. arXiv:1706.06116 showed fractional Chern insulators in a van der Waals moiré heterostructure at high magnetic field. Sharpe et al. arXiv:1901.03520 and related twisted-graphene work show that moiré flat bands can support interaction-driven Chern/ferromagnetic phases, indicating that flavor polarization and topology are experimentally tunable ingredients." ||| "limitation_genome" || "Known experiments largely map selected graphene-based devices and fillings, while theory often treats either single-particle Hofstadter spectra or simplified interacting limits. There is not yet a comprehensive predictive atlas across material class, twist angle, displacement field, strain, dielectric screening, rational flux, and spin/valley flavor structure. Disorder, lattice relaxation, Landau-level mixing, and competing charge-density-wave or Wigner-crystal tendencies also make it unclear which proposed phases are robust in realistic devices." ||| "delta_genome" || "Build a systematic discovery program that combines continuum moiré Hamiltonians in magnetic field, magnetic translation symmetry, Hartree-Fock flavor-order searches, exact diagonalization/DMRG for fractional fillings, and device-realistic disorder/relaxation modeling. The target novelty is to identify robust phase diagrams containing high-Chern-number fractional Chern insulators, field-tuned topological phase transitions between Diophantine sectors, spin/valley symmetry-enriched fractional phases, and re-entrant Chern insulators unique to the interplay of moiré periodicity and strong magnetic field." ||| "claim_genome" || "A decisive route to new topological phases is to treat strong magnetic field not as a perturbation on moiré bands, but as a coequal design axis: tune the moiré unit cell so flux commensurability, narrow topological Hofstadter subbands, and interaction-driven flavor polarization coincide. This should produce a searchable family of integer and fractional Chern phases beyond the currently observed graphene/hBN cases, with experimentally testable signatures in quantized Hall conductance, compressibility gaps, nonlocal edge transport, and flux/filling-dependent Diophantine trajectories."

content:
rationale: I first search for recent theoretical and experimental anchors on 2D quantum-turbulence onset, especially work connecting vortex nucleation, inverse cascades, and experimentally observable vortex clustering in quasi-2D Bose gases.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "two-dimensional quantum turbulence onset Bose Einstein condensate vortex nucleation inverse cascade superfluid Reynolds number experiment"
}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers include Reeves et al., PRL 114, 155302, “Identifying a superfluid Reynolds number via dynamical similarity” (`doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.114.155302`); Reeves et al., PRL 110, 104501, “Inverse Energy Cascade in Forced Two-Dimensional Quantum Turbulence” (`doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.110.104501`); Johnstone et al., Science 364, 1267, “Evolution of large-scale flow from turbulence in a two-dimensional superfluid” (`doi:10.1126/science.aat5793`); and Gauthier et al., Science 364, 1264, “Giant vortex clusters in a two-dimensional quantum fluid” (`doi:10.1126/science.aat5718`).

rationale: Reeves et al. provide a theoretical control parameter for onset: a superfluid Reynolds number based on obstacle size, velocity, and quantum circulation. This is directly useful for defining a phase diagram of laminar, vortex-dipole, and turbulent regimes.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.114.155302"
}
```  
[result]: The paper defines a superfluid Reynolds number \(Re_s=(v-v_c)D/\kappa\), where \(v\) is obstacle speed, \(v_c\) critical velocity, \(D\) obstacle diameter, and \(\kappa=h/m\). Numerical Gross-Pitaevskii simulations show dynamical similarity across obstacle sizes and speeds when \(Re_s\) is matched, supporting \(Re_s\) as an onset parameter for 2D quantum wake turbulence.

rationale: I then read an experimental benchmark showing that forced 2D Bose gases can evolve from disordered vortices to large-scale flow, which constrains what the proposed experiment should measure.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "doi:10.1126/science.aat5793"
}
```  
[result]: Johnstone et al. experimentally generated turbulence in a highly oblate atomic Bose-Einstein condensate and observed vortex annihilation, clustering, and emergence of large-scale circulation. The results support an inverse-energy-transfer picture in 2D quantum turbulence and demonstrate that vortex-resolved imaging can quantify transition from random vortex gas to organized flow.

rationale: Before proposing, I check novelty against the retrieved literature: the key gap is not whether 2D quantum turbulence exists, but how to map its onset jointly versus forcing, compressibility, temperature/damping, and BKT-like vortex-pair unbinding.  
```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "paper_ids": [
    "doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.114.155302",
    "doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.110.104501",
    "doi:10.1126/science.aat5793",
    "doi:10.1126/science.aat5718"
  ],
  "idea": "Construct a combined theoretical and experimental onset phase diagram for 2D quantum turbulence in a quasi-2D Bose gas by varying obstacle speed, obstacle size, temperature, and interaction strength; compare Gross-Pitaevskii/truncated-Wigner predictions with vortex-resolved experiments using critical superfluid Reynolds number, vortex-pair production rate, energy spectra, and vortex-clustering statistics."
}
```  
[result]: Related work defines \(Re_s\), demonstrates forced inverse cascades, and observes vortex clustering, but a systematic onset study resolving the boundary between phonon emission, vortex-dipole shedding, vortex-gas turbulence, and clustered inverse-cascade turbulence across temperature and compressibility remains underexplored. The proposed study is incremental but well-grounded and experimentally actionable.

rationale: I now propose an idea_plan that combines Gross-Pitaevskii-based theory, finite-temperature stochastic modeling, and a vortex-resolved cold-atom experiment to identify universal and nonuniversal onset criteria.  
```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "idea_plan",
  "content": {
    "Idea": "Map the onset of turbulence in two-dimensional quantum fluids by combining theory and experiment in a quasi-2D Bose-Einstein condensate driven by a moving optical obstacle. The central hypothesis is that turbulence onset is controlled primarily by a superfluid Reynolds number, \(Re_s=(v-v_c)D/\\kappa\), but that the critical threshold and post-onset state are shifted by compressibility, finite-temperature damping, and BKT-like vortex-pair physics. The study will distinguish four regimes: phonon-dominated flow, periodic vortex-dipole shedding, disordered vortex-gas turbulence, and clustered inverse-cascade turbulence.",
    "ImplementationSteps": {
      "Theory_GPE": "Simulate a quasi-2D condensate with the dissipative Gross-Pitaevskii equation using a Gaussian optical obstacle. Sweep obstacle velocity \(v\), diameter \(D\), healing length \(\\xi\), interaction strength, and damping parameter. Extract critical velocity \(v_c\), vortex nucleation rate, vortex-sign correlations, incompressible kinetic-energy spectra, and transition thresholds in \(Re_s\).",
      "Theory_FiniteTemperature": "Extend the model using stochastic projected Gross-Pitaevskii or truncated-Wigner simulations to include thermal fluctuations. Test whether finite temperature lowers the vortex-unbinding threshold and broadens the onset boundary between laminar and turbulent flow.",
      "Experimental_Platform": "Prepare a highly oblate atomic Bose-Einstein condensate, for example a quasi-2D \(^{87}\\mathrm{Rb}\) or \(^{23}\\mathrm{Na}\) gas, with tunable atom number, temperature fraction, and interaction strength. Drive the fluid with a blue-detuned laser obstacle of variable waist and speed, following the parameterization motivated by Reeves et al. and vortex-resolved measurements similar to Johnstone et al.",
      "Measurement_Protocol": "After each stirring pulse, image vortices using expansion or matter-wave interference. Reconstruct vortex positions and signs when possible. Repeat over a grid of \(v\), \(D/\\xi\), temperature, and hold time to build a statistical onset phase diagram.",
      "Analysis": "Classify the flow state using vortex number, vortex-pair production rate, vortex-sign clustering, nearest-neighbor vortex correlations, enstrophy, large-scale circulation, and incompressible kinetic-energy spectra. Identify whether onset collapses under \(Re_s\), or whether additional dimensionless parameters such as \(D/\\xi\), thermal fraction, and damping are required."
    },
    "ImplementationOrder": [
      "Define dimensionless control parameters: \(Re_s=(v-v_c)D/\\kappa\), \(D/\\xi\), Mach number \(v/c\), temperature fraction, and damping.",
      "Run zero-temperature Gross-Pitaevskii simulations to locate laminar, phonon-emitting, dipole-shedding, and turbulent regimes.",
      "Add finite-temperature stochastic or dissipative simulations to predict threshold shifts and fluctuation-driven vortex nucleation.",
      "Perform obstacle-stirring experiments in a quasi-2D condensate across the same parameter grid.",
      "Use vortex detection and spectral analysis to compare theory and experiment and produce an onset phase diagram.",
      "Test scaling collapse in \(Re_s\); quantify deviations caused by compressibility and finite-temperature effects."
    ],
    "Dataset": "A combined numerical and experimental dataset consisting of time-resolved condensate density images, vortex-position catalogs, vortex-sign estimates where available, obstacle parameters \(v,D\), condensate parameters \(\\xi,c,n,T/T_c\), simulated wavefunctions, kinetic-energy spectra, and derived turbulence-class labels. The experimental dataset would be modeled on vortex-resolved quasi-2D Bose-gas experiments such as Johnstone et al. Science 2019, while the theoretical parameterization follows Reeves et al. PRL 2015.",
    "EvaluationMetrics": {
      "OnsetThreshold": "Critical \(Re_s\) and uncertainty for first sustained vortex shedding and for transition to disordered turbulence.",
      "ScalingCollapse": "Variance reduction when plotting transition boundaries versus \(Re_s\) compared with raw \(v\), \(D\), or \(v/c\).",
      "VortexStatistics": "Vortex number growth rate, vortex-antivortex pair density, annihilation rate, and nearest-neighbor sign-correlation function.",
      "SpectralSignatures": "Appearance of an incompressible kinetic-energy spectrum consistent with inverse transfer or large-scale flow formation.",
      "ClusteringMetrics": "Onsager-vortex clustering fraction, large-scale circulation, and same-sign vortex cluster size distribution.",
      "TheoryExperimentAgreement": "Error between simulated and measured thresholds, vortex densities, spectra, and clustering observables across the parameter grid."
    },
    "ExpectedOutcome": "The study should produce a quantitative onset phase diagram for turbulence in 2D quantum fluids. A likely result is that \(Re_s\) captures the primary transition from laminar flow to vortex shedding, consistent with Reeves et al., but that the transition to fully developed vortex turbulence and inverse-cascade clustering depends additionally on \(D/\\xi\), Mach number, and finite-temperature damping. Experimentally, the work should identify a reproducible boundary between isolated vortex-dipole shedding and many-vortex turbulence, and theoretically it should clarify how classical-looking 2D turbulent phenomenology emerges from quantized vortices, phonons, and vortex-pair unbinding."
  }
}
```  
[result]: Proposed idea_plan is grounded in retrieved theoretical work on the superfluid Reynolds number and experimental work on large-scale flow and vortex clustering in 2D Bose gases.

prompt.full_prompt: Use an idea_plan to outline a theoretical and experimental study of turbulence onset in two-dimensional quantum fluids.

content:
Rationale: First, identify core experimental and theoretical literature on sonic/analog black holes in Bose-Einstein condensates, prioritizing papers with direct claims about Hawking-like radiation measurability.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "Bose-Einstein condensate analog black hole Hawking radiation observation measurable correlations experiment",
  "discipline": "physics",
  "max_results": 5
}
```  
[result]: Found key candidates: Lahav et al. `doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.105.240401` demonstrated a sonic black-hole analog in a BEC; Steinhauer `doi:10.1038/nphys3863` reported observation of quantum Hawking radiation and entanglement; de Nova et al. `doi:10.1038/s41586-019-1241-0` reported thermal Hawking radiation and temperature measurement; Carusotto et al. `doi:10.1088/1367-2630/10/10/103001` provided numerical predictions for BEC analog Hawking emission.

Rationale: Read the strongest experimental evidence paper to extract the measurable observables, namely density-density correlations and entanglement signatures across the horizon.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "doi:10.1038/nphys3863",
  "focus": "experimental setup, observables, evidence for Hawking-like radiation, measurement limitations"
}
```  
[result]: Steinhauer observed correlated phonon pairs emitted on opposite sides of a sonic horizon in an elongated BEC, using density-density correlation functions. The paper claims evidence for spontaneous Hawking radiation and entanglement, but the signal is weak and requires repeated experimental realizations and careful background subtraction.

Rationale: Read the later temperature-focused experiment to ground the plan in a measurable thermality criterion, not only pair-correlation detection.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "doi:10.1038/s41586-019-1241-0",
  "focus": "thermal spectrum measurement, Hawking temperature extraction, robustness of observable"
}
```  
[result]: de Nova et al. measured a Planck-like spectrum of emitted phonons from a BEC analog black hole and extracted an effective Hawking temperature consistent with the surface gravity prediction. The work emphasizes density correlations in momentum/frequency space and comparison against theoretical thermal spectra.

Rationale: Check whether a proposed investigation combining correlation-based detection, thermality fitting, and controlled parameter sweeps is incremental or still useful as a reproducibility/measurement-sensitivity study.  
```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "paper_ids": [
    "doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.105.240401",
    "doi:10.1038/nphys3863",
    "doi:10.1038/s41586-019-1241-0",
    "doi:10.1088/1367-2630/10/10/103001"
  ],
  "idea": "Systematically test whether analog black hole horizons in BECs produce measurable Hawking-like radiation by combining real-space density-density correlations, momentum-space phonon spectra, thermality fits, and horizon-parameter sweeps."
}
```  
[result]: The basic observation has precedent, so the novelty is not in first detection. A valuable investigation would be a controlled measurability and reproducibility study: quantify signal-to-noise, dependence on surface gravity, background thermal contamination, finite-size effects, and whether observed correlations satisfy both pair-production and thermal-spectrum criteria.

Rationale: Propose an evidence-grounded idea plan focused on measurability, using known BEC analog-horizon observables and adding systematic parameter sweeps and statistical validation.  
```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "idea_plan",
  "content": {
    "Idea": "Investigate whether analog black-hole horizons in elongated Bose-Einstein condensates produce measurable Hawking-like radiation by creating a tunable sonic horizon and jointly measuring: (i) cross-horizon density-density correlations from correlated phonon-pair emission, and (ii) the phonon spectrum’s consistency with a thermal Hawking distribution whose temperature scales with horizon surface gravity.",
    "ImplementationSteps": {
      "1_SetupBEC": "Prepare an elongated quasi-1D Bose-Einstein condensate, e.g. a dilute ultracold atomic gas, in a trap geometry similar to prior sonic-black-hole experiments. Engineer a flow profile with a subsonic exterior and supersonic interior region so that the transition point acts as an acoustic event horizon.",
      "2_TuneHorizon": "Use an optical potential or moving potential step to vary the flow velocity gradient at the sonic horizon. Record the local speed of sound, condensate density, flow velocity, horizon position, and estimated acoustic surface gravity.",
      "3_MeasureDensityCorrelations": "After horizon formation, take repeated in situ or time-of-flight density images. Compute equal-time density-density correlation functions G2(x,x') and identify off-diagonal correlation bands connecting points inside and outside the horizon, as expected for Hawking-partner phonon pairs.",
      "4_ExtractSpectrum": "Transform correlation data into momentum or frequency space to estimate the emitted phonon occupation spectrum. Fit the spectrum to a Planck-like distribution and extract an effective Hawking temperature.",
      "5_ControlExperiments": "Run matched controls with no horizon, purely subsonic flow, altered flow gradients, and deliberately increased initial thermal phonon populations. Compare observed correlations and spectra against these controls.",
      "6_StatisticalValidation": "Use bootstrap confidence intervals and likelihood-ratio model comparison to test whether a Hawking-like thermal pair-production model explains the data better than classical noise, initial thermal fluctuations, or horizon-independent excitations."
    },
    "ImplementationOrder": [
      "1_SetupBEC",
      "2_TuneHorizon",
      "3_MeasureDensityCorrelations",
      "4_ExtractSpectrum",
      "5_ControlExperiments",
      "6_StatisticalValidation"
    ],
    "Dataset": "Repeated experimental realizations of a tunable BEC sonic horizon, including raw density profiles, calibrated flow velocity and sound-speed maps, horizon surface-gravity estimates, equal-time density-density correlation matrices, momentum/frequency-resolved phonon spectra, and matched no-horizon/subsonic/thermal-background controls. Prior benchmarks should include Lahav et al. `doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.105.240401`, Steinhauer `doi:10.1038/nphys3863`, de Nova et al. `doi:10.1038/s41586-019-1241-0`, and Carusotto et al. `doi:10.1088/1367-2630/10/10/103001`.",
    "EvaluationMetrics": {
      "CorrelationSignalToNoise": "Amplitude of cross-horizon G2 correlation bands divided by background correlation fluctuations across repeated shots.",
      "PairProductionConsistency": "Spatial and momentum-space agreement between inside/outside phonon correlations and the predicted Hawking-partner dispersion relation.",
      "ThermalityFitQuality": "Goodness-of-fit of the phonon occupation spectrum to a Planck distribution, compared with nonthermal alternatives using AIC/BIC or likelihood ratios.",
      "TemperatureScaling": "Correlation between extracted Hawking temperature and independently estimated acoustic surface gravity, testing the expected proportionality T_H ∝ surface gravity.",
      "ControlRejection": "Statistical suppression or disappearance of Hawking-like signatures in no-horizon and purely subsonic controls.",
      "Robustness": "Sensitivity of inferred radiation to finite-size effects, imaging noise, initial condensate temperature, horizon lifetime, and background excitations."
    },
    "ExpectedOutcome": "If BEC analog horizons produce measurable Hawking-like radiation, the experiment should show statistically significant cross-horizon phonon-pair correlations, a thermal or near-thermal phonon spectrum, and an extracted temperature that scales with acoustic surface gravity while disappearing or weakening in no-horizon controls. The most realistic outcome is not a new first observation, since Steinhauer and de Nova et al. already reported evidence, but a rigorous reproducibility and measurability map identifying the parameter regimes where the Hawking-like signal is distinguishable from classical noise and thermal background excitations."
  }
}
```

content:
I’ll first anchor the proposal in the established supernova collective-oscillation literature, then identify the magnetic-field/spin-flavor ingredient and the gap between them.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"dense supernova neutrino flavor oscillations collective effects magnetic fields spin flavor conversion arXiv","discipline":"physics","k":5}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers include Duan, Fuller & Qian, “Collective Neutrino Oscillations,” arXiv:1001.2799; Volpe, Väänänen & Espinoza, “Extended evolution equations for neutrino propagation in astrophysical and cosmological environments,” arXiv:1302.2374; and Vlasenko, Fuller & Cirigliano, “Neutrino quantum kinetics,” arXiv:1309.2628. These establish dense-neutrino refractive effects and generalized spin/helicity-coherent kinetic descriptions relevant to magnetized supernovae.

I’ll read the collective-oscillation review because it defines the dense-supernova nonlinear flavor problem the proposed study must modify rather than reinvent.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:1001.2799","focus":"collective oscillations in core-collapse supernovae, neutrino-neutrino forward scattering, limitations of bulb-model treatments"}
```  
[result]: Duan et al. review how neutrino-neutrino forward scattering produces synchronized, bipolar, spectral-split, and multi-angle flavor evolution in supernovae. The review emphasizes simplified geometries and flavor Hamiltonians dominated by vacuum, matter, and self-interaction terms, without a systematic treatment of magnetar-scale magnetic-field-induced spin/helicity couplings.

I’ll read a quantum-kinetic/helicity-coherence source to ground the magnetic-field extension in a formalism that can include spin coherence and anisotropic media.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:1302.2374","focus":"extended neutrino evolution equations, spin coherence, magnetic moments, neutrino-antineutrino coupling in anisotropic environments"}
```  
[result]: Volpe, Väänänen & Espinoza derive extended mean-field equations that include neutrino-antineutrino pairing correlations and spin/helicity coherence beyond standard flavor-only evolution. The framework indicates that anisotropy and mass/magnetic-moment terms can couple sectors usually evolved separately, but it does not provide a dedicated magnetized core-collapse-supernova phenomenology with realistic magnetic-field profiles and collective oscillations.

I’ll extract the genome of the existing work to identify a precise study niche: combine nonlinear dense-neutrino flavor evolution with strong-field spin-flavor/helicity physics in supernova conditions.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["arXiv:1001.2799","arXiv:1302.2374"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted mechanisms: neutrino-neutrino self-interaction drives collective flavor conversion; extended mean-field equations allow helicity/spin coherence and neutrino-antineutrino couplings. Extracted limitation: the collective-oscillation literature generally omits explicit magnetar-strength B-field structure, while extended kinetic treatments are often formal and not mapped to observable supernova signal consequences.

I’ll check novelty against the likely nearby literature to avoid proposing a generic “add magnetic fields” study and instead target the unoccupied intersection of realistic B-field geometry, dense self-interactions, and observable signatures.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"Simulate core-collapse-supernova neutrino flavor evolution using extended mean-field/quantum-kinetic equations that couple flavor, helicity, and magnetic-moment spin-flavor terms in realistic magnetar-strength magnetic-field and matter profiles, quantifying changes to collective oscillations and detector signals.","against":["arXiv:1001.2799","arXiv:1302.2374","arXiv:1309.2628"],"k":5}
```  
[result]: The idea is adjacent to known collective oscillation and quantum-kinetic formalisms, but the novelty lies in a phenomenological parameter survey of magnetar-scale field geometry and strength embedded in dense supernova self-interaction regimes. The most defensible gap is to determine when magnetic spin/helicity couplings remain negligible versus when they seed or suppress fast/collective flavor conversion and alter time-, energy-, and angle-dependent observable signals.

```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","proposal":{"mechanism_genome":"Extend the supernova neutrino flavor Hamiltonian beyond vacuum + matter + neutrino-neutrino self-interaction terms by adding magnetic-moment-induced spin-flavor/helicity couplings in the extended mean-field framework of arXiv:1302.2374, then evolve the coupled density matrices through dense core-collapse profiles where self-interactions drive collective oscillations as reviewed in arXiv:1001.2799.","niche_genome":"Magnetorotational or magnetar-forming core-collapse supernovae with B ~ 10^13-10^15 G, anisotropic matter outflows, and high neutrino number density near and above the neutrinosphere, where collective flavor conversion and magnetic spin/helicity effects may coexist.","observation_genome":"The study will map how magnetic-field strength, radial falloff, angular geometry, and assumed neutrino magnetic moment modify synchronized/bipolar oscillations, spectral swaps, helicity conversion, and neutrino-antineutrino sector coupling; outputs will be flavor- and helicity-resolved spectra and predicted event-rate distortions in DUNE, Hyper-Kamiokande, and IceCube-like detectors.","limitation_genome":"The initial calculation should use prescribed one-dimensional and axisymmetric supernova background profiles rather than full radiation-hydrodynamic feedback, and it will rely on allowed or near-future-testable magnetic moments, so null results for Standard-Model-sized moments are possible.","delta_genome":"Compared with standard collective-oscillation studies that neglect explicit magnetic fields, and formal quantum-kinetic work that does not scan realistic supernova B-field morphologies, this project isolates the parameter boundary where magnetar-strength fields qualitatively change dense-neutrino flavor evolution.","claim_genome":"A systematic extended-mean-field simulation can determine whether strong supernova magnetic fields are merely a perturbation to collective oscillations or can seed observable spin-flavor/helicity signatures, providing falsifiable predictions for the next Galactic core-collapse neutrino burst."}}
```  
[result]: Proposal generated in the requested gene_genome schema, grounded in arXiv:1001.2799 and arXiv:1302.2374. The study is positioned as a targeted phenomenological bridge between dense-neutrino collective oscillation physics and magnetic-field-enabled extended flavor/helicity evolution. 
```
Final proposal: dense-supernova neutrino oscillations with strong magnetic fields will be studied by coupling collective neutrino-neutrino flavor evolution to magnetic-moment spin/helicity terms in realistic magnetar-like supernova profiles, then testing whether the resulting spectral and event-rate changes are observable.
```

content:
I’ll first anchor the prompt in review-level solar-flare evidence and laboratory reconnection evidence, then distill a cross-system research gap.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"magnetic reconnection particle acceleration solar flares laboratory plasma experiments review energetic electrons ions","discipline":"physics","limit":5}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers include Zharkova et al. 2011 (`doi:10.1007/s11214-011-9803-y`) on particle acceleration in solar flares, Drake et al. 2006 (`doi:10.1038/nature05116`) on Fermi acceleration in reconnecting islands, Yamada, Kulsrud & Ji 2010 (`doi:10.1103/RevModPhys.82.603`) on laboratory/space reconnection, Zhong et al. 2010 (`doi:10.1038/nphys1658`) on laser-plasma reconnection, and Ji et al. 2022 (`doi:10.1038/s42254-021-00419-x`) on magnetic reconnection in the laboratory.  

I’ll read the solar-flare review to identify the dominant acceleration mechanisms and observational constraints that a prompt should preserve.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"doi:10.1007/s11214-011-9803-y","focus":"solar flare particle acceleration mechanisms, observations, limitations"}
```  
[result]: Zharkova et al. summarize direct electric-field acceleration, stochastic/turbulent acceleration, shocks, and acceleration in reconnecting current sheets/islands as candidate flare mechanisms. Hard X-ray and radio signatures constrain nonthermal electron production, but the review stresses that linking reconnection geometry, turbulence, and observed particle spectra remains unresolved.  

Next I’ll read a laboratory-focused reconnection review to ground the comparison in experimentally controllable plasma regimes.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"doi:10.1038/s42254-021-00419-x","focus":"laboratory magnetic reconnection experiments, particle acceleration diagnostics, scaling to astrophysical plasmas"}
```  
[result]: Ji et al. review laboratory reconnection platforms including MRX, laser-produced plasmas, and high-energy-density experiments. They emphasize that laboratory systems can diagnose current sheets, guide fields, plasmoids, electric fields, and particle distributions, but scaling nonthermal acceleration to collisionless solar-flare conditions remains a key challenge.  

I’ll extract the transferable “genome” of the literature so the final prompt targets a precise gap rather than a broad topic.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["doi:10.1007/s11214-011-9803-y","doi:10.1038/nature05116","doi:10.1038/s42254-021-00419-x","doi:10.1038/nphys1658"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted mechanisms center on reconnection electric fields, island/plasmoid contraction, first-order Fermi acceleration, turbulence, and shock-like outflows. The main gap is a cross-regime framework connecting solar flare observables such as hard X-rays/radio bursts with laboratory diagnostics of reconnection geometry, guide field, plasmoid formation, and evolving particle energy spectra.  

I’ll now propose a research prompt that is evidence-grounded and framed as a comparative solar-laboratory physics problem.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","content":{"mechanism_genome":"Investigate how magnetic reconnection accelerates particles through parallel reconnection electric fields, contracting plasmoids/magnetic islands, Fermi-type reflection in reconnection outflows, and turbulence generated inside fragmented current sheets.","niche_genome":"Bridge solar flare physics and laboratory plasma experiments by comparing collisionless flare-like reconnection inferred from hard X-ray/radio observations with controlled reconnection platforms such as MRX and laser-produced plasma experiments.","observation_genome":"Use solar observables—hard X-ray footpoint/looptop spectra, radio bursts, EUV current-sheet morphology, and timing of nonthermal emission—alongside laboratory diagnostics of magnetic topology, reconnection rate, guide field, electric fields, plasmoid formation, and electron/ion energy distributions.","limitation_genome":"A central limitation is that solar flares provide rich nonthermal signatures but incomplete in situ field measurements, whereas laboratory experiments provide direct field and particle diagnostics but may differ in size, collisionality, plasma beta, guide-field strength, and Lundquist number from solar flare current sheets.","delta_genome":"Formulate a scaling study that identifies dimensionless regimes in which laboratory reconnection reproduces the dominant particle-acceleration channels inferred in solar flares, especially the transition from single X-line acceleration to plasmoid-dominated, turbulent acceleration.","claim_genome":"Research prompt: How do reconnection electric fields, plasmoid contraction, and turbulence jointly determine electron and ion acceleration in solar flares, and which laboratory plasma regimes can reproduce the same spectral, temporal, and spatial signatures of nonthermal particle production?"}}
```  
[result]: Proposed gene_genome prompt grounded in Zharkova et al. 2011, Drake et al. 2006, Ji et al. 2022, and Zhong et al. 2010. It emphasizes a decisive cross-regime comparison: solar flare signatures versus laboratory-controlled reconnection diagnostics. 
```

niche_genome: Bridge solar flare physics and laboratory plasma experiments by comparing collisionless flare-like reconnection inferred from hard X-ray/radio observations with controlled reconnection platforms such as MRX and laser-produced plasma experiments.

observation_genome: Use solar observables—hard X-ray footpoint/looptop spectra, radio bursts, EUV current-sheet morphology, and timing of nonthermal emission—alongside laboratory diagnostics of magnetic topology, reconnection rate, guide field, electric fields, plasmoid formation, and electron/ion energy distributions.

limitation_genome: A central limitation is that solar flares provide rich nonthermal signatures but incomplete in situ field measurements, whereas laboratory experiments provide direct field and particle diagnostics but may differ in size, collisionality, plasma beta, guide-field strength, and Lundquist number from solar flare current sheets.

delta_genome: Formulate a scaling study that identifies dimensionless regimes in which laboratory reconnection reproduces the dominant particle-acceleration channels inferred in solar flares, especially the transition from single X-line acceleration to plasmoid-dominated, turbulent acceleration.

claim_genome: Research prompt: How do reconnection electric fields, plasmoid contraction, and turbulence jointly determine electron and ion acceleration in solar flares, and which laboratory plasma regimes can reproduce the same spectral, temporal, and spatial signatures of nonthermal particle production?
```

content:
I’ll ground the prompt in stochastic thermodynamics results that explicitly connect finite-time operation, fluctuations, and efficiency bounds in nanoscale engines. Starting broad helps identify canonical constraints rather than overfitting to one engine model.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"non-equilibrium thermodynamics nanoscale heat engines efficiency constraints stochastic thermodynamics thermodynamic uncertainty relation efficiency maximum power","discipline":"physics","limit":5}
```
[result]: Found key candidates: Esposito et al., “Efficiency at maximum power of low-dissipation Carnot engines,” PRL 105, 150603 (2010), DOI:10.1103/PhysRevLett.105.150603 / arXiv:1006.2747; Pietzonka & Seifert, “Universal trade-off between power, efficiency, and constancy in steady-state heat engines,” PRL 120, 190602 (2018), DOI:10.1103/PhysRevLett.120.190602 / arXiv:1705.05817; Brandner et al., “Thermodynamics of micro- and nano-systems driven by periodic temperature variations,” PRX 5, 031019 (2015), DOI:10.1103/PhysRevX.5.031019. 
I’ll read the universal trade-off paper because it gives a compact constraint linking efficiency to power and fluctuations, directly relevant to nanoscale non-equilibrium limits.
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:1705.05817","sections":["abstract","main results","discussion"]}
```
[result]: Pietzonka & Seifert derive a universal bound for steady-state heat engines: approaching Carnot efficiency at finite power requires diverging power fluctuations. The result reframes nanoscale efficiency as jointly constrained by power output, dissipation, and current constancy, not by average energy flows alone.
I’ll read the low-dissipation finite-time Carnot result to capture the complementary constraint on cyclic nanoscale engines: efficiency at maximum power is bounded by dissipation asymmetry and finite-time irreversibility.
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:1006.2747","sections":["abstract","model","bounds","conclusion"]}
```
[result]: Esposito et al. show that for low-dissipation Carnot engines, efficiency at maximum power lies between ηC/2 and ηC/(2−ηC), with the Curzon-Ahlborn value emerging under symmetric dissipation. The relevant constraint is finite-time entropy production during hot/cold contacts, parameterized by dissipation constants.
I’ll extract a compact genome so the final prompt emphasizes mechanism, measurable observables, and limitations rather than asking a vague conceptual question.
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["arXiv:1705.05817","arXiv:1006.2747"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```
[result]: Extracted mechanisms: thermodynamic uncertainty relation for steady-state engines; low-dissipation finite-time entropy production for cyclic Carnot-like engines. Key observables: efficiency η, Carnot efficiency ηC, power P, entropy production, output-current variance, heat-current fluctuations, contact times, and hot/cold dissipation asymmetry. Limitations: bounds may assume Markovian steady states, linear/low-dissipation regimes, or idealized heat baths.
```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","content":{"mechanism_genome":"Use stochastic non-equilibrium thermodynamics to study how entropy production, finite-time dissipation, and thermodynamic uncertainty relations constrain nanoscale heat-engine efficiency. Frame the central mechanism as a trade-off among efficiency η, power P, heat/work-current fluctuations, and distance from Carnot efficiency ηC−η.","niche_genome":"Nanoscale heat engines operating far from the quasistatic limit, including steady-state autonomous engines and finite-time cyclic engines such as quantum dots, molecular junctions, Brownian heat engines, and low-dissipation Carnot-like cycles.","observation_genome":"Focus on measurable signatures: efficiency at maximum power, entropy production rate, heat absorbed from the hot bath, work-output fluctuations, power variance, Fano factors, hot/cold contact times, and dissipation asymmetry. Compare whether high efficiency is achieved by reducing dissipation, sacrificing power, or accepting large fluctuations.","limitation_genome":"Explicitly test the assumptions behind each bound: Markovian dynamics, local detailed balance, weak coupling to thermal reservoirs, steady-state versus cyclic operation, linear-response versus far-from-equilibrium driving, and whether quantum coherence or strong coupling modifies classical stochastic-thermodynamic constraints.","delta_genome":"The prompt should move beyond asking whether nanoscale engines can be efficient and instead ask which non-equilibrium constraint is active in a given operating regime: finite-time entropy production, fluctuation-induced precision loss, or dissipation asymmetry. It should require deriving dimensionless trade-off curves and identifying experimentally accessible observables.","claim_genome":"Research prompt: ‘Develop a theoretical and computational study of how non-equilibrium thermodynamics constrains the efficiency of nanoscale heat engines. Starting from stochastic thermodynamics with local detailed balance, derive efficiency bounds for both steady-state and finite-time cyclic engines in terms of entropy production, power output, and heat/work-current fluctuations. Use the thermodynamic uncertainty relation to analyze why Carnot efficiency at finite power requires divergent fluctuations, and use the low-dissipation Carnot framework to bound efficiency at maximum power under asymmetric hot/cold dissipation. Apply the framework to a concrete nanoscale model, such as a quantum-dot thermoelectric engine or Brownian heat engine, and map the regimes where efficiency is limited primarily by dissipation, finite cycle time, or fluctuation-induced loss of precision. Evaluate results using η/ηC, power, entropy production rate, variance of output power, and robustness to reservoir coupling assumptions.’"}}}
```
[result]: Proposed a physics research prompt grounded in arXiv:1705.05817 and arXiv:1006.2747, emphasizing universal power-efficiency-fluctuation trade-offs and finite-time low-dissipation efficiency bounds.

content:
I’ll anchor the study in two established facts: trapped-ion interferometers are already sensitive to motional phase accumulation, while QEC-metrology theory predicts gains when noise is correctable and the signal Hamiltonian is not erased by the code.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"quantum error correction metrology trapped ion interferometry precision measurement","discipline":"physics","max_results":5}
```  
[result]: Found relevant candidates: Kessler et al., “Quantum Error Correction for Metrology,” PRL 112, 150802, DOI:10.1103/PhysRevLett.112.150802; Arrad et al., “Increasing Sensing Resolution with Error Correction,” PRL 112, 150801, DOI:10.1103/PhysRevLett.112.150801; Ruster et al., “A matter-wave interferometer with a trapped ion,” Nat. Commun. 7, 12950, DOI:10.1038/ncomms12950. The candidates connect QEC-enhanced sensing theory with a concrete trapped-ion interferometry platform.

I’ll read the QEC-metrology theory first to identify the condition under which error correction helps rather than removes the signal.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1103/PhysRevLett.112.150802","sections":["abstract","main results","discussion"]}
```  
[result]: Kessler et al. show that QEC can restore Heisenberg-like scaling or extend coherence in metrology if the noise operators are correctable while the signal Hamiltonian has a component outside the Lindblad/noise span. The key design principle is to encode the sensor so dominant noise is syndrome-detectable but the parameter-dependent phase remains logical.

Next I’ll read the trapped-ion interferometry paper to ground the experimental proposal in realistic observables and noise sources.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1038/ncomms12950","sections":["abstract","experimental scheme","noise and limitations"]}
```  
[result]: Ruster et al. demonstrate a single trapped-ion matter-wave interferometer using spin-dependent displacements, recombining motional wave packets and reading out phase through internal-state populations. Reported limitations include motional heating, laser phase/intensity noise, magnetic-field noise, and contrast loss during interferometer time.

I’ll check novelty against nearby work, focusing on whether QEC has been tested specifically for trapped-ion interferometric phase measurements rather than generic trapped-ion sensing or generic QEC-metrology theory.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","query":"quantum error corrected trapped ion matter wave interferometer precision phase measurement","against":["10.1103/PhysRevLett.112.150802","10.1103/PhysRevLett.112.150801","10.1038/ncomms12950"],"max_results":5}
```  
[result]: No direct study was identified that applies an explicit QEC code to a trapped-ion matter-wave interferometer and compares corrected versus uncorrected phase sensitivity under controlled motional heating and dephasing. Closest work is theoretical QEC metrology and separate trapped-ion interferometry demonstrations, leaving a clear integration gap.

```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"idea_plan","content":{"Idea":"Test whether quantum error correction improves precision phase measurements in trapped-ion interferometry by encoding the interferometric spin/motional phase into a small logical sensor whose error syndromes correct dominant dephasing or motional-heating-induced contrast loss while preserving the signal Hamiltonian. The central hypothesis, grounded in QEC-metrology theory, is that improvement occurs only when the interferometric phase remains a logical operation not spanned by the corrected noise operators.","ImplementationSteps":{"1_platform_and_signal":"Use a linear Paul trap with one sensor ion for the spin-dependent matter-wave interferometer and two auxiliary ions for syndrome extraction, or simulate this first with a calibrated master-equation model. The measured parameter is an externally applied force, acceleration, or optical phase shift that produces a known interferometric phase.","2_baseline_interferometer":"Implement the unencoded Ruster-style trapped-ion interferometer: prepare a spin superposition, apply spin-dependent displacements to split motional wave packets, allow phase accumulation for interrogation time T, recombine, and infer phase from spin-population parity or Ramsey fringe shift.","3_noise_characterization":"Independently calibrate dominant noise channels: motional heating rate, spin dephasing from magnetic-field noise, laser phase/intensity noise, and spontaneous off-resonant scattering if relevant. Build a Lindblad model and identify which errors are correctable without projecting out the signal.","4_qec_code_design":"Choose a minimal code matched to the dominant noise. For spin dephasing, use a repetition or decoherence-free/logical subspace across multiple ions with stabilizer checks that detect phase flips while keeping the force-induced or interferometric phase as a logical Z/X rotation. For motional errors, test bosonic-style parity or binomial/cat-inspired motional encoding in simulation, then implement the simplest experimentally feasible syndrome measurement.","5_corrected_protocol":"Insert one or more syndrome-extraction rounds during the interferometer hold time. Use ancilla ions to measure error syndromes nondestructively, apply feed-forward correction, then complete recombination and readout. Compare against an otherwise identical protocol with no correction and with syndrome measurements but no feedback to isolate QEC benefit from overhead.","6_parameter_sweep":"Sweep interrogation time, engineered heating/dephasing strength, interferometer arm separation, and correction-cycle rate. Identify the regime where correction overhead is smaller than coherence/contrast gain.","7_data_analysis":"Estimate phase using maximum-likelihood or Bayesian inference from repeated shots. Fit Allan deviation and Fisher information versus interrogation time and noise strength, comparing unencoded, encoded-no-correction, and encoded-with-correction protocols."},"ImplementationOrder":["Build/calibrate baseline trapped-ion interferometer","Measure native and engineered noise channels","Construct Lindblad/sensitivity model satisfying QEC-metrology signal-vs-noise criteria","Select and simulate candidate logical encoding","Implement syndrome extraction on ions or motional mode","Run corrected and uncorrected phase-estimation experiments under matched conditions","Analyze precision gain, overhead, and operating regime"],"Dataset":"Experimental shot records from trapped-ion interferometry runs: control pulse settings, interrogation time, applied calibration phase/force, engineered noise level, syndrome outcomes, feed-forward actions, final spin populations/parity, motional sideband thermometry, and repeated phase-estimation trials. If hardware access is limited, generate a companion simulated dataset from a master-equation model parameterized by measured heating and dephasing rates.","EvaluationMetrics":{"phase_sensitivity":"Minimum detectable phase per sqrt(Hz) and Cramer-Rao-normalized estimator variance.","fisher_information":"Classical Fisher information from measurement outcomes and, in simulation, quantum Fisher information before readout.","contrast_lifetime":"Interferometer fringe contrast decay time with and without QEC.","allan_deviation":"Frequency/force/phase stability versus averaging time.","net_gain":"Ratio of corrected to uncorrected phase variance at fixed total experimental time, including syndrome-measurement overhead and ancilla errors.","break_even_condition":"Noise and interrogation-time region where QEC improves sensitivity beyond both unencoded and encoded-no-correction controls."},"ExpectedOutcome":"QEC should not universally improve trapped-ion interferometry; it should help in a specific regime where dephasing or heating dominates and syndrome extraction is faster and less noisy than the natural contrast decay. The expected result is a quantitative phase-sensitivity improvement at longer interrogation times, accompanied by a mapped boundary where correction overhead negates the gain. This would provide the first targeted test of QEC-metrology principles in a trapped-ion matter-wave interferometer."}}
```  
[result]: Proposed an evidence-grounded idea_plan linking QEC-metrology criteria from Kessler et al. with the trapped-ion matter-wave interferometer architecture and limitations reported by Ruster et al.  The plan emphasizes controlled corrected-vs-uncorrected comparisons and metrics that include QEC overhead.

content:
I’ll ground the pathway in established MEMS Casimir metrology and complex-geometry Casimir experiments, then turn the gap into an executable plan.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"MEMS micromechanical oscillator Casimir force measurement complex geometries gratings trench arrays lateral Casimir force"}
```  
[result]: Found core candidates: Chan et al., “Quantum Mechanical Actuation of Microelectromechanical Systems by the Casimir Force,” Science 291, 1941–1944 (2001), DOI: 10.1126/science.1057984; Decca et al., “Measurement of the Casimir Force between Dissimilar Metals,” Phys. Rev. Lett. 91, 050402 (2003), DOI: 10.1103/PhysRevLett.91.050402; Chan et al., “Measurement of the Casimir Force between a Gold Sphere and a Silicon Surface with Nanoscale Trench Arrays,” Phys. Rev. Lett. 101, 030401 (2008), DOI: 10.1103/PhysRevLett.101.030401; Chen et al., “Demonstration of the Lateral Casimir Force,” Phys. Rev. Lett. 88, 101801 (2002), DOI: 10.1103/PhysRevLett.88.101801.
The MEMS papers establish force-gradient readout, pull-in limits, calibration, and noise constraints that should define the measurement architecture.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["10.1126/science.1057984","10.1103/PhysRevLett.91.050402"]}
```  
[result]: Chan et al. showed Casimir attraction can actuate a torsional MEMS oscillator and cause nonlinear pull-in, using sphere–plate geometry to avoid parallelism sensitivity. Decca et al. used a micromachined torsional oscillator with electrostatic calibration and dynamic response to measure Casimir forces precisely at submicron separations.
The complex-geometry papers identify what must be extended: patterned surfaces introduce lateral/shape dependence, but the metrology must separate geometry effects from residual electrostatics and roughness.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["10.1103/PhysRevLett.101.030401","10.1103/PhysRevLett.88.101801"]}
```  
[result]: Chan et al. measured normal Casimir forces for a gold sphere above nanoscale silicon trench arrays and found deviations from simple proximity-force predictions. Chen et al. demonstrated a lateral Casimir force between sinusoidally corrugated surfaces, proving that lateral phase control can expose geometry-dependent vacuum forces.
A concise gap check helps make the pathway more than a repeat of sphere–plate or single-grating measurements.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"A MEMS torsional/frequency-shift platform with interchangeable patterned chips to map Casimir force gradients across separation, lateral phase, rotation angle, and geometry class, benchmarking scattering-theory predictions for non-PFA complex geometries."}
```  
[result]: Prior work separately demonstrated MEMS Casimir actuation/precision readout and patterned/corrugated surface effects. The comparatively open opportunity is an integrated MEMS metrology platform that performs multi-parameter geometry scans—normal, lateral, and rotational—on lithographically defined complex surfaces with common calibration and uncertainty treatment.
```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"idea_plan","content":{"Idea":"Develop a MEMS-based Casimir metrology platform for complex geometries by combining a high-Q torsional or flexural microelectromechanical oscillator with interchangeable nanofabricated test chips: trench arrays, sinusoidal gratings, crossed gratings, posts, holes, and rotated anisotropic patterns. The platform will measure normal and lateral Casimir force gradients via resonance-frequency shifts while scanning separation, lateral phase, and relative rotation, enabling direct tests of non-proximity-force-approximation predictions for structured surfaces.","ImplementationSteps":{"1_MEMS_design":"Fabricate a torsional MEMS oscillator with integrated capacitive or optical readout, using the sphere-on-oscillator or sphere-near-patterned-chip configuration as a robust baseline inspired by Chan et al. and Decca et al. Include electrostatic actuation electrodes for calibration and closed-loop control.","2_Geometry_library":"Create a library of patterned surfaces: nanoscale trench arrays similar to Chan et al. PRL 101, 030401; sinusoidal/corrugated gratings related to Chen et al. PRL 88, 101801; plus 2D lattices, rotated gratings, and asymmetric patterns to probe lateral and torque-like Casimir effects.","3_Calibration_protocol":"Calibrate separation using electrostatic force curves, interferometry, and contact/roughness characterization. Measure and subtract residual potential differences with Kelvin-probe-style voltage sweeps at every geometry and separation.","4_Measurement_modes":"Operate the MEMS in dynamic mode and infer force gradients from resonance-frequency shifts. Run scans over separation, lateral displacement, and rotation angle; for selected cases, compare static deflection and dynamic measurements to check systematics.","5_Model_comparison":"Compute theoretical predictions using proximity-force approximation, pairwise-additive approximations, and numerical scattering or boundary-element methods. Use the same measured topographies and optical/material parameters as model inputs.","6_Uncertainty_analysis":"Quantify uncertainties from separation error, residual electrostatics, roughness, patch potentials, oscillator spring constant, temperature, alignment, lateral phase error, and finite conductivity."},"ImplementationOrder":["Build and characterize baseline MEMS oscillator using a smooth sphere–plate geometry.","Validate electrostatic calibration and dynamic force-gradient readout against known sphere–plate Casimir results.","Introduce one-dimensional trench or grating samples and measure normal force-gradient deviations from PFA.","Add lateral translation control to measure phase-dependent lateral Casimir forces.","Add rotational alignment control for anisotropic or crossed-pattern geometries.","Benchmark all measurements against numerical scattering-theory calculations and release a calibrated dataset."],"Dataset":"A multi-geometry Casimir dataset containing raw resonance-frequency shifts, calibrated force gradients, applied voltages, absolute separations, lateral offsets, rotation angles, topography maps from AFM/SEM, roughness spectra, optical constants, temperature/humidity/vacuum logs, and uncertainty budgets for each patterned MEMS measurement.","EvaluationMetrics":{"Force_sensitivity":"Minimum resolvable force gradient or force, targeting sub-pN to pN-level equivalent sensitivity depending on oscillator stiffness and Q.","Separation_accuracy":"Absolute distance uncertainty below a few nanometers over roughly 50 nm–1 micrometer separations.","Geometry_signal":"Measured deviation from PFA for patterned surfaces as a function of separation, period, trench depth, lateral phase, and rotation angle.","Systematic_control":"Residual electrostatic contribution after compensation; repeatability across voltage sweeps, approach/retract cycles, and duplicate chips.","Theory_agreement":"Goodness-of-fit between measured gradients and scattering-theory predictions using independently measured material/topography inputs.","Reproducibility":"Consistency across multiple MEMS devices and nominally identical patterned samples."},"ExpectedOutcome":"The project should produce a reusable MEMS platform capable of mapping Casimir forces in nontrivial geometries, not just smooth sphere–plate configurations. It is expected to reveal measurable departures from simple proximity-force approximations in patterned surfaces, provide calibrated tests of lateral and rotational geometry-dependent Casimir effects, and generate a benchmark dataset for quantum-fluctuation force theory in realistic nanofabricated structures."}}
```
