content:
Rationale: I first identify authoritative candidates spanning both the source physics of phase-transition backgrounds and detector-facing inference, so the proposal is anchored in current limitations rather than a generic detection idea.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"stochastic gravitational wave background early universe phase transitions LISA sound waves foreground detection inference arXiv","discipline":"physics","max_results":5}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers include Caprini et al. “Detecting gravitational waves from cosmological phase transitions with LISA” (arXiv:1512.06239), Caprini et al. “Science with the space-based interferometer LISA. II: Gravitational waves from cosmological phase transitions” (arXiv:1910.13125), Hindmarsh et al. “Numerical simulations of acoustically generated gravitational waves at a first order phase transition” (arXiv:1504.03291), and Thrane & Romano “Sensitivity curves for searches for gravitational-wave backgrounds” (arXiv:1310.5300). These cover signal models, LISA detectability, and stochastic-search methodology.

Rationale: The LISA cosmology review is the most relevant detection-facing synthesis; I read it to extract the measurement bottlenecks and standard assumptions for phase-transition searches.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:1910.13125","sections":["phase transition signals","detection prospects","foregrounds and parameter estimation"]}
```  
[result]: The paper emphasizes that first-order phase transitions can yield peaked mHz stochastic spectra detectable by LISA, with contributions from sound waves, bubble collisions, and turbulence. It also stresses degeneracies with astrophysical foregrounds, uncertainty in source modeling, and the need for robust parameter inference rather than simple signal-to-noise thresholding.

Rationale: I then read the simulation paper most directly tied to the dominant source term, because detection templates are only as credible as the hydrodynamic model behind them.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:1504.03291","sections":["abstract","simulation results","spectral shape","discussion"]}
```  
[result]: Hindmarsh et al. find that long-lived acoustic waves in the plasma can dominate the gravitational-wave signal after a first-order transition, producing a characteristic peaked spectrum. The result supports sound-wave-based templates, but also points to modeling uncertainties from finite lifetime, nonlinearity, turbulence onset, and extrapolation from simulations to cosmological parameter space.

Rationale: Before proposing, I check whether the specific methodological angle—foreground-robust, physics-constrained, multi-band/model-marginalized detection of phase-transition backgrounds—is already saturated.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"A detection pipeline for early-universe phase-transition stochastic backgrounds that jointly fits hydrodynamic sound-wave/turbulence templates, LISA instrumental noise, compact-binary foregrounds, and optional decihertz/PTA cross-band priors using simulation-calibrated hierarchical Bayesian model selection."}
```  
[result]: Related work exists on LISA phase-transition forecasts, power-law-integrated sensitivity, and Bayesian stochastic-background inference, but there is no dominant standard pipeline that jointly marginalizes hydrodynamic source uncertainty and astrophysical foreground confusion in a multi-detector/multi-band framework. The novelty is strongest if the project treats source-model uncertainty as part of detection, not merely as post-detection interpretation.

```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","context":"early-universe phase-transition SGWB detection; evidence from arXiv:1910.13125 and arXiv:1504.03291"}
```  
[result]: Proposal synthesized below.  
 
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Develop a hierarchical Bayesian stochastic-background search in which the phase-transition signal is not a fixed broken power law but a family of simulation-calibrated hydrodynamic templates: sound-wave-dominated spectra, turbulence corrections, finite acoustic lifetime suppression, and nuisance parameters for bubble-wall velocity, transition strength, inverse duration, and reheating temperature. The inference would jointly fit these templates with LISA instrumental noise and astrophysical compact-binary foregrounds, using Bayesian evidence or simulation-based likelihood ratios as the detection statistic.",
  "niche_genome": "The project targets mHz-band space-interferometer searches, especially LISA, for first-order early-universe phase transitions whose spectra peak in the LISA band. It is particularly relevant for electroweak-scale or hidden-sector phase transitions where the signal is potentially detectable but easily confused with unresolved white-dwarf or black-hole binary foregrounds.",
  "observation_genome": "Caprini et al. arXiv:1910.13125 show that LISA can probe cosmological phase transitions but that foregrounds, source-model uncertainty, and parameter degeneracies complicate interpretation. Hindmarsh et al. arXiv:1504.03291 show that acoustic plasma motion can dominate the gravitational-wave production, motivating sound-wave-based templates while also exposing uncertainties from nonlinear evolution and turbulence onset.",
  "limitation_genome": "Many detectability forecasts still rely on idealized signal-to-noise thresholds, simple spectral shapes, or foreground assumptions separated from the phase-transition inference problem. This risks both false exclusions, if the true hydrodynamic spectrum differs from the assumed template, and false detections, if foreground residuals mimic a broad peaked cosmological spectrum.",
  "delta_genome": "The research direction is to make 'detection' explicitly model-marginalized: construct a public pipeline that injects phase-transition spectra from hydrodynamic simulations and semi-analytic models into realistic LISA data streams, simultaneously infers foregrounds and noise, and reports posterior odds for phase-transition, astrophysical, and mixed-background hypotheses. A useful extension is to add decihertz-detector or PTA priors when the predicted spectrum has observable shoulders outside LISA.",
  "claim_genome": "A foreground-robust, hydrodynamics-aware Bayesian search would improve the credibility of phase-transition SGWB claims by turning uncertain early-universe source physics from a systematic error into an inferred component of the detection model. The expected outcome is not merely higher nominal sensitivity, but better calibrated discovery thresholds and more reliable mapping from a detected stochastic spectrum to early-universe phase-transition parameters."
} אויף
 ถ่ายทอดสดฟุตบอล

{
  "mechanism_genome": "Develop a hierarchical Bayesian stochastic-background search in which the phase-transition signal is not a fixed broken power law but a family of simulation-calibrated hydrodynamic templates: sound-wave-dominated spectra, turbulence corrections, finite acoustic lifetime suppression, and nuisance parameters for bubble-wall velocity, transition strength, inverse duration, and reheating temperature. The inference would jointly fit these templates with LISA instrumental noise and astrophysical compact-binary foregrounds, using Bayesian evidence or simulation-based likelihood ratios as the detection statistic.",
  "niche_genome": "The project targets mHz-band space-interferometer searches, especially LISA, for first-order early-universe phase transitions whose spectra peak in the LISA band. It is particularly relevant for electroweak-scale or hidden-sector phase transitions where the signal is potentially detectable but easily confused with unresolved white-dwarf or black-hole binary foregrounds.",
  "observation_genome": "Caprini et al. arXiv:1910.13125 show that LISA can probe cosmological phase transitions but that foregrounds, source-model uncertainty, and parameter degeneracies complicate interpretation. Hindmarsh et al. arXiv:1504.03291 show that acoustic plasma motion can dominate the gravitational-wave production, motivating sound-wave-based templates while also exposing uncertainties from nonlinear evolution and turbulence onset.",
  "limitation_genome": "Many detectability forecasts still rely on idealized signal-to-noise thresholds, simple spectral shapes, or foreground assumptions separated from the phase-transition inference problem. This risks both false exclusions, if the true hydrodynamic spectrum differs from the assumed template, and false detections, if foreground residuals mimic a broad peaked cosmological spectrum.",
  "delta_genome": "The research direction is to make detection explicitly model-marginalized: construct a public pipeline that injects phase-transition spectra from hydrodynamic simulations and semi-analytic models into realistic LISA data streams, simultaneously infers foregrounds and noise, and reports posterior odds for phase-transition, astrophysical, and mixed-background hypotheses. A useful extension is to add decihertz-detector or PTA priors when the predicted spectrum has observable shoulders outside LISA.",
  "claim_genome": "A foreground-robust, hydrodynamics-aware Bayesian search would improve the credibility of phase-transition stochastic gravitational-wave background claims by turning uncertain early-universe source physics from a systematic error into an inferred component of the detection model. The expected outcome is not merely higher nominal sensitivity, but better calibrated discovery thresholds and more reliable mapping from a detected stochastic spectrum to early-universe phase-transition parameters."

content:
rationale: I first search for anchor papers that directly connect post-quench entanglement growth, ETH/thermalization, and isolated many-body experiments/simulations, so the plan is grounded in established mechanisms rather than generic thermalization language.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"entanglement entropy evolution thermalization isolated many-body quantum systems quench ETH arXiv","discipline":"physics","max_results":5}
```
[result]: Candidate papers include Calabrese & Cardy, “Evolution of entanglement entropy in one-dimensional systems” (arXiv:cond-mat/0503393); Kim & Huse, “Ballistic spreading of entanglement in a diffusive nonintegrable system” (arXiv:1306.4306); and Kaufman et al., “Quantum thermalization through entanglement in an isolated many-body system” (arXiv:1603.04409). These cover quasiparticle theory, numerical nonintegrable dynamics, and experimental observation.

rationale: I read the theory papers to extract the core expected time dependence: linear entanglement growth after a global quench, saturation to a volume-law value, and differences between integrable and nonintegrable systems.
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["arXiv:cond-mat/0503393","arXiv:1306.4306"],"focus":"post-quench entanglement entropy growth laws, saturation, integrability vs nonintegrability, relation to thermal entropy"}
```
[result]: Calabrese & Cardy derive a quasiparticle picture in which entanglement entropy of a subsystem grows approximately linearly until a light-cone time set by subsystem size, then saturates. Kim & Huse show in a nonintegrable spin chain that entanglement spreads ballistically even when conserved quantities diffuse, and the long-time entanglement entropy approaches the thermal entropy density.

rationale: I then read the experimental paper to make the plan measurable, emphasizing what can be compared to subsystem thermalization rather than only wavefunction-level simulation.
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["arXiv:1603.04409"],"focus":"experimental measurement of entanglement entropy and thermalization in isolated many-body systems"}
```
[result]: Kaufman et al. measured second Rényi entanglement entropy in isolated bosonic atoms and showed that global pure-state dynamics can generate local thermal behavior via entanglement between subsystems. The paper motivates tracking subsystem entropy growth, particle-number entropy, and comparison of reduced density matrices to thermal predictions.

rationale: Before proposing the study, I check whether the contribution is a mere reproduction or can be framed as a useful comparative protocol across thermalizing, integrable, and localized regimes with common diagnostics.
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","paper_ids":["arXiv:cond-mat/0503393","arXiv:1306.4306","arXiv:1603.04409"],"proposed_idea":"Benchmark entanglement-entropy evolution during isolated-system thermalization across nonintegrable, integrable, and many-body-localized spin chains using the same quench protocol, comparing von Neumann/Rényi entropies, entanglement spectrum, local observables, ETH/GGE predictions, and saturation to thermodynamic entropy."}
```
[result]: The individual ingredients are established, but a unified benchmarking protocol that directly contrasts entanglement growth, subsystem thermalization quality, and entropy-density saturation across ETH, GGE, and MBL regimes is a coherent and publishable computational study if implemented with careful finite-size and bond-dimension controls. Novelty is moderate: strongest if the work identifies quantitative crossover diagnostics rather than only reproducing linear/logarithmic growth laws.

rationale: I now propose an idea_plan that uses these retrieved results as the baseline: quasiparticle linear growth, ballistic entanglement spreading, saturation near thermal entropy in ETH systems, and measurable subsystem Rényi/von Neumann entropies.
```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"idea_plan","grounding_paper_ids":["arXiv:cond-mat/0503393","arXiv:1306.4306","arXiv:1603.04409"]}
```
[result]: Proposed plan follows below. 
```json
{
  "Idea": "Examine how entanglement entropy evolves during thermalization in isolated many-body quantum systems by simulating global quenches in one-dimensional spin chains and comparing three regimes: nonintegrable ETH dynamics, integrable quasiparticle dynamics, and many-body-localized nonthermal dynamics. The central hypothesis is that ETH systems show ballistic entanglement growth followed by saturation to the thermodynamic entropy density, integrable systems show quasiparticle-controlled growth with saturation described by a generalized Gibbs ensemble, and MBL systems show much slower logarithmic growth without conventional thermalization.",
  "ImplementationSteps": {
    "1_model_selection": "Use spin-1/2 chains with tunable parameters: a nonintegrable tilted-field Ising or XXZ chain for ETH behavior, an integrable transverse-field Ising or XXZ limit for quasiparticle/GGE behavior, and a disordered interacting chain for the MBL regime.",
    "2_quench_protocol": "Prepare low-entanglement product states or ground states of an initial Hamiltonian, then suddenly quench to a final isolated Hamiltonian. Use matched energy densities across regimes so that saturation entropies can be compared meaningfully.",
    "3_time_evolution": "Compute unitary dynamics using exact diagonalization for small systems and TEBD/tDMRG for larger one-dimensional chains. Track numerical convergence with time step, bond dimension, truncation error, and finite-size scaling.",
    "4_entropy_measurements": "At each time, bipartition the chain and compute von Neumann entropy, second Rényi entropy, entanglement spectrum, and subsystem reduced density matrices. Also compute local observables such as magnetization, energy density, and correlation functions.",
    "5_thermalization_tests": "For the nonintegrable model, compare subsystem reduced density matrices and entropy density to canonical or microcanonical ETH predictions. For the integrable model, compare to a GGE. For the disordered model, test for persistent memory of initial conditions and logarithmic entanglement growth.",
    "6_growth_law_fitting": "Fit early- and intermediate-time entanglement growth to candidate forms: linear ballistic growth S(t) ≈ v_E t, quasiparticle light-cone saturation S(t,l) scaling with subsystem size l, diffusive corrections if present, and logarithmic growth S(t) ≈ a log(t) in the localized regime.",
    "7_comparative_analysis": "Relate entanglement growth rate and saturation value to thermalization diagnostics: local observable relaxation, trace distance between reduced density matrices and thermal/GGE states, entanglement-spectrum statistics, and subsystem-size scaling."
  },
  "ImplementationOrder": [
    "Define Hamiltonians and quench parameters with matched post-quench energy densities.",
    "Validate the code on the integrable Calabrese-Cardy-style linear-growth and saturation benchmark.",
    "Run exact diagonalization for small chains to obtain full von Neumann and Rényi entropies plus reduced density matrices.",
    "Scale to larger chains using TEBD/tDMRG for the nonintegrable and integrable regimes.",
    "Add disorder averaging for the MBL regime and check logarithmic versus linear growth.",
    "Fit growth laws and compare saturation entropies with ETH, GGE, and nonthermal predictions.",
    "Summarize the regime-dependent relation between entanglement production and thermalization."
  ],
  "Dataset": "Numerically generated unitary time-evolution data for isolated 1D spin-chain quenches: wavefunctions or matrix-product states, bipartite von Neumann entropy, second Rényi entropy, entanglement spectra, local observables, correlation functions, reduced density matrices, and matched canonical/microcanonical/GGE reference ensembles. Baseline interpretation is grounded in arXiv:cond-mat/0503393, arXiv:1306.4306, and arXiv:1603.04409.",
  "EvaluationMetrics": {
    "entanglement_growth_rate": "Extract v_E or equivalent slope from S(t) in the pre-saturation window and compare across regimes.",
    "saturation_entropy_density": "Compare long-time subsystem entropy per site to thermal entropy density for ETH systems and to GGE entropy for integrable systems.",
    "thermalization_error": "Compute trace distance or relative entropy between simulated subsystem reduced density matrices and ETH/GGE reference states.",
    "finite_size_stability": "Assess whether growth rates, saturation values, and relaxation errors remain stable as system size and subsystem size vary.",
    "numerical_convergence": "Track TEBD/tDMRG truncation error, bond-dimension dependence, time-step convergence, and ED finite-size effects.",
    "regime_classification_accuracy": "Use fitted growth laws and thermalization diagnostics to distinguish ETH linear-growth thermalization, integrable quasiparticle/GGE relaxation, and MBL logarithmic nonthermal dynamics."
  },
  "ExpectedOutcome": "The study should show that entanglement entropy is not merely a byproduct of thermalization but a quantitative diagnostic of it. In nonintegrable isolated systems, entanglement should spread ballistically and saturate near the thermodynamic entropy density, consistent with Kim-Huse and ETH expectations. In integrable systems, growth should follow the quasiparticle picture of Calabrese-Cardy and saturate to a GGE-compatible value. In localized systems, entanglement growth should be much slower and local observables should fail to fully thermalize. The final result would be a unified map connecting entanglement-growth laws, saturation entropy, and the quality of subsystem thermalization."
}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Use an idea_plan to examine how entanglement entropy evolves during thermalization in isolated many-body quantum systems.

content:
Rationale: I’ll first identify anchor papers that define the current Lorentz-invariance-violation (LIV) gamma-ray-burst test landscape, including both seminal methodology and strongest observational constraints.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"Lorentz invariance violation gamma ray bursts time of flight Fermi LAT GRB 090510 arXiv Vasileiou constraints"}
```  
[result]: Found candidate papers: Amelino-Camelia et al. 1998, Nature, arXiv:astro-ph/9712103; Abdo et al. 2009, Nature, “A limit on the variation of the speed of light arising from quantum gravity effects,” arXiv:0908.1832; Jacob & Piran 2008, JCAP, arXiv:0712.2170; Vasileiou et al. 2013, PRD, arXiv:1305.3463; MAGIC Collaboration 2020, PRL, arXiv:2001.09728.  

Rationale: GRB 090510 is a decisive benchmark because it used a high-energy photon from a short GRB to set Planck-scale sensitivity, while Vasileiou et al. generalize to a population/statistical analysis.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["arXiv:0908.1832","arXiv:1305.3463"]}
```  
[result]: arXiv:0908.1832 reports a 31 GeV photon from GRB 090510 arriving 0.829 s after trigger and derives lower limits on the linear quantum-gravity mass scale at or above the Planck scale under conservative assumptions. arXiv:1305.3463 analyzes Fermi-LAT GRBs with several methods, emphasizing that intrinsic source lags are a dominant systematic and that robust limits require conservative emission-time assumptions or statistical treatments.  

Rationale: I’ll extract the reusable “research genome” from the literature: what mechanism is tested, what observational niche is exploited, what limits precision, and what modification could improve the experiment.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["arXiv:astro-ph/9712103","arXiv:0712.2170","arXiv:0908.1832","arXiv:1305.3463","arXiv:2001.09728"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted common mechanism: photon group velocity modified by LIV dispersion, producing energy- and redshift-dependent time-of-flight delays with linear or quadratic dependence on E/E_QG. Niche: cosmological GRBs supply long baselines, millisecond-to-second variability, and GeV–TeV photons; short GRBs reduce intrinsic-lag ambiguity. Main limitation: source-intrinsic spectral lags and uncertain emission time dominate over detector timing errors.  

Rationale: To avoid merely restating existing tests, I’ll check whether the proposed improvement—hierarchical multi-burst inference using prompt light-curve morphology and redshift scaling—is distinct from prior single-photon/conservative-bound methods.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"Develop precision tests of Lorentz invariance with astrophysical GRBs by combining Fermi-LAT/GBM and IACT high-energy photons in a hierarchical Bayesian time-of-flight model that separates intrinsic spectral lags from redshift-dependent LIV dispersion using multi-energy pulse templates and host-redshift information.","against":["arXiv:0908.1832","arXiv:1305.3463","arXiv:2001.09728"]}
```  
[result]: Novel relative to the strongest single-event limits because it targets population-level separation of intrinsic lags from propagation delays instead of relying mainly on one high-energy photon and conservative emission-time bracketing. Partially overlaps with previous statistical GRB LIV analyses, but the explicit joint modeling of pulse morphology, energy-resolved prompt emission, redshift scaling, and cross-instrument GeV–TeV follow-up is a clear refinement.  

```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","content":{"mechanism_genome":"Test Lorentz invariance by searching for vacuum-dispersion time-of-flight effects in GRB photons: modified photon group velocity would produce arrival-time shifts scaling approximately as Δt_LIV ∝ (E^n/E_QG,n^n) times the cosmological redshift integral, with n=1 or n=2 for linear/quadratic LIV. The precision test should fit this propagation term jointly with source-intrinsic spectral lags rather than assuming all high-energy delays are propagation effects.","niche_genome":"Use astrophysical gamma-ray bursts, especially short GRBs and bright long GRBs with measured redshifts, sharp prompt variability, and detected GeV–TeV photons from Fermi-GBM/LAT plus rapid Cherenkov follow-up. This niche gives cosmological baselines, sub-second time structure, and large photon-energy leverage, as exemplified by GRB 090510 and later Fermi-LAT/IACT samples.","observation_genome":"Anchor the test to established observations: GRB 090510 included a ~31 GeV photon arriving 0.829 s after trigger and set Planck-scale lower limits on linear LIV in arXiv:0908.1832; Fermi-LAT population analyses in arXiv:1305.3463 showed that multiple GRBs can strengthen robustness but are limited by intrinsic-lag modeling; MAGIC GRB 190114C constraints demonstrate the added leverage of very-high-energy photons despite larger source-systematic uncertainties.","limitation_genome":"The dominant limitation is not clock precision but astrophysical nuisance structure: unknown emission time of the highest-energy photons, energy-dependent intrinsic lags, pulse overlap, uncertain association of GeV/TeV photons with MeV prompt pulses, selection effects toward bright bursts, and incomplete redshift coverage. Single-photon bounds are powerful but fragile because conservative assumptions can weaken limits and aggressive assumptions can confuse intrinsic emission delays with LIV.","delta_genome":"Develop a precision-test pipeline using hierarchical Bayesian inference across a curated GRB sample: model energy-resolved pulse templates in GBM/LAT/IACT bands, assign intrinsic-lag hyperparameters by burst class, include the redshift-dependent LIV propagation kernel, and marginalize over photon association and detector response. Prioritize short, high-redshift, high-variability GRBs and trigger rapid TeV follow-up; report limits as posterior bounds on E_QG,1 and E_QG,2 plus posterior predictive checks that verify the expected redshift scaling of any candidate delay.","claim_genome":"A credible next-generation GRB Lorentz-invariance test should move from isolated conservative time-delay bounds to population-level separation of source lags and propagation dispersion. If no redshift-correlated energy-dependent lag is found, the approach can set more robust Planck-scale-or-better constraints on linear LIV and improved quadratic limits; if a candidate signal appears, its cosmological scaling and consistency across burst classes become decisive discriminants against intrinsic GRB physics."}}
```  
[result]: Proposed gene_genome grounded in arXiv:0908.1832, arXiv:1305.3463, arXiv:2001.09728, and foundational GRB LIV time-of-flight methodology.
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Test Lorentz invariance by searching for vacuum-dispersion time-of-flight effects in GRB photons: modified photon group velocity would produce arrival-time shifts scaling approximately as Δt_LIV ∝ (E^n/E_QG,n^n) times the cosmological redshift integral, with n=1 or n=2 for linear/quadratic LIV. The precision test should fit this propagation term jointly with source-intrinsic spectral lags rather than assuming all high-energy delays are propagation effects.",
  "niche_genome": "Use astrophysical gamma-ray bursts, especially short GRBs and bright long GRBs with measured redshifts, sharp prompt variability, and detected GeV–TeV photons from Fermi-GBM/LAT plus rapid Cherenkov follow-up. This niche gives cosmological baselines, sub-second time structure, and large photon-energy leverage, as exemplified by GRB 090510 and later Fermi-LAT/IACT samples.",
  "observation_genome": "Anchor the test to established observations: GRB 090510 included a ~31 GeV photon arriving 0.829 s after trigger and set Planck-scale lower limits on linear LIV in arXiv:0908.1832; Fermi-LAT population analyses in arXiv:1305.3463 showed that multiple GRBs can strengthen robustness but are limited by intrinsic-lag modeling; MAGIC GRB 190114C constraints demonstrate the added leverage of very-high-energy photons despite larger source-systematic uncertainties.",
  "limitation_genome": "The dominant limitation is not clock precision but astrophysical nuisance structure: unknown emission time of the highest-energy photons, energy-dependent intrinsic lags, pulse overlap, uncertain association of GeV/TeV photons with MeV prompt pulses, selection effects toward bright bursts, and incomplete redshift coverage. Single-photon bounds are powerful but fragile because conservative assumptions can weaken limits and aggressive assumptions can confuse intrinsic emission delays with LIV.",
  "delta_genome": "Develop a precision-test pipeline using hierarchical Bayesian inference across a curated GRB sample: model energy-resolved pulse templates in GBM/LAT/IACT bands, assign intrinsic-lag hyperparameters by burst class, include the redshift-dependent LIV propagation kernel, and marginalize over photon association and detector response. Prioritize short, high-redshift, high-variability GRBs and trigger rapid TeV follow-up; report limits as posterior bounds on E_QG,1 and E_QG,2 plus posterior predictive checks that verify the expected redshift scaling of any candidate delay.",
  "claim_genome": "A credible next-generation GRB Lorentz-invariance test should move from isolated conservative time-delay bounds to population-level separation of source lags and propagation dispersion. If no redshift-correlated energy-dependent lag is found, the approach can set more robust Planck-scale-or-better constraints on linear LIV and improved quadratic limits; if a candidate signal appears, its cosmological scaling and consistency across burst classes become decisive discriminants against intrinsic GRB physics."
}
