content:
rationale: I first search for anchor papers that explicitly combine dust charging, near-surface plasma sheaths, and transport/levitation in lunar or Martian environments. The goal is to avoid a generic electrostatics answer and ground the mechanism in published sheath and dust-transport models.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"lunar dust charging plasma sheath particle transport levitation photoelectron sheath simulations dynamic fountain model"}
```  
[result]: Found key lunar candidates: Stubbs, Vondrak & Farrell 2006, “A dynamic fountain model for lunar dust” (DOI: 10.1016/j.asr.2005.04.048); Poppe & Horányi 2010, “Simulations of the photoelectron sheath and dust levitation on the lunar surface” (DOI: 10.1029/2010JA015286); Nitter, Havnes & Melandsø 1998, “Levitation and dynamics of charged dust in the photoelectron sheath above surfaces in space” (DOI: 10.1029/97JE03523).
rationale: Because the prompt includes lunar or Martian environments, I add a Mars-focused search to capture triboelectric charging and atmospheric/surface transport rather than only airless photoelectron sheaths. This helps define the niche difference: lunar sheath-controlled lofting vs Martian saltation/dust-devil charging with weak plasma coupling.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"Mars dust charging electric fields dust devils particle transport plasma sheath triboelectric charging"}
```  
[result]: Found Mars-relevant candidates: Farrell et al. 1999, “Electric and magnetic signatures of dust devils from the 2001 Mars Surveyor Lander” (DOI: 10.1029/1999JE001088); Delory et al. 2006, “Oxidant enhancement in Martian dust devils and storms: Storm electric fields and electron dissociative attachment” (DOI: 10.1089/ast.2006.6.451); Kok & Renno 2008, “Electrostatics in wind-blown sand” (DOI: 10.1103/PhysRevLett.100.014501).
rationale: I read the most central lunar simulation paper because it quantifies the sheath potential structure and directly tests whether sheath electric fields can loft grains. This is the strongest evidence for a mechanism linking charging to vertical transport.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1029/2010JA015286"}
```  
[result]: Poppe & Horányi model the lunar photoelectron sheath with particle-in-cell methods and compute dust charging/forces near the surface. They find that ordinary photoelectron sheath fields can levitate only very small grains under restrictive conditions, and that sustained high-altitude transport requires either enhanced fields, localized gradients, or additional release mechanisms.
rationale: I read the dynamic fountain model next because it connects electrostatic lofting to ballistic particle transport and observable horizon-glow-scale dust distributions. This paper provides the transport picture complementary to the local sheath physics.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1016/j.asr.2005.04.048"}
```  
[result]: Stubbs, Vondrak & Farrell propose that UV and plasma currents charge lunar dust grains, which are then launched by surface electric fields and follow ballistic trajectories before redeposition. The model emphasizes terminator and wake-region electric-field enhancements as likely sites for lofting and horizontal redistribution, but it relies on uncertain grain release thresholds, cohesion, and near-surface field strengths.
rationale: I now synthesize the retrieved lunar sheath and fountain evidence with the Mars search results into a compact research genome. The emphasis is on the decisive gap: steady sheaths alone are usually too weak, so transport likely depends on intermittent high-field regions plus grain-release physics.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","content":{"mechanism_genome":"Dust grains acquire charge through photoemission, solar-wind/electron collection, secondary emission, and—in Martian saltation or dust devils—triboelectric collisions. The resulting q/m couples grains to near-surface electric fields in plasma sheaths; when electrostatic force exceeds gravity plus cohesion, grains loft, hop ballistically, or are horizontally redistributed, especially near terminators, shadow boundaries, wake regions, or storm/dust-devil fields.","niche_genome":"Best suited to airless or weakly collisional surface environments where sheath fields are not shorted out: lunar dayside/terminator/wake regions, shadow edges, and possibly asteroid-like regoliths. Martian application is more niche-specific: electrostatics modifies wind-driven saltation and dust-devil transport rather than acting as the sole lifting mechanism, because the atmosphere dominates drag and collisions.","observation_genome":"Poppe & Horányi 2010 (10.1029/2010JA015286) show with sheath simulations that ordinary lunar photoelectron sheaths can levitate only very small grains under limited conditions, implying that enhanced fields or extra release mechanisms are needed. Stubbs, Vondrak & Farrell 2006 (10.1016/j.asr.2005.04.048) frame lofted charged grains as a dynamic fountain: grains launch from high-field surface regions, follow ballistic paths, and redeposit, producing redistribution and possible horizon-glow-scale dust populations. Mars literature on dust devils and wind-blown sand indicates large electric fields and tribocharging, but transport remains coupled to atmospheric saltation.","limitation_genome":"The dominant uncertainty is not charging alone but grain release: cohesive/van der Waals forces, contact geometry, aggregate structure, and patchy surface potentials can overwhelm simple electrostatic lift estimates. Existing models often idealize sheath geometry, assume smooth grains and steady plasma conditions, and lack simultaneous in situ measurements of dust flux, electric field, grain charge, and plasma parameters at centimeter-to-meter scales.","delta_genome":"A useful next study would couple a kinetic plasma-sheath model, e.g. PIC or hybrid sheath solver, to a discrete-element regolith/contact model and ballistic or saltation transport module. It should scan illumination angle, shadow boundaries, plasma density, grain size, cohesion, and tribo/photoelectric charging, then output launch probability and hop-length distributions rather than only equilibrium levitation heights.","claim_genome":"Particle transport by dust charging is likely intermittent and boundary-localized: steady lunar photoelectron sheaths are usually insufficient for bulk lofting, but enhanced sheath gradients at terminators, wakes, shadow edges, or disturbed plasma regions can mobilize submicron-to-micron grains. On Mars, charging most plausibly acts as a modifier of wind-driven transport—altering thresholds, aggregation, and suspension—rather than replacing aerodynamic lifting."}}
```  
[result]: Proposed gene_genome grounded in Poppe & Horányi’s sheath constraints, Stubbs et al.’s dynamic fountain transport model, and Mars electrostatic saltation/dust-devil context. The proposed research direction targets the missing coupled problem: time-dependent charging + sheath fields + cohesive grain release + transport statistics.

prompt.full_prompt: Study how dust charging and plasma sheath dynamics affect particle transport in lunar or Martian surface environments.

content:
rationale: I first need representative experimental and review papers to anchor the viability assessment in demonstrated NV-center performance, especially for domain walls/skyrmions and nanoscale magnetometry limits.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "nitrogen-vacancy center diamond magnetometry nanoscale imaging magnetic textures domain walls skyrmions",
  "discipline": "physics",
  "k": 5
}
```  
[result]: Found relevant candidates: Rondin et al. 2014 review, DOI `10.1088/0034-4885/77/5/056503`; Tetienne et al. 2014 domain-wall NV microscopy, DOI `10.1126/science.1250113`; Dovzhenko et al. 2018 skyrmion imaging, DOI `10.1038/s41467-018-06626-2`; Gross et al. 2016 quantitative magnetic imaging, DOI `10.1038/nature16509`; Casola et al. 2018 review, DOI `10.1038/s42254-018-0003-y`.

rationale: The Rondin review is a useful baseline for sensor modalities, spatial-resolution/sensitivity tradeoffs, and practical limitations of single-NV and wide-field NV magnetometry.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "10.1088/0034-4885/77/5/056503",
  "sections": [
    "abstract",
    "magnetometry principles",
    "nanoscale magnetic imaging",
    "limitations"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: The review reports that NV centers offer room-temperature, optically readable magnetic-field sensing with nanoscale spatial resolution, with single-NV scanning probes achieving nanometer-scale proximity but limited throughput. It emphasizes tradeoffs among sensitivity, standoff distance, acquisition time, and vector reconstruction, all central to mapping nanoscale magnetic textures.

rationale: I then read experimental texture-imaging papers to verify that NV methods have actually mapped magnetic textures rather than only idealized fields.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "10.1038/s41467-018-06626-2",
  "sections": [
    "abstract",
    "methods",
    "results",
    "discussion"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: Dovzhenko et al. demonstrate stray-field imaging of individual skyrmions using scanning NV magnetometry, showing that nanoscale magnetic textures can be reconstructed from NV-measured magnetic fields. The work also highlights core constraints: finite NV-sample standoff blurs high spatial frequencies, inversion from stray field to magnetization needs modeling assumptions, and measurement speed can be limiting.

rationale: Before proposing a plan, I check whether the contribution should be framed as a new sensor demonstration or as a viability-assessment framework combining benchmarks, simulations, and reconstruction criteria.  
```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "claim": "A structured viability assessment of NV-center quantum sensors for mapping nanoscale magnetic textures using benchmark experiments, forward modeling, inverse reconstruction, and quantitative go/no-go metrics.",
  "against": [
    "10.1088/0034-4885/77/5/056503",
    "10.1126/science.1250113",
    "10.1038/s41467-018-06626-2",
    "10.1038/nature16509",
    "10.1038/s42254-018-0003-y"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: Existing papers demonstrate NV magnetometry and specific texture-imaging cases, while reviews summarize capabilities. A systematic viability framework with task-specific performance thresholds for texture classes, standoff/noise/acquisition-time sweeps, and reconstruction-fidelity metrics appears distinct from a single experimental demonstration.

rationale: The final plan should therefore assess viability quantitatively rather than merely advocate NV sensing; it should compare sensor modes, simulate realistic fields, and define thresholds for when magnetic-texture mapping is trustworthy.  
```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "idea_plan",
  "content": {
    "Idea": "Assess the viability of nitrogen-vacancy-center quantum sensors for nanoscale magnetic-texture mapping by combining literature benchmarks, realistic forward simulations, inverse magnetic reconstruction, and experimental validation criteria. The central hypothesis is that NV sensors are viable for room-temperature, noninvasive mapping of domain walls and skyrmions when the texture length scale exceeds the effective standoff-limited point-spread width and when reconstruction assumptions are explicitly constrained.",
    "ImplementationSteps": {
      "1_LiteratureBenchmark": "Compile demonstrated NV magnetic-imaging performance from key papers including Rondin et al. 2014, DOI 10.1088/0034-4885/77/5/056503, and Dovzhenko et al. 2018, DOI 10.1038/s41467-018-06626-2. Extract sensor type, NV-sample standoff, magnetic-field sensitivity, spatial resolution, acquisition time, vector-field capability, sample temperature, and demonstrated texture class.",
      "2_TextureForwardModeling": "Generate synthetic magnetic textures relevant to condensed-matter systems: ferromagnetic domain walls, Bloch/Néel skyrmions, vortex states, and antiferromagnetic textures with weak stray fields. Compute stray magnetic fields at realistic NV depths or tip-sample distances using magnetostatic Green's functions.",
      "3_SensorNoiseAndGeometryModel": "Apply NV-specific measurement models for scanning single-NV probes and wide-field NV ensembles, including photon shot noise, ODMR contrast, linewidth, bias-field orientation, finite pixel size, and standoff-distance blur. Sweep standoff distances from approximately 5 to 100 nm and acquisition times from fast imaging to high-SNR mapping.",
      "4_InverseReconstruction": "Recover magnetization or texture parameters from simulated and benchmark magnetic-field maps using regularized Fourier inversion, Bayesian parameter inference, and physics-informed fitting. Include uncertainty estimates because stray-field inversion is non-unique without assumptions about film thickness, magnetization magnitude, and texture symmetry.",
      "5_ViabilityDecisionAnalysis": "Define go/no-go regimes by comparing reconstruction fidelity against required spatial resolution and magnetic sensitivity. Identify when scanning NV is preferable for nanoscale fidelity and when wide-field NV is preferable for throughput, dynamics, or larger-area texture statistics.",
      "6_ExperimentalValidationPath": "Validate the framework using published NV texture maps and, if available, new measurements on a calibrated magnetic multilayer hosting domain walls or skyrmions. Cross-check reconstructed texture sizes against an independent method such as MFM, Lorentz TEM, XMCD-PEEM, or micromagnetic simulation."
    },
    "ImplementationOrder": [
      "1_LiteratureBenchmark",
      "2_TextureForwardModeling",
      "3_SensorNoiseAndGeometryModel",
      "4_InverseReconstruction",
      "5_ViabilityDecisionAnalysis",
      "6_ExperimentalValidationPath"
    ],
    "Dataset": "A hybrid dataset consisting of: published NV magnetometry benchmarks from DOI 10.1088/0034-4885/77/5/056503 and DOI 10.1038/s41467-018-06626-2; digitized or author-provided magnetic-field maps where available; simulated stray-field maps for domain walls, skyrmions, vortices, and weak-stray-field antiferromagnetic textures; and optional validation data from a calibrated thin-film magnetic sample measured by scanning or wide-field NV microscopy.",
    "EvaluationMetrics": {
      "SpatialResolution": "Minimum resolvable texture length scale versus NV-sample standoff and pixel size; target is resolving features comparable to or smaller than the domain-wall width or skyrmion diameter with less than 20% size error.",
      "MagneticSensitivity": "Field noise floor in nT/sqrt(Hz) to uT/sqrt(Hz), depending on NV modality, and the resulting minimum detectable stray field from the texture.",
      "ReconstructionError": "Normalized root-mean-square error between true and reconstructed magnetization or texture parameters in simulation; report separate errors for field reconstruction and magnetization inversion.",
      "ParameterAccuracy": "Error in extracted skyrmion diameter, domain-wall position, chirality-sensitive field signature, or magnetic moment density.",
      "UncertaintyCalibration": "Coverage of confidence intervals from Bayesian or bootstrap reconstructions; viable if reported uncertainties match empirical errors.",
      "Throughput": "Area mapped per unit time at a specified signal-to-noise ratio, separating scanning-NV nanoscale mapping from wide-field ensemble imaging.",
      "PerturbationRisk": "Qualitative and quantitative assessment of optical heating, microwave drive, bias-field perturbation, and sample compatibility."
    },
    "ExpectedOutcome": "NV-center sensors are expected to be viable for nanoscale magnetic-texture mapping when strong stray fields are present, the NV-sample standoff is kept below the texture length scale, and reconstruction is constrained by known sample geometry. Scanning NV should provide the highest spatial fidelity for individual skyrmions and narrow domain walls, while wide-field NV should be more viable for larger textures, statistical imaging, and dynamics. The main limitations will be standoff-induced spatial filtering, slow acquisition for high-resolution scans, weak stray fields in compensated antiferromagnets, and non-unique inversion from magnetic field to magnetization."
  }
}
```  
[result]: Proposed an evidence-grounded `idea_plan` for a quantitative viability study of NV-center magnetic-texture mapping. The plan treats NV sensing as promising but conditional, with explicit resolution, sensitivity, reconstruction, and perturbation criteria.

prompt.full_prompt: Use an idea_plan to assess the viability of quantum sensors based on nitrogen-vacancy centers for mapping nanoscale magnetic textures.

content:
rationale: I first locate recent, citable anchors on programmable Rydberg arrays and on photon-mediated atom–atom couplings, because the requested genome should combine both rather than restating only van der Waals Rydberg physics.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"programmable Rydberg atom arrays quantum simulator photon mediated interactions photonic crystal waveguide arXiv","discipline":"physics","k":5}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers include Ebadi et al., “Quantum phases of matter on a 256-atom programmable quantum simulator” (arXiv:2012.12281), Browaeys & Lahaye, “Many-body physics with individually controlled Rydberg atoms” (arXiv:2002.07413), and Douglas et al., “Quantum many-body models with cold atoms coupled to photonic crystals” (arXiv:1501.07888). The first two establish programmable Rydberg-array capabilities; the third establishes engineered photon-mediated interactions via structured photonic reservoirs.

rationale: I read the Rydberg-array paper to extract what is already programmable and what remains constrained by native interactions.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:2012.12281","sections":["abstract","main results","discussion"]}
```  
[result]: Ebadi et al. demonstrate coherent many-body dynamics and quantum phases in defect-free, reconfigurable arrays up to 256 neutral atoms using Rydberg blockade interactions. The interaction graph is largely set by geometry and isotropic van der Waals tails, motivating schemes for more flexible coupling range, sign, and connectivity.

rationale: I then read the photonic-crystal/waveguide proposal because it is the most direct evidence that photons can mediate tunable, long-range spin interactions between neutral atoms.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:1501.07888","sections":["abstract","model","results"]}
```  
[result]: Douglas et al. show that atoms coupled to photonic crystal band edges can acquire coherent, tunable atom–atom interactions mediated by virtual photons, with interaction length and sign controlled by detuning and band structure. The proposal emphasizes that nanophotonic reservoirs can implement spin models not easily available from direct free-space interactions.

rationale: I extract the research genome by contrasting native Rydberg arrays with photonic-reservoir-mediated coupling, preserving only claims supported by the retrieved sources.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["arXiv:2012.12281","arXiv:1501.07888"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted mechanisms: Rydberg blockade gives strong local nonlinearities in programmable geometries; photonic band-edge modes provide virtual-photon-mediated coherent couplings whose range/sign can be engineered. Key limitation: no single retrieved work demonstrates a large, tweezer-programmable Rydberg array integrated with a low-loss structured photonic mediator.

rationale: I check novelty against the combined concept—Rydberg programmability plus photon-mediated nonlocal couplings—rather than against either component alone.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"Integrate programmable optical-tweezer Rydberg atom arrays with a structured photonic mediator, such as a nanophotonic crystal waveguide or cavity band-edge mode, to implement dynamically reconfigurable photon-mediated interactions beyond native van der Waals coupling.","against":["arXiv:2012.12281","arXiv:1501.07888","arXiv:2002.07413"],"criteria":["mechanism novelty","experimental gap","physics payoff"]}
```  
[result]: The components are established separately, but the integrated platform is an open gap: programmable Rydberg arrays have not yet broadly incorporated engineered photonic reservoirs as a second interaction channel. The physics payoff is high because one could compare native Rydberg blockade, cavity/waveguide-mediated exchange, and their interference in the same programmable geometry.

```propose
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Use an optical-tweezer Rydberg array as the programmable matter platform and couple selected Rydberg or ground-Rydberg transitions to a structured photonic mode, e.g. a photonic-crystal waveguide band edge or high-cooperativity cavity. Native Rydberg blockade supplies strong local nonlinearity, while virtual photons mediate additional coherent atom-atom interactions whose range, sign, and spatial profile are tuned by atom-photon detuning, photonic density of states, and array placement.",
  "niche_genome": "This targets the gap between large-scale, reconfigurable Rydberg quantum simulators, exemplified by arXiv:2012.12281, and engineered photon-mediated spin models in nanophotonic reservoirs, exemplified by arXiv:1501.07888. The niche is programmable many-body physics with nonlocal and potentially sign-changing interactions not fixed solely by geometric van der Waals tails.",
  "observation_genome": "Existing Rydberg arrays can prepare and probe many-body phases in hundreds of atoms, but their interaction graph is primarily determined by atom positions and Rydberg-state van der Waals interactions. Photonic-crystal theory shows that virtual photons near a band edge can generate tunable long-range coherent atom-atom couplings. Combining these observations suggests a hybrid simulator where photon-mediated exchange can be switched on as a second, independently engineered interaction channel.",
  "limitation_genome": "Major limitations are atom-surface decoherence and trapping near nanophotonic structures, photon loss and disorder in the mediator, maintaining Rydberg coherence in the presence of optical materials, and separating coherent photon-mediated exchange from dissipative collective decay. Scaling from few atoms near a photonic device to defect-free tweezer arrays with site-resolved control is the central experimental bottleneck.",
  "delta_genome": "The project should first demonstrate a minimal two- to ten-atom Rydberg tweezer array coupled to one engineered photonic mode and measure distance- and detuning-dependent coherent exchange or conditional level shifts. It should then benchmark hybrid interaction graphs against pure van der Waals arrays by varying photonic detuning, array geometry, and Rydberg state. A decisive delta would be observing tunable interaction sign or range at fixed atomic geometry, which native Rydberg blockade alone cannot provide.",
  "claim_genome": "A programmable Rydberg array with photon-mediated couplings would form a new quantum-simulation architecture: one retaining the site-resolved initialization/readout and strong nonlinear blockade of neutral atoms while adding reservoir-engineered, reconfigurable nonlocal interactions. If coherent photon-mediated coupling exceeds loss and inhomogeneity, the platform could realize spin models, frustration patterns, and transport phenomena inaccessible to current geometry-only Rydberg arrays."
}
```-offsetof
Final done. Need ensure format ends with propose only? It has prose. Good.

prompt.full_prompt: Generate a gene_genome output for a project investigating photon-mediated interactions in programmable arrays of Rydberg atoms.

content:
Rationale: I first locate canonical kinetic-simulation papers tying relativistic collisionless shock formation to nonthermal particle acceleration, because the prompt asks for physics rather than phenomenology alone. The search targets “Weibel/current-filamentation,” “PIC,” “relativistic jets,” and “cosmic rays.”  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"relativistic collisionless shock formation Weibel instability PIC particle acceleration cosmic rays jets","discipline":"physics","max_results":5}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers included Medvedev & Loeb 1999, ApJ, `astro-ph/9904363`, on Weibel-mediated relativistic shocks; Spitkovsky 2008, ApJL, `arXiv:0802.3216`, on Fermi acceleration in relativistic collisionless shocks; Sironi & Spitkovsky 2009, ApJ, `arXiv:0901.2578`, on magnetized pair shocks and obliquity; and Sironi, Spitkovsky & Arons 2013, ApJ, `arXiv:1301.5333`, on maximum particle energy in relativistic shocks.  

Rationale: I read the Spitkovsky paper because it is a compact reference for the basic mechanism: shock self-formation from plasma instabilities and emergence of a nonthermal tail. This helps anchor the mechanism/observation fields in simulation evidence.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:0802.3216","focus":["shock formation mechanism","particle acceleration","spectral index","simulation limitations"]}
```  
[result]: The paper reports first-principles PIC simulations of unmagnetized relativistic pair shocks. Counterstreaming flows generate Weibel-like magnetic turbulence that mediates the shock and enables repeated shock crossings, producing a power-law nonthermal tail with index about 2.4; the simulations are limited by finite box sizes and pair-plasma assumptions.  

Rationale: I then read the maximum-energy paper because high-energy cosmic ray production depends less on whether acceleration starts and more on how far it can proceed in magnetized jet conditions. This provides constraints from magnetization and turbulence scale.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:1301.5333","focus":["maximum energy","magnetization dependence","cosmic ray relevance","time scaling"]}
```  
[result]: Sironi, Spitkovsky & Arons show that relativistic shocks can accelerate particles efficiently only in weakly magnetized regimes where self-generated turbulence can scatter particles upstream. The maximum energy grows rapidly at early times but later more slowly, roughly as diffusive scattering in small-scale turbulence permits, implying finite magnetization and limited turbulence coherence constrain ultra-high-energy cosmic-ray production.  

Rationale: To sharpen the research contribution, I check whether a combined “shock-formation plus cosmic-ray ceiling” framing is already saturated. The novelty target is not simply “PIC shocks accelerate particles,” but a predictive map connecting jet magnetization/obliquity and Weibel turbulence growth to cosmic-ray spectral cutoffs.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","paper_ids":["astro-ph/9904363","arXiv:0802.3216","arXiv:0901.2578","arXiv:1301.5333"],"claim":"Relativistic plasma jets form collisionless shocks through Weibel/current-filamentation turbulence; the same turbulence controls Fermi injection, spectral slope, and maximum cosmic-ray energy, with magnetization and field obliquity setting whether jets are efficient high-energy cosmic-ray sources."}
```  
[result]: The claim is well supported but not novel as a broad statement. A more distinctive angle is to formulate a parameter-space criterion linking shock formation time, upstream magnetization, field obliquity, and turbulence coherence length to an observable cosmic-ray cutoff and accompanying high-energy photon/neutrino signatures.  

Rationale: I now propose a compact research genome grounded in the retrieved literature: Weibel-mediated shock formation from `astro-ph/9904363`/`arXiv:0802.3216`, acceleration constraints from `arXiv:0901.2578`/`arXiv:1301.5333`, and the open gap of connecting microscopic PIC scalings to jet-scale cosmic-ray observables.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","content":{"mechanism_genome":"In weakly magnetized relativistic plasma jets, counterstreaming upstream and downstream particles excite Weibel/current-filamentation instabilities that generate small-scale magnetic turbulence, thicken the collisionless shock transition, and scatter energetic particles across the shock. Repeated shock crossings then yield relativistic diffusive/Fermi acceleration, while upstream magnetization and magnetic-field obliquity determine whether particles can return upstream or are advected downstream.","niche_genome":"Relativistic collisionless shocks in AGN, GRB, and pulsar-wind-like jets where Coulomb collisions are negligible and kinetic plasma instabilities mediate dissipation. The most favorable cosmic-ray niche is low-sigma, quasi-parallel or effectively turbulence-dominated jet regions; highly oblique or superluminal magnetized shocks suppress repeated crossings.","observation_genome":"PIC simulations show spontaneous formation of Weibel-mediated shocks and nonthermal power-law particle tails, with Spitkovsky 2008 (`arXiv:0802.3216`) finding a relativistic-shock spectrum near p≈2.4 in unmagnetized pair plasma. Magnetized-shock studies such as Sironi & Spitkovsky 2009 (`arXiv:0901.2578`) and Sironi, Spitkovsky & Arons 2013 (`arXiv:1301.5333`) indicate that magnetization and obliquity regulate acceleration efficiency and maximum energy growth.","limitation_genome":"Current first-principles simulations cover limited spatial dynamic range, reduced mass ratios or pair plasmas, idealized field geometries, and times far shorter than jet lifetimes. The main uncertainty is extrapolating small-scale Weibel turbulence and finite-time maximum-energy growth to macroscopic AGN/GRB jet environments and to ultra-high-energy cosmic-ray cutoffs.","delta_genome":"Develop a multiscale criterion that couples shock-formation time, Weibel turbulence coherence length, upstream sigma, and obliquity to a maximum cosmic-ray energy and escaping-particle spectrum. This would bridge PIC-derived microphysics with jet-scale transport models and predict when relativistic jets produce merely GeV–TeV nonthermal particles versus PeV–EeV cosmic rays.","claim_genome":"Relativistic plasma jets become efficient high-energy cosmic-ray accelerators only when the same self-generated turbulence that forms the collisionless shock also persists far enough upstream to permit repeated particle returns; thus shock formation and cosmic-ray production are not separate processes but a single magnetization- and obliquity-controlled kinetic cycle."}}
```  
[result]: Proposed gene genome is grounded in the retrieved PIC-shock literature and frames the research opportunity as a quantitative bridge from microphysical shock formation to observable high-energy cosmic-ray limits.

prompt.full_prompt: Explore the physics of shock formation in relativistic plasma jets and its connection to high-energy cosmic ray production.

content:
rationale: I first search for lattice-QCD finite-temperature/density studies that provide usable anchors: hadronic screening observables for structure, and finite-density methods such as Taylor expansion or imaginary chemical potential. These establish what can be computed reliably and what gaps remain for a new computational study.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"lattice QCD hadron screening masses chiral crossover finite temperature baryon chemical potential Taylor expansion imaginary chemical potential hadron structure","discipline":"physics","limit":5}
```
[result]: Found candidates including arXiv:1504.05274 on hadron screening masses across the chiral crossover, arXiv:2002.02821 on QCD crossover at finite baryon density, and arXiv:2202.09184 reviewing finite-density lattice-QCD methods. Results emphasize screening correlators as robust finite-T observables and Taylor/imaginary-µ methods as standard workarounds for the sign problem.

rationale: I read the finite-temperature screening-mass paper because it is directly tied to hadronic observables and chiral-restoration signatures. It helps define which hadron channels and correlators are numerically mature enough for a structure-oriented extension.
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:1504.05274"}
```
[result]: The paper reports 2+1 flavor lattice-QCD calculations of meson and baryon screening masses near the chiral crossover. It finds parity doubling in baryonic screening masses above the crossover and channel-dependent restoration patterns, supporting screening correlators as sensitive probes of in-medium hadron structure.

rationale: I read a finite-density lattice-QCD paper to ground the density component in a feasible computational strategy rather than proposing direct real-µ simulations. The goal is to connect hadronic correlators to controlled expansions around µB=0.
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:2002.02821"}
```
[result]: The study determines the QCD crossover line and thermodynamic observables at nonzero baryon density using lattice-QCD expansions around µB=0. It demonstrates continuum-extrapolated Taylor-expansion methodology and quantifies curvature of the crossover line, but it does not compute detailed hadronic structure observables.

rationale: I extract the core genome of the retrieved literature to isolate mechanism, limitations, and the opening for a new computational study. This focuses the proposal on extending known finite-T/µ methods from bulk thermodynamics and screening masses to hadron-structure observables.
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["arXiv:1504.05274","arXiv:2002.02821"],"schema":"gene_genome"}
```
[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = spatial screening correlators plus Taylor expansion in µB/T; niche = QCD near the crossover at finite temperature and small baryon density; observation = chiral/parity restoration visible in screening spectra and crossover shifts with µB; limitation = few direct calculations of hadronic structure matrix elements or form-factor-like responses at finite T, µB; delta = combine screening correlators with external-current insertions and µB derivatives.

rationale: I check novelty against the retrieved works: the proposed study should not merely recompute screening masses or bulk crossover curvature. The novelty target is a correlator-level map of in-medium hadron structure responses with controlled finite-density derivatives.
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"Compute finite-temperature and small-finite-density modifications of pion and nucleon structure using lattice-QCD spatial screening correlators with vector/axial/tensor current insertions, extracting screening-state matrix elements and Taylor coefficients in µB/T.","against":["arXiv:1504.05274","arXiv:2002.02821"]}
```
[result]: Novelty appears moderate-to-high. Prior work established screening masses and finite-µB thermodynamics, but a systematic calculation of current-insertion screening matrix elements and their µB Taylor coefficients for pion/nucleon structure channels is not covered by these anchors.
```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"idea_plan","content":{"Idea":"Develop a lattice-QCD computational study of in-medium hadron structure by computing finite-temperature and small-finite-density modifications of pion and nucleon screening-state matrix elements. The study will extend established finite-T screening-correlator methods and finite-µB Taylor-expansion methods to current-insertion observables, targeting vector, axial, and tensor structure channels near the QCD crossover.","ImplementationSteps":{"1_ensembles":"Use existing 2+1 flavor lattice-QCD ensembles at multiple temperatures below, near, and above the crossover temperature Tc, with several lattice spacings for continuum control. Work initially at µB=0 and add finite-density dependence through Taylor coefficients in µB/T or imaginary-µB validation runs.","2_correlators":"Compute spatial two-point screening correlators for pion, rho, nucleon, and parity-partner baryon channels to reproduce and validate known screening-mass behavior such as chiral/parity restoration patterns reported in arXiv:1504.05274.","3_current_insertions":"Compute three-point spatial correlators with local and conserved vector currents, axial currents, and tensor currents inserted between hadronic screening operators. Extract screening-state matrix elements analogous to finite-T form-factor or charge-response observables.","4_density_expansion":"Evaluate derivatives of the two- and three-point correlators with respect to µB/T at µB=0 using Taylor-expansion operators, following the finite-density methodology used for crossover studies such as arXiv:2002.02821. Optionally cross-check selected observables using imaginary chemical potential.","5_analysis":"Fit two- and three-point screening correlators using correlated Bayesian or multi-state fits, propagate renormalization constants for currents, and perform continuum and temperature-interpolation analyses. Compare the temperature and density dependence of structure observables with screening masses and chiral-restoration indicators."},"ImplementationOrder":["Generate or obtain finite-T 2+1 flavor ensembles and validate scale setting","Reproduce known hadron screening masses at µB=0","Compute vector, axial, and tensor current-insertion spatial correlators","Extract finite-T screening-state matrix elements","Compute µB/T Taylor coefficients for two- and three-point functions","Perform continuum, finite-volume, and excited-state systematic studies","Map structure observables across T and small µB and compare to chiral/parity restoration"],"Dataset":"2+1 flavor lattice-QCD gauge ensembles at temperatures spanning approximately 0.8Tc to 1.5Tc, with multiple lattice spacings and volumes. Observables include meson and baryon spatial two-point screening correlators, three-point correlators with vector/axial/tensor current insertions, and µB derivatives of these correlators at µB=0. Literature anchors: arXiv:1504.05274 for hadron screening masses near the chiral crossover and arXiv:2002.02821 for Taylor-expanded finite-density lattice-QCD methodology.","EvaluationMetrics":{"screening_validation":"Agreement of extracted pion, rho, nucleon, and parity-partner screening masses with established finite-T screening-mass trends, including parity doubling above Tc.","fit_quality":"Correlated chi-squared per degree of freedom, posterior stability under fit-window and state-count variation, and bootstrap/jackknife uncertainty consistency.","structure_observables":"Temperature dependence of vector charge response, axial screening matrix element, tensor response, and ratios to their low-temperature values.","density_response":"First and second Taylor coefficients in µB/T for screening masses and current-insertion matrix elements, with uncertainty estimates and imaginary-µB cross-checks where available.","systematics":"Continuum extrapolation stability, finite-volume dependence, current-renormalization uncertainty, excited-state contamination, and sensitivity to strange/light quark masses.","physics_benchmarks":"Correlation of structure modifications with Tc, chiral restoration, parity doubling, and known crossover curvature at finite µB."},"ExpectedOutcome":"The study should produce the first systematic map of finite-temperature and small-finite-density changes in pion and nucleon structure-like lattice observables using screening-state matrix elements. Expected findings include visible suppression or restructuring of axial/tensor responses near Tc, approximate restoration patterns linked to parity doubling, and quantified leading µB dependence of hadronic structure observables in the regime where Taylor expansion is controlled. This would bridge the gap between finite-T screening spectroscopy and finite-density thermodynamics by adding current-sensitive hadron-structure information."}}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Use an idea_plan to develop a computational study of lattice QCD predictions for hadron structure at finite temperature and density.

content:
Rationale: I first search for experimental/theoretical anchors spanning single-particle Anderson localization, transport suppression, and disorder-driven phases in ultracold gases. The goal is to ground the synthesis in well-cited controlled-disorder platforms: optical speckle, quasiperiodic lattices, and disordered Hubbard systems.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"controlled disorder ultracold atomic gases Anderson localization transport Bose glass phase transition speckle quasiperiodic lattice review experiment","discipline":"physics","limit":6}
```
[result]: Candidates found: arXiv:0804.1621 “Direct observation of Anderson localization of matter waves in a controlled disorder” (Billy et al.); arXiv:0804.2609 “Anderson localization of a non-interacting Bose-Einstein condensate” (Roati et al.); arXiv:1004.4666 “Disordered quantum gases under control” (Sanchez-Palencia & Lewenstein); arXiv:1501.05661 “Observation of many-body localization of interacting fermions in a quasirandom optical lattice” (Schreiber et al.); arXiv:1408.1411 “Observation of a disordered bosonic insulator from weak to strong interactions” (D’Errico et al.); arXiv:1509.00020 “Measurement of the mobility edge for 3D Anderson localization” (Semeghini et al.).

Rationale: I read the broad review to extract the organizing mechanisms: how disorder is engineered, which control knobs matter, and what phases/transitions are expected beyond clean-gas behavior. This gives a framework linking localization and transport to phase diagrams.
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:1004.4666","sections":["abstract","introduction","disorder engineering","Anderson localization","interacting bosons and Bose glass"]}
```
[result]: The review emphasizes that ultracold gases allow tunable disorder strength, correlation length, dimensionality, and interactions. It identifies Anderson localization from interference in random/quasiperiodic potentials, interaction-driven delocalization or glassiness, suppressed diffusion, and disorder-induced Bose-glass regimes between superfluid and Mott/insulating phases.

Rationale: I read paired experimental anchors: one for direct matter-wave localization in controlled optical disorder, and one for many-body transport arrest in a quasirandom lattice. Together they span noninteracting/single-particle localization and interaction-sensitive dynamical phase-transition phenomenology.
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":["arXiv:0804.1621","arXiv:1501.05661"],"sections":["abstract","methods","main results","discussion"]}
```
[result]: Billy et al. observed exponential tails and halted expansion of a 1D Bose gas released into a laser speckle potential, with localization controlled by disorder amplitude and energy filtering. Schreiber et al. used an interacting Fermi-Hubbard chain with quasiperiodic disorder and measured long-lived charge-density imbalance, showing a crossover/transition from thermalizing transport to many-body localized dynamics as disorder increased.

Rationale: I extract a structured genome from the retrieved papers, separating mechanism, platform niche, empirical signatures, and caveats. This makes the final claim evidence-grounded rather than a generic review statement.
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["arXiv:1004.4666","arXiv:0804.1621","arXiv:1501.05661"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```
[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = tunable disorder reshapes eigenstates and scattering, producing Anderson localization, suppressed diffusion, and interaction-dependent MBL/Bose-glass behavior; niche = optical speckle/quasiperiodic lattices in low-dimensional ultracold gases with controllable interactions; observation = exponential localized density profiles, nonexpanding clouds, persistent imbalance, and disorder-dependent insulating response; limitation = finite size/time, harmonic confinement, interaction/heating effects, and model dependence of quasiperiodic versus truly random disorder; delta = move from qualitative localization sightings to controlled mapping of dynamical/thermodynamic transition boundaries.

Rationale: I check novelty relative to the retrieved literature by asking what remains underconstrained: simultaneous control of disorder statistics, interactions, and transport probes across localization-to-phase-transition regimes. The useful synthesis is therefore not “disorder localizes atoms,” but a roadmap for measuring crossovers among Anderson, glassy, and many-body localized regimes.
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claim":"Controlled disorder in ultracold gases should be used as a multidimensional tuning parameter—strength, correlation length, quasiperiodicity/randomness, dimensionality, and interaction—to connect microscopic localization signatures with macroscopic transport arrest and disorder-driven phase transitions.","against":["arXiv:1004.4666","arXiv:0804.1621","arXiv:1501.05661","arXiv:1408.1411","arXiv:1509.00020"]}
```
[result]: Novelty is moderate as a synthesis: the components are established, but combining disorder statistics, interaction tuning, and transport/phase-boundary observables into one experimental design remains a current organizing challenge. Literature supports the claim while leaving open quantitative universality, finite-size scaling, and distinctions between quasiperiodic MBL, speckle Anderson localization, and Bose-glass criticality.

```propose
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Controlled optical disorder modifies the single-particle spectrum and many-body dynamics by tuning scattering, interference, and interaction-assisted dephasing. In weakly interacting or noninteracting gases, sufficiently strong speckle or quasiperiodic disorder produces Anderson localization: expansion halts and density profiles acquire exponential localized tails, as in arXiv:0804.1621 and arXiv:0804.2609. With interactions, the same control knobs can either delocalize atoms through collisions or stabilize nonergodic/insulating behavior, including many-body localization-like persistent imbalance in quasiperiodic Fermi-Hubbard chains as in arXiv:1501.05661 and Bose-glass/disordered-insulator regimes discussed in arXiv:1004.4666 and observed in arXiv:1408.1411.",
  "niche_genome": "The key niche is ultracold atoms in engineered optical disorder, especially 1D/3D speckle potentials, quasiperiodic optical lattices, and disordered Hubbard systems. These platforms uniquely permit independent tuning of disorder amplitude, disorder correlation length or wavelength ratio, dimensionality, kinetic energy/tunneling, interactions via Feshbach or lattice control, and initial nonequilibrium states for transport measurements.",
  "observation_genome": "Core signatures include suppressed expansion, exponential density tails, reduced diffusion constants, persistent charge- or spin-density imbalance, loss of thermalization, and disorder-dependent insulating response. At larger scales, controlled disorder changes phase behavior by shifting or inserting insulating regimes between superfluid, metallic/thermalizing, and Mott-like phases; in 3D, mobility-edge measurements such as arXiv:1509.00020 connect localization to energy-resolved transport thresholds.",
  "limitation_genome": "Current interpretations are limited by finite observation time, finite system size, trapping inhomogeneity, heating, imperfect isolation, and the fact that quasiperiodic disorder and true random speckle do not always share the same universality. Interaction effects also complicate the separation between Anderson localization, glassy transport slowdown, many-body localization, and conventional insulating behavior.",
  "delta_genome": "A strong next step is a unified experiment that scans disorder strength, disorder correlation/statistics, and interaction strength while measuring both microscopic localization observables and macroscopic transport/phase observables. The main delta over isolated demonstrations is to map continuous trajectories from Anderson-localized single-particle behavior through interaction-modified transport to Bose-glass or MBL-like phase boundaries using the same atomic platform and calibration.",
  "claim_genome": "Controlled disorder is not merely a perturbation in ultracold gases; it is a programmable axis for engineering localization, transport arrest, and disorder-driven phase transitions. The best-supported conclusion from the retrieved literature is that disorder strength and structure determine whether atoms expand diffusively, localize through interference, or enter interaction-dependent insulating/nonergodic regimes, while the unresolved frontier is quantitative universality across random versus quasiperiodic disorder and across single-particle, many-body, and thermodynamic transition limits."
}
``` JSImport
```

prompt.full_prompt: Investigate how controlled disorder affects localization, transport, and phase transitions in ultracold atomic gases.

content:
I’ll first identify prior modified-gravity work that touches both galactic dynamics and expansion cosmology, so the research question is not merely generic but targeted at an unresolved joint-test gap.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"modified gravity galaxy rotation curves cosmological expansion MOND f(R) TeVeS SPARC Pantheon"}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers include Lelli et al. “SPARC: Mass Models for 175 Disk Galaxies…” arXiv:1606.09251; McGaugh et al. “Radial Acceleration Relation…” arXiv:1609.05917; Bekenstein “Relativistic gravitation theory for the MOND paradigm” arXiv:astro-ph/0403694; Skordis & Zlosnik “New Relativistic Theory for Modified Newtonian Dynamics” arXiv:2007.00082; Saffari & Rahvar “f(R) gravity: From the Pioneer anomaly to the cosmic acceleration” arXiv:0708.1482; and Brout et al. “The Pantheon+ Analysis” arXiv:2202.04077.  

SPARC/RAR define the empirical galaxy-side target: any proposed gravity law must reproduce detailed rotation-curve phenomenology, not just asymptotic flatness.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:1606.09251"}
```  
[result]: Lelli et al. present SPARC, 175 disk galaxies with high-quality rotation curves and 3.6 μm photometry, enabling baryonic mass modeling. This dataset is widely used to test MOND-like laws and halo models across surface brightness and morphology.  

To connect galactic modified dynamics with a relativistic/cosmological framework, I’ll read a modern MOND-motivated theory rather than only phenomenological rotation-curve fits.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"arXiv:2007.00082"}
```  
[result]: Skordis & Zlosnik construct a relativistic MOND theory designed to recover MOND behavior in galaxies while being compatible with relativistic cosmology. The work motivates testing whether a single covariant model can satisfy both weak-field galactic constraints and homogeneous-background cosmological observations.  

Now I’ll check whether the proposed angle—joint inference on rotation curves and expansion history with shared modified-gravity parameters—goes beyond existing single-domain or loosely connected tests.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","paper_ids":["arXiv:1606.09251","arXiv:1609.05917","arXiv:2007.00082","arXiv:0708.1482","arXiv:2202.04077"],"claim":"A single modified-gravity model should be fit jointly to SPARC galaxy rotation curves and late-time expansion data such as Pantheon+/BAO/H(z), with common parameters linking the galactic acceleration scale to the FLRW background, to test whether dark matter and dark energy can both be avoided."}
```  
[result]: Existing work separately establishes precise rotation-curve benchmarks, relativistic MOND-like frameworks, f(R)-style cosmic-acceleration models, and modern supernova expansion constraints. A direct hierarchical joint fit enforcing shared parameters between galaxy rotation curves and expansion-history data appears underdeveloped and is a clear research-question niche.  

```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Test a covariant modified-gravity model whose weak-field limit changes the radial acceleration law in galaxies while its homogeneous FLRW limit modifies the Friedmann equation. The same small set of theory parameters—especially any MOND-like acceleration scale or scalar/vector-field coupling—would be shared across both regimes rather than refit separately.","niche_genome":"Jointly confront modified gravity with SPARC rotation curves and late-time expansion probes such as Pantheon+, BAO, and cosmic-chronometer H(z) data. The niche is the cross-scale consistency test: can one gravity law explain both galactic mass discrepancies and cosmic acceleration without invoking cold dark matter plus a cosmological constant?","observation_genome":"SPARC provides detailed baryonic mass models and rotation curves for 175 disk galaxies, while Pantheon+ and related BAO/H(z) measurements constrain the expansion history. Prior work such as relativistic MOND and f(R)-inspired models suggests possible mechanisms, but galaxy-scale success and cosmological-background success are often assessed separately.","limitation_genome":"A model that fits rotation curves may fail the expansion history, and a model tuned for cosmic acceleration may not reproduce the radial-acceleration relation or detailed galaxy-to-galaxy rotation curves. Additional risks include stellar mass-to-light degeneracies, external-field effects, covariance among cosmological datasets, and constraints from CMB/lensing/growth that are not captured by background expansion alone.","delta_genome":"Instead of asking whether modified gravity can fit galaxies or cosmology in isolation, ask whether a single parameter-shared theory can pass a simultaneous likelihood analysis across SPARC and expansion data. The key delta is enforcing cross-regime parameter consistency, for example testing whether the galaxy acceleration scale is statistically tied to H0 or another cosmological scale.","claim_genome":"Research question: Can a single covariant modified-gravity model, with parameters shared between its weak-field galactic limit and its FLRW cosmological limit, simultaneously fit SPARC galaxy rotation curves and Pantheon+/BAO/H(z) expansion data well enough to remove the need for both particle dark matter and a cosmological constant?"}}
``` 
[result]: Proposal generated in the requested gene_genome schema. 
```

prompt.full_prompt: Formulate a research question about whether modified gravity models can simultaneously explain galaxy rotation curves and cosmological expansion data.

content:
Rationale: I first identify anchor papers spanning fluctuation electrodynamics, hyperbolic metamaterial heat transfer, and quantum-vacuum/Casimir constraints so the research genome is grounded in known mechanisms and limits.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "metamaterials vacuum fluctuations near-field radiative heat transfer hyperbolic metamaterials Casimir Lifshitz",
  "discipline": "physics",
  "max_results": 6
}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers included Joulain et al. 2005 (`doi:10.1016/j.surfrep.2004.12.002`) on thermally excited surface waves, Rosa/Dalvit/Milonni 2008 (`doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.100.183602`) on Casimir-Lifshitz theory for metamaterials, Biehs/Tschikin/Ben-Abdallah 2012 (`doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.109.104301`) on hyperbolic metamaterials as near-field blackbody analogs, and Miller et al. 2015 (`doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.115.204302`) on limits to near-field heat transfer.

Rationale: The Biehs et al. PRL is the most directly relevant mechanism paper: it links metamaterial dispersion engineering to enhanced radiative transfer from vacuum/thermal fluctuations through high-k modes.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.109.104301"
}
```  
[result]: Biehs et al. show that hyperbolic metamaterials can support a broadband continuum of high-wave-vector modes, producing near-field radiative heat flux that can exceed conventional blackbody exchange. The enhancement depends on anisotropic effective permittivity and evanescent-mode tunneling, but practical performance is constrained by material loss, finite unit-cell size, and breakdown of the effective-medium approximation.

Rationale: I read the metamaterial Casimir paper to incorporate the “vacuum fluctuations” side, especially what passive metamaterials can and cannot do under causality and dispersion.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.100.183602"
}
```  
[result]: Rosa, Dalvit, and Milonni analyze Casimir-Lifshitz forces involving metamaterials and emphasize that realistic dispersion and dissipation strongly limit idealized negative-index or magnetic-response proposals. The paper supports treating vacuum-fluctuation manipulation as a broadband, causal-material-design problem rather than relying on narrowband effective negative refraction.

Rationale: I extract a compact genome from the retrieved literature to separate established mechanisms from open deltas.  
```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "doi:10.1016/j.surfrep.2004.12.002",
    "doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.109.104301",
    "doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.100.183602",
    "doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.115.204302"
  ],
  "schema": "gene_genome"
}
```  
[result]: Extracted genes: mechanism = fluctuation-electrodynamic control of LDOS, surface-polariton tunneling, and hyperbolic high-k channels; niche = nanoscale gaps, mid-IR thermal wavelengths, anisotropic multilayers/metasurfaces; observation = orders-of-magnitude near-field heat-transfer enhancement over far-field blackbody limits; limitation = loss, nonlocality, finite patterning scale, passivity/causality bounds, and effective-medium breakdown.

Rationale: Before proposing, I check whether the promising delta—co-designing heat transfer and vacuum-force landscapes in tunable nonlocal metamaterials—is distinct from simply enhancing heat flux with passive hyperbolic media.  
```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "idea": "Inverse-design tunable, nonlocal hyperbolic/ENZ metastructures that jointly shape near-field radiative heat transfer and Casimir-Lifshitz pressure by controlling the same vacuum-fluctuation LDOS spectrum.",
  "against_paper_ids": [
    "doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.109.104301",
    "doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.100.183602",
    "doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.115.204302"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: The idea is adjacent to existing work but not redundant: prior papers establish enhancement mechanisms, metamaterial Casimir constraints, and heat-transfer bounds, while the proposed delta is a joint inverse-design/control program that treats heat flux and vacuum stress as coupled objectives under realistic nonlocal and causal constraints.

```propose
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Use fluctuation electrodynamics to engineer the electromagnetic local density of states and evanescent tunneling spectrum with anisotropic hyperbolic, ENZ, and phase-change/graphene metastructures. The same vacuum and thermal fluctuation modes that mediate near-field radiative heat transfer also contribute to Casimir-Lifshitz stresses, so the program co-designs high-k mode density, impedance matching, loss, and spectral tunability rather than optimizing heat flux alone.",
  "niche_genome": "Nanoscale and mesoscale gaps from roughly 10 nm to 1 µm, especially mid-infrared thermal wavelengths where phonon-polariton, plasmon-polariton, hyperbolic, and ENZ resonances are accessible. Target platforms include multilayer hyperbolic metamaterials, patterned metasurfaces, van der Waals polaritonic slabs, graphene-tunable films, and phase-change materials for actively switchable radiative heat transfer and vacuum-force control.",
  "observation_genome": "Prior work shows that surface electromagnetic waves and evanescent tunneling can produce radiative heat transfer far above the far-field blackbody limit, and that hyperbolic metamaterials can broaden this enhancement by supporting large-k channels. Casimir-Lifshitz studies of metamaterials show that idealized negative-index intuition is insufficient: realistic dispersion, dissipation, and causality dominate vacuum-fluctuation forces.",
  "limitation_genome": "Passive effective-medium designs are limited by absorption, finite unit-cell cutoff of high-k modes, nonlocal response, roughness, thermal stability, and stringent gap control. Fundamental bounds on near-field transfer also constrain achievable enhancement through material susceptibility and loss. Casimir-pressure changes may be small, parasitic, or mechanically destabilizing unless included explicitly in the design objective.",
  "delta_genome": "Develop a causal, nonlocal inverse-design framework that jointly optimizes spectral heat flux, Casimir-Lifshitz pressure, and mechanical stability in tunable metamaterials. The key delta is to treat vacuum-fluctuation manipulation and radiative heat-transfer engineering as one coupled control problem, using active material tuning to switch between high-conductance, insulated, and force-balanced states.",
  "claim_genome": "A research program centered on tunable nonlocal metamaterials can move the field from static near-field heat-transfer enhancement toward programmable control of vacuum-fluctuation spectra, enabling thermal switches/rectifiers and mechanically stable nanoscale heat engines whose radiative conductance and Casimir forces are co-designed under realistic dispersion, loss, and causality constraints."
}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Generate a gene_genome output for a research program on using metamaterials to manipulate vacuum fluctuations and radiative heat transfer.

content:
I’ll first anchor the prompt in canonical work connecting entanglement to emergent geometry, then identify a concrete gap that can be turned into a research prompt.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"emergence of spacetime geometry from entanglement patterns quantum gravity AdS/CFT tensor networks Einstein equations","discipline":"physics","limit":5}
```  
[result]: Found core candidates: Ryu & Takayanagi `hep-th/0603001` on holographic entanglement entropy; Van Raamsdonk `1005.3035` on spacetime connectivity from entanglement; Swingle `0905.1317` on MERA/AdS geometry; Faulkner et al. `1312.7856` on deriving Einstein equations from entanglement; Pastawski et al. `1503.06237` on holographic quantum error-correcting codes.

The two most useful anchors are Van Raamsdonk for the conceptual mechanism and Faulkner et al. for turning entanglement constraints into gravitational dynamics.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["1005.3035","1312.7856"]}
```  
[result]: `1005.3035` argues that reducing entanglement between CFT subsystems geometrically disconnects the dual spacetime, suggesting entanglement is a glue for classical geometry. `1312.7856` shows that the entanglement first law plus holographic entropy can imply linearized Einstein equations around AdS, linking entanglement equilibrium to gravitational dynamics.

I’ll extract the reusable “genome” of the existing research program: mechanism, niche, observations, and limitations.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["hep-th/0603001","1005.3035","0905.1317","1312.7856","1503.06237"],"schema":["mechanism_genome","niche_genome","observation_genome","limitation_genome","delta_genome","claim_genome"]}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: geometry is inferred from entanglement entropy, mutual information, and error-correcting tensor-network structure; strongest evidence comes from AdS/CFT, RT/HRT surfaces, MERA-like discretizations, and entanglement-first-law derivations. Main limitations are dependence on asymptotic AdS, large-N semiclassical states, static or perturbative regimes, and incomplete treatment of dynamical locality emergence.

To avoid merely restating “spacetime from entanglement,” I’ll check novelty around dynamical reconstruction from full entanglement patterns rather than single-region entropies.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"Infer emergent classical spacetime geometry from time-dependent multipartite entanglement patterns in quantum gravity models, testing when locality, curvature, and Einstein-like dynamics arise from mutual information and conditional mutual information constraints.","against_paper_ids":["hep-th/0603001","1005.3035","0905.1317","1312.7856","1503.06237"]}
```  
[result]: Partly novel. Prior work establishes entropy-area relations, tensor-network geometry, and perturbative Einstein dynamics; a sharper open direction is to study dynamical, multipartite entanglement data as an inverse problem for emergent metric, locality, and curvature beyond static AdS examples.

```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","content":{"mechanism_genome":"Treat classical spacetime geometry as an effective reconstruction from quantum entanglement structure: use bipartite and multipartite entanglement measures—entanglement entropy, mutual information, conditional mutual information, and entanglement-wedge connectivity—to infer adjacency, distance, curvature, and causal propagation in an emergent bulk graph or tensor-network geometry.","niche_genome":"Quantum gravity, especially AdS/CFT-inspired models, holographic tensor networks, spin-network or group-field-theory-like graph states, and semiclassical regimes where one can compare entanglement-derived geometry with known bulk metrics or Einstein-like dynamics.","observation_genome":"Ryu-Takayanagi entropy-area relations (`hep-th/0603001`) connect boundary entanglement to bulk surfaces; Van Raamsdonk (`1005.3035`) argues that reducing entanglement disconnects spacetime; Swingle (`0905.1317`) relates MERA entanglement structure to hyperbolic geometry; Faulkner et al. (`1312.7856`) derive linearized Einstein equations from entanglement first-law reasoning; holographic codes (`1503.06237`) show how locality and bulk reconstruction can arise from entanglement/error-correction structure.","limitation_genome":"Existing results are strongest for AdS/CFT, large-N semiclassical states, static or perturbative backgrounds, and entropy-area data for selected regions. It remains unclear how generic time-dependent multipartite entanglement patterns produce robust locality, smooth curvature, causal cones, and Einstein-like dynamics outside highly symmetric holographic settings.","delta_genome":"Formulate the problem as a dynamical inverse-geometry task: given evolving multipartite entanglement data from a quantum gravity model, reconstruct the minimal emergent metric or graph geometry and test when it becomes classical by checking locality, curvature smoothness, causal propagation, and approximate gravitational equations.","claim_genome":"Research prompt: How and under what conditions do time-dependent multipartite entanglement patterns in quantum gravity models give rise to an effective classical spacetime geometry? Develop a reconstruction framework that maps entanglement data to emergent distance, adjacency, curvature, and causal structure, then test whether the reconstructed geometry exhibits semiclassical locality and Einstein-like dynamics beyond static AdS/CFT examples."}}
```  
[result]: Proposed a physics research prompt grounded in holographic entropy, entanglement-as-connectivity, tensor-network geometry, and entanglement-first-law derivations, with the open contribution focused on dynamical multipartite reconstruction of classical spacetime.ablytyped 

```gene_genome
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Treat classical spacetime geometry as an effective reconstruction from quantum entanglement structure: use bipartite and multipartite entanglement measures—entanglement entropy, mutual information, conditional mutual information, and entanglement-wedge connectivity—to infer adjacency, distance, curvature, and causal propagation in an emergent bulk graph or tensor-network geometry.",
  "niche_genome": "Quantum gravity, especially AdS/CFT-inspired models, holographic tensor networks, spin-network or group-field-theory-like graph states, and semiclassical regimes where one can compare entanglement-derived geometry with known bulk metrics or Einstein-like dynamics.",
  "observation_genome": "Ryu-Takayanagi entropy-area relations connect boundary entanglement to bulk surfaces; Van Raamsdonk argues that reducing entanglement disconnects spacetime; Swingle relates MERA entanglement structure to hyperbolic geometry; Faulkner et al. derive linearized Einstein equations from entanglement first-law reasoning; holographic quantum error-correcting codes show how locality and bulk reconstruction can arise from entanglement structure.",
  "limitation_genome": "Existing results are strongest for AdS/CFT, large-N semiclassical states, static or perturbative backgrounds, and entropy-area data for selected regions. It remains unclear how generic time-dependent multipartite entanglement patterns produce robust locality, smooth curvature, causal cones, and Einstein-like dynamics outside highly symmetric holographic settings.",
  "delta_genome": "Formulate the problem as a dynamical inverse-geometry task: given evolving multipartite entanglement data from a quantum gravity model, reconstruct the minimal emergent metric or graph geometry and test when it becomes classical by checking locality, curvature smoothness, causal propagation, and approximate gravitational equations.",
  "claim_genome": "Research prompt: How and under what conditions do time-dependent multipartite entanglement patterns in quantum gravity models give rise to an effective classical spacetime geometry? Develop a reconstruction framework that maps entanglement data to emergent distance, adjacency, curvature, and causal structure, then test whether the reconstructed geometry exhibits semiclassical locality and Einstein-like dynamics beyond static AdS/CFT examples."
}

prompt.full_prompt: Create a research prompt on the emergence of classical spacetime geometry from entanglement patterns in quantum gravity models.

content:
Rationale: I first search for experimentally demonstrated recyclable thermoset/vitrimer platforms that balance high Tg, mechanical performance, and repeat reprocessing, so the plan can reuse proven dynamic chemistries rather than speculate from scratch.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"recyclable thermoset high glass transition temperature mechanical strength retention reprocessing cycles vitrimer epoxy dynamic covalent bonds","discipline":"materials","limit":5}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers include Montarnal et al., *Science* 2011, DOI: `10.1126/science.1212648` on transesterification vitrimers; Garcia et al., *Science* 2014, DOI: `10.1126/science.1251484` on recyclable poly(hexahydrotriazine) thermosets; and Christensen et al., *Nature Chemistry* 2019, DOI: `10.1038/s41557-019-0249-2` on diketoenamine closed-loop recyclable networks.

Rationale: The vitrimer paper is the key baseline because it shows solid-state network rearrangement and reprocessing without losing crosslink density, but its classical β-hydroxy ester chemistry often trades off creep resistance and high-temperature stiffness if not designed with rigid monomers.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1126/science.1212648"}
```  
[result]: Montarnal et al. introduced permanent covalent networks with associative transesterification exchange: the networks can flow/reprocess above a topology-freezing temperature while retaining insolubility and crosslinked architecture. The evidence supports using exchangeable ester/β-hydroxy ester motifs for reshaping and welding, but high Tg depends strongly on monomer rigidity and catalyst/exchange kinetics.

Rationale: I then read a high-strength recyclable thermoset example to identify rigid-network motifs that raise Tg and modulus, even if the recycling mechanism differs from vitrimer-style associative exchange.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_id":"10.1126/science.1251484"}
```  
[result]: Garcia et al. reported poly(hexahydrotriazine) networks from aromatic diamines and paraformaldehyde, giving strong, solvent-resistant thermosets that can be chemically recycled under acidic conditions. The main design lesson is that rigid aromatic amine-derived network segments can give high stiffness/Tg, but purely acid-depolymerizable systems do not directly guarantee repeated thermal reprocessing with shape/strength retention.

Rationale: To make the proposal novel, I check whether a combined rigid aromatic high-Tg epoxy vitrimer with protected catalyst localization and anti-creep reinforcement has already been reported as the exact solution.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","query":"rigid aromatic epoxy vitrimer high Tg recyclable thermoset multiple reprocessing cycles retained tensile strength nanofiller localized transesterification catalyst"}
```  
[result]: Related work exists on epoxy-acid vitrimers, dynamic imine/urethane networks, and recyclable aromatic thermosets, but the exact combination of a rigid high-Tg aromatic epoxy vitrimer, latent/localized transesterification catalyst, and reactive nanofiller reinforcement for strength retention over ≥5 cycles appears underexplored. Novelty is incremental but plausible if framed as a structure–kinetics optimization for high Tg plus low damage accumulation.  

Rationale: I now convert the retrieved mechanisms into a concrete experimental plan: use associative exchange for repeat processing, borrow rigid aromatic motifs for Tg/mechanics, and add reinforcement/catalyst control to limit strength decay.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"idea_plan","grounded_in":["10.1126/science.1212648","10.1126/science.1251484","10.1038/s41557-019-0249-2"]}
```  
[result]: Proposal generated below.  
```json
{
  "Idea": "Develop a high-Tg recyclable epoxy vitrimer based on rigid aromatic epoxy/anhydride or epoxy/diacid monomers containing exchangeable beta-hydroxy ester linkages, reinforced with low-load functionalized silica or graphene oxide, and using a latent/localized transesterification catalyst to enable hot-press reprocessing while suppressing creep and mechanical degradation during service. The concept combines Montarnal-style associative vitrimer exchange with Garcia-style rigid aromatic thermoset design: the network remains covalently crosslinked during use, but above the topology-freezing temperature it can relax stress, weld, and be remolded over multiple cycles.",
  "ImplementationSteps": {
    "monomer_selection": "Synthesize or formulate a rigid aromatic epoxy network using bisphenol-F epoxy, naphthalene/spirobifluorene epoxy, or triglycidyl p-aminophenol with aromatic diacid/anhydride curing agents to introduce abundant beta-hydroxy ester exchange sites while maximizing segmental rigidity for Tg > 160-200 °C.",
    "dynamic_network_design": "Tune ester concentration, crosslink density, and catalyst level so that stress-relaxation half-time is short at 180-220 °C but very slow below Tg. Compare zinc acetylacetonate, triazabicyclodecene salts, and thermally latent organic catalysts.",
    "reinforcement_strategy": "Add 0.5-3 wt% epoxy- or carboxyl-functionalized silica nanoparticles or graphene oxide so fillers covalently couple into the network. This should reduce crack growth and preserve tensile/flexural strength during grinding and hot-press reprocessing.",
    "processing_protocol": "Cure panels, grind or cut them into flakes, then hot-press at a temperature above topology-freezing temperature but below decomposition temperature. Repeat for at least five cycles using identical pressure/time conditions.",
    "control_materials": "Benchmark against a non-dynamic aromatic epoxy thermoset, an unfilled vitrimer, and a chemically recyclable but non-hot-reprocessable network to separate the effects of exchange chemistry, rigidity, and reinforcement.",
    "failure_analysis": "After each cycle, measure gel fraction, swelling ratio, FTIR ester integrity, catalyst leaching, filler dispersion, void content, and fracture surfaces to determine whether strength loss arises from network degradation, porosity, oxidation, or filler debonding."
  },
  "ImplementationOrder": [
    "Formulate rigid aromatic epoxy/anhydride vitrimer library with varied ester density and catalyst loading.",
    "Screen Tg, storage modulus, gel fraction, and stress-relaxation kinetics to identify candidates with Tg > 160 °C and practical reprocessing temperature below degradation onset.",
    "Introduce functionalized nanofillers into the best two network formulations and optimize dispersion/covalent coupling.",
    "Perform five grind-and-hot-press reprocessing cycles under controlled atmosphere and pressure.",
    "Quantify mechanical retention, thermal stability, network integrity, and microstructural damage after each cycle.",
    "Select the composition that maximizes Tg, strength retention, and reprocessing speed; then scale to larger molded coupons."
  ],
  "Dataset": "Experimental dataset of 30-60 formulations varying aromatic epoxy identity, curing agent, ester density, catalyst type/loading, filler type/loading, and reprocessing conditions. For every formulation and cycle, collect DSC Tg, DMA storage modulus and tan delta, TGA onset temperature, tensile strength/modulus/elongation, flexural strength, fracture toughness, stress-relaxation curves, gel fraction, swelling ratio, FTIR/Raman spectra, SEM fracture images, and void/filler dispersion metrics.",
  "EvaluationMetrics": {
    "thermal_performance": "Tg by DSC/DMA >= 160 °C, preferably >= 180 °C; 5% TGA mass-loss temperature at least 50 °C above reprocessing temperature.",
    "reprocessability": "Stress-relaxation time to 1/e modulus <= 10 min at hot-press temperature; successful remolding/welding after >= 5 cycles with gel fraction >= 90%.",
    "mechanical_retention": "Tensile and flexural strength retention >= 85-90% after five reprocessing cycles; modulus retention >= 90%; fracture toughness retention >= 80%.",
    "dimensional_stability": "Low creep below Tg, quantified by <1% strain over 24 h under selected service load and temperature.",
    "damage_control": "Void fraction <2%, no large filler agglomerates, and no significant growth of carbonyl/oxidation degradation peaks after repeated processing.",
    "circularity": "Mass recovery after each reprocessing cycle >= 95%, with optional chemical digestion route for end-of-life monomer or oligomer recovery if ester exchange alone is insufficient."
  },
  "ExpectedOutcome": "The expected material is a recyclable high-performance thermoset with Tg around 170-210 °C, high modulus and strength comparable to structural aromatic epoxies, and less than 10-15% tensile/flexural strength loss after five hot-press reprocessing cycles. The key hypothesis is that associative transesterification preserves crosslink density during reshaping, while rigid aromatic segments and covalently coupled nanofillers prevent the usual losses in Tg, creep resistance, and fracture strength."
