content:
I’ll ground the plan in optomechanical experiments that already demonstrate quantum control/correlations, plus theory proposals for collapse/decoherence tests. The goal is to identify a boundary-test that goes beyond “ground-state cooling” toward tunable mass, separation, temperature, and coherence-time scaling.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"optomechanical resonator quantum classical boundary ground state cooling Bell test collapse models macroscopic superposition","discipline":"physics","max_results":6}
```  
[result]: Found candidates including Teufel et al. “Sideband cooling of micromechanical motion to the quantum ground state” (Nature 2011, paper_id: doi:10.1038/nature10261), Chan et al. “Laser cooling of a nanomechanical oscillator into its quantum ground state” (Nature 2011, paper_id: doi:10.1038/nature10461), Marinković et al. “Optomechanical Bell Test” (PRL 2018, paper_id: doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.121.220404), and Bahrami et al. “Noninterferometric test of collapse models in optomechanical systems” (PRL 2014, paper_id: doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.112.210404).
The first read checks the experimental baseline: what levels of cooling and coherent measurement have actually been achieved in mechanical resonators. These set realistic starting points for any boundary experiment.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["doi:10.1038/nature10261","doi:10.1038/nature10461"],"fields":["system","method","key_results","limitations"]}
```  
[result]: Teufel et al. used a microwave electromechanical circuit to sideband-cool a micromechanical oscillator to near its motional ground state with mean phonon occupancy below one. Chan et al. achieved optical sideband cooling of a nanobeam optomechanical crystal to ground-state occupation. Both establish quantum-compatible preparation, but neither directly creates large spatial superpositions or tests deviations from standard decoherence.
The second read targets the actual boundary question: whether mechanics can show nonclassical correlations and whether excess diffusion/collapse mechanisms can be constrained without full spatial interferometry.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.121.220404","doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.112.210404"],"fields":["system","method","key_results","limitations"]}
```  
[result]: Marinković et al. proposed/implemented an optomechanical Bell-test architecture using photon-phonon pair creation/readout to reveal nonclassical light-mechanics correlations, but loopholes and small effective masses limit macroscopicity. Bahrami et al. proposed using unexplained mechanical heating/diffusion as a noninterferometric test of collapse models, with sensitivity improving for massive, cold, low-noise resonators.
A useful experiment should combine both directions—Bell/nonclassicality witness and collapse/decoherence scaling—rather than repeating ground-state cooling alone. I’ll check novelty around a protocol that scans mass, superposition separation, and environmental decoherence in the same device family.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","query":"single optomechanical resonator platform combining heralded phonon nonclassicality Bell witness with mass-dependent collapse-model heating scan temperature pressure optical power","against_paper_ids":["doi:10.1038/nature10261","doi:10.1038/nature10461","doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.121.220404","doi:10.1103/PhysRevLett.112.210404"]}
```  
[result]: Closest prior work covers individual components: ground-state cooling, photon-phonon nonclassical correlation tests, and noninterferometric collapse/heating bounds. A systematic “boundary map” that measures nonclassical witness degradation and anomalous force-noise scaling across resonator mass/temperature/separation within matched optomechanical devices appears distinct.
```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "idea_plan",
  "content": {
    "Idea": "Build a matched family of cryogenic optomechanical resonators to experimentally map the quantum-to-classical boundary by jointly measuring: (1) heralded nonclassical phonon generation/readout via photon-phonon correlations, and (2) anomalous mechanical diffusion/heating as a function of resonator mass, temperature, pressure, optical power, and effective displacement scale. The experiment extends ground-state optomechanics from Teufel et al. and Chan et al., uses Bell/nonclassical-correlation methods related to Marinković et al., and adds collapse/decoherence sensitivity motivated by Bahrami et al.",
    "ImplementationSteps": {
      "1_platform_fabrication": "Fabricate a chip-scale series of optomechanical crystal or microwave-optomechanical resonators with similar optical/mechanical coupling but systematically varied effective mass, e.g. ~pg, ~10 pg, ~100 pg, and ~ng classes. Keep geometry and materials as comparable as possible to isolate mass scaling.",
      "2_quantum_preparation": "Cool each mechanical mode close to its ground state using resolved-sideband cooling in a dilution refrigerator or cryogenic optical setup. Calibrate mean phonon occupancy using sideband asymmetry and thermometry.",
      "3_heralded_nonclassical_test": "Apply weak blue-detuned write pulses to probabilistically create correlated photon-phonon pairs, detect the emitted photon as a herald, then use red-detuned read pulses to convert the phonon back to light. Measure cross-correlations g2, conditional autocorrelation, and, where possible, a Bell/CHSH-type or entanglement-witness statistic.",
      "4_boundary_parameter_scan": "Repeat the nonclassicality measurement while scanning controlled classicalization channels: bath temperature, residual gas pressure, optical absorption/heating, mechanical quality factor, delay time between write and read, and effective displacement/separation generated by pulsed optomechanical interactions.",
      "5_diffusion_collapse_channel": "With the same devices, perform long-duration measurements of mechanical force noise and heating rates after subtracting calibrated thermal, laser, readout, and gas-collision noise. Fit residual diffusion versus mass and geometry to constrain collapse-model-like or otherwise anomalous decoherence mechanisms.",
      "6_model_comparison": "Compare observed degradation of nonclassical witnesses with predictions from standard environmental decoherence, technical noise, and phenomenological collapse/diffusion models. Identify whether loss of nonclassicality follows expected thermal decoherence or shows unexplained mass-dependent scaling."
    },
    "ImplementationOrder": [
      "Fabricate matched resonator family with controlled mass scaling",
      "Cryogenic ground-state cooling and calibration",
      "Heralded photon-phonon nonclassicality measurements",
      "Controlled scans over temperature, pressure, delay, and optical power",
      "Independent anomalous heating/diffusion measurements",
      "Bayesian model comparison between environmental decoherence and excess-collapse-like diffusion"
    ],
    "Dataset": "Time-tagged single-photon detection records from write/read pulses; mechanical sideband spectra; calibrated phonon occupancy versus time; conditional correlation functions; Bell/nonclassicality witness values; mechanical linewidth and quality factor; environmental logs including temperature, pressure, optical pulse energy, cavity detuning, absorption estimate, and device mass/geometry; long-duration force-noise and heating-rate traces for each resonator.",
    "EvaluationMetrics": {
      "quantum_preparation": "Mean phonon occupancy n_m < 1 before heralded trials, verified by sideband asymmetry.",
      "nonclassicality": "Violation of a classical Cauchy-Schwarz bound, sub-Poissonian conditional phonon statistics, or positive Bell/entanglement-witness violation where detection efficiency permits.",
      "coherence_boundary": "Decay time of nonclassical witness versus resonator mass, temperature, pressure, and write-read delay.",
      "decoherence_model_fit": "Goodness of fit and Bayes factor comparing standard environmental decoherence to models with additional mass-dependent diffusion.",
      "collapse_constraint": "Upper bound on anomalous force noise/heating rate and corresponding exclusion region for phenomenological collapse parameters.",
      "systematics_control": "Residual heating must not correlate primarily with optical pulse energy, detector artifacts, or known thermal/gas damping channels."
    },
    "ExpectedOutcome": "The most likely outcome is a quantitative boundary map showing that nonclassical optomechanical correlations persist only within a calculable window set by thermal decoherence, optical absorption, and mechanical damping. If residual heating scales with mass or geometry beyond calibrated environmental noise, the platform could set improved constraints on collapse-like models. Even a null result is valuable: it would unify two previously separate approaches—heralded optomechanical nonclassicality and noninterferometric collapse-model tests—into a single controlled experimental program for locating the quantum-classical boundary in mechanical resonators."
  }
}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Use an idea_plan to propose experiments testing the boundary between quantum and classical behavior in optomechanical resonators.

content:
I first search across high-conductivity Li solid electrolytes to anchor the prompt in known structure families, dopant strategies, and synthesis constraints rather than asking for generic “new materials.”  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"lithium solid electrolyte high ionic conductivity electrochemical stability doped LLZO LGPS halide argyrodite review synthesis","discipline":"materials","max_results":6}
```  
[result]: Found candidates spanning oxide garnets, sulfide LGPS/argyrodites, and halide electrolytes. Key real paper_ids include DOI:10.1038/nmat3066, DOI:10.1002/anie.200701144, DOI:10.1038/nenergy.2016.30, DOI:10.1038/s41563-018-0165-5, DOI:10.1038/s41563-021-01083-z, and DOI:10.1038/s41563-023-01674-2.

I read representative oxide and sulfide papers because they define two contrasting design regimes: air/moisture-tolerant, high-voltage oxides versus highly conductive but interfacially reactive sulfides.  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper_ids":["DOI:10.1002/anie.200701144","DOI:10.1038/nmat3066","DOI:10.1038/nenergy.2016.30"]}
```  
[result]: Murugan et al. introduced garnet Li7La3Zr2O12 with ~10^-4 S cm^-1 room-temperature conductivity and high stability versus Li metal/high-voltage cathodes after high-temperature sintering. Kamaya et al. reported LGPS Li10GeP2S12 with ~12 mS cm^-1 conductivity; Kato et al. later showed Si/P/S/Cl LGPS-like sulfides reaching ~25 mS cm^-1 but requiring controlled sulfide synthesis and interface management.

I then extract a design genome to translate those papers into variables a materials-discovery prompt should require: framework chemistry, mobile-Li topology, dopant sites, processing windows, and stability targets.  
```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","paper_ids":["DOI:10.1002/anie.200701144","DOI:10.1038/nmat3066","DOI:10.1038/nenergy.2016.30"],"schema":"gene_genome"}
```  
[result]: Extracted genes: garnet LLZO benefits from Al/Ga/Ta/Nb substitutions that stabilize cubic Li pathways but needs dense sintering; LGPS-type sulfides benefit from mixed tetrahedral/octahedral Li channels and Si/Ge/Sn/P/Cl tuning but suffer oxidation/moisture sensitivity; synthesis genes include ball milling, sealed-tube sulfide annealing, hot pressing, SPS, and excess Li compensation.

I check novelty against recent halide and argyrodite trends to avoid proposing only canonical LLZO/LGPS rediscovery and to push toward hybrid search spaces with better voltage stability.  
```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","query":"discover Li solid electrolytes high conductivity wide electrochemical window doped garnet LGPS argyrodite halide synthesis parameters dopant sites","against_paper_ids":["DOI:10.1038/s41563-018-0165-5","DOI:10.1038/s41563-021-01083-z","DOI:10.1038/s41563-023-01674-2"]}
```  
[result]: Novelty is strongest if the prompt explicitly asks for cross-family candidate generation: halide chlorides/bromides/oxyhalides for high-voltage cathode compatibility, doped garnets for Li-metal compatibility, and sulfide/argyrodite derivatives only when coatings or interface-stabilization routes are specified. The prompt should require computed electrochemical stability, Li-vacancy/interstitial tuning, and experimentally realistic synthesis windows.

I now propose the requested research prompt as a structured gene_genome, with explicit candidate chemistries, dopant sites, and synthesis parameters.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","content":{"mechanism_genome":"Develop a materials-discovery workflow for solid-state lithium electrolytes by linking crystal-framework topology, Li-vacancy/interstitial concentration, anion polarizability, and dopant-controlled phase stabilization to room-temperature Li-ion conductivity and electrochemical stability. Explicitly search garnet oxides Li7La3Zr2O12-based, LGPS-type sulfides Li10MP2S12 where M = Ge, Si, Sn, argyrodites Li6PS5X where X = Cl, Br, I, and halide/oxyhalide electrolytes such as Li3YCl6, Li3InCl6, Li2ZrCl6, Li3ScCl6, mixed Cl/Br systems, and oxygen-substituted halides.","niche_genome":"Target candidate chemistries for all-solid-state batteries requiring conductivity ≥1 mS cm^-1 at 25 °C, calculated electrochemical stability window preferably ≥0–4.3 V vs Li/Li+, low grain-boundary resistance, and compatibility with Li metal or high-Ni layered oxide cathodes. Prioritize underexplored substitutions: Ta/Nb/W/Mo on Zr sites in LLZO, Al/Ga/Fe on Li sites or grain-boundary sites in garnets, Si/Sn/Ge mixing on tetravalent sites in LGPS, P/Sb/As substitution on P sites in sulfides, Cl/Br/I mixing on halogen sites in argyrodites and halides, and Y/In/Sc/Zr/Hf mixed-metal halide frameworks.","observation_genome":"Use retrieved evidence as anchors: LLZO garnets can offer broad apparent stability but require cubic-phase stabilization and dense sintering; LGPS-family sulfides reach 10–25 mS cm^-1 but face oxidative and moisture instability; sulfide chlorine substitution and Li sublattice disorder can sharply raise conductivity; halide electrolytes are promising near high-voltage cathodes but often need optimization of Li percolation and moisture/process stability. Ask the model or researcher to report for each candidate: composition, dopant site, intended defect chemistry, predicted migration barrier, conductivity estimate, decomposition products, voltage window, density/porosity target, and interfacial mitigation strategy.","limitation_genome":"The prompt must distinguish thermodynamic electrochemical window from kinetic/passivating stability and must not rank materials by bulk conductivity alone. Penalize candidates containing scarce/toxic/costly elements such as Ge or excessive In unless justified. Require synthesis feasibility and phase-purity checks because high calculated conductivity can be invalidated by impurity phases, grain-boundary blocking, Li loss during sintering, sulfide hydrolysis, or halide hygroscopicity.","delta_genome":"Research prompt: 'Discover solid-state lithium-ion electrolyte materials with both high room-temperature Li-ion conductivity and wide electrochemical stability windows. Generate a gene_genome output for each proposed material family. Include candidate chemistries, exact dopant sites, charge-compensation mechanism, Li-vacancy/interstitial strategy, target crystal structure, expected migration pathway, calculated/predicted electrochemical stability window, and practical synthesis parameters. Consider garnet LLZO derivatives, LGPS/argyrodite sulfides, and halide/oxyhalide electrolytes. For dopants, explicitly evaluate Ta/Nb/W on Zr sites, Al/Ga on Li sites, Cl/Br/I on anion sites, Si/Sn/Ge on tetravalent sulfide sites, Sb/As on P sites, and Y/In/Sc/Zr/Hf mixing on halide metal sites. For synthesis, specify precursor choices, atmosphere, ball-milling time, calcination/annealing temperature and duration, sealed ampoule or inert-gas conditions, hot pressing/SPS pressure and temperature, excess-Li compensation, pellet density target, and post-synthesis moisture handling. Rank candidates by conductivity, electrochemical window, synthesizability, air/moisture tolerance, cost, and interfacial compatibility with Li metal and 4.3–4.5 V cathodes.'","claim_genome":"This prompt should yield an evidence-grounded candidate set rather than a single-material guess: oxide garnets as stable Li-metal-facing electrolytes, sulfides as maximum-conductivity baselines requiring interface control, and halide/oxyhalide compositions as high-voltage-compatible discovery targets. The strongest outputs will combine dopant-site-resolved defect design with synthesis-parameter constraints and stability-window verification."}}
```  
[result]: Proposed a gene_genome research prompt grounded in LLZO, LGPS/LGPS-like sulfides, argyrodites, and halide/oxyhalide electrolytes, with required fields for chemistries, dopant sites, and synthesis parameters.

prompt.full_prompt: Develop a research prompt for discovering solid-state electrolyte materials with high lithium-ion conductivity and wide electrochemical stability windows. Explicitly ask for gene_genome output including candidate chemistries, dopant sites, and synthesis parameters.

content:
Rationale: I’ll anchor the workflow in proven earth-abundant Na cathode families, especially layered oxides where crystal framework, Fe/Mn ratios, dopants, and voltage window are tunable design variables. The search targets primary literature with reusable composition/voltage evidence.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "earth abundant sodium ion battery cathode P2 layered oxide Fe Mn dopant voltage DOI"
}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers include Yabuuchi et al., “P2-type Nax[Fe1/2Mn1/2]O2 made from earth-abundant elements...”, Nature Materials 2012, DOI: 10.1038/nmat3309; Ong et al., “Voltage, stability and diffusion barrier differences between sodium-ion and lithium-ion intercalation materials”, Energy Environ. Sci. 2011, DOI: 10.1039/C1EE01782A; and several Mg/Ti/Li-doped P2 Na–Mn–Fe oxide studies aimed at suppressing phase transitions.
Rationale: This paper is directly aligned with the prompt: it demonstrates an Fe/Mn layered oxide cathode using earth-abundant elements and gives a practical baseline framework and voltage range.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "doi:10.1038/nmat3309"
}
```  
[result]: Yabuuchi et al. report P2-type Nax[Fe1/2Mn1/2]O2 as an earth-abundant Na-ion cathode with reversible Na extraction/insertion. The key reusable genes are a P2 layered Na framework, Fe:Mn ≈ 1:1 redox-active transition-metal layer, and operation in a moderate upper-voltage window to avoid severe structural degradation.
Rationale: A discovery workflow should not only copy known compositions; it should screen voltage, stability, and Na mobility computationally before synthesis. Ong et al. provides the computational rationale for comparing Na intercalation voltages and diffusion across frameworks.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "doi:10.1039/C1EE01782A"
}
```  
[result]: Ong et al. show that Na intercalation materials require explicit screening for average voltage, thermodynamic stability, and Na migration barriers because Na behavior can differ substantially from Li analogues. This supports a workflow combining DFT/phase-diagram filtering with voltage and diffusion targets rather than relying on Li-ion intuition.
Rationale: I’ll convert the retrieved evidence into design genes that can drive a concrete materials-discovery pipeline. The extraction emphasizes framework choice, Fe/Mn ratios, dopant levers, and target voltage windows.  
```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "doi:10.1038/nmat3309",
    "doi:10.1039/C1EE01782A"
  ],
  "schema": "gene_genome",
  "focus": "sodium-ion cathodes made from earth-abundant elements; crystal framework, transition-metal ratios, dopants, voltage targets"
}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = Na intercalation in layered/prismatic frameworks with Fe/Mn redox and DFT-screened voltage/stability; niche = low-cost Na cathodes using Fe, Mn, Mg, Ti, Al rather than Co/Ni-heavy chemistries; observation = P2 Na–Fe–Mn oxides are viable baselines, while voltage/stability/migration barriers must be screened explicitly; limitation = high upper cutoffs can trigger phase transitions/oxygen activity, and Fe/Mn ordering or Jahn–Teller-active Mn3+ can degrade cycling.
Rationale: Before proposing the workflow, I’ll check whether the design is merely a known composition or a distinct screening strategy across a constrained earth-abundant chemical space.  
```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "idea": "materials discovery workflow for P2/O3 Na-layered oxide cathodes using earth-abundant Fe-Mn-rich transition-metal layers, Mg/Ti/Al dopants, DFT voltage/stability/diffusion filters, and synthesis validation around 2.0-4.0 V",
  "against_paper_ids": [
    "doi:10.1038/nmat3309",
    "doi:10.1039/C1EE01782A"
  ]
}
```  
[result]: The baseline P2 Na–Fe–Mn cathode and computational voltage screening are known. The novelty lies in the integrated workflow: constrained earth-abundant composition generation, explicit dopant-ratio search to suppress phase transitions, multi-objective voltage/stability/diffusion ranking, and closed-loop experimental validation.
```propose
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Na-ion intercalation/deintercalation in layered oxide cathodes, prioritizing P2-type prismatic Na frameworks and optionally O3-type layered frameworks as secondary candidates. The transition-metal slab should be Fe/Mn-rich to use earth-abundant redox centers, with Fe3+/Fe4+ and Mn3+/Mn4+ contributing capacity. Computational screening should evaluate average Na extraction voltage, Na migration barriers, phase stability against competing oxides, and structural changes at high desodiation.",
  "niche_genome": "Earth-abundant sodium-ion cathodes for low-cost grid or stationary storage, avoiding Co and minimizing or excluding Ni. Primary search space: Nax[Fe_aMn_bM_c]O2 with x ≈ 0.6–1.0, Fe:Mn centered near 1:1 based on P2-Nax[Fe1/2Mn1/2]O2 evidence, and M drawn from abundant/stabilizing dopants such as Mg, Ti, Al, and possibly Li in small amounts. Preferred crystal framework target: P2 layered oxide for Na mobility and demonstrated Fe/Mn viability; O3 layered oxide retained as a fallback for higher Na content.",
  "observation_genome": "Yabuuchi et al. demonstrated that P2-type Nax[Fe1/2Mn1/2]O2 made from earth-abundant Fe and Mn can operate as a rechargeable Na-ion cathode, establishing P2 Na–Fe–Mn layered oxide as a strong baseline. Ong et al. showed that Na cathodes require explicit screening of voltage, stability, and diffusion because Na intercalation behavior differs from Li analogues. Therefore, the workflow should enumerate Fe/Mn-rich layered oxides, calculate voltage profiles and convex-hull stability, filter for Na mobility, and then synthesize the highest-ranked P2 candidates.",
  "limitation_genome": "High upper-voltage charging can induce P2-to-O2-like transitions, oxygen redox/oxygen loss, transition-metal migration, or irreversible structural distortion. Mn3+ Jahn–Teller activity and Fe/Mn ordering may reduce cycle life. Dopants such as Mg2+, Ti4+, or Al3+ can improve structural stability but may reduce redox-active transition-metal content and capacity. The workflow must therefore optimize a tradeoff between voltage, capacity, stability, Na mobility, and dopant dilution.",
  "delta_genome": "Proposed workflow: 1) generate P2/O3 Nax[Fe_aMn_bM_c]O2 candidates with Fe:Mn ratios around 70:30, 60:40, 50:50, 40:60, and 30:70; 2) add 0–15 mol% Mg, Ti, or Al dopants on the transition-metal layer to suppress phase transitions and reduce Jahn–Teller/oxygen-instability risks; 3) use DFT to calculate Na-vacancy ordering, average voltage versus Na/Na+, energy above hull, and Na migration barriers; 4) downselect candidates targeting 2.5–3.8 V average voltage, upper cutoff ideally ≤4.0 V, energy above hull near synthesizable limits, and low Na migration barriers; 5) synthesize top candidates by solid-state or sol-gel routes and validate by XRD/Rietveld refinement, galvanostatic cycling, GITT/PITT, and post-cycling structural analysis.",
  "claim_genome": "A practical discovery target is a P2-layered Na_x[Fe_0.45–0.55Mn_0.35–0.50M_0.02–0.10]O2 cathode, where M = Mg, Ti, or Al, optimized for an Fe:Mn ratio near 1:1 but adjusted to reduce Mn3+ distortion and high-voltage structural collapse. The voltage target should be an average discharge voltage of roughly 2.8–3.4 V versus Na/Na+ with a screened operating window around 2.0–4.0 V. The expected outcome is an earth-abundant Na cathode with lower cost and better structural robustness than undoped Fe/Mn layered oxides while retaining useful reversible capacity."
}
```
