content:
I’ll ground the prompt in demonstrated autonomous lab capabilities: robotic closed-loop catalyst discovery (Burger et al.) and high-throughput/self-driving materials optimization (MacLeod et al.). Then I’ll propose a catalyst-focused research prompt that emphasizes synthesis–characterization–active-learning integration.

```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "autonomous materials laboratory robotic synthesis rapid characterization active learning catalyst discovery",
  "discipline": "materials",
  "max_results": 5
}
```

[result]: Found relevant candidates including Burger et al., “A mobile robotic chemist” Nature 2020, DOI: `10.1038/s41586-020-2442-2`; MacLeod et al., “Self-driving laboratory for accelerated discovery of thin-film materials” Science Advances 2020, DOI: `10.1126/sciadv.aaz8867`; and related closed-loop Bayesian optimization studies for materials discovery.

Burger et al. is directly catalyst-relevant because it couples a mobile robot, automated experimentation, and algorithmic search for photocatalysts for hydrogen evolution. I’ll read it first to anchor the mechanism and observation fields.

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "10.1038/s41586-020-2442-2"
}
```

[result]: The paper demonstrates a mobile robotic chemist that autonomously conducts photocatalytic hydrogen-production experiments, navigating a lab, executing synthesis/testing workflows, and using an optimization algorithm to improve photocatalyst performance. It shows that robotic experimentation can explore catalyst formulation space more efficiently than manual screening.

MacLeod et al. is useful for the broader autonomous materials laboratory pattern: integrated deposition, characterization, and Bayesian optimization. I’ll read it to capture transferable design principles for rapid characterization and closed-loop learning.

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "10.1126/sciadv.aaz8867"
}
```

[result]: The paper reports a self-driving laboratory that combines automated thin-film fabrication, rapid optical characterization, and machine-learning-guided experiment selection. It demonstrates closed-loop optimization of materials properties and highlights bottlenecks around objective selection, measurement throughput, and model uncertainty.

I’ll extract the research genome from these papers to identify the core mechanism, niche, observed capability, and remaining gap for catalyst discovery.

```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "10.1038/s41586-020-2442-2",
    "10.1126/sciadv.aaz8867"
  ],
  "schema": [
    "mechanism_genome",
    "niche_genome",
    "observation_genome",
    "limitation_genome",
    "delta_genome",
    "claim_genome"
  ]
}
```

[result]: Extracted genome: closed-loop robotic experimentation plus active learning accelerates materials search; strongest evidence is in photocatalyst screening and optoelectronic thin-film optimization. Key limitation is that many systems optimize narrow scalar objectives and do not fully integrate multi-modal rapid characterization, mechanistic constraints, and catalyst stability/selectivity tradeoffs.

I’ll check novelty against existing autonomous catalyst discovery work and then formulate a prompt that pushes beyond single-objective screening toward multi-objective, mechanism-aware catalyst discovery.

```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "idea": "An autonomous materials laboratory that combines robotic catalyst synthesis, rapid multi-modal characterization, and uncertainty-aware active learning to discover catalysts optimized jointly for activity, selectivity, stability, and synthesis feasibility.",
  "against_paper_ids": [
    "10.1038/s41586-020-2442-2",
    "10.1126/sciadv.aaz8867"
  ]
}
```

[result]: Novelty appears moderate-to-high as a research prompt: prior work demonstrates autonomous optimization, including photocatalysis, but the proposed direction emphasizes multi-objective catalyst discovery, rapid characterization beyond a single performance assay, and active-learning policies that incorporate uncertainty, mechanistic descriptors, and long-term stability.

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "gene_genome",
  "proposal": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Build a closed-loop autonomous materials laboratory in which robotic liquid/solid-phase synthesis generates catalyst libraries, rapid in-line or at-line characterization measures structure and performance, and uncertainty-aware active learning selects the next experiments. The learning loop should jointly use composition, synthesis parameters, structural descriptors, and catalytic outcomes rather than optimizing only a single activity metric.",
    "niche_genome": "Discovery of new heterogeneous or photo/electrocatalysts where large compositional and processing spaces make exhaustive screening impractical, especially for reactions such as hydrogen evolution, CO2 reduction, oxygen evolution, or selective oxidation.",
    "observation_genome": "Burger et al. demonstrated that a mobile robotic chemist can autonomously execute and optimize photocatalytic hydrogen-production experiments, while MacLeod et al. showed that self-driving laboratories can combine automated fabrication, rapid characterization, and Bayesian optimization for accelerated materials discovery.",
    "limitation_genome": "Existing autonomous materials demonstrations often optimize narrow objectives, rely on limited characterization channels, or underweight catalyst durability, selectivity, and mechanistic interpretability. Catalyst discovery also faces noisy measurements, batch effects, deactivation, and tradeoffs between synthesis feasibility and performance.",
    "delta_genome": "Design a next-generation autonomous catalyst-discovery platform that performs multi-objective active learning over activity, selectivity, stability, cost, and synthesizability; integrates rapid spectroscopic, structural, and catalytic assays; and uses model uncertainty plus mechanistic priors to decide when to exploit, explore, or run confirmatory replicates.",
    "claim_genome": "A mechanism-aware, multi-objective autonomous laboratory can discover higher-performing and more robust catalysts with fewer experiments than human-guided screening or single-objective Bayesian optimization, while producing interpretable structure–synthesis–performance relationships suitable for follow-up mechanistic validation."
  }
}
```

[result]: Proposed research prompt generated in `gene_genome` schema.

prompt.full_prompt: Generate a research prompt for autonomous materials laboratories that combine robotic synthesis, rapid characterization, and active learning to discover new catalysts.

Rationale: I’ll scan for ASD evidence around synaptic adhesion molecules, rare damaging variants, CNVs, and noncoding regulation, then synthesize a structured output.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"autism spectrum disorder synaptic adhesion molecules rare variants copy number variants noncoding regulatory regions"}
```

[result]
Found relevant literature themes: NRXN/NLGN/SHANK synaptic adhesion/scaffold genes; rare de novo variants in ASD; recurrent CNVs including 16p11.2, 15q11-q13, 22q11.2; noncoding regulatory disruption affecting neurodevelopmental gene expression.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper":"rare variants synaptic adhesion autism NRXN NLGN SHANK review"}
```

[result]
Rare variants in synaptic adhesion and postsynaptic scaffold genes are repeatedly implicated in ASD. NRXN1 deletions and point variants, NLGN3/NLGN4X mutations, and SHANK2/SHANK3 variants alter synapse formation, excitatory/inhibitory balance, and plasticity.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper":"copy number variants autism synaptic genes regulatory regions"}
```

[result]
CNVs contribute substantially to ASD risk. Some directly disrupt coding sequence of synaptic genes, while others alter dosage of multiple neurodevelopmental genes. Regulatory CNVs may affect enhancers or topologically associated domains controlling neuronal expression.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper":"noncoding regulatory variants autism neurodevelopment enhancers"}
```

[result]
Whole-genome studies suggest enrichment of de novo noncoding variants in fetal brain enhancers, promoters, conserved regulatory elements, and chromatin contacts linked to ASD risk genes. Interpretation remains limited by cell-type specificity and incomplete functional validation.

```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","focus":"ASD synaptic adhesion rare variants CNVs noncoding regulatory regions"}
```

[result]
Extracted candidate components:
- Mechanism: synaptic adhesion disruption, altered synapse formation, E/I imbalance
- Niche: ASD genetics, neurodevelopment, synaptic biology
- Observations: rare variants and CNVs in NRXN/NLGN/SHANK pathways; noncoding enhancer disruption
- Limitations: incomplete penetrance, pleiotropy, regulatory annotation uncertainty
- Delta: integrate coding CNVs and noncoding regulatory variants around synaptic adhesion networks
- Claim: ASD risk can arise from convergent perturbation of synaptic adhesion gene dosage and regulation

```action
{"tool":"genome_diff","baseline":"ASD coding variant models","candidate":"integrated coding CNV plus noncoding synaptic adhesion regulatory model"}
```

[result]
Novel delta identified: instead of treating rare coding variants, CNVs, and noncoding elements separately, the candidate model frames them as convergent perturbations of synaptic adhesion dosage, timing, and cell-type-specific regulatory control.

```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claim":"ASD synaptic adhesion risk integrates rare variants CNVs and noncoding regulatory disruption"}
```

[result]
Partially novel synthesis. Individual components are well established, but the explicit unified emphasis on rare variants, CNVs, and noncoding regulatory regions converging on synaptic adhesion molecule expression and dosage is a useful organizing hypothesis.

```action
{"tool":"propose","output":{"Idea":"Develop an integrated ASD synaptic-adhesion risk model that combines rare coding variants, copy number variants, and noncoding regulatory disruptions affecting genes such as NRXN1, NLGN3/NLGN4X, SHANK2/SHANK3, CNTNAP2, and related adhesion/scaffold pathways.","ImplementationSteps":{"1_variant_collection":"Aggregate ASD whole-genome, exome, and CNV datasets with emphasis on rare de novo and inherited variants in synaptic adhesion genes and their regulatory landscapes.","2_regulatory_annotation":"Map noncoding variants to fetal and postnatal brain enhancers, promoters, chromatin accessibility peaks, eQTLs, and 3D chromatin contacts linked to synaptic adhesion genes.","3_network_integration":"Construct a dosage-sensitive synaptic adhesion network connecting coding disruptions, CNV dosage effects, and enhancer/promoter perturbations.","4_functional_prioritization":"Prioritize variants predicted to alter neuronal expression timing, synapse formation, excitatory/inhibitory balance, or adhesion-complex assembly.","5_validation":"Test top candidates using neuronal cell models, CRISPR perturbation of enhancers, and synaptic phenotyping assays."},"ImplementationOrder":["1_variant_collection","2_regulatory_annotation","3_network_integration","4_functional_prioritization","5_validation"],"Dataset":"ASD whole-genome sequencing cohorts, simplex family de novo variant datasets, CNV callsets, fetal brain enhancer maps, single-cell chromatin accessibility data, neuronal eQTL resources, and chromatin conformation datasets.","EvaluationMetrics":{"genetic_enrichment":"Enrichment of rare damaging coding variants, CNVs, and noncoding regulatory variants near synaptic adhesion genes in ASD cases versus controls.","network_convergence":"Degree to which independent variant classes converge on shared synaptic adhesion and scaffold pathways.","regulatory_predictiveness":"Ability of enhancer/promoter annotations and chromatin contacts to prioritize ASD-associated noncoding variants.","functional_effect":"Measured changes in synaptic adhesion gene expression, synapse density, dendritic spine morphology, or excitatory/inhibitory synaptic balance.","clinical_relevance":"Association between integrated burden scores and ASD phenotypic severity, language delay, cognitive measures, or comorbid epilepsy."},"ExpectedOutcome":"A unified framework in which ASD risk is explained partly by convergent disruption of synaptic adhesion molecule dosage and regulation through rare variants, CNVs, and noncoding regulatory changes, producing altered neurodevelopmental synapse formation and circuit balance."}}

content:
Rationale: I’ll search for recent evidence on dopaminergic neuron vulnerability in Parkinson’s disease, extract recurring genes/regulatory elements, compare candidate mechanisms, and then propose a structured output.

```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "dopaminergic neuron vulnerability Parkinson's disease genes regulatory elements single cell ATAC GWAS enhancer"
}
```

[result]
Found papers and reviews highlighting dopaminergic neuron subtype vulnerability, PD GWAS loci, enhancer/promoter dysregulation, and cell-type-specific regulatory programs. Recurrent genes include SNCA, LRRK2, GBA1, PRKN, PINK1, DJ-1/PARK7, VPS35, MAPT, TMEM175, GCH1, RIT2, BST1, ATP13A2, DNAJC6, SYNJ1, and SLC6A3. Regulatory factors include NR4A2/NURR1, PITX3, EN1/2, LMX1A/B, FOXA1/2, ASCL1, MEIS2, and REST-associated repression.

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper": "Single-cell transcriptomic and chromatin accessibility studies of human midbrain dopaminergic neurons in Parkinson's disease"
}
```

[result]
Key findings: vulnerable ventral tier substantia nigra dopaminergic neurons show high oxidative phosphorylation demand, dopamine handling stress, calcium signaling burden, lysosomal/autophagy signatures, and mitochondrial dysfunction. Regulatory enrichment observed near dopaminergic neuron identity genes and PD GWAS loci.

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper": "Parkinson's disease genetics and cell-type-specific regulatory annotation"
}
```

[result]
PD-associated variants are enriched in neuronal enhancer regions, including regulatory elements active in midbrain dopaminergic neurons. Candidate regulatory loci implicate SNCA expression control, LRRK2 immune/autophagy pathways, GBA1 lysosomal function, MAPT haplotype-linked regulatory variation, and TMEM175 lysosomal potassium channel biology.

```action

  "tool": "extract_genome",

}
```

[result]
Extracted candidate biological components:
- Candidate loci: SNCA, LRRK2, GBA1, PRKN, PINK1, PARK7, VPS35, MAPT, TMEM175, GCH1, RIT2, SLC6A3, TH, ALDH1A1.
- Regulatory elements: SNCA promoter/enhancer regions, MAPT haplotype-linked regulatory regions, dopaminergic neuron identity enhancers, NR4A2/PITX3/FOXA2/LMX1A-bound enhancers, oxidative stress response elements, lysosomal/autophagy regulatory regions.
- Pathways: mitochondrial quality control, mitophagy, lysosomal/autophagy function, alpha-synuclein aggregation, dopamine metabolism, calcium handling, oxidative stress, proteostasis, neuroinflammation.

```

[result]
Core Mendelian/high-confidence PD genes overlap with pathways of alpha-synuclein toxicity, lysosomal stress, and mitochondrial dysfunction. GWAS and regulatory candidates add dopaminergic neuron identity, dopamine synthesis/transport, and neuronal resilience pathways. Strong convergence observed around lysosome-mitochondria-dopamine stress axes.

```action
{

  "claim": "Candidate dopaminergic neuron vulnerability loci in Parkinson's disease can be prioritized by integrating PD GWAS, single-cell RNA-seq, single-cell ATAC-seq, enhancer-promoter maps, and perturbation validation in iPSC-derived dopaminergic neurons."

```

Claim is consistent with current field direction. Novelty is moderate: many individual components are established, but integrated prioritization across vulnerable neuron subtypes and regulatory elements remains an active research opportunity.

Key regulatory elements include SNCA enhancers/promoters, MAPT haplotype-linked regulatory regions, dopaminergic identity enhancers bound by NR4A2/PITX3/FOXA2/LMX1A, lysosomal/autophagy enhancers, oxidative-stress response elements, and mitochondrial quality-control regulatory regions.",

content:
Rationale: I’ll survey epilepsy genetics literature for ion channel mutation mechanisms, polygenic susceptibility evidence, and genotype-specific treatment ideas, then synthesize a gene_genome-style output.

```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "epilepsy susceptibility genes ion channel mutations polygenic risk genotype specific treatment SCN1A KCNQ2 CACNA1A GABRA1"
}
```

[result]
Found literature clusters around monogenic epilepsy genes including SCN1A, SCN2A, SCN8A, KCNQ2, KCNQ3, KCNT1, CACNA1A, CACNA1H, GABRA1, GABRG2, GRIN2A, and HCN1. Polygenic risk studies implicate common-variant burden across synaptic, ion channel, neurodevelopmental, and excitability pathways.

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "ids": [
    "PMID_SCN1A_Dravet_review",
    "PMID_epilepsy_GWAS_polygenic_risk",
    "PMID_KCNQ2_developmental_epileptic_encephalopathy",
    "PMID_precision_medicine_epilepsy"
  ]
}
```

[result]
Key extracted points:
- SCN1A loss-of-function is strongly associated with Dravet syndrome; sodium-channel blockers can worsen seizures in many SCN1A-related cases.
- SCN2A and SCN8A can involve gain- or loss-of-function effects, with treatment implications depending on variant effect and developmental stage.
- KCNQ2/KCNQ3 mutations reduce M-current potassium conductance and increase neuronal excitability.
- CACNA1A and other calcium-channel genes contribute to epilepsy and migraine/ataxia overlap syndromes.
- Epilepsy polygenic risk reflects cumulative common variants of small effect, not only rare monogenic mutations.
- Genotype-informed therapy hypotheses include avoiding sodium-channel blockers in SCN1A LoF, considering sodium-channel blockers in selected SCN8A GoF, retigabine-like KCNQ openers conceptually for KCNQ loss-of-function, and mTOR inhibitors for mTOR-pathway epilepsies.

```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "schema": "gene_genome",
  "focus": "epilepsy susceptibility genes, ion channel mutations, polygenic risk, genotype-specific treatments"
}
```

[result]
Draft genome fragments:
- mechanism_genome: altered neuronal excitability through sodium, potassium, calcium, chloride/GABAergic, and glutamatergic channel dysfunction; rare high-effect variants plus common polygenic load.
- niche_genome: epilepsy susceptibility and precision epilepsy genetics.
- observation_genome: SCN1A, SCN2A, SCN8A, KCNQ2, KCNT1, CACNA1A, GABRA1/GABRG2, GRIN2A repeatedly implicated.
- limitation_genome: variant functional effects are heterogeneous; PRS portability and clinical utility remain limited.
- delta_genome: integrate variant functional class with PRS and phenotype to stratify treatment.
- claim_genome: genotype-specific treatment hypotheses can improve seizure control and reduce contraindicated drug exposure.

```action
{
  "tool": "genome_diff",
  "baseline": "generic epilepsy genetics summary",
  "candidate": "ion-channel-centered polygenic and pharmacogenomic gene_genome"
}
```

[result]
Differences identified:
- Adds explicit rare/common genetic architecture.
- Distinguishes channel subtype mechanisms.
- Adds genotype-specific treatment hypotheses.
- Notes functional ambiguity of variants as a limitation.
- Stronger precision-medicine framing than baseline.

```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "idea": "Combine ion channel rare variant functional annotation with epilepsy polygenic risk to generate genotype-specific treatment hypotheses."
}
```

[result]
Novelty status: moderately novel synthesis rather than wholly new. Prior literature supports precision epilepsy genetics, but integrated rare-variant functional class + PRS + drug-selection framework remains an active translational area.

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": "Epilepsy susceptibility genes are mainly about neuronal excitability: SCN1A loss of function in Dravet suggests avoiding sodium-channel blockers, while SCN2A/SCN8A gain-of-function may sometimes motivate sodium-channel blocker hypotheses; KCNQ2/KCNQ3 loss reduces M-current and could point toward potassium-channel-opening strategies; CACNA1A and GABA receptor genes add calcium and inhibitory signaling mechanisms. Polygenic risk adds background liability from many common variants, but it is not yet decisive clinically. Overall claim: combine ion-channel mutation class, PRS burden, seizure phenotype, and drug response history for genotype-specific treatment selection.",
  "extra_notes": {
    "top_genes": ["SCN1A", "SCN2A", "SCN8A", "KCNQ2", "KCNQ3", "KCNT1", "CACNA1A", "GABRA1", "GABRG2", "GRIN2A"],
    "treatment_hypotheses": "avoid sodium blockers in SCN1A LoF; consider sodium blockers for selected sodium-channel GoF; explore KCNQ openers for KCNQ LoF; use PRS as background modifier"
  },
  "missing_schema_fields": true
}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Provide a gene_genome output on epilepsy susceptibility genes, emphasizing ion channel mutations, polygenic risk, and genotype-specific treatment hypotheses.

content:
Rationale: I’ll survey relevant neuroscience and microbiome literature, extract recurring mechanisms linking gut-derived metabolites to BBB integrity and neuroinflammation, compare against existing study designs, then propose a structured research direction.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"gut microbiome metabolites blood brain barrier integrity neuroinflammation short chain fatty acids indoles bile acids"}
```

[result] Found studies implicating short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs), tryptophan metabolites including indoles, secondary bile acids, and TMAO in modulating endothelial tight junctions, microglial activation, astrocyte signaling, and systemic inflammation.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper":"Braniste et al. 2014 gut microbiota influences blood-brain barrier permeability"}
```

[result] Germ-free mice showed increased BBB permeability and altered tight junction protein expression. Colonization or SCFA treatment partially restored BBB integrity, suggesting microbiota-derived metabolites can regulate barrier function.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper":"Erny et al. 2015 host microbiota constantly control maturation and function of microglia"}
```

[result] Microbiota depletion altered microglial maturation and immune responsiveness. SCFA supplementation rescued aspects of microglial dysfunction, supporting a metabolite-mediated gut-brain immune axis.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper":"tryptophan metabolites aryl hydrocarbon receptor astrocytes microglia neuroinflammation"}
```

[result] Microbial tryptophan metabolites can activate AhR signaling in astrocytes and immune cells, influencing inflammatory tone in the CNS. Indole derivatives may suppress or reshape neuroinflammatory responses depending on context.

```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","text":"Gut microbiome-derived SCFAs, indoles, and bile acids influence BBB tight junctions, endothelial inflammation, microglia activation, and astrocyte-mediated immune signaling."}
```

[result]
{
  "mechanism_genome": ["SCFA-mediated tight junction regulation", "AhR activation by indole metabolites", "bile-acid receptor signaling", "microglial inflammatory modulation"],
  "niche_genome": ["gut-brain axis", "BBB dysfunction", "neuroinflammation", "microbiome metabolomics"],
  "observation_genome": ["germ-free animals exhibit increased BBB permeability", "SCFAs can restore barrier-associated proteins", "microbial metabolites affect microglial maturation"],
  "limitation_genome": ["causal metabolite-specific effects remain hard to isolate", "many studies use broad microbiome perturbations", "human translation is limited"],
  "delta_genome": ["integrate metabolomics with BBB assays and neuroimmune profiling"],
  "claim_genome": ["microbiome-derived metabolites causally regulate BBB integrity and neuroinflammatory state"]
}
```

```action
{"tool":"genome_diff","baseline":"existing studies often test broad microbiome depletion or single metabolite classes","candidate":"comparative multi-metabolite perturbation across SCFAs, indoles, and bile acids with BBB and neuroimmune readouts"}
```

[result] Candidate differs by testing multiple metabolite families in parallel, combining BBB permeability, endothelial tight junction expression, microglial activation states, cytokine profiling, and metabolomic validation.

```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"parallel causal testing of gut microbiome-derived SCFAs, indoles, and bile acids on BBB integrity and neuroinflammation using in vitro BBB models and mouse validation"}
```

[result] Moderately novel. Individual metabolite classes are studied, but systematic comparison across metabolite families with matched BBB and neuroinflammatory readouts appears less common.

```action
{"tool":"propose","schema":"gene_genome","content":{"mechanism_genome":["Gut microbiome-derived metabolites modulate BBB endothelial tight junctions through SCFA receptor signaling, AhR activation by indole derivatives, and bile acid receptor pathways.","Metabolite-driven changes in BBB permeability alter CNS immune exposure and contribute to microglial and astrocytic inflammatory activation.","Systemic cytokine changes may mediate part of the metabolite-BBB-neuroinflammation relationship."],"niche_genome":["Neuroscience","gut-brain axis","blood-brain barrier biology","neuroinflammation","microbiome metabolomics"],"observation_genome":["Germ-free or antibiotic-treated animals often show altered BBB permeability and disrupted tight junction expression.","SCFAs such as acetate, propionate, and butyrate have been associated with restoration of BBB integrity.","Microbial tryptophan metabolites can regulate neuroimmune signaling through AhR pathways.","Microglial maturation and inflammatory responsiveness are sensitive to microbiome status and microbial metabolites."],"limitation_genome":["Prior work often perturbs the whole microbiome, making it difficult to assign effects to specific metabolite families.","Many studies focus on either BBB integrity or neuroinflammation, but not both in the same experimental framework.","Human relevance is limited by species differences and difficulty measuring CNS exposure to gut-derived metabolites.","Dose, timing, and combinatorial interactions among metabolites remain insufficiently characterized."],"delta_genome":["Perform a comparative study of SCFAs, indole derivatives, and secondary bile acids using an in vitro human BBB model followed by targeted mouse validation.","Measure BBB integrity using TEER, fluorescent tracer permeability, and tight junction protein expression including claudin-5, occludin, and ZO-1.","Assess neuroinflammatory consequences using microglial activation markers, astrocyte reactivity markers, and cytokine panels.","Use targeted metabolomics to confirm metabolite exposure in serum, gut contents, and brain-associated compartments.","Compare single-metabolite treatments with defined metabolite mixtures to identify additive or antagonistic effects."],"claim_genome":["A metabolite-resolved framework will identify which gut microbiome-derived metabolites most strongly preserve or disrupt BBB integrity and determine whether these barrier effects predict neuroinflammatory activation.","The study will clarify causal links between microbial metabolic output, BBB function, and CNS immune state in a neuroscience-relevant gut-brain axis model."]}}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Develop an idea_plan to study the impact of gut microbiome-derived metabolites on blood-brain barrier integrity and neuroinflammation.
Explore how chronic stress alters hippocampal neurogenesis across adolescence and adulthood. Include potential biomarkers and translational relevance.

content:
rationale: I first search for anchor literature spanning flame-retardant polymer mechanisms, intumescent/low-smoke strategies, and EV battery fire-safety context so the generated research prompt is grounded rather than generic.  
```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "fire resistant low smoke polymer composites intumescent nanofiller electric vehicle battery enclosure flame retardant review",
  "discipline": "materials",
  "limit": 5
}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers returned included: `doi:10.1016/j.mser.2008.09.002` on flame-retardant polymer materials and nanocomposites; `doi:10.1016/j.progpolymsci.2015.04.010` on intumescent flame-retardant systems; and `doi:10.1126/sciadv.aas9820` on lithium-ion battery safety materials.

rationale: I read the broad flame-retardant polymer review to identify established mechanisms and persistent limitations relevant to enclosure-scale composites.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "doi:10.1016/j.mser.2008.09.002"
}
```  
[result]: Laoutid et al. summarize halogenated, phosphorus/nitrogen intumescent, mineral-hydroxide, and nanocomposite flame-retardant approaches. The review emphasizes that reducing peak heat release can conflict with mechanical performance, processability, smoke suppression, and additive loading constraints.

rationale: I then read the intumescence-focused review because EV battery enclosures need barrier-forming behavior under severe heat flux, not only ignition delay.  
```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper_id": "doi:10.1016/j.progpolymsci.2015.04.010"
}
```  
[result]: Alongi, Han, and Bourbigot describe intumescent systems as acid source/carbon source/blowing source chemistries that expand into protective char layers. The review highlights opportunities in hybrid intumescent systems with layered silicates, carbon nanofillers, and inorganic smoke-suppressing phases, but notes durability and formulation compatibility challenges.

rationale: I extract a compact genome from the retrieved evidence, focusing on design logic for low-smoke, fire-resistant structural composites rather than single-additive screening.  
```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_ids": [
    "doi:10.1016/j.mser.2008.09.002",
    "doi:10.1016/j.progpolymsci.2015.04.010",
    "doi:10.1126/sciadv.aas9820"
  ],
  "schema": "gene_genome"
}
```  
[result]: Extracted pattern: combine condensed-phase char formation, inorganic barrier reinforcement, and smoke/toxic-gas suppression while preserving mechanical integrity and manufacturability. EV battery safety literature adds the need to tolerate localized thermal-runaway heat fluxes and delay enclosure breach.

rationale: I check novelty around a prompt that explicitly couples low-smoke flame retardancy, structural enclosure mechanics, and EV thermal-runaway exposure rather than treating flame retardancy as a coupon-only polymer property.  
```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "idea": "A research prompt for multifunctional EV battery-enclosure polymer composites using halogen-free intumescent chemistry, mineral smoke suppressants, and hybrid nano/micro barriers, evaluated under cone calorimetry plus battery thermal-runaway-relevant heat flux and mechanical retention tests."
}
```  
[result]: The combination is plausible and moderately novel as a research framing: individual components are well known, but the integrated enclosure-level prompt—low smoke, structural retention, halogen-free chemistry, and thermal-runaway-relevant validation—is less commonly specified.

```propose
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Develop halogen-free polymer composites for EV battery enclosures that combine phosphorus/nitrogen intumescent char formation with inorganic barrier and smoke-suppressing phases such as layered silicates, expandable graphite, magnesium/aluminum hydroxides, zinc borate, or transition-metal oxides. The target mechanism is a cohesive expanded char that lowers heat release, suppresses smoke and corrosive gases, slows heat transfer from thermal runaway, and preserves load-bearing enclosure integrity.",
  "niche_genome": "Electric vehicle battery pack enclosures where metals are being partially replaced by lightweight polymer or fiber-reinforced composites, but the material must resist ignition, delay burn-through, limit smoke generation, avoid halogenated/toxic emissions, and maintain mechanical performance after high-heat exposure.",
  "observation_genome": "Prior reviews identify intumescent systems as effective condensed-phase fire barriers and polymer nanocomposites/mineral fillers as useful for reducing peak heat release and smoke. Battery safety literature indicates that enclosure materials must survive localized thermal-runaway events rather than only pass standard flammability tests.",
  "limitation_genome": "Conventional halogenated flame retardants can generate corrosive and toxic smoke; high mineral-hydroxide loadings can increase density and degrade processability or toughness; conductive carbon additives may complicate electrical insulation; and many studies rely on small coupon tests without enclosure-relevant heat flux, impact, vibration, or post-fire mechanical retention.",
  "delta_genome": "Prompt: Explore a lightweight, low-smoke, fire-resistant polymer composite for EV battery enclosures by designing a hybrid halogen-free formulation that couples intumescent phosphorus/nitrogen chemistry with smoke-suppressing inorganic fillers and mechanically reinforcing fibers. Compare matrix options such as polypropylene, polyamide, phenolic, epoxy, or recyclable thermoplastic composites; vary filler architecture from micro-scale mineral additives to nano-clays, graphene-derived barriers, expandable graphite, or ceramic-forming additives; and evaluate the trade-off among flame retardancy, smoke density, toxicity, electrical insulation, impact resistance, processability, recyclability, and enclosure-level thermal-runaway protection.",
  "claim_genome": "A successful EV battery-enclosure composite should be treated as a multifunctional fire-safety structure, not merely a flame-retarded plastic: the strongest research direction is an integrated halogen-free intumescent/barrier/smoke-suppressant composite validated by cone calorimetry, smoke-density testing, toxic gas analysis, UL-94 or equivalent screening, thermal-runaway-simulated heat exposure, and post-exposure mechanical testing."
}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Develop a prompt exploring fire-resistant, low-smoke polymer composites for electric vehicle battery enclosures.

content:
Rationale: I’ll first scan for representative work on cerebellar contributions to social cognition and predictive processing, extract a conceptual “genome,” compare it against the requested prompt space, check novelty, and then propose a research prompt.  

```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "cerebellum social cognition predictive processing mentalizing autism prediction error neuroscience review"
}
```  

[result]  
Found relevant clusters:  
1. Cerebellar role in internal models and prediction errors beyond motor control.  
2. Cerebellar involvement in social cognition, mentalizing, affective prediction, and autism spectrum conditions.  
3. Cerebro-cerebellar loops connecting posterior cerebellum with prefrontal, temporoparietal, and default-mode regions.  
4. Predictive processing accounts suggesting cerebellum updates models for social sequence expectations.

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "ids": [
    "VanOverwalle_2014_cerebellum_social_cognition",
    "Sokolov_2017_cerebellum_social_prediction",
    "Schmahmann_2019_cerebellar_cognitive_affective_syndrome",
    "Diedrichsen_2019_cerebellar_internal_models",
    "Moberget_Ivry_2019_prediction_cerebellum"
  ]
}
```  

[result]  
Key points extracted:  
- Posterior cerebellar lobules Crus I/II are repeatedly implicated in social cognition and mentalizing.  
- Cerebellum may encode sequence-based models of action, intention, and social event structure.  
- Predictive coding frameworks treat the cerebellum as supporting rapid model updating and error correction.  
- Social deficits in cerebellar lesion, developmental cerebellar disruption, and autism studies suggest a role in socio-cognitive calibration.  
- Limitations: many studies are correlational, tasks are heterogeneous, and causal circuit-level tests remain sparse.

```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "source": "literature_cluster",
  "schema": [
    "mechanism_genome",
    "niche_genome",
    "observation_genome",
    "limitation_genome",
    "delta_genome",
    "claim_genome"
  ]
}
```  

[result]  
```json
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Posterior cerebellar circuits compute prediction errors and update internal models for temporally structured social information.",
  "niche_genome": "Human social cognition, mentalizing, action prediction, autism-related social processing, and cerebellar-prefrontal/temporoparietal circuits.",
  "observation_genome": "Crus I/II activation during mentalizing and social sequence tasks; social impairments after cerebellar disruption; altered cerebellar connectivity in autism.",
  "limitation_genome": "Evidence is often correlational, coarse in spatial/temporal resolution, and rarely links circuit-level prediction errors to trial-by-trial social learning.",
  "delta_genome": "Test whether cerebellar stimulation or lesion status selectively alters prediction-error updating during dynamic social inference.",
  "claim_genome": "Cerebellar circuits contribute to social cognition by supporting predictive models of others’ actions, intentions, and affective trajectories."
}
```

```action
{
  "tool": "genome_diff",
  "base": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Cortical mentalizing network supports social cognition via representation of beliefs and intentions.",
    "niche_genome": "Default-mode network and temporoparietal junction in theory of mind.",
    "observation_genome": "TPJ/mPFC activation during belief attribution tasks.",
    "limitation_genome": "Limited mechanistic account of timing, sequence learning, and prediction-error updating.",
    "delta_genome": "Add cerebellar-cortical predictive processing circuit model.",

content:
Rationale: I’ll survey relevant closed-loop DBS concepts for treatment-resistant depression (TRD), extract core design elements, compare novelty against known adaptive DBS approaches, and then propose a structured research concept.  

```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "closed-loop adaptive deep brain stimulation treatment-resistant depression biomarkers subcallosal cingulate nucleus accumbens"
}
```

[result]  
Found literature themes:  
- TRD DBS targets include subcallosal cingulate cortex, ventral capsule/ventral striatum, nucleus accumbens, medial forebrain bundle.  
- Closed-loop DBS is emerging but less established in depression than Parkinson’s disease or epilepsy.  
- Candidate feedback signals include local field potentials, intracranial EEG biomarkers, autonomic features, sleep/activity rhythms, and patient-reported mood states.  

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper": "Adaptive deep brain stimulation and biomarkers for neuropsychiatric disorders"
}
```

[result]  
Key extracted points:  
- Psychiatric symptoms fluctuate over slower timescales than motor symptoms.  
- Closed-loop stimulation for depression may require multimodal biomarkers rather than a single neural feature.  
- Safety constraints are critical because stimulation changes can affect mood, anxiety, impulsivity, or hypomania risk.  

```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "source": "closed-loop DBS for treatment-resistant depression literature"
}
```

[result]  
```json
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Adaptive stimulation modulates dysfunctional cortico-limbic circuits using real-time neural or behavioral biomarkers.",
  "niche_genome": "Patients with severe treatment-resistant depression who are candidates for invasive DBS.",
  "observation_genome": "Mood states and depressive symptoms may correlate with distributed limbic network electrophysiology, sleep disruption, autonomic arousal, and behavioral passivity.",
  "limitation_genome": "Biomarkers are patient-specific, depression changes over long timescales, and closed-loop policies risk overfitting to noisy mood labels.",
  "delta_genome": "Combine intracranial neural signals with wearable and ecological momentary assessment data to guide stimulation adjustments.",
  "claim_genome": "A multimodal adaptive DBS strategy could outperform fixed stimulation by tailoring stimulation to evolving symptom-state signatures."
}
```

```action
{
  "tool": "genome_diff",
  "baseline": "open-loop DBS for treatment-resistant depression",
  "candidate": "multimodal closed-loop DBS using neural, wearable, and self-report signals"
}
```

[result]  
Differences identified:  
- Baseline: fixed stimulation parameters adjusted during clinic visits.  
- Candidate: stimulation policy updated according to biomarkers and symptom-state estimates.  
- Novel delta: use slower-timescale mood prediction models rather than moment-to-moment symptom triggers alone.  
- Risk: causal contribution of stimulation versus spontaneous mood variation may be hard to isolate.  

```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "idea": "closed-loop DBS strategy for TRD using intracranial LFPs, wearable physiology, actigraphy, and ecological mood reports"
}
```

[result]  
Novelty assessment: moderate.  
- Closed-loop psychiatric DBS has precedent.  
- Combining implant-derived neural signals with wearable/digital phenotyping for TRD remains experimentally important.  
- Stronger novelty would come from a staged trial comparing fixed DBS, biomarker-informed DBS, and reinforcement-learning-guided adaptive DBS.  

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "schema": "gene_genome",
  "content": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Closed-loop DBS for treatment-resistant depression would adapt stimulation parameters in cortico-limbic targets such as subcallosal cingulate cortex or ventral capsule/ventral striatum based on inferred depressive state. The central mechanism is that pathological affective-network states can be detected from intracranial local field potentials, autonomic physiology, sleep/activity patterns, and ecological mood ratings, then disrupted or stabilized through personalized stimulation.",
    "niche_genome": "Adults with severe treatment-resistant depression already eligible for DBS implantation, especially participants with chronic symptoms despite pharmacotherapy, psychotherapy, electroconvulsive therapy, or ketamine/esketamine trials. The study niche is an early-phase translational neuroscience trial emphasizing feasibility, safety, and biomarker validation rather than immediate pivotal efficacy.",
    "observation_genome": "Depressive worsening is expected to coincide with convergent signatures such as reduced activity, disrupted sleep regularity, altered heart-rate variability, increased negative affect reports, and patient-specific limbic electrophysiological patterns. A closed-loop system can be tested by first collecting open-loop stimulation and baseline data, then training individualized classifiers to estimate symptom-state transitions.",
    "limitation_genome": "The major limitations are small sample size, heterogeneity of depression circuits, noisy self-report labels, delayed mood responses to stimulation, and ethical risks from autonomous parameter changes. The study should therefore impose conservative stimulation bounds, clinician oversight, sham or crossover periods where feasible, and predefined stopping criteria for hypomania, suicidality, anxiety worsening, or cognitive adverse effects.",
    "delta_genome": "The key experimental delta is to compare three conditions: standard fixed DBS, biomarker-informed scheduled adjustment, and closed-loop adaptive adjustment. The adaptive policy would initially use rule-based thresholds and later evaluate model-predictive or reinforcement-learning approaches only after safety and biomarker reliability are established. Data sources would include intracranial LFPs, wearable actigraphy, sleep metrics, heart-rate variability, smartphone ecological momentary assessments, and clinician-rated depression scales.",
    "claim_genome": "If successful, the project would show that multimodal biomarkers can safely guide adaptive DBS in treatment-resistant depression and produce stronger symptom reduction, faster relapse detection, fewer stimulation-related side effects, and better personalization than conventional open-loop DBS."
  }
}
```

content:
I’ll first gather evidence around Huntington’s disease repeat expansion biology, modifier genes, somatic instability, and therapeutic targets, then synthesize it into a genome-style idea representation.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"Huntington disease HTT CAG repeat expansion modifier genes somatic instability therapeutic targets"}
```

[result]
Found sources:
1. “Genetic Modifiers of Huntington’s Disease” — discusses FAN1, MLH1, MSH3, PMS1/PMS2, and DNA repair pathways as modifiers of age at onset.
2. “Somatic instability of the expanded HTT CAG repeat in Huntington disease” — reviews tissue-specific repeat expansion, striatal vulnerability, mismatch repair mechanisms.
3. “Therapeutic approaches for Huntington’s disease” — covers antisense oligonucleotides, RNAi, allele-selective silencing, gene editing, lowering mutant huntingtin, and DNA repair targeting.

```action
{"tool":"read","source":"Genetic Modifiers of Huntington’s Disease"}
```

[result]
The article reports that genome-wide association studies have identified DNA maintenance genes as modifiers of Huntington’s disease onset. FAN1 variants can delay onset, while mismatch repair genes including MSH3, MLH1, PMS1, and PMS2 influence onset timing. The findings suggest that inherited variation affects the rate of somatic CAG expansion, thereby changing disease trajectory.

```action
{"tool":"read","source":"Somatic instability of the expanded HTT CAG repeat in Huntington disease"}
```

[result]
Expanded HTT CAG repeats are unstable in somatic tissues, especially in brain regions such as the striatum and cortex. Somatic expansion is promoted by mismatch repair activity, particularly MutSβ involving MSH2/MSH3, and downstream repair factors. The article emphasizes that repeat length in vulnerable neurons may continue to increase over life, potentially crossing toxicity thresholds.

```action
{"tool":"read","source":"Therapeutic approaches for Huntington’s disease"}
```

[result]
Therapeutic entry points include lowering mutant HTT using antisense oligonucleotides, RNA interference, viral delivery platforms, allele-selective targeting of SNP-linked mutant alleles, splice modulation, CRISPR-based editing, and targeting DNA repair pathways to slow somatic expansion. Clinical challenges include delivery to the brain, long-term safety, allele selectivity, and biomarker selection.

```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","text":"HTT CAG repeat expansion causes Huntington disease; modifier genes include FAN1, MSH3, MLH1, PMS1, PMS2; somatic instability is driven by mismatch repair and DNA repair mechanisms; therapeutic entry points include HTT lowering, allele-selective silencing, CRISPR editing, and modulation of somatic expansion."}
```

[result]
Extracted genome:
{
  "mechanism_genome": "HTT CAG repeat expansion and somatic repeat instability driven by DNA repair and mismatch repair pathways",
  "niche_genome": "Huntington disease neurodegeneration in striatal and cortical neurons",
  "observation_genome": "Modifier genes in DNA maintenance pathways alter age at onset and disease progression",
  "limitation_genome": "Current HTT-lowering strategies face delivery, selectivity, and long-term safety challenges",
  "delta_genome": "Shift from static inherited repeat length to dynamic somatic expansion as a therapeutic substrate",
  "claim_genome": "Targeting somatic instability and HTT expression may slow Huntington disease progression"
}

```action
{"tool":"genome_diff","genome_a":"HTT CAG expansion / modifier genes / somatic instability / therapeutic entry points","genome_b":"general Huntington disease neurodegeneration model"}
```

[result]
Difference summary:
- Adds modifier gene architecture beyond HTT itself.
- Emphasizes somatic repeat instability as an ongoing pathogenic process.
- Identifies DNA repair/mismatch repair as modifiable pathways.
- Adds therapeutic entry points beyond symptomatic care.

```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","idea":"Use modifier-gene-informed somatic repeat instability biomarkers to stratify Huntington disease patients for HTT lowering or DNA repair pathway modulation."}
```

[result]
Novelty assessment:
The idea is moderately novel. Prior studies have linked modifier genes and somatic instability to age at onset, and therapeutic programs already target HTT lowering. The combination of modifier genotype, somatic instability biomarkers, and therapy stratification remains an active translational opportunity but is not entirely unprecedented.

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Alpha-synuclein propagation through gut-derived vagal pathways drives mitochondrial depolarization and dopamine neuron loss in sporadic Parkinson’s disease.","niche_genome":"Prodromal Parkinson’s disease patients with constipation, REM sleep behavior disorder, and microbiome dysbiosis.","observation_genome":"Short-chain fatty acid imbalance and intestinal permeability correlate with neuroinflammation and motor conversion risk.","limitation_genome":"Current dopaminergic therapies improve symptoms but do not restore gut barrier integrity or halt peripheral-to-central protein spread.","delta_genome":"Reframe disease interception around enteric immune-metabolic resetting rather than central neurotransmitter replacement.","claim_genome":"A synbiotic plus vagal-inflammatory biomarker strategy can delay conversion from prodromal to clinical Parkinson’s disease."},"idea_plan":{"Idea":"Develop a microbiome-directed synbiotic intervention to formulation enriched for butyrate-producing taxa for 24 weeks.","4":"Monitor vagal tone, inflammatory biomarkers, stool metabolomics, and motor/non-motor symptom progression.","5":"Compare alpha-synuclein seeding activity before and after intervention."},"ImplementationOrder":["Cohort screening","Baseline microbiome and biomarker profiling","Synbiotic personalization","Intervention and clinical phenotyping.","EvaluationMetrics":{"Primary":"Change in alpha-synuclein seeding activity and intestinal permeability markers","Secondary":"Change in constipation severity, REM sleep behavior disorder score, inflammatory cytokines, and UPDRS motor score","Exploratory":"Microbiome diversity, butyrate abundance, and vagal tone indices"},"ExpectedOutcome":"The intervention will content:
I’ll gather known regulators of oligodendrocyte differentiation and remyelination in MS, then synthesize them into a gene_genome-style output.

```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "genes regulating oligodendrocyte differentiation remyelination multiple sclerosis OPC differentiation"
}
```

[result] Found recurring gene targets and pathways: OLIG1, OLIG2, SOX10, MYRF, NKX2-2, PDGFRA, CSPG4/NG2, LINGO1, RXRG, TCF7L2, ID2/ID4, NOTCH1/HES5, WNT/CTNNB1, BMP4/SMAD, CXCR4, CXCL12, CNTF, BDNF, NRG1/ERBB signaling, MOG, MBP, PLP1, MAG.

I’ll read summaries on transcription factors and inhibitory pathways relevant to remyelination failure.

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "source": "review_articles",
  "focus": "oligodendrocyte lineage transcription factors and MS remyelination failure"
}
```

[result] OLIG2 and SOX10 maintain oligodendrocyte lineage identity; MYRF drives terminal differentiation and myelin gene expression. OLIG1 nuclear translocation is associated with repair/remyelination. Inhibitory pathways include LINGO1, Notch, Wnt/beta-catenin, BMP signaling, and inflammatory cytokine-related pathways.

Next I’ll extract an initial genome-style representation.

```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "text": "Oligodendrocyte precursor cells differentiate into mature myelinating oligodendrocytes via lineage transcription factors such as OLIG2, SOX10, NKX2-2, and MYRF, while remyelination in MS is limited by chronic lesion inflammation, inhibitory pathways including LINGO1, Notch/HES5, Wnt/CTNNB1, and BMP/SMAD signaling, and failure of OPC recruitment or maturation."
}
```

[result] Draft genome: mechanism = transcriptional and signaling regulation of OPC differentiation; niche = MS lesions and remyelination; observation = pro-differentiation genes and inhibitory pathways identified; limitation = human lesion heterogeneity; delta = integrate pro- and anti-differentiation regulators; claim = targeting inhibitory pathways or boosting lineage programs may enhance remyelination.

I’ll compare this draft against what a complete gene_genome should contain.

```action
{
  "tool": "genome_diff",
  "schema": "gene_genome",
  "required_fields": [
    "mechanism_genome",
    "niche_genome",
    "observation_genome",
    "limitation_genome",
    "delta_genome",
    "claim_genome"
  ],
  "draft": "transcriptional and signaling regulation of OPC differentiation in MS lesions"
}
```

[result] Missing structured separation among mechanism, niche, observation, limitation, delta, and claim. Needs explicit gene examples and MS-specific context.

I’ll check novelty of the synthesized angle.

```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "idea": "A gene_genome framing that combines positive oligodendrocyte lineage regulators with inhibitory lesion-state pathways to prioritize remyelination targets in MS."
}
```

[result] Moderately novel as a synthesis; individual genes and pathways are well established, but combined genome-style framing may be useful for hypothesis generation.

Now I’ll propose the final gene_genome output.

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "gene_genome": "Mechanism: oligodendrocyte differentiation and remyelination are controlled by OLIG1/OLIG2, SOX10, NKX2-2, MYRF, and downstream myelin genes MBP, MOG, MAG, PLP1, while MS lesions contain inhibitory signals such as LINGO1, NOTCH1-HES5, WNT/CTNNB1, BMP4-SMAD, ID2/ID4, and inflammatory cytokine programs that block OPC maturation. Niche: chronic active and inactive MS plaques with recruited PDGFRA+ CSPG4/NG2+ OPCs that often fail to become mature myelinating oligodendrocytes. Observation: pro-differentiation regulators increase lineage commitment and myelin production, whereas LINGO1 blockade, modulation of RXRG, suppression of Wnt/BMP/Notch signaling, or enhancement of trophic signals like BDNF, CNTF, NRG1-ERBB may improve remyelination. Limitation: gene effects vary by lesion stage, inflammatory state, age, species model, and whether the readout is OPC recruitment, differentiation, or functional myelin repair. Delta: prioritize combinations such as activating SOX10/MYRF/RXRG while inhibiting LINGO1/Wnt/BMP/Notch in human MS lesion-like systems. Claim: remyelination failure in MS is best modeled as an imbalance between intrinsic OPC lineage transcriptional programs and extrinsic inhibitory lesion signals.",
  "extra_notes": "Important genes: OLIG1, OLIG2, SOX10, MYRF, NKX2-2, PDGFRA, CSPG4, LINGO1, RXRG, TCF7L2, NOTCH1, HES5, CTNNB1, BMP4, ID2, ID4, MBP, MOG, MAG, PLP1."
}
```

content:
I’ll search for current evidence linking cortical interneuron classes to visual critical period plasticity, then extract a gene_genome and draft an experimental idea integrating single-cell and circuit approaches.

```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "cortical interneuron diversity critical period plasticity visual cortex single cell circuit mechanisms parvalbumin somatostatin VIP",
  "discipline": "neuroscience"
}
```

[result]
Found relevant themes:
- PV interneuron maturation gates onset of ocular dominance plasticity.
- Perineuronal nets and GABAergic inhibition regulate closure of the critical period.
- SST and VIP interneurons modulate dendritic inhibition and disinhibitory circuits.
- Single-cell RNA-seq has revealed molecularly distinct interneuron subclasses in visual cortex.
- Circuit-level approaches include optogenetics, two-photon calcium imaging, patch-seq, and monocular deprivation assays.

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "ids": [
    "Hensch_2005_critical_period_inhibition",
    "Kuhlman_2013_PV_visual_plasticity",
    "Takesian_Hensch_2013_balancing_plasticity_stability",
    "Tasic_2018_mouse_visual_cortex_scRNAseq",
    "Fu_2015_VIP_disinhibition_visual_cortex"
  ]
}
```

[result]
Key notes:
- Enhancing inhibition can trigger early critical period plasticity.
- PV interneurons are strongly implicated in critical period timing and ocular dominance plasticity.
- VIP interneurons can disinhibit pyramidal neurons through suppression of SST cells.
- SST cells regulate dendritic integration and may shape input-specific plasticity.
- scRNA-seq studies identify multiple transcriptomic subclasses within PV, SST, VIP, Lamp5, and other interneuron families.
- Gap: many studies treat interneuron groups broadly rather than linking molecular subtype identity to circuit function during critical period transitions.

```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "paper_notes": {
    "mechanism": "Interneuron maturation and subtype-specific inhibition regulate timing and expression of visual cortical critical period plasticity.",
    "niche": "Mouse primary visual cortex during pre-critical, peak critical, and post-critical period stages.",
    "observation": "PV interneuron maturation gates ocular dominance plasticity; VIP/SST circuits regulate dendritic and disinhibitory components of visual responses.",
    "limitation": "Limited mapping from transcriptomic interneuron diversity to causal circuit functions across critical period stages.",
    "delta": "Combine single-cell profiling, spatial transcriptomics, circuit recording, and perturbation to map subtype-specific interneuron contributions.",
    "claim": "Distinct interneuron subclasses may control separate phases or features of critical period plasticity."
  }
}
```

[result]
```json
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Subtype-specific inhibitory circuit maturation regulates the opening, expression, and closure of visual cortical critical period plasticity.",
  "niche_genome": "Developing mouse primary visual cortex across pre-critical, peak critical period, and post-critical period stages, with focus on GABAergic interneuron subclasses.",
  "observation_genome": "PV interneuron maturation gates ocular dominance plasticity, while SST and VIP interneuron circuits shape dendritic inhibition, disinhibition, and visual response gain.",
  "limitation_genome": "Existing work often links broad interneuron classes to plasticity but does not causally connect transcriptomic interneuron subclasses to circuit-level computations during critical period transitions.",
  "delta_genome": "Integrate single-cell RNA-seq or multiome profiling with in vivo circuit imaging, optogenetic perturbation, and monocular deprivation to identify subtype-specific regulators of plasticity.",
  "claim_genome": "Molecularly distinct interneuron subclasses make separable causal contributions to the timing and form of visual cortical critical period plasticity."
}
```

```action
{
  "tool": "genome_diff",
  "baseline_genome": {
    "mechanism_genome": "PV-mediated inhibition gates visual cortical critical period plasticity.",
    "niche_genome": "Mouse V1 ocular dominance plasticity.",
    "observation_genome": "PV maturation and perineuronal nets correlate with critical period opening and closure.",
    "limitation_genome": "Focuses mainly on PV interneurons.",
    "delta_genome": "Manipulate inhibition to alter critical period timing.",
    "claim_genome": "Inhibition controls critical period plasticity."
  },
  "candidate_genome": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Subtype-specific inhibitory circuit maturation regulates the opening, expression, and closure of visual cortical critical period plasticity.",
    "niche_genome": "Developing mouse primary visual cortex across pre-critical, peak critical period, and post-critical period stages, with focus on GABAergic interneuron subclasses.",
    "observation_genome": "PV interneuron maturation gates ocular dominance plasticity, while SST and VIP interneuron circuits shape dendritic inhibition, disinhibition, and visual response gain.",
    "limitation_genome": "Existing work often links broad interneuron classes to plasticity but does not causally connect transcriptomic interneuron subclasses to circuit-level computations during critical period transitions.",
    "delta_genome": "Integrate single-cell RNA-seq or multiome profiling with in vivo circuit imaging, optogenetic perturbation, and monocular deprivation to identify subtype-specific regulators of plasticity.",
    "claim_genome": "Molecularly distinct interneuron subclasses make separable causal contributions to the timing and form of visual cortical critical period plasticity."
  }
}
```

[result]
Novel differences:
- Moves beyond PV-centered model to transcriptomic subclasses across PV, SST, VIP, Lamp5, and possibly Sncg/Serpinf1 interneurons.
- Connects molecular identity to in vivo functional plasticity.
- Introduces stage-resolved analysis: pre-critical, peak critical, post-critical.
- Adds causal perturbation at subclass level rather than broad inhibitory manipulation.

```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "idea": "Use single-cell multiome and spatial transcriptomics to identify interneuron subclasses whose maturation predicts ocular dominance plasticity, then causally test them with subtype-targeted optogenetics/chemogenetics and two-photon imaging during monocular deprivation.",
  "criteria": {
    "not_only_PV": true,
    "single_cell_component": true,
    "circuit_component": true,
    "critical_period_visual_cortex": true,
    "causal_perturbation": true
  }
}
```

[result]
Novelty score: moderate-high.
Risk:
- Genetic access to fine interneuron transcriptomic subclasses may be difficult.
- Single-cell dissociation may distort activity-dependent transcription.
- Ocular dominance plasticity assays require careful developmental timing.
Recommended refinement:
- Use Patch-seq or targeted spatial transcriptomics to bridge molecular type and circuit function.
- Use intersectional viral strategies or enhancer-based AAVs for finer subtype targeting.
- Compare normal development with dark exposure or enriched environment to separate maturation from sensory-experience-dependent plasticity.

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",

content:
rationale: I’ll first survey core findings on hippocampal cross-frequency coupling during navigation and memory retrieval, then extract reusable “genome” elements, compare them, check novelty, and end with a proposed idea_plan.

```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "hippocampal theta gamma cross-frequency coupling spatial navigation episodic memory retrieval"
}
```

[result]  
Found relevant themes: hippocampal theta phase organizing gamma amplitude during navigation; slow/fast gamma channels linked to CA3/entorhinal inputs; theta-gamma coupling implicated in episodic encoding and retrieval; human intracranial EEG studies show memory-related hippocampal PAC.

```action
{
  "tool": "read",
  "paper": "Hippocampal theta-gamma coupling during spatial navigation and memory retrieval"
}
```

[result]  
Paper summary: Theta phase modulates gamma bursts during active navigation. Gamma sub-bands may correspond to distinct routing of information: slow gamma often associated with CA3-mediated retrieval/completion, fast gamma with entorhinal sensory/spatial input. During episodic retrieval, hippocampal theta-gamma coupling increases around successful recollection.

```action
{
  "tool": "extract_genome",
  "source": "navigation and episodic retrieval PAC literature",
  "schema": [
    "mechanism_genome",
    "niche_genome",
    "observation_genome",
    "limitation_genome",
    "delta_genome",
    "claim_genome"
  ]
}
```

[result]  
```json
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Theta phase coordinates gamma-band activity to segment and route hippocampal computations.",
  "niche_genome": "Rodent and human hippocampus during spatial navigation and episodic memory tasks.",
  "observation_genome": "Theta-gamma PAC increases during navigation, encoding, and successful memory retrieval.",
  "limitation_genome": "Many studies examine either navigation or episodic retrieval separately, often with different species, tasks, and recording modalities.",
  "delta_genome": "A unified within-subject design could test whether the same PAC signatures generalize across spatial and episodic retrieval contexts.",
  "claim_genome": "Cross-frequency coupling may be a shared neural code for organizing hippocampal spatial and episodic representations."
}
```

```action
{
  "tool": "search",
  "query": "theta slow gamma fast gamma CA3 entorhinal hippocampus retrieval navigation"
}
```

[result]  
Found evidence that slow gamma may index CA3-to-CA1 communication and memory retrieval, whereas fast gamma may index entorhinal-to-CA1 input and current spatial/environmental information.

```action
{
  "tool": "genome_diff",
  "genome_a": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Theta phase coordinates gamma-band activity to segment and route hippocampal computations.",
    "niche_genome": "Spatial navigation and episodic memory retrieval.",
    "observation_genome": "PAC changes with navigation and recollection.",
    "limitation_genome": "Navigation and episodic retrieval are often studied separately.",
    "delta_genome": "Test shared and dissociable PAC signatures across both domains.",
    "claim_genome": "Theta-gamma coupling provides a common organizational code."
  },
  "genome_b": {
    "mechanism_genome": "Slow and fast gamma reflect partially distinct hippocampal input streams.",
    "niche_genome": "CA1 interactions with CA3 and entorhinal cortex.",
    "observation_genome": "Slow gamma is associated with retrieval/completion; fast gamma with encoding/current sensory-spatial input.",
    "limitation_genome": "Sub-band interpretations vary across species and task designs.",
    "delta_genome": "Separate slow-gamma and fast-gamma PAC during both navigation and episodic retrieval.",
    "claim_genome": "Gamma sub-band-specific PAC can reveal computational state transitions in hippocampal memory."
  }
}
```

[result]  
Key difference: one genome emphasizes general PAC as a shared code; the other emphasizes gamma sub-band specificity. Promising synthesis: design a study that tests whether slow- and fast-gamma PAC differentially support spatial navigation, episodic encoding, and episodic retrieval within the same participants or animals.

```action
{
  "tool": "novelty_check",
  "idea": "Within-subject study comparing hippocampal theta-slow-gamma and theta-fast-gamma coupling during virtual spatial navigation, episodic encoding, and episodic retrieval."
}
```

[result]  
Novelty assessment: moderately novel. Prior work links theta-gamma coupling to navigation or memory separately, but fewer studies directly compare spatial navigation and episodic retrieval using matched behavioral structure, sub-band-specific PAC, and representational decoding in the same subjects.

rationale: Based on the simulated literature scan, the strongest plan is to unify navigation and episodic retrieval under one analytic framework, focusing on theta phase coupling to slow versus fast gamma amplitude.

```action
{
  "tool": "propose",
  "args": {
    "idea_plan": "Idea: Study whether hippocampal theta phase organizes slow-gamma and fast-gamma amplitude differently during spatial navigation versus episodic retrieval. ImplementationSteps: collect intracranial EEG or high-density rodent hippocampal LFP during virtual navigation, object-location encoding, and cued episodic retrieval; preprocess signals; compute PAC; split gamma into slow and fast bands; align coupling to behavioral epochs; decode location and memory success; compare PAC patterns across tasks. ImplementationOrder = task design -> recording -> preprocessing -> PAC analysis -> decoding -> statistics. Dataset: human epilepsy iEEG if available, otherwise rodent CA1/CA3 LFP in virtual or real maze plus memory retrieval task. Metrics include modulation index, phase-locking value, decoding accuracy, retrieval success correlation, mixed-effects statistics. ExpectedOutcome: slow-gamma PAC should increase during retrieval and route-completion states, fast-gamma PAC should increase during active sensory-guided navigation and encoding.",
    "gene_genome": "This project is about theta-gamma coupling in the hippocampus. Mechanism is theta organizing gamma bursts. The niche is spatial navigation and episodic memory. Observations are that PAC rises in navigation and successful recall. The limitation is prior studies are fragmented. Delta is to compare both tasks in one design. Claim is that sub-band PAC indexes hippocampal computational mode.",
    "extra_field_confidence": "pretty good",
    "notes": {
      "possible_title": "Theta-Gamma Sub-band Coupling as a Shared Code for Hippocampal Spatial and Episodic Retrieval"
    }
  }
}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Develop an idea_plan for studying cross-frequency coupling in the hippocampus during spatial navigation and episodic memory retrieval.

content:
Rationale: I will survey evidence on early-life inflammation, spinal dorsal horn sensitization, glial priming, and cortical pain-circuit plasticity, then synthesize a testable neuroscience proposal.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"early life inflammation long term pain sensitivity spinal cord cortical plasticity microglia"}
```

[result]  
Found studies linking neonatal inflammatory insult to persistent mechanical/thermal hypersensitivity, altered spinal dorsal horn synaptic transmission, microglial priming, and changes in cortical pain-processing regions including somatosensory cortex, anterior cingulate cortex, and prefrontal cortex.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper":"Neonatal inflammation and persistent alteration of nociceptive processing"}
```

[result]  
Key points extracted: neonatal inflammatory injury can lower adult pain thresholds; spinal cord shows enhanced excitatory transmission and altered inhibitory tone; effects may depend on sex, timing, and immune challenge type.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper":"Microglial priming after early-life immune activation and chronic pain vulnerability"}
```

[result]  
Early immune activation may create a latent primed glial state. Later injury or stress can trigger exaggerated cytokine release, BDNF signaling, and synaptic remodeling in dorsal horn circuits.

```action
{"tool":"read","paper":"Cortical plasticity after early-life pain and inflammation"}
```

[result]  
Evidence suggests early inflammatory pain changes maturation of cortical pain networks. Reported alterations include somatosensory map reorganization, anterior cingulate hyperexcitability, and altered descending modulation from prefrontal/brainstem systems.

```action
{"tool":"extract_genome","text":"Early-life inflammation may induce latent spinal and cortical plasticity that increases lifelong pain vulnerability."}
```

[result]  
mechanism_genome: neonatal inflammation → immune/glial priming → altered spinal excitation/inhibition → cortical pain-network remodeling → persistent hypersensitivity  
niche_genome: developmental neuroscience, chronic pain vulnerability, early immune challenge  
observation_genome: adult animals exposed to neonatal inflammation show lower pain thresholds and altered nociceptive processing  
limitation_genome: unclear causal ordering between spinal and cortical plasticity  
delta_genome: need longitudinal multimodal mapping from early inflammation to adult pain phenotype  
claim_genome: developmental inflammatory events bias nociceptive circuits toward persistent sensitization

```action
{"tool":"genome_diff","baseline":"early-life inflammation causes persistent spinal sensitization","candidate":"early-life inflammation produces coordinated spinal-cortical plasticity that determines adult pain sensitivity"}
```

[result]  
Novel delta: shifts from spinal-only sensitization to a systems-level developmental model linking dorsal horn plasticity, cortical maturation, and descending pain modulation.  
Potential weakness: requires longitudinal causal evidence rather than cross-sectional correlation.

```action
{"tool":"novelty_check","claim":"Early-life inflammation gates adult pain vulnerability through staged spinal microglial priming followed by cortical pain-network remodeling."}
```

[result]  
Partially novel. Prior work supports neonatal inflammation, spinal sensitization, and cortical changes individually. Less established is the temporal-causal model in which spinal microglial priming drives later cortical reweighting of pain networks.

```action
{"tool":"propose","Idea":"Early-life inflammation creates a latent pain-vulnerability state by first priming spinal dorsal horn microglia and excitation/inhibition balance, then altering maturation of cortical pain circuits such as S1, ACC, and mPFC. This spinal-cortical developmental cascade increases adult pain sensitivity and susceptibility to chronic pain after later-life injury.","ImplementationSteps":{"1_Model":"Use a neonatal inflammatory challenge model, such as hindpaw carrageenan or LPS exposure, with sham controls.","2_LongitudinalPhenotyping":"Measure mechanical and thermal sensitivity across development into adulthood.","3_SpinalMechanisms":"Assess dorsal horn microglial activation, cytokine expression, BDNF-TrkB signaling, excitatory synaptic strength, and inhibitory interneuron function.","4_CorticalPlasticity":"Use in vivo calcium imaging, electrophysiology, or fMRI-like functional mapping to examine S1, ACC, and mPFC responses to sensory stimulation over development.","5_CausalPerturbation":"Block neonatal microglial activation or inflammatory cytokine signaling and test whether adult cortical remodeling and pain hypersensitivity are prevented.","6_DescendingModulation":"Evaluate whether altered prefrontal or brainstem descending control mediates adult hypersensitivity after early inflammation."},"ImplementationOrder":["Establish neonatal inflammation model","Perform longitudinal pain-behavior assays","Profile spinal dorsal horn immune and synaptic changes","Map cortical pain-circuit maturation","Apply early anti-inflammatory or microglial-targeted intervention","Test adult reinjury or stress challenge","Model spinal-cortical predictors of pain sensitivity"],"Dataset":"Longitudinal mouse or rat cohort with neonatal inflammation/sham treatment, developmental behavioral pain measures, spinal cord immunohistochemistry/RNA-seq/electrophysiology, cortical imaging or electrophysiology, and adult reinjury challenge outcomes.","EvaluationMetrics":{"Behavioral":"Mechanical withdrawal threshold, thermal latency, spontaneous pain-like behavior, adult reinjury-induced hypersensitivity duration","Spinal":"Microglial morphology, Iba1/P2RY12/TMEM119 markers, cytokine levels, BDNF-TrkB signaling, EPSC/IPSC balance in dorsal horn neurons","Cortical":"Evoked sensory response amplitude, cortical map size, ACC/mPFC excitability, functional connectivity between pain-related cortical regions","Causal":"Degree to which neonatal immune blockade prevents adult hypersensitivity and cortical remodeling","Predictive":"Accuracy of early spinal inflammatory markers in predicting adult pain sensitivity"},"ExpectedOutcome":"Early-life inflammation will produce persistent but initially latent spinal immune priming and altered dorsal horn synaptic balance. During maturation, these spinal changes will be associated with abnormal cortical pain-network development and weakened descending inhibitory control. Animals with the strongest early spinal immune signatures will show the greatest adult pain hypersensitivity, especially after a second injury or stressor."}
```

prompt.full_prompt: Explore how early-life inflammation shapes long-term pain sensitivity through spinal cord and cortical plasticity.
